name: Switzer Falls Trail
formatted_address: 701 Angeles Crest Hwy, Tujunga, CA 91042, USA

google_reviews.text:
This trail after the storms has been transformed to an epic waterfall hike with Emerald green rock pools, fresh snow melt, and Mountain Views that take your breath away. HikeBytes made a YouTube video showing how to get to lesser known routes to the upper falls and the pools, including route over
lower falls and through canyon to base of the powerful Upper Switzer fall. It’s in season NOW. View conditions @hikebytes YouTube channel.

This place is one of my favorite hikes cause the waterfall at the end is well worth it and its a great place to take some gr8 pictures. The trail it self is challenging which i like it has alot of incline on the way there and back and when you get to the waterfall there is a way for you to get on top of the waterfall from the right hand side of the trail which leads to more hikes.

One of the most amazing hike trail in LA! It starts very easily and then from the middle part, u can choose hike to the mountain(which is kinda dangerous and difficult) to see the fall or hike back just enjoy the chill atmosphere!

This trail is 70% shaded by the forest 🌳 but I recommend put fly repellent on! Casue the creek very runing slow, mosquitoes are crazy here!

Also I recommend come here early Casue parking space are limited! U can park right next to the trail bridge at the very bottom!
Nice Trail that a lot of shade along with the creek. Also have challenges that some part of trail just located next to the cliff. Easy not easy. But fun.
Nice trail along the creek it is not hard but at some point if you go all the way to waterfall trail is nerrow and some people who afraid of heights cannot make it

name: Palos Verdes Estates Shoreline Preserve
formatted_address: 809 Paseo Del Mar, Palos Verdes Estates, CA 90274, USA

google_reviews.text: A great place to relax and unwind.  Perfect views of the Pacific.   Grassy, with walkways.   Perfect for picnics.   Dogs welcomed! (Just clean up after your pets!)
Views that will stay in your mind forever, relaxing amazing place to ease your mind and enjoy with family and friends 🧡
One of my favorite stops when traveling around Palos Verdes!

Their are few places on the California coast that offer expensive views of what is called the Queens Necklace. Palos Verdes to Point Mago.
Also views views of Catalina Island, and sometimes, the off shore islands. This Palos Verdes road wraps around some of most expensive views homes on the coast. Stop in at Terranea, and indulge in happy hour as the sun sets.

Great place to stop, Smell The Roses🌹, Look At The Clouds & take in the view. Especially if you're on a Sportbike, which I was. (Yamaha R1). Pictures attached of one of my favorites to ride & which was a rental from my Brother from Another Mother, Jeff Marchesani.
If you're reading this Jeff, Take Care & I love ❤️ you, Brother ❣️

Don't forget your prayers & to feed the poor.

Best Regards from The Baddest Doctor Around.

name: Turnbull Canyon
formatted_address: Turnbull Canyon Rd, Whittier, CA 90601, USA

google_reviews.text: This place kicked my butt in the best possible way! I was feeling bold, so I took the trail up to the antenna tower with all the graffiti. It’s a very steep uphill climb almost the entire way with little tree cover, so make sure you’re up for it! I’ll definitely be returning to explore some of the other trails that I missed.

Park before trailhead. Elementary school nearby has small lot on weekends. No sidewalk and watch for blind spots on turns before trailhead. No bathrooms or shaded rest areas. Bring water and hat or some sort of shade. Minimal tree cover. Good view of city from both sides of the hilltop. Trail has trail posts to mark trail names but no signs. You might get turned into maintenance roads and get trapped by barbed fences. Doable without poles. From school up to water tower and back is 8 miles. If you go down by Forrest lawn and back, it’s 12.
The view is amazing I will totally be coming back!

Limited parking because of residential permits.
About 2 blocks or 10 minutes away from trail, Free or Street parking available near Hoover Elementary School on Alta Ave.
Turnbull Canyon, Rattlesnake Ridge, and Sumac Trail Loop
Did the whole loop. Counter clockwise is shaded incline with an easy decline down. Beautiful hike & midway was great views. Also, saw a small snake.
Graffiti ruins the signs trail info. & too much dog poop. No restrooms.

Good trail. Easy enough for the novice and challenging for the more advanced hiker by simply picking up the pace.
3 stars, in my opinion, a good trail head in an urban environment should be accessible. Permit parking surrounding a major trailhead? Very sad Whittier. Won’t be back.

name: Trail Canyon Falls
formatted_address: Tujunga, CA 91042, USA

google_reviews.text: Fun hike! Out and back, have to cross the creek at least 6 times and the rope support down hill to the waterfall base is a fun little adventure. The waterfall was full and rushing, cold but very refreshing. Did the hike in the afternoon, half the hike was out in the open and in the sun but still lots of shade throughout. Be ready for a climb about halfway through that gets you to a nice overlook view of the fall. Lots of dogs on the trail, be ready to assist up and down the drop to the base of the waterfall. Please remember to pack out your trash and doggie bags!

Beautiful hike I did with a friend on 2/9/26. We both had that Monday off of work and he talked me into joining him on this waterfall hike. We arrived at the trailhead around 7:40am and were the first people on the Trailhead. There are many creek crossings on this trail, so bring waterproof hiking boots or a pair of river shoes with good tread. I broke in a new pair of Keen boots and my feet stayed dry throughout the hike despite stepping well into water many times. Much of the trail is unshaded so a full brimmed hat is recommended.

It was rewarding coming on a weekday and starting early because we had the trail all to ourselves on our way in and the waterfall all to ourselves for over an hour except for 15-20 minutes when another couple showed up for a bit and then left. On the hike back, after 10:30am we saw more pairs and small groups hiking in. The trail is fairly easy, about 4.5 miles out and back. I'd say it's safe to include children as long as you're prepared for the creek crossings and know to expect the steep descent to the falls using the ropes. Definitely one I want to do again when it gets a bit warmer.

Did this hike on January 19th, 2026
Super fun hike but pls know that you will have to cross the water many times to get to the waterfall. This part makes it fun but you can be better prepared for this if you have the right shoes! I had my closed toe river shoes with traction which helped me hike and step in the water anytime. Some part of the hike is exposed so bring a hat. Overall, an easy hike! We saw lots of kids and dogs having a good time and playing in the waterfall!

Beautiful place to relax, but please be careful.
WARNING ⚠️⚠️⚠️ to all hikers who park along the side road: My vehicle was broken into and robbed yesterday. It’s clear the people responsible know exactly what they’re doing.
Please do not leave any valuables in your vehicle and be aware that parking in this area puts you at risk of this happening. If you experience anything similar, make sure to file a police report and stay alert.
Stay safe out there.

Trail Canyon Falls in the Angeles National Forest is one of Southern California’s most satisfying waterfall hikes — a rugged, winding escape from the city that rewards you with both scenic tension and lush payoff. From the moment you leave the parking area and follow the creek‑lined path, the trail feels alive with murmuring water, rustling leaves, and the steady rhythm of your boots across dirt and rock. The route climbs gently at first, shaded by sycamores and dotted with refreshing stream crossings, but don’t let the easy start fool you — the terrain grows steeper and more demanding as you approach the falls, with loose soil, exposed roots, and ropes affixed to help hikers descend the final stretch safely.
Reaching the waterfall itself is a moment of quiet triumph. Even when the flow is modest, the cascade crashing into its shallow pool feels like a secret reward hidden deep in the canyon. On warm days, the mist on your face and the cool pool at the base make it worth every uphill step. This hike is best done early — mid‑day sun and summer heat can make the exposed sections challenging — and plenty of water is a must.
Crowds are present on popular weekends, but the trail still offers pockets of solitude and spectacular views that remind you why SoCal hikers keep returning.

name: The Brush Canyon Trail
formatted_address: Canyon Dr, Los Angeles, CA 90068, USA

google_reviews.text:
Visited Brush Canyon Trail on January 13, 2026, and started the hike around 4 PM.

The hike took about 2 hours and 20 minutes round trip, and I walked all the way to the area just below the Hollywood Sign. Parts of the trail were a bit strenuous, but the effort was absolutely worth it.

I was lucky to catch a beautiful sunset, and the entire atmosphere of the hills changed as evening set in. The views were incredible.

On the way back, I even saw a Coyote howling in the distance. It was a little scary at first, but it kept its distance and nothing happened.

The trail wasn’t too crowded during my hike, which made the experience even better. I would definitely do this hike again the next time I visit Los Angeles.

I really loved this trail, it is steep.
There is a ranch there that offers horse rides, there is shade in multiple places.
I had so much fun the views were beautiful and breathtaking at every stop.
The sign itself is cool to hike up to and there's another trail going towards other things it is definitely worth the hike.

Kicked off the New Year with this incredible trek!

Started at Brush Canyon and made my way up to the Hollywood Sign, hit the Mt. Lee peak, and pushed all the way to the Wisdom Tree.

The day started out perfectly clear, but about halfway through, it started drizzling, which actually made the atmosphere even more special. Despite the mist, had an awesome views throughout.

Standing behind the Hollywood Sign letters and then looking out from the Wisdom Tree never gets old. It’s a beautiful hike with great elevation and rewarding vistas of the entire city.

A perfect, refreshing start to the year!

It’s a decent hike to the Hollywood Sign, taking about 2–3 hours and considered easy to moderate difficulty. Near the end, you can take a short detour to view the sign from both the front and the back. There is plenty of parking available at the start of the trail.

Lovely Trail and a favorite when visiting Hollywood!
From the Trailhead to behind the sign, you will take approximately 1 hour depending on your pace. The roundtrip distance is about 5 miles.
The beginning can feel a bit steep (first 30 minutes) but after that, the road  has a moderate elevation gain and you will be amazed by the wonderful city views and some horse riders.
Be aware of the lack of shade. If you arrive early (around 8 or 9 am) some trees and the side of the hill will help to keep you fresh, but around 11 or noon the Trail is mostly exposed. So, bring sunscreen, a hat and water.
Also, it's good to know that portable restrooms are available only at the parking lot and not on the Trail.

name: Ferndell Nature Trail
formatted_address: Fern Dell Dr, Los Angeles, CA 90027, USA

google_reviews.text: This park is thoughtfully designed and offers something for everyone. There are wide grassy areas with shade for relaxing, winding nature paths with flowing water and lush plants, and even spots where you can spot turtles. The playground is well-equipped and great for kids, while the walking trails are perfect for exploring and can even take you toward the observatory. It’s a well-rounded, peaceful place to spend time, whether you live nearby or are just passing through.

Beautiful on a dry and cool January day. It only took about 25min up to Griffith Observatory, with plenty of stops for photos. It's busy but not terrible. We had an easy time getting a parking spot. Lovely little hike.

Epic views of Los Angeles and a beautiful view while exercising! So many trail options and ample parking. A great way to escape the madness of the city and just appreciate nature, a good audio book or podcast. We did about 5mi round trip and got to see the Hollywood sign up close. I recommend bringing sunscreen and water. There’s picnic tables around the park to have lunch or to just chill and sit on a tree stump. Highly recommend this place if you’re in need of connecting with nature and self.

Great trail that leads to longer trails going into griffith park. Weekend is difficult parking in the area but if you're able to, weekdays are manageable.
Love the little coffee shop at the convergence of ferndell and the fire road trail into griffith. Also a park if kids want to run around and play.

Such a tranquil and beautiful nature walk that for a second you forget you're right in the middle of the city. I love being out in nature and surrounded by greenery and taking a leisurely walk with a cup of coffee and this is that place. You may keep going and take a hike up to Griffith observatory from the same path. There's benches around so you can sit down and read a book or just sit and listening to the running water from the little ponds.

name: Fullerton Loop - MTB & Hiking Trail
formatted_address: Fullerton, CA 92832, USA

google_reviews.text: Perfect for an easy stroll.Very minimal incline to begin with and provides multiple routes options. Nestled in the hills of Fullerton the are was clean and safe. Home surround some of the trail but it doesn't take away from the natural beauty of the trail. January after the rain was beautiful.
Been looping these trails for over 15 years and its one of my favorite. Great for beginners and intermediate riders. I even started riding my scooter on the trails.
Great urban trail. All bike skill levels can have fun here. If 10miles is too long there are easy ways to cut off sections.

About 900 feet of vert if going north towards Laguna Lake and coming back via Hiltscher park. It’s a bit less about 850 if turning left at Hiltscher and going clockwise. Only one bathroom at Laguna park. It’s ten miles of gravel, bit of road and horse trails. Saw plenty of hares and squirrels, some goats and cows at a farm, birds at Laguna. You’ll hit some lights at intersections and waited 15 minutes for signals.

Great hiking trail, easy I'd say especially in the first few miles which makes it suitable for families as well. Good parts of the trail are shaded & the trail at the first few miles is quite wide. Saw many mountain bikers since the trail is tempting for such a sport. Later on & after you finish 6 miles of the hike, there will be a bit of hills, so prepare for some climbing & be careful with the steep descending. I finished the whole trail, just over 11 miles in around 4hrs.

name: Big Santa Anita Canyon
formatted_address: Chantry Flat Rd, Arcadia, CA 91006, USA

google_reviews.text: I got here on a weekend while it say's it was open and ended up doing a 10 mile hike to Mt. Wilson. While on the trail you will pass the old Stuevant campsite i think i used it as the halfway mark, i recommend using stuff on your skin to keep bugs off because while i was almost there theres a part that has bugs that will just fly around you and bug making you want to go back but i managed to just get past them and make it to the end. It's a long trail but it isn't too dangerous. Theres a part where you'll probably be at a ledge and i thought the whole thing was kind of hidden but it's really cool to adventure through. The way back is way simpler and faster i think. And oh right after the ledge part theres a few places you could go in the water and relax for a while if needed. You could bring sandwhiches & also; theres no trash cans or litter anywhere.

We went on January 4th 2025 during the holiday season, so there were many cars. Even though parking was paid, there were no available spots. We had no choice but to park along the roadside and walk a long distance. We challenged ourselves to walk along the cycling trail. Later, we saw others doing the same, but unlike them, we completed the entire trail, which took us 4 hours. It’s worth noting that this is a one-way trail, so you must return the same way you came. Along the way, you can see traces of past wildfires. As you get closer to the top (the circular structure), you’ll barely see anyone, and the silence becomes unsettling, especially as the sky quickly darkens around 3 PM. We ended up running downhill. Families with kids are not recommended to take this trail, as it requires significant perseverance.

Good hike with a lot of stream crossings (01/26). Parked along the road near the parking lot and was ticketed. Dozens of other cars parked along the road but we were the only car 30 mins after sunset. Displayed an America the beautiful pass to no avail.

We did Sturtevant falls trail which is about 3.6 miles out and back from chantry flats parking. The first .6 mile of the trail is a steep pavement road which can be a bit challenging while coming back from the hike. Rest of the hike is really pleasant with minimal elevation gain and is shaded throughout. Falls at this time (June end) have less water given the rains this year. But the pool is quite refreshing. Parking here is a headache. We reached around 11 am and had to wait quite a lot to get a free parking space. People had parked on the road way before the actual parking starts. So I would suggest reaching here super early or later in the afternoon when most of the crowd is going back. Overall, a very nice hike. We had 3 kids with us aged 6, 4 and 2.5 yrs. The elder ones had no problem walking the entire trail. We will definitely come back when there is more water to the falls.
The trail to the waterfall was not too hard. Nice view with the sounds of water since your walking alongside the river

name: Los Leones Canyon
formatted_address: 510 Los Liones Dr, Pacific Palisades, CA 90272, USA

google_reviews.text: My favorite hike in LA hands down. Not too long, about 1.5 miles up and 1.5 miles back. Beautiful view at the top that makes it well worth it. I was devastated when it closed after the fires and I'm overjoyed that its back open and so green and vibrant after the recent rains. Plenty of street parking by the trailhead and glclean public restrooms. What more could you ask for.

This is a great hike with little to no shade. Full sun for 4+ miles. Please do not bring your dog who is covered in fur out to hike with you if it is hotter than 70 degrees and please do not bring your dog without a portable bowl and water to drink. I saw so many dogs forced on this hike that were panting like crazy in the 78 degrees heat. It’s way too hot for them! It was insane! Please use your brain for your pets sake.
Great hike! Just the right amount of uphill. Lots of great views of the ocean, LA, PV, Catalina, etc

Open as of today July 25 2025. Sad to walk amongst charred once-was trees but there is new growth.

Dark ash covers the path... it's a cemetery for the former plants & wildlife. I wouldn't take dogs on the trail since the ash will be toxic to their paws as they lick.

On the bright side, the ocean views are now more vast and expansive, starting earlier on the trail than before.

My husband and I completed the hike up and down the mountain in 2 hours and 40 minutes, and we thoroughly enjoyed the experience. Along the way, I noticed some people hiking with their dogs, even though the signs clearly stated that dogs were not allowed. However, the dogs seemed well-trained and didn’t cause any problems.

name: Hacienda Hills Trailhead
formatted_address: S 7th Ave &, Orange Grove Ave, Hacienda Heights, CA 91745, USA

google_reviews.text:
After eating a vegetarian buffet at Hsi Lai Temple, I was ready to walk off those calories.I searched for nearby hiking trails, and this was the closest one that came up. So I decided to give it a try.

The parking lot for the trail has about five or six spots, and I was lucky to grab the last one. There's also street parking available, I believe.

Since it was a spontaneous hike, I didn't bring a water bottle. Iwas planning to hike for about an hour. The trail was beautiful and well-kept. After about an hour, I asked a hiker on her way back how much farther it was. She said probably another half hour. She noticed I didn't have a water bottle and suggested I turn back and bring one next time. I took her advice since it was about 80 degrees in the middle of the day. The funny thing is, it's only February.

I'll probably come back to finish the trail next time -- with a water bottle. I'd recommend this trail -- just come prepared.

Went on a hot day, so the sun was out on the first half, made it to the top to the  water tower with beautiful views, the second half was a nice cool down with a lot trees for shade, free parking and street parking. I recommend it 10/10

One of my favorite trails for hiking yet I haven't memorized the trail names. It rained last night and the trail was still open today which was nice but some areas were very muddy. Apparently there are coyotes and definitely mosquitoes and flies in certain areas. There's very limited parking at the preserve entrance but there's plenty of parking along the school though also limited so get here early! Great views at the top of the trails and some areas feature luscious green growth. There are sometimes birds overhead and you can hear power lines buzzing above. There's a porta-potty bathroom at the entrance as well as WHEELCHAIR ACCESS.

Excellent hike, lots of shade. First 1/3 is a steep climb and switchbacks then a shaded valley with lots of ferns. 3/3 is uphill switchbacks out of the valley. Lots of people complaining about “dog poop” not realizing it’s actually coyote droppings. Note the red berries in their poop is common food for coyotes. Mountain lion droppings have also been found in the area, and if you’re quiet you’ll stumble upon deer! Currently with the rain, lots of poison oak is growing so dress accordingly. Park in the neighborhood or by the middle school.

Dope hike. Numerous trails and a good variety to them. Coyote trail is basically a stairmaster with gorgeous views. Watch out for snakes though!! Also there's a parking lot but there's not much to it, most likely you'll be parking in the street. Small price to pay though, this is a solid hike.

name: Wonder View Trailhead
formatted_address: Wonder View Dr, Los Angeles, CA 90068, USA

google_reviews.text:
Amazing view of LA from the top by the Hollywood Sign!
The hike takes about an hour or even less, and the view at the top is absolutely stunning. If you’ve only seen the Hollywood Sign from the front, I highly recommend coming up here to see it from the back — plus you get incredible panoramic views of the city. It can get windy, so bring something warm. And take your time on the way up — the trail is pretty steep.

The Wonder View Trail is a challenging and steep trail to hike but well worth it for the views at the summit. You can see the Hollywood sign from behind it, and views of both valleys below on a clearer day. Highly recommend if looking for a challenging hike in the Hollywood Hills.

This is the shortest and probably nicest way to hike the Hollywood Sign, and the steepest as well. If the gate near the trailhead is locked, just use the trail on the righr side to go under it. Also, the trail is rocky and steep in parts, but if my grandpa could do it all the way to the Sign and back pretty easily, you probably can as well.
Great hiking trail! Took the long route back to my car! So, hiked to the Wisdom Tree to the Hollywood sign and finally took the road to the car! Great hike, great walk all together!

Lovely hike, mostly on a paved road. The views of the reservoir and big houses are nice, but of course the view of the Hollywood sign is what you come for. We  took the left fork and didn't see much of the sign until turned around halfway through and walked back. It was behind us the whole time! We walked with our little ones (8 and 10) and the 3.6 miles was lot for them, but they did fine. It'sa very flat hike and worth the climb up the hill back to our car.

name: First Water, Mt. Wilson Trail
formatted_address: Mt Wilson Trail, Sierra Madre, CA 91024, USA

google_reviews.text:
Did this hike before Eaton Canyon!

The hike to First Water via Mt. Wilson is a beautiful and relaxing experience. The trail is mostly open to the sun with little shade, so be prepared. The views are stunning, and the journey is well worth it. A great hike for those who enjoy open trails and a peaceful atmosphere!

What a beautiful, serene hiking trail. The views were so gorgeous and the sound of the small waterfall was very soothing. This trail had spectacular views of the mountains and the city. However, it's all uphill. It took us 3.8 miles out/back and I would rate this trail moderate. A great trail to workout your legs and glutes.

Parked in the residential area near trail park (public restrooms, playground) and walked up to the beginning of the trail. It is a popular hike even at 6:00 a.m. on a Saturday. It took us 2 hours roundtrip to First water. It is steep at times and narrow trails at times, but it's worth it!!! There was a small steady stream of water. Bring bug spray, the skeeters were out (near the water). Great way to start the morning!!!

I wasn’t amazed by this trail but it wasn’t too bad either. Some nice views along the way but the trailhead was disappointing given there was no water or anything else to see. Might go back at some point but won’t be my first choice.

Great hike, very steep and not easy for beginners. It's best to get there super early in the morning or after 4 pm on Spring/Summer season, there's very little shade the first 1.5 miles and it can get very hot. Hiking sticks are recommended if you have some

name: Firebreak Trail to Griffith Observatory
formatted_address: Western Canyon Rd, Los Angeles, CA 90068, USA

google_reviews.text: This is such a great adventure. It's free and parking is free as well. Look up early in the morning to go take a hike. You also get very nice views up there. It's nice to see a lizard and try to catch a lizard as well. It's nice to be out of the city and have a big view of the city itself. The trail is nice and it is taken care of

I recently had a great hike in Griffith Park, starting from one of the Observatory Trailheads, and it’s a perfect urban escape.
The signage is clear, offering several short, accessible options like the West Observatory Trail (0.7 mi) or the Loop Trail (0.2 mi), making it ideal for a quick workout or a relaxed family outing. The trails themselves are mostly wide, dirt fire roads, which were well-maintained and easy to navigate.
The best part, of course, is the views. Even on an overcast or slightly hazy day (like when I was there), the vistas are stunning. As you hike up the wide, dusty path, you get that iconic perspective of the Griffith Observatory dome rising over the green hills behind you. Then, when you look out over the city, you are treated to sweeping, classic views of the Hollywood Hills and the Los Angeles skyline in the distance.
Quick Tips:
1. Parking: Parking near the Observatory can be a challenge, so consider starting your hike from a lower access point like Fern Dell Drive (which is listed as only 0.1 mi away from this junction) and walking up.
2. Sun: The trail pictured is quite exposed, so definitely bring water and sun protection on clear days, even if it feels cool at the start.
3. Footwear: Standard athletic shoes are generally fine for these wide paths.
This is a quintessential L.A. hike that's perfect for both locals and visitors. It offers a great payoff in views for a minimal time commitment and effort!

Went here on a Saturday morning. Parking can be hard to find, so you gotta drive around for a bit.

When I come to L.A. I make it a point to hike up this trail in the morning. It's a short, strenuous hike (depending on your lifestyle), but absolutely worth the views. The hike is steep the majority of the way, especially the first 2/3 of the way, but you'll get majestic views of L.A. (and the ocean on a non-smoggy/foggy day). It ends at Griffith Observatory. On the way down you'll enjoy the 180⁰ views of the city. Its stunning!

Overall, and absolute must when in L.A. You start off at the Ferndell Nature Trail to listen to the serene sounds of the natural creek and walk through the natural landscape that had been utilized by the Tongvas. Take it slow if you're not a hiker. It's about a 30 minute hike up, but maybe an hour or so if you're new to hiking.

Our daily favorite hiking area.
Cannot park at the park. After sunset, do parking across the Los Feliz street, street parking.

I ran into hummingbirds, dragonflies, squirrels, and even some horseback riders along the way. The hike is pretty easy overall, but there are spots where the trail gets steep. It took me about two and a half hours to reach the Hollywood Sign. There are a few water stations along the trail, but I’d still recommend bringing your own bottle. You’ll find benches to rest and recharge. The area is watched by cameras, and a helicopter flies by now and then, so you know they’re keeping the trail safe. Every now and then you’ll see riders cruising by on horses. The nature out there is beautiful and full of variety. And yes, you can totally snap a great photo once you get up to the Hollywood Sign.

name: Santa Ynez Falls
formatted_address: Santa Ynez Waterfall Trail, Pacific Palisades, CA 90272, USA

google_reviews.text: Walk 0.6 miles onto the trailhead. At the post saying “warning non maintained” dive in, cross the creek bed and go less than a mile. The terrain is essentially flat, but you need the ability to climb in and out of the creek beds and across the rocks. Round trip is approximately 3 miles from the trailhead at the road.

Absolutely stunning but completely covered with poison oak. The trail has been wiped out by recent rains and is in desperate need of maintenance.  You basically follow the creek up in which ever way you can. We made it to the waterfall sign and had to turn around because all the trails were totally blocked by poison oak. Proceed with caution.

Great hiking trail, lots of water streams to cross. Mostly shaded but some spots feel hot. Some parts can be walked on trails and others wading. Look for frogs , tadpoles, and lots of lizards like the ones in the pictures. Around 2 miles each way. Would not recommend for small children as some parts are steep and/or hard to climb. Not necessarily a waterfall, there is a natural pool that can be knee deep, but not much water.

Moderate hike, not kid friendly. Shaded
Hiking path was pretty much overgrown or whiped out so easy to not know where the exact trail is, so much so that we went off the path a couple of times and encountered wild bees. Uugh. So keep walking up the water path. Stream as the only real guide, but can be frustrating as you keep crisscross the stream.  Would be amazing if the path was more established.

Great hike! Parking is on the side of the road. Start of the hike is through a neighborhood but the rest is nature. Its a decent hike to the waterfall with a lot of stream crossings. Large likely hood you’ll get your shoes wet.

name: Limekiln Canyon Park
formatted_address: 19585 Rinaldi St, Porter Ranch, CA 91326, USA

google_reviews.text:
Limekiln Canyon Park in Porter Ranch is one of my favorite places to hike in the Valley. The scenery is absolutely breathtaking — beautiful hills, rock formations, and peaceful trails that feel far from the city.

What makes it even better are the multiple stream crossings. My dog loves splashing in the water and cooling off along the trail, which makes the experience even more fun.

It’s clean, scenic, and the perfect spot to enjoy nature and spend quality time outdoors — especially if you have a dog who loves the water.

Great hike in the middle of neighborhood. Creek add more to the atmosphere. Parking can be tricky, especially on weekends, when coffee track stands at the entrance. Plenty of picnic tables, but no bathrooms...

Limekiln Canyon Park is one of my favorite hidden gems in the Valley! The trails are calm and shady, with the sound of water running through the creek — it feels like a mini mountain getaway right in the city. My dog absolutely loves splashing around and exploring the water (as you can see 😄). It’s a great spot to reset, enjoy nature, and take a quiet walk without having to drive far. Just beautiful year-round!

Great place to walk, jog, bring your RC Crawler, Family time in nature (instead of screen time).
Enjoy all seasons there, easy trails, slight elevation changes.

My wife and I came here today since the weather was nice with some clouds. My wife needed a nice walk in nature to release some stress so I figure this would be a good place. I tried doing the street parking which there were some before Corbin on Rinaldi or on the other side too. My wife wanted to use the bathroom first so I just parked my car at the Porter Ranch town center and walked from there to the hiking trail. It's a pretty good trail about 1.7 miles and has a nice small river so you can enjoy the sound of the water. When we made it to 1 particular spot with a swing and tire, I think that was the end of the trail so we stopped.

name: Wilacre Park, Mountains Recreation & Conservation Authority
formatted_address: 3431 Fryman Rd, Studio City, CA 91604, USA

google_reviews.text: This circular trail is a great choice if you’re looking for a calm escape into nature. Large trees line much of the route, keeping it cool and pleasant even on warmer days. Along the way, you’ll notice a gently flowing creek that adds to the tranquil setting. The path shifts between cozy, close-in stretches and wider clearings that are perfect for stopping to rest or enjoy the view. It’s a beautiful walk that feels refreshing from start to finish and easy to recommend.
Amazing hike but was only barely able to find parking at 7am on a Saturday. Came back to a very long line of cars waiting for a parking spot to open up.  Plan to park a ways away from the trailhead.

When I hiked here years ago, I went from a size 14 to a size 8.  Now I'm back enjoying the multiple trails.  I mean multiple!  I just discovered a new one near Tree People. But I didn't explore it very well because on one part it had so many spiderweb along the side of the mountain.   I love the views, the flowers, and the maintained paths.  I hiked over 3 hours and still didn't hit all the trails.   I am so happy I started hiking again, and so will my tight pants. Lol. I'm looking for them to get loose again.   Oh, and now they have 2 restrooms! One where you park and the other at Tree People top of first hill near parking.    Hiking is for the body, soul, and camera. If you are anything like me, you are going to have to add more space on your phone. Challenge... can you find the swing? Hint: The sound of the stream is calming when using it.

Wilacre Park offers a beautiful hiking trail that is both challenging and rewarding. The park is well-maintained and features breathtaking views of the surrounding mountains and valleys. However, there are a few downsides to the trail that hikers should be aware of.

The trail starts off steep for the first 10 minutes, which can be challenging for some hikers. However, once you get past the initial climb, the trail becomes relatively easy and enjoyable. If you stick to the simple loop, the trail is rated as an easy hike. The loop is surrounded by beautiful wildflowers that add to the beauty of the hike. The view from the top of the loop is stunning and definitely worth the climb.

However, one downside to the trail is the presence of dog droppings. It seems that many dog owners use the park and do not pick up after their pets. This can be quite unpleasant and detract from the overall hiking experience. It is important to watch your step and be mindful of the droppings on the trail.

Another downside to the trail is that it is easy to get lost if you choose the wrong track. This happened to us and ended up taking us 2 hours to complete the trail instead of the anticipated hour. It is important to bring a map or use a hiking app to avoid getting lost.

Overall, the hiking trail at Wilacre Park is worth exploring. The stunning views and beautiful wildflowers make it a worthwhile experience. However, it is important to be mindful of the dog droppings and just stick to the main road to avoid getting lost on the trail.

Beautiful hike! Make sure you take a water bottle with you, it is a moderate hike.  Nice little amphitheatre at the top and quite a few benches to have a picnic lunch as well. There is parking, be patient..you'll get a spot...people are coming and going all the time. Dogs on leash are welcome...bring water for you're furry family member as well. Enjoy!

name: Charlie Turner Trailhead
formatted_address: 2840 W Observatory Rd, Los Angeles, CA 90027, USA

google_reviews.text: Pretty - but really foggy lately with the marine layer.  Tried to go before 10am for free parking but it was still too early that the fog covered most of the view form the top. Still beautiful and good workout.

This trailhead starts right at the Griffith Observatory parking lot, which can get crowded, and the trail is basically a fire road that climbs up to Mt Hollywood. Just don't park here at the observatory, save some money and park down at Fern Grotto and hike up to here.

Great experience doing a mini hike with family. I loved the sweeping views of LA, the Hollywood sign (though far) and the LA style greenery. Definitely worth a visit. The trails are dusty but not too bad. Parking is free at the bottom, but you’ll need to hike up which isn’t too far but goes uphill. Parking near the trail is at the observatory which costs $15/hour right now. Highway robbery!

Easy access, great views and great trail connections!
I did a quick run up the hill for exercise, tho too crowded for all intents and purposes on a Sunday.
It was only when looking back from the Griffith Observatory that I realized the tourism aspect of the climb, as museum goers contemplated it as the same trail that not only leads up Mount Hollywood, it also leads to the Mount Lee trail, near the Hollywood Sign!

It's so beautiful out here. Nature is amazingly refreshing. Best views of the city overlooking Griffith Observatory... just go for a walk...enjoy the fresh air...pick any trail ... take on hiking...100% benefit mind, body and soul🙂👍

name: Wildwood Canyon Trailhead
formatted_address: Wildwood Cyn trail, Burbank, CA 91501, USA

google_reviews.text: This trail has seen better days. It's a pretty steep incline in a lot of areas where the trail has eroded away. The ruins of stairs and guide rails show where a safer trail used to be. The paths are pretty narrow at points, maybe down to 2ft wide. I didn't have any issue going up, but it's a workout. Coming down I lost my footing a few times in steeper areas. I didn't have gear but would recommend trekking poles and shoes with good traction. Zero shelter from trees so be sure to have water and sunscreen. The views are stunning. Id prob skip this one and opt for trails that are better kept.

The Vital Link trail is a gnarly climb up a ridge, offering great views of Glendale and the San Fernando Valley. It is exposed and really hot in the summer; I wouldn’t recommend it. There are also a few shorter loops you can do using Wildwood Canyon Road.

Dogs on leash allowed! There is even a poop bag dispenser at the trailhead, near the portable toilet for humans. Your dog should have good trail skills, as the Vital Link trail is steep and slippery in places. I really do not recommend it for dogs in the summer.
The finest hiking trail in Burbank offers a challenging ascent, and the breathtaking views at its conclusion are a truly rewarding experience.
Very beautiful views but very steep and dusty. Plan on getting dirty and bring plenty of water!

Perfect hiking trail: compact, yet dynamic with beautiful scenery!
There is an option of going all the way to the tower for the ambitious hikers.

name: Powder Canyon
formatted_address: 2345 Fullerton Rd, La Habra Heights, CA 90631, USA

google_reviews.text:
This is one of my absolute favorite local trails. The loop I usually take takes about 45–55 minutes and I’d rate the difficulty around 2 out of 5—manageable, but still a great workout.
Instead of an out-and-back, I created a loop route that starts at Powder Canyon, continues through Black Walnut Trail, and comes back via Nogales Trail. The route begins with a steady uphill climb, reaches the top, and then finishes with a nice downhill section, which makes it both challenging and enjoyable.
Even though it’s under an hour, it really gets your heart rate up, and the surrounding greenery and open space make it a refreshing place to exercise. Parking is available nearby, and I’ve marked the route with arrows on the map—definitely check it before you go, as it helps a lot.
Highly recommended if you’re looking for a short but effective hike close to home.

Hidden gem of a park. Great for hikes runs and walks. For kids and grown-ups, you'll enjoy the rolling hillsides, quiet nature sounds and a variety of trails that would please any exercise aficionado . A strong 3.75. Star trail. Missing, are  facilities , trail guides and signage.  Beyond that,  have fun and happy trails!
Plenty of free parking. Trails are a variety of flat and some steep parts. Nice views of the snowy mountains after the rain.
Amazing views and substantial shade if hiking before noon.

Have been coming here for around almost 10 years. I've done the slopes and walked the straight paths. Always a great scenic experience with many friendly passerby. It's not always packed full of people so don't expect much crowds. Parking is in a dirt road but not too bad and as a new pup dad it was perfect for a nice early afternoon stroll down the straight paths with our 1 year old. I have hiked and walked during several different times of the day and never encountered any issues. As a nature lover, I enjoy observing the local wildlife like deer, blue birds, and squirrels. ***There is shade on the straight paths for those looking for a nice little stroll in nature****

name: Monrovia Canyon Park
formatted_address: 1200 N Canyon Blvd, Monrovia, CA 91016, USA

google_reviews.text:
Made plans for a day date which included starting with a short hike to a waterfall. We parked for $5 (or if you wanted a longer walk, you can park before the ranger entrance and hike more) but we wanted a short hike that would take about 1 hour. It was great to be in nature along trees, rock cliffs, running waters. Parts of the trail were wide and parts were narrow. Twice we had to cross on small rocks through the stream of water. There were small man made dams with small portions of the waterfall (about 3 to 4) then we arrived at the final waterfall. It was invigorating to see it. High cliffs all around in almost 270 degrees and the waterfall about 30 to 40ft long falling into a small pool and eventually going down the rest of the hill side to the lower falls. The day was hot at 9am and the sky was clear and blue (a few white clouds). Then we turned back. The whole hike took about 65 minutes.
If we didn't have plans for the rest of the day we could have brought some food and had lunch at the picnic tables near the parking spots.
There are longer trails if you are interested in longer hikes. There is a nature center with some activities for children ( it was closed when we went),
but could be fun for your kids. There are restrooms near the nature center building.

Recommend to buy an annual pass.
Few trails and I enjoy the ones leading to the small waterfall.
More trees 🌲 growing out since Covid.
A little hard to find parking in weekends so be there early morning or afternoon.
Will run into bear 🐻, and don’t run! They rather not to bother you.

The trail full of small gravels so recommend you to wear hiking shoes otherwise a little slippery go uphill or down hill.
What I love about the trail is well maintained and most of the trails are shaded by trees.
If you are senior citizens, please bring your hiking shoes and hiking poles for safety reasons.  Some uphills or downhills areas need to walk slowly with caution; the trail is slippery not much traction coz full of tiny rocks/gravels.
Close to waterfalls you will encounter few Ricky area and most of journey is peaceful and easy hike.

Park near ticket booth, or there are two more parks when drive up and restrooms available near the parking lot.

This is my favorite place to hike. I been. Waiting for it to open up again after their restoration.  Today I finally went. Definitely much water in the river and waterfall . I love to come here weekly . Its peaceful here and shady , a plus since i dont need to worry about sunburn and rattle snake  .  Definitely  entry level for hiking , bring waterproof shoes 👞 and hiking stick will help because some gravel trails.  Not too crowded,  this place is well maintained,  rangers there are amazing . Bathrooms are clean , multiple parking places and alot of easy hiking trails. See my photos and video .

This park is a small nice park that recently opened in 2025 nestled in a canyon. The hikes here are quite nice, like the Waterfall Trail or the Bill Cull to Cunningham Overlook one as they provide pretty nice views. The water is nice and flowing here all year and the park has variable fees based on the day you visit.

I’m an avid hiker and I can confidently say that this has been one of the most beautiful hikes in my life. I visited on a calm Monday morning and felt like I was hiking in my own private garden. It will not be an exaggeration to say that this place resembles the garden of Eden. Parking is five dollars and there are plenty of parking spots inside the park. As expected this park is very similar to Eaton canyon with its water features and slight inclines,  but the difference here is that there is no graffiti anywhere. This place looks like unexplored land that has been nicely maintained by the city of Monrovia.

name: Hellman Park Trailhead
formatted_address: 5700 Greenleaf Ave, Whittier, CA 90601, USA

google_reviews.text: Really great hiking trail but there’s only 4 spots to park. You might have to park a few blocks away in the neighborhood. Watch for signs
Great experience, the mountains are very high for running . It has been and real adventure.
Wow. What a hike! 1000 elevation gain in 30 minutes. Great hike and beautiful views!

Amazing views, and great wildflowers in the spring. A bit of a butt-burner right off the bat, but once you getthrough the initial climb, you have lots of options to veer off onto different trails to explore the preserve.  Parking is terrible, so be prepared to park a ways away and use the walk to the trailhead as a warm up. Also, note the gate doesn't open until 9am, so no need to get there any earlier than that.

Got drunk this morning and invited my wife for a hike...the steep incline sobered me up real quick and once my whining and complaining stopped I was able to catch my breath and noticed the views are crazy.  Watch out for those black beetles that squirt acid piss oh and the tiny Flys having orgys in the air by the thousands all along the trail.  ✌🏾

name: Beaudry Loop Trailhead
formatted_address: 3110 Beaudry Terrace, Glendale, CA 91208, USA

google_reviews.text:
Nice fire road loop to bag Tongva Peak and Mt Thom the easy way (ie not going straight up the firebreak from Brand Park). It’s about a 6 mile loop, about 2 hours, depending on how fast your dog is. Great views from the top, though the trail itself is a fire road 🥱

Not much shade, do not recommend doing this in the summer with a dog.

The North Beaudry Trail is amazing! It's perfect for hiking, mountain biking, and trail running. The trail isn’t too technical or narrow, offering plenty of space to share with mountain bikers. It's also a great spot to take your dogs for a walk. Plus, North Beaudry connects to the Brand and Verdugo trails. Highly recommend!

We loved this hike.. very green and so many beautiful views. Not easy though.. great workout, 3 miles to the top where the FCC towers are. Take plenty of water for you and your pets… wear sunscreen and hats. We were glad to go on a cold day, not sure I’d do this on a hot one. If you do, I recommend going early in the morning or late afternoon before sunset.

Great hiking trail. It gets hard at the beginning & for around 3 Miles, continuous ascent & it gets further difficult as you approach the peak of the mountain. Lovely scenery & beautiful nature. Lots of sun, so be careful & have water on you. No restrooms. No parking lot, you park on the street side.

This trail offers a moderate to hard level hike, with the difficulty varying based on one's fitness level. Opting for the clockwise direction allows for strategic breaks during the uphill sections and presents stunning views from the summit. While descending, hikers may encounter snakes crossing the trail, so caution is advised. Additionally, the trail boasts patches of vibrant wildflowers, enhancing the overall experience.

name: Griffith Park Trails
formatted_address: 2715 N Vermont Canyon Rd, Los Angeles, CA 90027, USA

google_reviews.text:
Used be good. More and more dog shit caused by dog mind humans.
I miss those days with free parking, now $10 a hour.
Small nice daily hiking, nothing special.

Its range of trails, with multiple peaks. You can plan based on the difficulty and time. Mt Hollwood Summit has great view of Griffith Observatory as well as Hollwwood sign. You can start from Merry go round parking lot, then head towards Bee Rock Trail and follow the signs. Adding pics of map and signs along the way.

Ok, this is a great hike. Start east to west going upwards for a harder hike. The climb at the top is a little tough so focus, no cell phone texting and please wear the right shoes. The view is spectacular. I did see people with, dogs and children. 9:00am is a good time to start. At that time you should find parking. No shade so wear your sun screen. Their are plenty of restaurants In close proximity after your hike. There is also a bathroom close to the parking area. Very safe hike.
This is a nice trailhead that can be used to access the Griffith Park trails for free if you park along the road. The trail is a maintained fire road.

Great spot to hike. Very scenic and variety of things to see. Fun to do hiking loop. Not too hard. Does get steep near the top. Bring some water and good shoes. Saw a rattlesnake on the trail, so keep eyes open 🐍 👀

name: Flint Canyon Trail
formatted_address: 4170-4206 Hampstead Rd, La Cañada Flintridge, CA 91011, USA

google_reviews.text: This trail is awesome. A short half mile hike to the top of the mountain which takes about 12 to 15 minutes. The hills are easy to hike for young and old. I do suggest that you wear hiKing shoes with good traction since the paths are dirt and sand. The best part of this trail is a lot of trees and shades. Perfect on a warm day. There are also some benches that you can rest along the way. One thing.though is that there's is no parking at the entrance. You need to park on Hampstead RD and walk a short distance to the entrance of the trail.

Loved the hike. Small one, but still it’s good. The total hike was around 4 miles. Don’t get lost as there are lots of trails around and they all look similar. The difficulty level depends on the one you take.
Beautiful trail! It's clean, well maintained, and not busy even on the weekends. Truly a gem.
Very clean and very beautiful trail, not crowded was the best part, went on Sunday aroud 3pm and we saw only 10 people in trail.

Update: Have taken this so many times since my initial review and love this trail. This was initially to create a video for Mari Copeny aka LittleMissFlint for her 17th birthday.

Once you realize the path begins behind the sign, enjoy the path that winds through a community and offers amazing outdoor feel to Cherry Canyon.

name: Ernest E. Debs Regional Park
formatted_address: 4235 Monterey Rd, Los Angeles, CA 90032, USA

google_reviews.text: It’s a good kinda hidden park, it has a small man’s made lake with a beautiful city view. It also had picnic tables and a good vibe to it. You could complete the whole park in 1:30 mins. Good for dog walking with leash,there is a lot of coyotes in the mornings and late evenings. They’ll leave you alone if you just let them pass. We technically are in there home. But overall it’s a chill spot for a quick 8k step count with some nature around you

A hidden gem. Ernest Debs is the low-key Griffith Park with short but mighty incline loops perfect for a 1 hour hike. I take the loop from the parking lot up to the dirt trail just below the fire road. The view of the city is a nice midway point before the last climb. The route is about a 30 ft incline. There’s also a turtle pond in the middle.
I haven't been to the park for a long time. The pond on the mountain is much dirtier, there are fewer small animals, and the swings are gone. I feel a little lost.

Nice trails and also has a park over looking LA. The view is great, once you walk down a shot easy path (there are a few paths, some are uphill but there are flat paths also for the elderly). A great place to take a picnic lunch and do some easy hiking with a great view.

Free parking, feel free!
This park has several entrances, but most of them are free. This is quite a steep mountain. We highly recommend using active wear specifically with the upcoming hot weather. Mountain biking can be fun to ride here. This park also allows you to find small creatures and look at the LA Syline! Recommended to come with friends just because of safety reasons. No restrooms nearby. Pet friendly park.

name: Deukmejian Wilderness Park
formatted_address: 3429 Markridge Rd, Glendale, CA 91214, USA

google_reviews.text: Scenic hiking in Glendale.  Great views.  Well maintained trails.  Not terribly difficult but can get your heart rate up if you want it to be.  Benches and a small grassy park area.  Bathrooms and a nature center.  Not a lot of parking even with an overflow lot.  I didn't have any issues finding a spot today but I could see where there might be challenges from time to time.  The girls and I enjoyed our hour or so we were there.   Recommended.

This was the jumping off point for a great hike to Mt. Lukens. Water and clean bathrooms parking lot adjacent. Old Stone Barn - former winery turned Visitor center , was closed when I went . Will definitely return

This wilderness park is right next to the mountains and provides many hiking opportunities of all levels.

There is a museum that has interactive exhibits geared to teach us about the local environment, the flora and fauna, and the wildlife found in the area. The museum also gives us a brief history of the people who have called the surrounding area home.

We were fortunate enough to come on a weekend as part of a childrens interactive hike. They gave the kids brief introductions to scientific terms, took us on a short hike, and then arts and craft time where they made mosquito repellent necklaces. It's really cool to expose kids to the outdoors and science. This location does a great job of that.

There are also picnic areas where families can come and enjoy a day at the park and enjoy the scenery. I got the impression that it can get hot in the summer, so if you do come during that time, bring plenty of water.

I recently moved to this area and am thrilled to have found this wilderness park 5 minutes from my home. Easy to moderate hiking trails with breathtaking views and every time I go I see 1-2 bunnies 🥹 There is also a small park located near the parking lot with picnic tables as well as a “barn” / educational center with a small grape vine  field in the back. Amazing spot!!

This place is a hidden gem and we are all sleeping on it!! It’s tucked up in the foothills of Glendale but so easy to get to. There are a handful of trails for hiking, all with different levels of difficulty so there’s something for everyone.

What I really loved was the education center that explained the history of the area and the different types of wildlife that live in the area.

Overall, I highly recommend Deukmejian Wilderness Park and will absolutely be back.

name: Elysian Park Trail
formatted_address: Elysian Park Dr, Los Angeles, CA 90012, USA

google_reviews.text:
The park itself is awesome.  Huge. Lots of space for my dog and I to hike and play. I took off 2 stars because of the people. Many of the people like to invade other people's space. They let their dogs invade people's space. They are aggressive, rude, disrespectful and inconsiderate. My dog and I have been harassed by too many European Americans to ignore.
Same with some of the people with loud cars and music blaring. The ice cream trucks and their extra  loud music. It is not a peaceful place to go.
La vista super impresionante! Wow, the view of Los Angeles County and beyond is very impressive and beautiful!

Best park in East Los Angeles!
The community garden on top of the hill has a stunning view and it has bowls and water for dogs.
There are multiple trails and it's possible to cut through less walked trails.
Just be mindful of the holes everywhere made by gophers.
Alone women should probably not head to the trails at Elysian Park after sunset, there is no security, no cameras, and it's not safe to walk there, out of past experience.

It is a good place for picnic 🧺 and the best part it is just a few minutes walk to Angel’s Point where you can see the Los Angeles Skyline. Yesterday we went to another side of Angel’s Point which is a lookout point to see Dodgers Stadium 🏟️ and watched the fireworks!
Plenty free parking and hike was about a 2 mile loop. Not a difficult hike and a garden area to give dogs water 🥣

name: Caballero Canyon Trail
formatted_address: 4047 Reseda Blvd, Tarzana, CA 91356, USA

google_reviews.text: Had a nice hike at the lower hectic an cabellero canyon Trail first half was a challenge due to steep incline on the way up.The second half was a breeze.I recommend it for a good workout an beautiful views free parking aswell

Beautiful trail. Pretty steep, but worth the view. We did the loop, going up the west trail and down the east trail. Lots of mountain bike features. Flowers were beautiful (early May). Not too many people, but the people we did see were very friendly. My pup loved it.

You have to park on street, three different trails are there. You can have your dog on leash. You can see many rabbits there. The paths that goes over the hills are a little bit hard but the one that goes in your right is flat and easy to go. Have fun and don’t forget close your mouth because of lots of mosquitoes,

great spot to get away from the city noise.
you can make an easy hike, or really pick up the pace and make it a workout

The best thing about Caballero Canyon is that it connects to many other trails, giving you multiple options. By connecting to other trails, you can make your hike or mountain biking ride as long or as difficult as you want.
Lots of free parking on the street. No restrooms or water available.

name: O'Melveny Park
formatted_address: 17300 Sesnon Blvd, Granada Hills, CA 91344, USA

google_reviews.text:
My dog and I’s favorite park in the whole valley! Beautiful scenery, friendly people, a variety of trails to choose from, and wide open green grass to relax in

The mission point trail is no joke and you might have to butt scoot down some sections if you go counter-clockwise haha! Even my dog had to go slow!

Be careful of coyotes, this is their home so they are everywhere here. I go almost everyday and I always see at least one within a few feet. Keep your distance & be loud and they will leave you alone. Just always be aware of you’re surroundings and there will be no worries
I returned after years I had frequented. Still a beautiful place for families & solo hikes! Had a wonderful time d doing the omelveny trail  🫶🏾😁✝️

Great hicking experiences here, suited for every level of skill.  Take my dog at least once a week.  Just wish all people would follow the rule that "ALL dogs must be on a leash".  Clearly posted when entering the park!  Very clean and clean restrooms.  Water fountains for dogs as well.  Good picnic area.
Great hiking location, the hills are challenging and the summit offers great views of the sfv.

Beautiful park, lots of wildlife, (I saw bluejays, cottontail rabbits, and coyotes in 1hr) well maintained. Park is open sunrise to sunset, despite that Google Maps currently lists it as closed at 6pm.

name: Eaton Canyon Nature Center
formatted_address: 1750 N Altadena Dr, Pasadena, CA 91107, USA

google_reviews.text: Great hike to the waterfall.  I expect to see more water. However, it was still beautiful. It's not a long distance to the  waterfall,  but we continued up the hill. Completing 9.5 miles out and back. The weather was great.  This would definitely be more challenging during the summer because of the heat, but you will see more leaves by then.  The water was cold, and although there were rocks and logs to assist with crossing the creeks, waterproof shoes/ boots are recommended.

Wonderful place for easy hikes or walking! Definitely worth to explore around. There is lot of water in this week and you need to cross it couple of times. Be prepared as it’s cold and to get a little wet up to the knees.
The parking usually gets full quickly but there’s extra space in the ground.

Was in the Pasadena area and wanted to try a hike in the San Gabriel Mountains.  Came on Sunday mid-morning, so the parking was pretty full, but people were constantly coming and going.  I wasn't quite sure which trail to take to get to the waterfall, so followed others to the left and stayed on the main trail.  There's several small trails that veer off the main path, but I stayed on the main path.

After about 30 minutes, you'll get to a small sign that says Eaton Canyon Falls 1/2 mile ahead.  I followed that sign to the right, but the trail quickly disappeared.  It's hard to tell where the trail continues, but basically follow the water upstream.  Since there's not an obvious path, you'll have to cut back and forth across the water carefully picking your own path.  I'd rate this a moderate hike as some parts require some balancing, small jumps, some climbs, and some descents.  After about 45-60 minutes, you finally reach the end of the hike where the waterfall is located.  It rained a few days before my hike so the water was flowing at a decent rate. I was told by locals it can only be a trickle during the dry periods.

The hike fun with plenty of shade after the first 15 minutes of open trails.  The trails and paths were plenty wide and free of debris.  Nice hike with plenty of scenery and wildlife.  Will definitely come back and try some of the smaller trails, but still concluding at the waterfall.
Completed my very first full hike and can honestly say it wasn’t that bad. This trail is beautiful with a few creeks before you make it to the waterfall.

You have to make a reservation before hiking here. I picked the 2-5 time slot and it was a good time because it wasn't very crowded at all. It's a beautiful hike. Not too hard; good for all ages and for pets. The falls are amazingly beautiful! Not as big as Bonita Falls (where you dont need a reservation), but still gorgeous! A pool at the bottom of the falls, but it was too chilly to get wet. The scenery on the way to the falls is beautiful. You cross the stream back and forth several times. There was plenty of parking when we went. I wondered if there was another way in without making a reservation but after visiting, you have to go through a gate where they ask to see your ID and reservation #. You're at the Nature Center and they have bathrooms inside. So the only way is to make a reservation in advance. Well worth it though and I'm so glad I checked it out before heading there.

name: Aliso Canyon Park
formatted_address: 18041 Rinaldi St, Granada Hills, CA 91344, USA

google_reviews.text:
Very easy hike/walk. We were surprised that there is a little water running through the park. Two trails. Straight or to the right is an equestrian trail. There is a corral to unload as well. Very short walking trail. Not sure where the horse trail goes. There is a small trail that leads up a hill which we did not go.
The other is to the left, cross over a small bridge. Wider groomed trail. Very easy to walk. Shorter trails with shade.
Picnic areas with tables. Did not notice grilles. There is a restroom near the entrance to the park.
Quiet on a Thursday morning.

This hiking trail is it! Once you cross the bridge, there are two ways you can go. To the left; shorter trails. The right; longer trails. There are easy trails you can go on or if you’re up for the challenge, you can take the steep trails. There are snake holes everywhere so be careful while walking. There are also little bunnies hopping around. I noticed there’s a lot of horse poop on the trails and dog poop but other than that, it’s a gorgeous site. Early mornings when they open, are the best due to the weather being cold and everything seems fresh. Not a lot of people around that time either. There’s a lot of parking but be careful when you drive down the driveway; it’s steep! A lot of benches with a roof and without. Bathrooms are across from the benches. Everyone is respectful and says hi to you. I only saw one tree and a few poles with graffiti. Some areas have trespassing signs so be aware of your surroundings.

I was so happy to see that I could rate Alyson Canyon Park because my family and I hike there almost every day, and have since 2016! Even when COVID hit, we kept hiking, staying connected with nature and getting great exercise.

The trail is beautiful, gorgeous trees, with three little creek crossings and an optional steep section that’s totally worth it. In the afternoons, watching bunnies hop across the path is such a joy! The park is also great for mountain biking, and I often see respectful dog owners enjoying the trail with their pups.

It’s truly a special place for outdoor lovers. Highly recommend!

Came to this park after work and started the adventure after crossing the small bridge.
Lots of parking, separate lot for horse trailers and bigger vehicles. Picnic area and vault toilets.
After crossing the bridge and turning right, you'll walk on one of many paths, this one was wide and spacious with hard packed dirt in some areas and soft sand in others. Lots of different flora and fauna. There are so many trails that will have you deviating from your original path.
Lots of rock Cairns at different points along the trail.
Some dumped debris, like a climbing structure and a rusted car panel. The oak Grove was beautiful and towards the end of my journey, I finally felt the warmth of the sun.
Not a lot of water in either creeks, but I imagine when it rains, it'll really flow. A great spot to take a nice urban walk in a quiet canyon.

Once you get on the trails it's a good but short hike on the left path option.  Didn't go right on the horse path but that's a hiking option for humans as well.  Lots of parking, picnic tables at the front near parking.   Once you get on its an easy hike until you get to the end where you can turn back or continue up a steep but short incline to Eddlestone park.

name: Coldwater Canyon Park
formatted_address: 12601 Mulholland Dr, Beverly Hills, CA 90210, USA

google_reviews.text:
Coldwater Canyon Park is such a stunning spot for a hike! There are some tricky inclines that add a bit of a challenge, but overall, it’s not too intense. One thing to note is that there aren’t signs everywhere, so it can be a little confusing to navigate—but honestly, that just makes it feel more adventurous!

I also love that they have picnic tables and benches where you can sit and take in the views. There’s so much to enjoy here, and I can’t wait to go back again!

Beautiful place to go for a walk! Amazing sunset! I love all the beautiful places to sit and look out over the horizon. You can hear the birds and you can also hear people talking on the other trails below which is kind of interesting. There are wild cucumber vines everywhere on the ground and covering some of the trees. Overall it's a unique place worth a frequent visit.
I wish I could go every day

This is the perfect walking trail that is dog friendly is so beautiful calm and peaceful out here especially in January my dog's actually love the openness the fresh air we enjoy coming here as often as we can
This park is decent and the trails are well maintained. The views from here are meh though. This park is also a connection from Willacre Park to the Betty Dearing Trail.

A hidden gem in Los Angeles. I would've never known there was a cool performance area hidden in the hills of Los Angeles. I'm not one to fancy the outdoors. Coming from New York City, I feel more comfortable amongst man-made environments but every once in a while I'll be introduced to a natural setting that takes over my earthly avoidance and speaks to my soul. So please don't invite me for a hike or even to the beach most days but, this little area of conservation is peaceful during the early morning. The projects that are worked on by this organization TreePeople sounds like a very forward thinking, positive, act for Los Angeles. I am not familiar with the while park but this little Oasis was a nice find. I look forward to attending some of the events here.

name: San Vicente Mountain Park
formatted_address: 17500 Mulholland Dr, Los Angeles, CA 90049, USA

google_reviews.text: This is a park on the top of a mountain that has some nice views from it and a picnic area. It is also home to a Nike lookout tower used during the Cold War, that you can climb up for some nice views. It is accesible by many trails that cross the hills.

Gorgeous views and a wonderful place to take a walk/hike, you can make the walk harder by taking the side hills and getting more vertical distance.. from the start of the trail to the park it is 3.9 miles and 666' of vertical elevation gain..
Always keep an eye out as there are some gorgeous places to take pictures..

What an amazing experience here! The drive to the site was a little scary because I am definitely not used to driving on the edge of a mountain. The sights are well worth it though and will steal your breath away! If you get there in time for the sunset you will not be disappointed. My sister faced her fear of heights here because she didn't want to miss the beautiful view. The pictures do not do it justice, you have to go in person!

The have a nice water fountain that you can refill your bottle with and a nice big watering bowl for dogs.  They also have bathrooms.

The views are amazing, it's only about 8.3 miles the entire way.

You can lime up the believes silo of the missiles, which has the best view of the valley, downtown, Santa Monica, and Catalina island on a good day, which is almost everyday in LA. Good for exercise and dog walking, and biking. The birds are cheering for you.

name: Murphy Ranch Park
formatted_address: 16200 Las Cumbres Dr, Whittier, CA 90603, USA

google_reviews.text: Nice loop trail for beginners or someone getting back into day hikes. Some hills, challenging for some but the trail isn’t long. Nice views, dog friendly, usually not too busy
Fun hiking trail. Get there super early. Small parking lot but lots of street parking.

Great place to hike. The trails are different, ranging from straight and easy to full on hike up hill. The trails are easy to follow, there's no having to guess. Great exercise for everyone into hiking. Also great to just be out in the open air, little bit of wilderness. Plenty of thing's to look at (tree's) and small animal's.
Great little hiking spot, nice trails well kept. Beautiful views.  Dog friendly.
First time there it was amazing place easy but challenging a lot to explore definitely will be back to explore more

name: Captain's Roost
formatted_address: 2715 N Vermont Ave, Los Angeles, CA 90027, USA

google_reviews.text: This is a great (and easy) hike! We started at the top of Griffith Park (at the Observatory) and followed the trails from the parking lot. We also timed it for sunset so it wasn't too hot and made for great pictures. The trail through the palm trees was super cool and I 100% recommend this hike!

I hiked up Firebreak trail to Griffith Observatory, then followed the signs to Mt. Hollywood. I wasn’t able to reach my final destination in time but I made it to Captain’s Roost. I had to work in the afternoon.

The hike up from the street is steep but the trails themselves are very wide and soft underfoot. Perfect for families or groups of friends. I took a .1 mile shortcut to Captain’s Roost that started behind the sign. It looked a bit challenging and I had to scramble a tiny at the beginning—but that’s what made it fun for me!

The view is phenomenal and if you enjoy being outside, this hike is a must. I would recommend going early because there is no shade. Wear a hat and sunscreen, and bring water with electrolytes. I hope to come back to Griffith Park when I have more time to explore the trail system.

There were bathrooms at the street level trailheads as well as outside the observatory on the edge of the parking lot. There was also a water bottle filling station by the bathrooms outside.

What a fantastic (and simple) walk!  We followed the paths from the parking lot at the Observatory, which is the highest point in Griffith Park.  In order to avoid the heat and take beautiful photos, we also scheduled it for sunset.  I heartily suggest this walk, and the route through the palm palms was really interesting!

I am visiting from Louisiana. This was my 2nd trip to find the Hollywood Sign, mission accomplished. We paid to park on the West Observatory Rd. Then hiked to Captain’s Roost was beautiful. On this path you can view the ocean, Los Angeles city line, Griffith Observatory, and movie star watch. The best time to visit is sunset. My dog and pals had a great time!

Nice stopping point for a break while hiking up to Mt. Hollywood summit. Less than an hour from the observatory parking lot but over an hour and moderate hike from the bottom. It’s easy terrain just all uphill that’s why moderate.

name: Runyon Canyon Park
formatted_address: 2000 N Fuller Ave, Los Angeles, CA 90046, USA

google_reviews.text:
Visited with my family on January 4, 2026.

Runyon Canyon Park is one of the best hiking spots in Los Angeles. The trail offers great views of the city, and from certain points you can see Downtown LA, Griffith Observatory, and even the Hollywood Sign from a unique perspective.

The terrain goes downhill first and then climbs back up, which can be a bit challenging but still very manageable for most people. Just make sure to carry water, especially on sunny days.

Whenever I’m in Los Angeles, this is a hike I never miss.

Incredible views and even better hike there is two paths you could take both are very scenic and incredible views that you cannot see anywhere else. There are steep climbs and also easy crimes. You can choose whatever you would like. Both are pretty fun. I heard the whole truck goes in the loop, but I came down and went around both places going up.

Great local fitness place, not many tourists, alot of beautiful fit people.  Paved road all the way to the top except some people opt for a dirt track.  I would bring trail shoes as the dirt is loose and slippery plus your shoes will get very dusty and dirty.  The path is very steep so you feel it up and coming down.  There is water stations along the way but recommend bringing your own.  There are no toilets on the mountain and not much shade so recommend sunscreen.

Excellent hike with beautiful views, a must do if it's your first visit to LA. There's a pretty steep part, but it's not too bad. Parking can be a real pain, especially on weekends. There are bike stalls (see photo).
A wonderful place. A few years ago I lived not far from this park and used to hike here often. How nice it is to relive that experience! It’s definitely one of the coolest places in LA.

name: Peter F. Schabarum Regional Park
formatted_address: 17250 Colima Rd, Rowland Heights, CA 91748, USA

google_reviews.text: My husband and I visited the park area while staying in the area. Each morning I enjoy going for a walk and found this close to our hotel. This is a HUGE park and I would love to come back and explore. There are paved paths to take and walk along. There were several playground areas for different ages of children. Lots of bathroom areas. There was both a small and large dog park area. One thing I noticed is that there were several trails within the area and some went quite high. This would be an awesome place to come explore the trails, walking paths, and get some good workouts in. There were also areas with workout stations for individuals to use.

Beautiful and very large park — perfect for spending a few hours outdoors. There are multiple trails that wind across, around and above the park, from easy walks to some more intense hikes with limited shade, so come prepared with water and sunscreen. The views on the more intense hikes are great. Entry fee is $10 per vehicle, and it’s absolutely worth it for how well-kept the park is.
There are plenty of picnic areas, clean restrooms, a small creek (dry during our November visit), and nice recreational areas for kids. A great place to relax, explore, or hike for a few hours surrounded by nature.
Both the park and all the hikes are dog friendly, but dogs need to stay on the leash especially because there's a lot of wild nature on the hikes

My family loves this park. They are charging entry fees for parking inside the park now but its worth it in my opinion. They have hiking trails, walking paths, a creek bed you can walk threw if its dry. Three separate playgrounds. There's places you can grill and unlimited picnic tables. They have alot of bathrooms. There's horses at the end of the trail. Soccer fields at the top of the park but you can't always drive up there. The dog park is really nice. Beautiful and well maintained park. If you dont want to pay the 10 dollars you can park at the mall and walk over.

(Update: Closed Monday and Tuesday open Wednesday thru Sunday $10 Parking)
Nice for early morning long walks on the trails in the spring ,very beautiful.. im only there for 1.5hrs then go on about my day.
"This place used to be open and free parking all week and $7 on the weekend". Now you have to pay $10bucks every day and they are only open Wednesday thru Sunday and closed Monday and Tuesday.  That's kinda sucks. I'm sure alot of people feel the same way because when I paid  to enter the park that morning the parking was empty compared to what i'm used to seeing. 70% empty lot, people are parking outside the park and walking in, I may just do that from now on since I'm walking anyways.
