A beautiful blend of nature and history. The exhibits about George Washington Carver’s life are incredibly moving, and the walking trail is serene and well-maintained. Perfect for families or anyone wanting to learn and reflect.
Beautiful and safe for all staff and patrons alike.

Great Park! I was
impressed how maintained the park is. Shot out to the community, they do their part to make sure that the park is kept clean and safe.

name: West Hollywood Park
formatted_address: 647 N San Vicente Blvd, West Hollywood, CA 90069, USA

google_reviews.text: I live in West Hollywood and I absolutely love this park. It’s got great basketball courts, two dog parks, pickle ball, swimming, a fantastic gym, a library, easy parking, and you can walk to Santa Monica Blvd. I bring my pups here almost every day.

The Park is a nice, well-maintained space perfect for relaxation and outdoor activities. It offers great facilities, including a modern playground, courts, and a dog park. The green spaces are a good option to relax and the park often hosts community events. Its location is convenient, with plenty of nearby amenities.
Beautiful and peaceful. Clean environment. Parking is a bit pricey but worth it.

I love this park before there is security so I feel safe. The park is well lit and there is a nearby parking garage. I play my gong here sometimes and I like that there is a lot of foot traffic from people going to the bars, restaurants, and clubs nearby.

The newly renovated West Hollywood Park looks fantastic. It's not a huge park but manage to include a series of amenities and really feels like a place for the community.

Around the beautiful green space is a short trail with some benches. West Hollywood library and Rec Center are on one side of the park, so is a small playground; on the other side are the dog park and basketball practice courts. There is a unisex restroom near the dog park.

The park also got a great view of the design center across the street.

name: Harold A Henry Park
formatted_address: 890 S Lucerne Blvd, Los Angeles, CA 90005, USA

google_reviews.text: Me and my great grandsons love this park one of the few sand Parks left in all of Los Angeles.  he can play there for hours, and never get bored.

When I arrived here it seemed eerily familiar. Then I realized that this park is the first pickup location of Uber eats in 2014. Before it got so popular. We would come here and pick up a cooler that had ice cream, Sodas and sandwiches and hope someone would order! They would even give us driver's anything remaining.

It was good to bring your young kids n small dogs here in the afternoon around 4 pm. Playground was fun for the kids. N there usually a bunch of small dogs gather here. I stumbled on it today n my two Maltipoos have net a few other dogs n they had lots of fun.
Nice place to be at. Kid friendly, nice scenery, and it's somewhat clean. The best thing I'd have to say is here, is the view of this tree in a tree.
This is a cute very clean and shaded part, out visit there today was awesome there was quite a few families with their children. Great atmosphere and we will be back.

name: Helen Keller Park
formatted_address: 12521 Vermont Ave, Los Angeles, CA 90044, USA

google_reviews.text: Not all that. Too many homeless around. Kids wandering without parents supervison. Has a good play area for kids and basketball courts. But wouldn't recommend to be here at night.

Stoppe through LA on our way to Japan for spring break 2025. This park was on the same street our relatives stayed on in Gardena. Did our basketball drills🏀 side note Gardena Bowl for Lunch after was amazing! It’s a bowling alley with a little Hawaiian brunch place with huge portions

[UPDATE] Still it is the closest and my favorite ballot drop box location! Just dropped off my ballot for the election. 10/8/24

———

Clean park. It has one of the election ballot drop box, which was the main reason why I paid a visit. I ran there, so I took rest and enjoyed the quiet time for 10 minutes or so once the folks went through the park with the leaves blower. I suppose it is a Thursday thing in the morning. The water fountains near the ballot drop box don’t seem to work. But the ones mounted on the side of the building did.
Nice place, so relaxing! The kids enjoyed playing in the playground area!!

I love this family friendly park. It saddens me that I don't see more parents there with their children especially when it has extended Summer Nights!! Plenty of barbecues to enjoy your family without spending a lot of money!!

name: Arnold Cypress Park
formatted_address: 8611 Watson St, Cypress, CA 90630, USA

google_reviews.text:
The park renovation is impressive which reopened in May 2025! I remember this park was so dated and not as inviting. But with all the new additions, resurfacing, and more, I'm sure more people will be excited to use this park. I've visited here a few times already to walk the track and get in a workout in the outdoor fitness area.

There's plenty to offer at this local park. Baseball/softball fields, batting cages, eight pickleball courts, one volleyball court, one basketball court, playgrounds for older and younger kids, a marked walking/jogging track around the park that is approximately 1/2 mile for one lap.

There's plenty of parking as well as EV charging spots. I noticed that there only few benches or seating areas available for resting or relaxing. There are also shaded bleachers next to the pickleball courts. There are also some covered picnic tables.

Looking forward to visiting this park more!

This is a really nice, well-maintained facility. It’s a great place for large or small parties. There’s a pavilion with picnic table seating for about 100 adults and additional smaller pavilions nearby. The grills and eating areas are clean. The baseball diamond look brand new.

8 brand new pickleball courts with bright LED lights at night. Courts are free to use and have individual paddle holders for cue to each court. Covered bleachers available on west side. Middle area courts lack enough sitting area for cue waiting and inhibit playing due to cue standing. There's plenty of parking adjacent courts. Echargers available next to courts. May 2025 was Grand Opening for entire park.

Update: closed for reconstruction likely until
Late 2025 or early 2026

Pickleball, lights go off at 10 pm. Crowded around 7 pm to 830 pm. Not a lot of people playing before 6 pm.

Most frequently people play in the morning from 8:30 to 11:30 a.m.

Pick up pickleball here. No reservations here. Unless there are lessons on the far side court.
There are a total of six courts here.

Two parking lots located here. First Parking lot is next to the baseball fields. Second parking lot located next to picnic area

One volleyball court next to the baseball field and parking lot. Cement flooring.
Basketball courts are on the opposite side and there are no lights during the night time.

There are two baseball fields total here. One next to the parking lot and the other on the four corner of the park.
Eversince the city renovated on this park, it's been the a hit spot for everyone. All the upgrades are top notch! Great place to play sports, walks, playground for kids!

name: Vermont Square Park
formatted_address: 1248 W 47th St, Los Angeles, CA 90037, USA

google_reviews.text:
This is a great park for the community. The area is very large for its size. It has exercises machine, basketball courts large playground with slides and swings. In addition it has restrooms. If you go during the week is less busy than the weekend with more toddlers running around.
Picnic tables onsite with bbq grills.
I recommend this place if you go early afternoons.
Decent small neighborhood park. There are swings , basketball court picnic tables, and workout gear.  It's pretty clean, too
The park it's self is 4 out of 5 cleaned. They fixed what was broken so it's not bad. The issue is sometimes the restrooms are locked or taken up for to long.
Surprisingly clean and peaceful park. The surrounding homes are very nice as well.

I had an enjoyable time in Vermont Square Park with family and friends. It is an excellent place to spend a day in a tidy and well-kept neighborhood. It is strongly suggested for a fun and restful visit!

name: St. James Park
formatted_address: St James Pl &, St James Park W, Los Angeles, CA 90007, USA

google_reviews.text:
Cool little park.
Kinda hidden, so if u can find it we'll good for you. That means your one of the cool kids 😎 lol

i wouldn't really boast about this park being that big of a deal. it's a small patch of grass with minimal shade and the grass is patchy or at least it was when we visited. we were hoping there would be a small walking trail or something to see or do for entertainment but there really wasn't. there is a building which we didn't get near but it looked like the windows were boarded up and run down. i hope they can add something here or make it a better park altogether. i highly suggest not going out of your way to visit like we did as it was a mistake. the only upside was that there was parking available but aside from that i wouldn't recommend visiting.

Early mornings are so much better if your dogs are not dog friendly. They can enjoy the park to themselves. But it gets a bit crowded after 1pm. Dogs are loose and cross to the side of the street and it can get a bit scary if cars are constantly driving by. It would be great if we can fence it up so as dog owners we can feel a bit more safe letting the dogs loose.

A cute little mini-park tucked along the side of the beautiful + historic Mt. Saint Mary University. Many big, shady trees + lots of pretty grass. Popular for dog walks. Many well-behaved dogs come here for off-leash play. Small garden in center. 1 working water fountain. Relatively safe area even after dark. Pretty neighborhood lends to a nice walk too. Street parking is almost always an available. Popular with students to study + stretch.  A few homeless folks do hang here now + then but I've never had any problems over the many years I've been coming here. No fenced dog area so watch them carefully.  No playground. No restrooms. No benches. Nearby mini-market 1 street away. A historical marker across street has interesting story of this place too. Worth a read. Please pick up your dog waste + trash, friends, so we can all enjoy.
Friendly neighborhood historic park. Be sure to read the history of St James Park on the angel walk sign when you're there.

name: Mountain View Park
formatted_address: 1401 W Riverside Dr, Burbank, CA 91506, USA

google_reviews.text: A new favorite park of mine in Burbank! Visited here in June 2025. Lots of shade and tables, the playground was excellent, and lots of green space to enjoy. The trees provided excellent shade! I loved being able to see the mountain view from one of the tables I sat at. And just a short walk around the corner from Basecamp, one of my favorite cafes! Will come back!

Wonderful small clean park.
Families gather for birthdays, tennis, basketball, kite etc.
Small Gem in the heart of Burbank. Easy parking. Would recommend.

It is not a very big park in Burbank, but here you can play tennis and basketball. There are also picnic tables, kids playground and restroom. No parking inside the park, but you can park on streets surrounding the park.

Excellent neighborhood park in Burbank.....

No dedicated parking available, you have to park across the roads both sides on the park.....

Public restrooms available inside the park....

Moderate size playing field and Tennis and basket ball court available....

Nice place to spend time with family..... excellent kids activity area.....Sitting bench is also available...

Don't think they have BBQ place....

Very small but has a big enough playground for kids, my kids loved it, didn't want to leave, and as we were leaving asked when we were going back, it's nice and clean,  has clean bathrooms as well, will definitely go back a'd would recommend to take your kids here, oh a'd it has a basketball court, and tennis courts as well,

name: Maxwell Park
formatted_address: 2655 W Orange Ave, Anaheim, CA 92804, USA

google_reviews.text: Playground more than 50% boarded up. Equipment missing. Metal exposed through the plastic coating. No good for toddlers at this point. Boring for older ones right now. Injury hazard for all. Go to Twila Reid instead. Only 1.5 miles away.

I wanted to update my visits to this park. I've visited  several times since my previous review and I don't  have anything negative to say about any of my times spent here. It's crowded on the weekends due to people playing sports but always plenty of parking and no problems with walking my dog. I am excited about the future dog park that will be opening soon. It looks like it ready for visitors and it's going to be the best dog park in the area. They have a real nice dog park once the gates are open and because of being Anahiems newest dog park they have done a real good job. It looks like it will be bigger then the others and nice see my photos I posted.

Good park, I just went there for a walk with my dog and we both enjoyed the fresh air. Definitely will be going back.

The fields are flat and void of serious holes. There is 92% grass which is good but the dirt spots aren't that big of a deal for it to be noticeable when you're playing a sport. The size of the fields are just big enough. Looks like a great spot!
Dog park here is great. Everything is as clean as it can, and you can feel safe walking here in the afternoons.
Newly built Park no shade and no drinking fountains on the park

name: Fern Dell Nature Preserve
formatted_address: 5375 Red Oak Dr, Los Angeles, CA 90068, USA

google_reviews.text: Beautiful and walkable trail with real flowing water. Cute cafe nearby with amazing drinks and pastries. Overall a lovely little spot, perfect for a walk with friends or a date :)

It's just an amazing eco trail! If you are with a child, then take a comfortable pair of shoes with you, there can be wet and slippery roads. Everything is like in a living forest! Very picturesque streams!A great place to hide from the heat, fuss, with the children it is very fun there like a mysterious park! I recommend comfortable waterproof shoes and clothes. if stroller then all-terrain vehicle

Here’s one of my favorite natural areas within Los Angeles and among the more forest like of Griffith Park!
Who would’ve thought to discover such an urban wilderness, minutes from the hustle and bustle of LA?
Just off of Los Feliz Boulevard by the northernmost end of Western Avenue, this part of Griffith Park is like a park of it’s own! Fern Dell Drive continues north as the main road for instant access into the woods here.
Parking along Fern Dell Drive is possible where permitted and where several small trails meander beside this rustic corridor, flanked by upscale residential neighborhoods. There’s small clearings with concrete picnic tables and trash containers. A ranger station is near the bottom of the hill. Parking is also available in lots beyond the station.

Great find yesterday on line! Trail has so many level options from beginner to a bit more challenging. Some parts are heavily shaded other routes 🌞. Beautiful plants, some wildlife and many happy doggies on walks with their persons. Water running splendor. Very peaceful. I highly recommend for short to long ventures. Seems like multiple experiences in one location. On hot days go super early.

I did and im prompt to visit this morning after seeing it suggested for a hiking trail online. It is a wonderful location that has many versatile trails. In different directions at different levels of hiking or walking. Water Little ponds and falls run through the Walking trail and is a wonderful addition to the ombiance. Said it was a 2 and a 1/2 mile loop but I only did 40 minutes.. Its dog friendly people friendly baby friendly and just a wonderful experience of nature.

name: South Pasadena Nature Park
formatted_address: 100 Pasadena Ave, South Pasadena, CA 91030, USA

google_reviews.text: Nice little park, with a trail that goes along the golf course to San Pascual Park in Pasadena. It’s an easy, shaded trail that is good for the summer.

Lovely quiet park to jog through or take your kids. Explore the labyrinth, and find the flower and monarch displays. My kids love to run on the colorful skateboard ramps, and we even saw a gopher! The park is always changing as well. There's a strong community of volunteers who enhance this park year round, so it's always fun to see what they put in next.

really cute small park, easily accessible by transit (and Metro Micro if you live in the north-east LA zone from Atwater to Highland Park)

the volunteers have done an incredible job of maintaining the park and restoring the native plant life. this is more of a walking around kinda park, not really any seating. I had a good time sitting on a rock and ready my book tho

There are plenty of parking. There is an opening to the top from mini golf park or enter all the way down at the end of the park.

The entrance to start hiking is where the stair and labyrinth at. If you don't want stair, start hiking to the right of the labyrinth There is a path, it lead up.

Easy hiking path.

Very nice walking trail. One side is through the trees and very shady. The other side is a nicely designed walking and biking path. We passed a golf course and a mini-golf course. Very peaceful trail, short enough to walk with your kiddo or dog. Don't miss the gorgeous views of the elegant LA River.

name: El Dorado Nature Center
formatted_address: 7550 E Spring St, Long Beach, CA 90815, USA

google_reviews.text:
★★★★★

A visit to El Dorado Nature Center is the perfect reminder of how peaceful a simple nature walk can be. The trails are easy and well-maintained, making them accessible for just about anyone who wants to enjoy some time outdoors.

The scenery is beautiful the entire way—lush greenery, shady trees, little streams, and plenty of spots where you might catch glimpses of birds, turtles, or other wildlife. There are multiple trail options, so you can keep it short and leisurely or wander a bit longer depending on your mood.

One of my favorite things about this place is how many stopping points there are along the way. Benches are thoughtfully placed throughout the trails, giving you plenty of chances to pause, take in the views, listen to the birds, and just soak in the calm atmosphere.

It’s a quiet, refreshing escape from the city and a perfect spot for a relaxed walk, a mindful moment in nature, or a peaceful afternoon outdoors. Highly recommend! 🌿🦆🌳

A real gem in the middle of urban sprawl. This park has over two miles of easy trails to walk wooded and riparian scenery. Hard to believe this area used to be flat farmland. There are two man-made lakes connected by a curving stream with an abundance of birds and wildlife. The many trees offer welcome shade to hikers year around. I highly recommend...

A nice short hike into a forest in the middle of the city. There are two ponds here and a nature center, and three main path options. You have to pay $6-$8 to park here, but you can avoid the fee by parking outside in the neighborhoods, which adds 1/2 mile walk over the freeway. Also, you can also see small fish, squirrels, and water turtles here.

We just revisited El Dorado Nature Center as a family, and once again it did not disappoint. We walked all three trails — short loop, quarter-mile, and the full two-mile loop — and the experience was peaceful, immersive, and beautifully maintained.

This place may be tucked inside a bustling part of Long Beach, but once you’re in, it feels like you’ve entered a different world. Lush native habitat, shaded paths, wooden bridges, flowing streams, and quiet wildlife make it an ideal escape for both adults and kids. We spotted ducks, turtles, squirrels, and even a blue jay — all within a short walk.

What stands out most:
•	The paved sensory trail is great for strollers and accessible walking.
•	The educational signs about plants, birds, insects, and local ecology make it a perfect learning walk for kids.
•	The park is exceptionally well preserved — quiet, clean, and clearly cared for by the city and community.
•	Even the small things like benches, railings, and signage are thoughtfully placed and well-integrated into the landscape.

This is our second visit in a few years, and it’s even better than we remembered. While the center isn’t huge, it’s so well designed that you can easily spend an hour or two wandering without getting bored. It’s a great spot for reflection, gentle exercise, or just introducing kids to nature in a safe and curated way.

Highly recommend for families, nature lovers, or anyone looking for a peaceful break close to the city. We’ll absolutely be back again.

Great place to walk or hike to enjoy nature. I saw lizards, squirrels, turtles,  dragonflies, and even bass! There is a  2-mile, 1-mile, and 1/4-mile (paved) trail. The nature center there has some souvenirs to purchase, as well some great information about the history of the area and it's native animals. There is a fee to get in and the fee varies depending on what day you go. There are plenty of benches on each trail and alot of it is
shaded.

name: Stoneview Nature Center
formatted_address: 5950 Stoneview Dr, Culver City, CA 90232, USA

google_reviews.text:
This review is for the hike trail that connects the stone view nature center to the Kenneth hanh state park.
Our scouts did a hike here and it was amazing. It was a bit challenging for younger ones but anyone grade 4 and above would have no issues.

From the nature center, follow the path, cross the bridge, get to the Japanese garden. When you get there turn left and follow the red entrance sign to continue on the trail. When you come across another split, turn left to keep going. You are at the right place when you come across a dried up waterfall or water fountain looking area.

Continue up hill until you reach your first Pagoda with amazing views to your left (Hollywood sign) and views to your right.

Continue pass that part to get to 2 sticks built tent or teepee. That was the end of our hike before we rounded back. This was a good ppace for snack break with plenty of shades. All this took roughly 2 hours round trip.

Kiddos loved it and lots to see along the path.

Just discovered this place, although it's been open for 8 years already. Beautiful and quiet nature center with California drought tolerant flora. They have a little quail cage too that houses six adorable female quails they call divas. There's also a labyrinth marked by rocks that fun to walk through.

I saw a bird at the lake in Kenneth Hahn State Recreation Area this morning (October 29). A fishing line was tightly wrapped around its right leg, almost cutting it off, and had started to tangle around the other leg too.
I told the lady at the front desk of Stoneview Nature Center, and she responded so kindly and quickly. I truly appreciate her warm care and hope the bird can recover soon.
The staff at Stoneview Nature Center are always welcoming and gentle. It’s one of my favorite places — full of kindness and peace. 🕊️🌿
This place is amazing! It is so quiet and tranquil. And you can walk over to Kenneth Hawn State Park via the walking bridge just make sure you get back before the gate closes!

Nice little community garden, easy parking maybe 20 spaces... The trail serves as a Midway point between Kenneth Hahn and the top of the  Culver City stairs... Definitely a hidden gem. Go left if you want to go to Kenneth Hahn's, right to the Stairs.  Slight incline (compared to treadmill 5-6) to the stairs wear decent shoes, not difficult.

name: Walnut Creek Nature Park
formatted_address: 701 Frazier St, Baldwin Park, CA 91706, USA

google_reviews.text: Nice peaceful place for a quiet walk or jog. Has a concrete pathway. I went in the morning/noon on a weekday.  Has a play area for kids, with swings and seesaw. Parking is ok but I can see it getting full if it's the weekend.

Awesome little nature park in Baldwin Park, located right in the middle of its residential area. It has a small trail walk area, some simple exercise areas, log climbing for kids from 3 to 12 years, and a picnic area. My boys love it very much.
Very small nature park overlooking the San Gabriel river and 10 freeway. Great location for pictures and small gatherings. My son loves the obstacle course.

This park in at the end of a cul de sac, very secluded and private. Fun little park for young and old. The park has a 3/4 mile loop track for walking and biking. It has a nice playground area for 2 to 10 year old kids. There picnic table all around the park. Exercise areas all over the park as well. The park sign states that it closes at 4 pm but it is not so. I believe the gate closes at sunset.
Quite little playground. Easy parking, dog friendly.

name: Santa Fe Dam Nature Center
formatted_address: San Gabriel River Trail, Irwindale, CA 91706, USA

google_reviews.text: Nice park near Azusa at the base of Los Angeles National Forest. You can bike/walk from along the San Gabriel River all the way to Highway 39. Ample parking and they were not charging the $12 parking.

This is a good place for a stop durring a ride (cycling) to & from the San Gabriel Canyons of Azusa.. there are hiking trails or for Mountain bikes.. I've seen coyotes stroll thru this area,,so keep alert when with pets & kids.
Very nice park, and a great place to go on hikes and has a good biking trail.

On behalf of the Santa Fe Dam Nature Center, I would like to extend my upmost gratitude to you and your hard work to make the Nature Center reopening a reality.

We are now open to public on Saturday morning from 10:00 to 12:00. Wildflower walk will be conducted at 10:30.

There' still a lot more work need to be done but we have reached a major milestone thanks to all the volunteers effort.

Thank you.

Water fountain for people & pets. Nature trail & Interpretive trail, with benches. Educational if you're interested in native plants. Quiet spots ideal for birdwatching. Small parking area. Watch out for animal droppings.

name: Robert E. Ward Nature Preserve
formatted_address: 2245 N Euclid St, Fullerton, CA 92835, USA

google_reviews.text: This was my 3rd time visiting and it’s always a pleasant hike. I wish they would allow more trails around the preserve to be open to the public, there’s a lot of the Coyote Hills that are limited despite being mostly public land.

Great place to hike, steep hills to climb and really work on your legs and buns of steel, during the spring I'm sure it's lush and green but for now in the winter the bushes are a tad dry, watch out for bikes.

Not a loop trial... trail only on the south side that runs along houses/backyards. No parking on Euclid street - unless you want to chance parking in the church's lot nearby.  Google maps had us parking on Armstead which has a trail head, but is a neighborhood cul de sac, so not really much in the way of public parking.
Otherwise... nice trail even after recent rains. Minimal foot traffic. Frequent bikers that mostly use the bike pathway. Horses have used the trail but we did not see any when we were there.
The weather was great. It's a great place to hike but definitely needs to be done with a guide. As a first time visitor having someone guide me to complete the entire loop would have helped.
Love this mountain bike trail, the Fullerton Loop. I've done it probably 40+ times now, ...so any questions, please don't hesitate to ask!

name: Nature Friends of Los Angeles
formatted_address: 423 Yucca Trail, Sierra Madre, CA 91024, USA

google_reviews.text: Very peaceful solo getaway. It feels like you’re in another country up in the canyon. Sierra Madre Creek was flowing. The room was quiet and comfortable. Zak was helpful. Downtown Sierra Madre is 5 min back down the hill, with great food and coffee. Syndicate Coffee has amazing pastries and espresso. Some of the best hikes in SoCal. 5-6 trailheads within a 20 min drive along the 210 corridor into the San Gabriels from my Sierra Madre base. Several with waterfalls if there’s been good winter rains. I had my road bike with me as well, and got some good cycling in along the rolling foothills over to Arroyo Seco, Alta Dena, Pasadena etc. Overall a great stay!!

Nature friends is one of the most tranquil places that I have ever been.  One can stay here, and or have an event at a much lower cost than pretty much anywhere else.  everything is vintage and "lived in", so don't expect it to be like a four star hotel.  What you can expect is peacefulness, remoteness and charming prewar architecture, surrounded by natural beauty.

This beautiful and rustic space has been the home of so many wonderful memories over the years. I’ve attended weddings, taken educational workshops, enjoyed professional dancers and musicians traveling through, watched theatrical experiences, enjoyed movie nights, shared wonderful dinners… the list goes on. So happy that Nature Friends is easily accessible for folks like myself here in LA. I also love that it’s situated right at the edge of the Angeles National Forest - you never know what amazing wildlife you’ll witness.

I wanted that to be my wedding venue so about 4 months ago I asked for a tour and after the tour I decided it would work for what I had envisioned so I put down the $300 deposit and reserved a day in July 2018. Fast forward to February. I'm postponing my wedding to 2019. Me and my fiancé were told that if we cancelled with over 3 months notice we could get our deposit back. They are now saying they don't have a reservation under our name or any record of a deposit. I know I have the receipt somewhere and I will find it and I will get my money back but this is not a trustworthy establishment. I still wanted to get married here in 2019 and this is not about the money at all. To have someone sit you down in your office, write down the date in front of them, take their money, thank them for their business, shake their hand, and then tell them there have no record is so shameless. After seeing how unprofessional and untrustworthy they are they have lost my respect and my business. I do not recommend them to anyone.

I live down the street less than a block away from this place, and most of the comments are false. Is a shame that they are using the phrase "Friends of Nature" so they can generate business, specially when they have nothing to do with nature.  Second, the closest trail trailhead is Mount Wilson; which is approximately 1.8 miles from there, and you have to walk down to the city and then up in order to reach it.
No ⭐ from my part, the only one ⭐ you see is forced by the system, not mine.

name: Nature Park
formatted_address: 4201 Ball Rd, Cypress, CA 90630, USA

google_reviews.text: "Nature Park" is a funny name as it is actually a flood control basin that people are not allowed in. It is a big hole dug in the ground for storm water to run to with pumps that can then pump it out to nearby Carbon creek. The hole has had mostly nursery type trees planted around its edge and then the whole thing is fenced off to keep people safe....  Now who ever thought to name it "Nature Park" one must wonder if they did so as some sort of joke.... Its in no way natural nor a park in any sense of either of those words...
This is a great little Nature Park you cannot go wrong take the family enjoy the day take a picnic as we did and have a nice lunch with Mother Nature Norwalk radar out
Creepy and Unsettling atmosphere. Especially in the middle section of Nature Park. I don’t care if it’s manmade its creepy. I don’t recommend going alone…
This place is never open. Have lived in Cypress all my life and have never been. Seems like a waste of space.
Serves a great purpose year round and is a beautiful to stroll when it is open during the summer.

name: Whittier Narrows Nature Center Associates
formatted_address: 1000 Durfee Ave, South El Monte, CA 91733, USA

google_reviews.text: The last Sunday of every month they host a free event that is just incredible. A master musician brings all his instruments and creates an amazing soundbath for the community. No tickets needed, just show up at noon the last Sunday each month. What a gift ...Absolutely incredible.

The Nature Center was great parking was free I had a great walk and there's a lot of dead animals you can touch and snakes and it was great for my 6 and two year old I would definitely recommend and I will go back
Very educational for kids with park ambiance. Quiet and very relaxing area for families with kids. The best thing is its Free

$10 a day to park at a public park—even at 6am on a weekday—is an absolute disgrace. The only people here that early are seniors in our community trying to stay active and healthy, and instead of supporting that, you’re blocking access unless they pay up. It’s shameful.

This is supposed to be a park for the public, not a money grab. Seniors shouldn’t have to pay just to walk and exercise. And what about all the homeless living here—how much are they paying? Nothing. Yet the people who actually follow the rules and just want to use the park responsibly are the ones getting punished.

Public Works should be embarrassed for approving something like this. It’s a slap in the face to the community.

I visited the Whittier Nature Center last July, and although this review is long overdue, the experience has truly stayed with me. I had found a tiny bird in need of help, and Colleen, the sweetest and most knowledgeable person, kindly guided me through what to do. She told me to bring the bird in, and once I arrived, she took over with such care and expertise, explaining the next steps for the little one.

While I was there, I had a brief tour of the grounds, and I was amazed by how peaceful and clean everything was. The center’s mission is truly beautiful, and you can feel the dedication of the volunteers in every corner. I haven’t stopped thinking about this place since my visit and would love to volunteer myself once my schedule allows.

Thank you, Colleen, and all the amazing volunteers for the incredible work you do!

name: Marsh Street Nature Park
formatted_address: 2944 Gleneden St, Los Angeles, CA 90039, USA

google_reviews.text:
Went looking for Public art and stumbled on this beautiful gem of a park.
Plenty of parking.
Lots of exercise machines in really good shape (i.e. no graffiti).
Lots of open spaces to throw a football or toss a Frisbee.
Skate Park is at the edge of the park. Park is parallel to the bike path that runs along the LA River.
It's like a corner neighborhood park, will definitely be back again.

This is not for the park itself which is not bad. It's small. But the location is why this is being mentioned. It is by the LA river which had a nice bike path going next to it and few restaurants along it. So, is it's a good day, and that can be said on most days, it's a nice walk or bike ride (with bikes available to rent at the spoke restaurant)

Always love coming down here to take a walk, sometimes alone sometimes with my pup. I love how calm and chill it is here specially during sun down. I highly recommend it if you just want to take a nice stroll or nice bike ride. The wildlife here is pretty cool.

It’s clean, and has a dirt path around the outside with circuit training devices and clean bathrooms. It also has a ChargePoint station if you have a plug in electric car. It’s a great little picnic nook and it has an access point to the LA River. If that’s all you want in a little neighborhood park, then this park is great.
There was a small wedding chapel. Also the playground were small an far from the skate park. There was a plaque and an open field unfenced for dogs to play.

name: Whittier Narrows Natural Area
formatted_address: Rosemead Blvd, South El Monte, CA 91733, USA

google_reviews.text: Beautiful, relaxing and the wildlife ( mostly birds) take you right out of the city! Easy to forget you are smack dab in the middle of Los Angeles California 🦆

Nice local nature area. Mostly flat and paved so easy hiking/bicycling. At the moment lots of water in the San Gabriel River due to recent storms so lots of running water and everything is nicely green. You can do a nice long hike either north towards the San Gabriels or South. A few bicycles, a couple of people on horseback and a few hikers. Plenty of parking at Whittier Narrows park.

Paradise of affordable housing, sustainable living, and freedom lifestyle for nature-in-touch homeless people, their stylish homemade homes, and their deliberately decorated installation art of garbage piles.

Great place to get away from the city. This nature center is nestled right into a little bit of heaven. There is a handicapped accessible trail, Whittier Narrows Police Dept is there, a little museum with #insects #gemsandminerals #taxidermy and they even have outdoor nature classes and events for the  whole family. They have night hikes and this was also the place where the Orignal Tarzan was filmed.
A great place for a little walk or a bike ride. Love the little lakes and wildlife

name: Stone Barn Nature Center
formatted_address: 3429 Markridge Rd, Glendale, CA 91214, USA

google_reviews.text: Love this place! We discovered it when my kid was a young toddler and I’m pretty sure it’s the reason he loves Nature Centers. Nice array of displays, not too big, and a great kids area with books and toys. There’s a lovely mosaic outside and some fantastic hikes. Good picnicking area and sometimes you catch a beautiful sunset.

Stone Barn Nature Center is a small educational center dedicated to the wildlife and community. Back in the early 19th century this building was a residence, barn and a winery. The history of the stone barn dates to 1898 when the property was acquired by George Le Mesnager, a French emigrant and prominent Los Angeles vintner, from Dr. Benjamin Briggs, the founder of La Crescenta. Between 1914 and 1918, George’s son Louis constructed the original stone barn and shed along with several other buildings that no longer exist. They were used for storing equipment and grapes until 1920, when Prohibition ended the winemaking industry and forced the Le Mesnagers into the water industry.

As the turn of the century, this center is a place to learn about the wild animals in the Cresenta valley. It’s a great place for kids and family. Overall, it’s a nice center.
This place is great! Jacquie is so knowledgeable, and told us about a great route to go hiking. Really lovely for birding if you go early :)
Fantastic! Recommend visiting and going for a hike.

name: Chilao Campground
formatted_address: unnamed road, Palmdale, CA 93550, USA

google_reviews.text: On the weekend, bring earplugs if you want to sleep. The chill party vibe is very cool about 8pm with folks hanging out at all the spots. Some people even come, set up a dinner hang, and leave without actually camping. But about midnight if you want to sleep you could be next to a crew that just won't STFU. Beautiful spot though. Would be an all-timer if quiet hours were enforced.

Great sights and a great time. It didn't have running water but it was the middle of the summer. The restrooms were vault toilets so they could only be so clean but, every single one always had toilet paper. The check in system was interesting (in a good way) it was mostly based off the honor system where you fill out a tag with how long you'll be there put the money in an envelope then put it in a box. Make sure you bring cash and a pen.

It was EMPTY when we went, and I mean it. I expected it to be a little crammed in summer but in total there were four other people at the entire site. It was amazing as once you walk away it's like you have the forest to yourself.

There are loads of hiking trails, a visitor center nearby, and a creek which was stuffed with tadpoles this time of the year... for 12 dollars a night. If you can handle the long twisty drive up I highly suggestyou go! There are no off-roading or fire roads involved to get to the site so take any vehicle you want. If my Ford focus can make it easily you can too.

One of the best breakfast of the year!
This place is located at the top of done of mountains and the view is magnificent.   So beautiful and chili place.  Thank you Chilao!

Our visit in May offered comfortable weather, although nights can get chilly; packing a jacket or blanket is wise, but you won't be shivering in your tent. Days are filled with ample sunshine, perfect for outdoor adventures.

The grounds are impeccably maintained, and the restrooms can be better. However, it's also worth noting that there's no water source nearby, and unfortunately, no water spigot available near the restroom facilities. So bring your own dinking water and gray water.

The campsites themselves are generously spaced out, and reservations can be conveniently made through the government website. Interagency access holders even enjoy discounted rates. Remember to bring your own firewood as local trees aren't suitable for burning, minimizing the risk of uncontrollable fires due to sparks. Check the entrance for current fire restriction.

While the lack of water nearby might be a downside for some, there are plenty of opportunities for hiking in the surrounding areas, offering breathtaking views and exploration.  The lot loop around, great for biking, not suggest to do it with kid, because of the hills.

Overall, this campsite is a fantastic choice for nature enthusiasts. Remember to respect the environment by keeping it clean and leaving no trace. Let's preserve the beauty of our natural surroundings for future generations to enjoy.

Awesome place to camp! Close-ish to the city, with toilet stalls (no running water this time of year). Gets very cold at night, so bring the right gear! Beautiful Silver Mocassin Trail connects directly to the campground.

name: Glenn Camp Campground
formatted_address: Devils Canyon Dam Truck Trail, Azusa, CA 91702, USA

google_reviews.text: This campground is very nice and peaceful to camp at. The Glenn Canyon Falls, a small 200 foot waterfall, is near here and can be located by following the Glenn Creek to observers left all the way until it ends at the falls.

The campground is fine. Though the outhouse is pretty disgusting. Beware of aggressive drivers who work at the Dam. They are very disrespectful, drive way too fast and on the weekends. The place is closed on weekdays until the debris removal is complete but open on weekends only. There are conflicting reports if the area is open beginning Friday evening
Beautiful, clean campground. San gaberial river near by. Fishing platform for fishing. You have to bike in or hike in with your gear. It's about 6.5 mile to the campground from your car.

See my review for Azusa Canyon West Fork Devils Dam TT.
I didn't camp here, just rode by on bike. Cannot access Cogswell Dam 1 mile up road. Killer hill to ride down though.
Campground seems kinda boring. No trails that I saw. Just hike in 6 miles on paved road. This is what city folk call roughing it. At least there is a river and fishing.
Beautiful area, especially in Azusa Canyon. 6+ miles back. Bikepacking trip, it was awesome. YouTube... RedWhite&Blue 78

name: Rancho Ybarra
formatted_address: 3150 Big Tujunga Canyon Rd, Tujunga, CA 91042, USA

google_reviews.text: Great place to be away from the city.
Very beautiful place. Peaceful and the we're blessed to be alive enjoying the beauty of nature and God's Love.

Awesome place to camp or to visit for one day. The owners are super friendly.  I would recommend this to family's who want to have fun .  They have a big pool,basketball court and a huge park to play soccer oh and a river. It's safe and it's a Christian park so you know most of the people are believers of our LORD N SAVIOR JESUSCHRIST!🤗

It's been a great place to have alone with God and away from where you live. You must bring your own things and it does need more TLC but I don't go for the commodities. I go to have a time alone with God and the nature and privacy of each room is great!

I spent one night at Rancho Ybarra as part of a training with my church and I'm glad it was only one night! The overall experience was terrible! The whole place is in dire need of repair. Giving it just 1 star doesn't even begin to explain how bad it was. Don't camp here!!!

As soon as we opened the bedroom doors, the smell of dirty bathrooms punched our nostrils, and the people with sensitive gag reflexes got teary-eyed right away. The smell didn't go away even after we let the door and windows open for a few minutes. The bedroom carpet in the rooms we used, and the carpet in the meeting room, were completely stained with what looks like spills from years ago. The floors were completely covered with crumbs, and they looked like no one bothered to vacuum for our stay. I saw a couple of dead roaches lying around too.

The people in my room got flea and tick bites. Bedbugs? For the looks of it, this place hasn't been fumigated for a long time. The water in our shower didn't heat up at all, even after we let it run for a while. Those of us who wanted to shower at night did so with cold water.

None of the restrooms stalls outside closed properly. Every single latch I tried was broken. The communal shower rooms are also in need of repair as shower heads and stalls are corroded and rusty. There were no streams or lakes. There is only a small body of stale water, mossy, green and bubbly.

One thing is to stay at a place that is rustic and purposefully sparse but CLEAN (those actually make for a very nice experience), and another is to stay at a rundown place that is dirty and poorly maintained. Camp Ybarra is in desperate need of  major renovations and until they do it, you’re better off choosing to stay elsewhere.

name: Hopkins Wilderness Park
formatted_address: 1102 Camino Real, Redondo Beach, CA 90277, USA

google_reviews.text: Very small trail. Got campsites but don't think it's not ideal. Better for a short hike/walk. Peaceful & quiet. Not a lot if people. Small lake's a plus.

Such an amazing beautiful park. My children love this. They even do birthday parties! There's turtles and fish in the pond and there's tons of walking space that kids love to roam around in. There's a bathroom (not a porta-potty, which is great). If you do a birthday party there they even have Park rangers that will help you during the day! They even have a place that you can plant your own garden but I'm not familiar with how you participate that program.

Tucked right in the middle of the city, this place feels like a hidden gem. The trails are peaceful, the greenery is lush, and it offers the perfect escape without needing to go far. It was the kind of day that fills your soul—good food, fresh air, and nature just a few minutes from the coast.

Can’t ask for a better way to spend an afternoon.

Hopkins Wilderness Park is a nice and peaceful area. At the entrance, there is a recreation center if you wish to enlist your children there or volunteer. There is about 3-5 picnic area all come with restrooms and a BBQ grill with 3-4 seating areas. Along the trail there is an outdoor stadium with seats that can accommodate many people. It's a nice stroll to have with the family, especially if they're into the outdoors. The main pond has turtles, fish, tadpoles, insects and many more. A good little place to get away from the hustle and bustle of the city life and enjoy what God has created.

Such a lovely, quiet place to eat a packed lunch and get away from the hustle and bustle of the work week. The little stream and pond were relaxing to observe. Watched the turtles and fish in the pond as well as some little local critters while walking around in the rest of the park. Good amount of free parking. Nice to enjoy a park that's not geared towards sports and playgrounds - just a little bit of nature.

name: Bear Canyon Campground
formatted_address: Bear Canyon Campground, Altadena, CA 91001, USA

google_reviews.text: An amazing hike to the campground, but the campground is pretty lame. There is 5 major waterfalls and 26 creek crossings to get to this campground.

We did the trail from Switzer trailhead to bear canyon campground. It was ~9mi trail (out and back). The whole trail passed through the water stream. Very scenic view. Lot of bird humming is heard in the whole stretch. We found few wildflower blooms on our way. Since there is lot of switching sides of the stream throughout the trail, your shoes would get literally wet. So, recommend wearing waterproof shoes and waterproof socks to save your feet 🥶. Carry ample drinking water. Campground is primitive equipped with fire pit, picnic bench, shade, cast iron heating plank for heating ur food. You need to have pre-paid adventure recreation pass to part your vehicle at the trailhead.

A steep trail that isn’t on many lists—it climbs to a ridge with canyon views. The top is rocky, with a small stone bench someone built. I set up a small picnic there and watched hawks circling. Bring sturdy shoes, as some parts get a bit slippery. It’s challenging but unbelievably quiet

Hiked in Saturday morning from Miller camp ground, we spent the night and hiked back Sunday morning

Good backpacking trip for beginners

A Backcountry campground, not much more than a stove, decaying Picnic Table, and a flat area to pitch your tent. A nice, peaceful campground off the beaten path. In 2014 the trail to the campground had many felled trees blocking the path, not sure if they are still there. Beware of Poison oak!

name: Gould Mesa Trail Camp
formatted_address: Gould Mesa, Arroyo Blvd, La Cañada Flintridge, CA 91011, USA

google_reviews.text: Very tranquill trail and easy to hike. A little elevation.

Great backpacking site for beginners. The trail in is about 2.5 miles. The 1st mile is very urban and not super pretty. The last 1.5 miles follows the stream and is beautiful and shady. There are 3 stream crossings before you reach the campsite. All along the trail are bridges, picnic tables, stone walls and benches for resting. The campsite itself is pretty with a fire ring at every site. There are several places to hitch a horse. The campground has about 6 sites in total but not every site has a picnic table. Some sites have a log to sit on by the fire if you don’t want to pack a chair. There was no toilet paper in the vault toilets and the bear safe trash cans were overflowing. There was one “long term camper” but he didn’t get us any trouble. The main trail runs just beside the campground with a steady stream of trail runners, mountain bikers and hikers. The stream runs just on the other side of the trail and is wonderfully cool and refreshing. Pack water shoes or extra clothes if you have kiddos that like to explore.

This is a decent campsite right along the Gabrielino Trail and is close to the water and the city. There is also so good quality restrooms here as well, and I have not seen anyone camp here on my hike.

If you visit, PLEASE throw trash away in designated spots, or pack it out! This campground is next to a very busy trail that is frequented by locals. The bathrooms were clean and there is currently running water in the stream below the camp. Cute little campground that’s not too far away, which is perfect for a quick weekend getaway from the city!

This is one of my favorite hikes in LA.  It's a 5.2 mile hike if you go left at the camp grounds.  It's pretty easy and goes right along the river to a big dam waterfall.  The hike back up is more challenging, but totally doable.  Great place to picnic and stroll along!

Once you hit the bottom of the canyon there is shade. The trail is not maintained.

name: Oakwilde Campground
formatted_address: Angeles Crest Hwy, Mt Lukens Truck Trail, La Cañada Flintridge, CA 91011, USA

google_reviews.text: Cool little adventure and campsite if you know what you’re getting yourself into in terms of the backcountry. It’s a closed/abandoned small backcountry campsite so obviously don’t expect any campsite amenities whatsoever. It’s overgrown in general but the trail is quite obvious, though I may not have attempted if I didn’t have a GPS device to help. We took a trail off of the 2 (right across the road from “old dark canyon trail) and followed down the canyon until we met up with Dark Canyon Trail which led us directly to Oakwild Campground, just shy of 2 miles total one way. From this route, there were incredibly steep and very shoddy loose dirt switchbacks to get down and up from the 2. Doable, but not for the beginner or the faint of heart. It was hard going down and hard coming back up + a little sketchy depending on your risk aversion levels. If you have your setup ready for backcountry camping this was a cool overnight backcountry trek. Plenty of flowing river water along the way as of 9/27/24.
Only a few flat areas left, some with half-buried tables. The river is nearby and very serene. Still nice area to camp if you have no expectations of facilities.
PERMANENTLY CLOSED - only a few ruins are left. Don't expect to camp here but you might see some deer.
Not a CAMPGROUND but a TRAIL camp(hike in only). Please make sure you mention it on the website to avoid to be deceived!

name: Rio de Los Angeles State Park State Recreation Area
formatted_address: 1900 N San Fernando Rd, Los Angeles, CA 90065, USA

google_reviews.text: My partner fell and injured her ankle today at 10:35 am walking on their walking path. They had a big unmarked hole on the ground. The park also has overgrown grass throughout the property. It is a big and busy park (especially Saturday’s) it should be up-kept better. Also, why is only one side of the gates open…? Last weekend one gate side (exit) was barely opened, I had to get off the car to open it correctly and safely to go in. The only up side is the parking spaces (a lot) and activities (Soccer, Basketball, Tennis, etc.) on it.

The girls’ bathrooms are usually disgusting here. I try not to use them. The parking lot also smells like urine. On hot days, it’s the worst! However, the kids’ classes and coaches here are the best!! They offer tennis, soccer, softball, basketball, ballet, parkour, gymnastics, boxing, fitness, volleyball, track, cross country, golf, AND MORE almost year-round for the low, low price of $10 per 8-week class! I highly recommend this park and all its programs, but use the bathroom at home. 👍🏽

I wasn’t immediately charmed by this park as a good place to walk my dog but I ended up being pleasantly surprised.   Seems like a great park for kids and families; soccer and baseball fields, a large basketball court. Found a trail that goes along the railroad tracks with a nice variety of foliage.  There is a large parking lot and it was quite full. Not my favorite park but it was nice for a change.

This park is amazing.  It's jammed packed with the best standard park attractions.  Kiddie area,  baseball field,  tennis court, and an 8 hoop basketball court and a brand new turfed soccer field. There's plenty of parking.  There are memorials for people who have served our county and first responders. I haven't gone deeper into the park so I don't know if there are tables and grills here

This is a big recreation park that offers a fantastic variety of athletic and leisure facilities to the public. There are a few of big fields for soccer and baseball, multiple basketball courts, a playground and exercise area, a dog park...The park is so big that simply walk around it would be a good relax. A park office with public restrooms located near the main gate.

The one thing I don't like about the park is that it's located in the middle of nowhere. There are almost no local business around and no one really walk over here, you really have to plan a trip.

name: Hoegee Trail Camp

google_reviews.text: Good place to take a rest after you hike up from lower winter creek trail. It’s mostly under the shadow so a good place to stop by. Lots of mosquitoes in the summer though. Winter is the best time as you can see winter creek for real!
This is a nice rest stop along the trail to Mt Wilson, and this also a nice place to camp on the trail. This place is also inside a thicket of oak trees.
this bear visit us every hour , it know how to bite the rope for food.
Great trail! Going counter clockwise on the loop you will feel the steepest incline of the trail, until you reach the campground. Lots of shade.

So I ended up in Hoagees on a day that ended up pouring rain in March, so my experience may be different than yours. The home up is nice.   There is some expectation gain so although you get exercise it is not a tough climb. The campground itself is pretty primitive,  which is fine depending on your expectations. The bathrooms (pit toilets) are rough. They are at least 10 years past needing to be replaced. There is no way,  but when we went the stream was flowing right next to camp. Water is good but obviously needs to be filtered. Peaceful location depending on the number of people. Wood fires were permitted when we were there.  For being this close to LA, it is a nice place to take an entry level backpack without too much trouble. The scenery is very pretty. Do it as a day hike first then come back for an overnight.  I think you'll enjoy.

name: Spruce Grove Trail Camp

google_reviews.text: Beautiful view and hike, moderate to easy . There is an amazing water fall at the end

Another perfect backpacking campground in Los Angeles, fewer sites where you can set up, might get little snug

The campground is pretty good though a bit small. There is a lot of tree cover so it can get cold as there is not much sunshine coming through. If you need to pee just go outside as the facilities smell really bad.  Overall a good place to camp in the spring and summer.

name: Limekiln Canyon Trail (Rinaldi Entrance)

google_reviews.text:
Parking: Free and on the street, lots of spots.

Great when there's a stream. Lots of plants, trees and some birds. Dirt paths, rocks, some steep hills. Picnic benches are scattered around the park. Careful walking off trail near the tunnel--lots of broken booze bottles everywhere.

There are pull up bars and stuff for legs nothing really for kids. No bathrooms either.

Beautiful spot right at the heart of Porter Rancho where you can hike, enjoy nature and take some fresh Oxygen.. The day of our visit was right after the rain storms so we had the additional benefit of the creek running in from of us..
The park is very nice right now. The rain this year has brought plenty of water to the creek. Do watch out for snakes!

As previously posted this place is so beautiful and so lovely. It's another hidden gym in the valley near actually in Porter ranch, california. Worth a visit as it's very inspirational the moment you walk onto the trail and the deeper you go it's just so scenic.

Nice trail...went with the kids under 9 they all enjoyed the stream...the underpass, different types of wild flowers, and the day we went was nice partly cloudy, so the trail didn't seem hard at all....

name: Blufftop Trail

google_reviews.text: beautiful and quiet trail down to the rocks, a bit steep but totally worth it, parking right in front on the side of the road. the whole area is nice to drive around, as it goes along the cliffs next to the ocean.
Beautiful  place  if you like surf, hiking  rhe view  and the sunset  is amazing

Serene, peaceful, beautiful and not a spot to miss to enjoy a amazing sunset! This spot is it! With a clear view of South Bay all the way up to Santa Monica, Malibu, and Downtown Los Angeles this place is it.
Beautiful, clean, picturesque, not very crowded, serene, calm, quite i can go on and on and on....
One of the most scenic trails in LA county. Great for sunsets year round and spectacular during the Spring with the blooming rapeseeds!

name: Stocker Corridor Trail

google_reviews.text: I had a wonderful time. I went with my cousin around 4:14pm this past Monday on the 9th. Everything is beautiful, peaceful, and relaxing and diffently refreshing and the views of Los Angeles and it'd neighborhood views are beautiful and traffic wasn't so loud people were friendly and their dogs as well. Highly recommended for beginners and its very easy make sure to bring snacks, wear comfortable shoes, wear a hat and wear comfortable loose clothing as well.

Stocker Corridor Trail is a dirt trail on the hillside that goes along Stoker St. The elevation change is very modest so it is suitable for anyone. Although it is not very scenic, it is nice to be out in the nature and away from the city traffic.

Stocker Corridor Trail is the start of the Park to Playa trail that goes all the way to the Pacific.

I don't think we did the whole trail because the path we took was along the side of the road and not very long. It is a nice dirt trail if you want to just stretch your legs or have a quick jog. Very easy and fun for kids. Depending on where you park, you get to have an amazing view of the city and the mountains behind.
Its not a long trail, but it does offer a stunning view as well as easy access, making it a safe destination for a casual walk or run.

A nice local trail.
If you need a little outing, take your family
And enjoy nature

name: Mount Wilson Trail Park

google_reviews.text: While the trail to Mt. Wilson is closed, Mt. Wilson Trail Park is open. It’s a small park next to Lizzie’s, and the main benefit if you’re hiking is the bathroom. Besides that, there’s not much here: a very small playground, and a grassy area with a picnic table or two.

It’s great it has shade almost all the way to the Observatory after First Water (FW), this is for the first 2.2 miles (exposed).
Love the shade and green, the creeks 😍, overall has very good maintenance. Hiked my way up, ran the way down.
Does have some very short steep/technical crossings, specially last 2 miles, meaning it’s highly recommended for a good training session.  WBB👌🏽😎
Reason for 4 stars is I prefer green, humid trails with lots of water, not so common here in LA that is more of a dry area/city.

The day of my visit was misty and foggy but the layer burned off by the end of the run and the views were spectacular. It was slightly humid but there was lots of shade and the trail is well maintained. The hike to the top is difficult, it's mostly a gradual climb with some steeper inclines. There are rocky areas and small water crossings.

Tough trail with steady climb all the way. No parking except onthe street. You can go all the way up to Mt Wilson for 14 plus miles or cut it short at some point. Lots of shade along the way. On rainy days it will be cold and humid so layer up. Trail is easy to follow and well marked. The second half past Orchard Camp has a couple of rough spot but doable with care

Beautiful trail, easy with moderate grade. We go about 4 miles up and down. Beautiful canyon below, be careful escape can be difficult and hard even for experienced hikers. Lots of scenery and animals.

name: Bluff Trail Park at One Westbluff
formatted_address: 7925 S Bluff Trail Rd, Los Angeles, CA 90045, USA

google_reviews.text: Short little paved walking path with views of the wetlands and the city. There were flowers and some cacti along the trail. Not really my cup of tea since I like more of a challenge and found the view underwhelming, although I’m sure the overcast weather didn’t help.

Easy mostly flat walking trail

Ample free street parking

Great views of the west side
Nice, safe trail behind a row of houses, but fairly underwhelming views.

Just found this park and love it. Picnic tables, benches, incredible views, nice grass, some shade from the relatively small trees. No rest rooms. Across the street from LMU so it’s incredible easy to get to. There is a side walk along the street which is relatively street. They built a nice curving sidewalk up the far side of the park making the incline easier if you walk thru the park. One trash can along with a dog station with plastic bags will hopefully help keep the park clean.
Short, paved, wildlife, well maintained. Benches placed throughout trail. Several look out points as well. Short enough to complete 2 miles in 30-40 minutes.

name: North Atwater Park Trail
formatted_address: 3914-3970 Chevy Chase Dr, Los Angeles, CA 90039, USA

google_reviews.text: Great bike trail cool random bridges to get from one side of rhe river to the other
Tiny little park with plenty of shade to chill under.
Very short trails in a small nature park.
I mean I love this whole area but this trail, which is separate from the river path that runs by the horse area, this is a self contained nature trail.  Beware of hobos!
Mostly shaded hike up to the Rose Bowl (and beyond)

name: Juanita Cooke Trail
formatted_address: Juanita Cooke Greenbelt Trail, Fullerton, CA 92835, USA

google_reviews.text: Ran the 9-mile loop called "Parks Road, Rosecrans, Castlewood, and Juanita Cooke Loop" on allTrails on a rainy Friday morning in December. The trails weren't too muddy, and the route was easy to follow. Parked at Laguna Lake Park, and spent around 1.5 miles on the Juanita Cooke trail before connecting with Hiltscher trail. This trail was pretty wide and flat. Even during the rain, I never went more than ankle deep in any water. Saw a few dogs being walked too. There were ornament-laden trees on this trail around Christmas time, close to Laguna Lake.

For beginners to intermediate and pro runners.  A great place to train and enjoy easy workouts. Great parking space.  Safe lots of people and busy trails with friendly people.

Update.  Been going to this park / court for the last year. So far, great place for walks,  jogs runs biking ect. Trails can be from 2 - 3 miles and up to 6 to 8 miles depending on trail you choose. Most day's and nights,  you'll find quite a few people parked here, either on the upper parking area or on the lower.  Most park on the lower, due to more safety and increased people traffic.  Pet friendly,  keep on leashes please. Can also be use as a horse trail. During the holidays or rain season usually the area is quiet and empty.  But most nights,  up to 8 or 9, you'll find many on the trail via bike.

Nice walk but don't expect to see much wildlife until you get to the lake. The lake area itself is quite nice with ducks, turtles and some odd looking birds. I parked near a Chase bank as I didn't see a starting parking lot. Note that if you walk to the end of the lake, I would suggest you do walk back the same way you got there (the Juanita Cooke Trail) and not walk back through the Laguna Lake Park trail as after the park you will have to walk quite a bit on a busy street with no sideway.

The Juanita Cooke Trail is a hidden gem for nature enthusiasts seeking a tranquil escape. This well-maintained trail offers a peaceful and scenic journey through lush landscapes, including picturesque meadows and groves of trees. With its moderate difficulty level, it provides a satisfying hike without being overly challenging. The trail’s serenity and natural beauty make it a perfect choice for individuals and families looking to reconnect with nature and enjoy a rejuvenating outdoor experience.

Easy to run/walk or ride your bike. I walk this trail 5 times a week. From the beginning of the trail to Imperial Highway and back is a little under 6 miles. Most people that ride the trail are on Mountain Bikes but I've seen 1 person on a Road Bike and 1 person on a Beach Cruiser.

name: Veteran’s Park Trail
formatted_address: 501 Herondo St, Hermosa Beach, CA 90254, USA

google_reviews.text:
So many cute, lovely doggies and dog owners!

I sat on an open bench at Veterans Park Trail and met so many kind people and their adorable doggies in the hour I was there.

I wish I had a picture of Jack (dog) and Laura (human) or any of the other cute, friendly dogs

The path is very well landscaped and taken care of.

The benches along the park are clean .
There are trash bags and bins to easily pick up after your dog.
Great place for a morning stroll. The mulch on the pathway really cushions your joints, allowing you to walk further than you would  on the concrete sidewalk.

Very nice to walk down and see the houses, but other than that, not much to do. But if you only want to walk and have it low difficulty, it's very nice. Personally, I enjoy to run the trail. It's four miles out and back, and the wood chips make it softer than concrete and dirt, but not as much as grass.
Really lovely path to go for a walk or jog on.  Lots of trees and pretty wide space for being between two roads.
Excellent and well maintained park trail! It's a nice trail with flowers, trees, squirrels.  Will definitely take this trail again!

name: Mt. Latuna Trail, Trailhead
formatted_address: Mount Latuna Trail, Sun Valley, CA 91352, USA

google_reviews.text: I found this trail by searching google and without doing so I don't think I would have found it. I gave the trail a four-star rating because I had to park a block away from the entrance of the trail which was oddly located in the back of a High School. Once I got pass finding the trail I did have a pleasant experience.  I was a bit concerned from an earlier review on seeing coyotes but I didn't encounter any during my hike which was a little over 3 miles.

Trail had a coyote prob about 60 lbs. Beginning not well kept, possibly poison ivy. Couldn’t get more than 10 min cuz of the coyote. Saw it hunting a squirrel which was cool, but I didn’t want to be the one hunted. Switched over to olive terrace which is a good trail only 10 min down the road

The potential was there. It looked liked a nice, challenging little trail. But much like the previous review, we ran into a couple of coyotes about ten minutes into it. Went at about 4:45 pm. The bigger one saw us and started getting curious so we had to turn back :-(

ENTRANCE TO TRAIL CLOSED!!! Was so mad...took an Uber ($15) from my hotel to this trailhead...big sign saying “No Trespassing-$1000 fine” Obviously, had to ask Uber driver to turn around and drive me back to hotel...total bust & waste of time....
No bathrooms and no parking on street next to school. Must park on side street. Trail head is at the end of the road behind highschool.

name: Edison Trails Park
formatted_address: 1600 S Garfield Ave, Monterey Park, CA 91755, USA

google_reviews.text: As a medium hiker, this is not for me…only for beginners. But whats important is you had fun.
Recommend this little park to bring your kids . It’s peaceful next to a hiking trail and friendly.

The restrooms were not clean when I visited, there was flies everywhere, and the lighting is very dim and poor. Other than that, its peaceful, and it has plenty of shades. Tables, grills, and water fountains are available. There is a hiking trail for anyone interested. The park is divided into 2 small parks and is separated by a street. I wouldn't recommend bringing a ball to this park; it's more for barbecues and small gatherings.
Careful with bugs, we got bitten a lot.
My kids wanted to go hiking on Thanksgiving, so I came across this place. It was a perfect distance & on the end of the trail, there is a park where they got to play. My kids loved it & it felt safe.
I had been living around the area for years, this is my first time coming to this park to do a little morning exercise, definitely a hidden gem !

name: Zev Yaroslavsky LA River Greenway Trail
formatted_address: 4250 Coldwater Canyon Ave, Studio City, CA 91604, USA

google_reviews.text: There's no parking! But once I got to it, it's another nice bit of the LA River walking path.
Still needs work, but the parts that're done look great
Great food Great service I love the place
Very clean and refreshing
Great park

name: Nora Kuttner Recreational Trail
formatted_address: Castlewood Dr, Fullerton, CA 92833, USA

google_reviews.text: Ran the 9-mile loop called "Parks Road, Rosecrans, Castlewood, and Juanita Cooke Loop" on allTrails on a rainy Friday morning in December. The trails weren't too muddy, and the route was easy to follow. Spent a bit less than a mile on the Nora Kuttner trail. Very nice and wide trail, easy to run even in the rain. Came on to it from Castlewood to the west, and departed on to Bud Turner trail running alongside Euclid and taking me back to Laguna Lake parking. Good views from this trail. Climbed a bit at first from the trailhead, but then went down a lot till Euclid.
It was great. Hillsy! Great view at the top. A little short, but fun.

There are two entrances,  by parks rd and Castlewood, with a few spots on the side of the road and close to castle trail. By Euclid,  you can park by laguna Lake Park.  The overall length is around 1.5 to 2.5 miles, depending on the route. The area is great for quick hikes for dogs for horses and runners and joggers. Steep inclines. Soon to come is the opening of the other side, where it's being master planned.  That should add around 3 miles extra of trails. There are great little dirt ramps for bikes. Overall,  it's a small park ready for a grandeur future.
For now, it's a small park for niche exercise or quick hikes on steep inclines. Need a larger park... castle wood and laguna park..all next door.
One of our favorite trails to hike. I must say,this inclines are great for begginers. I enjoy this trail,as the bikers have their own path.

Me and my family are avid hikers and we love a good trail. But this park does not offer good trails or views. AS WELL AS NO WHERE TO PARK. We were lucky to park on a culdesack and walk across the convent path to the trail.

You get a good view of people's backyard but not much else. Most of the trail is gravel or dirt and I truly hate how short it is, then you end up walking on the sidewalk and you have to cross a busy intersection before you reach the other side of the trail which is very dangerous as the cross walk pedestrian lights do not work and you literally just have to run across.

There were at least a decent amount of bushes and some trees not just desert. Coming from Texas, southern California trails fall short of fantastic. I will keep searching for other trails until I find one my family enjoys.

name: Hawks Pointe Trail
formatted_address: 4097 Rosecrans Ave, Buena Park, CA 90621, USA

google_reviews.text: Short but demanding.  Via walk or hike, its length is around 2 miles to 2.5 round trip.  But the path is demanding! If you are a walker, it's an excellent course for starters.  For runners , it will make those quads 🔥 burn. Excellent trail for training.  Park on the side of Ralph park and visit also castlewood trail, another great hike slash workout that is around 3 to 4 miles round trip.
Really nice trail I loved it.🥰
Nice park
great plac for walkse
Gravely with white loose stones for about half the trail. About 250 feet climb to the water tower. Nice view of the surrounding area to the ocean on a clear day.

name: Brand Library Hiking Trail
formatted_address: Glendale, CA 91207, USA

google_reviews.text: Was not expecting this level of difficulty going in but had fun. Extremely steep incline. Great views.

Hidden Gem for Hikers and Nature Lovers

The loop itself is around 3.5 to 4 miles depending on the route you take, with moderate elevation gain—not too strenuous, but enough elevation changes and rocky sections to keep it interesting.

It’s suitable for intermediate hikers, and you can easily connect to other trails in the area if you want to extend your hike.

Parking is usually available, though it can fill up on weekends.

I love this hike. I began to do it at least once a week now. There are multiple options to head up to the top. Halfway up, there is a great viewpoint that allows you to see downtown LA, downtown Glendale, and the Burbank airport.
Beautiful views the entire way up.
This is a fairly steep hike on the way up, and a little slippery on the way back down. It will definitely make you stop multiple times on the way up to catch your breath. I love the challenge and the cardio workout it gives you.
Depending on what day of the week you come and at what time, parking can be a little challenging, especially on the weekends as there are often weddings or group picnics, etc.
According to tracking on my app, when I start at the parking lot and go to the furthest point up, it’s an almost 1900ft elevation.

Beautiful, but quite a tricky trail! If you enter where the Seven Trees sign is, expect steep incline and areas of loose gravel/dirt. Despite its relatively short length, make sure you have the right shoes, and plenty of water. The view from the top is gorgeous, so the difficulty will be worth it! There’s an easier alternative route to the top - follow the asphalt path behind the Doctor’s House (the historical building just west of the library) for a longer but much less demanding path which leads to the same summit.

Brand lateral trail loop. It is a short trail, 2.7 miles, but very steep for the 1st 1 mile when you do hiking counter-clock wise. You can park your car in the Brand Park. It’s free. The park is closed at 10pm. There is beautiful city view when you go up there. The trail is closed one hour after sunset. Will visit there for taking night view of LA. It’s a perfect place for it

I've been hiking this trail for years. I started taking my kids when they were 3 & 5. I carried the 3 year old on my shoulders most of the way but you have to be in good shape for that. There's a lot of switchbacks and it gets steep in some areas, but it levels off and you get a bit of a break before the climb starts again. It would take about 2+ hours going up with the kids cause its an elevation gain of about 1000 ft, but the wide open panoramic views are worth it. We also stop for pictures or they stop to investigate bugs or rabbits running around the trail. There are a couple tables at the summit and we would often have snacks before heading back. There are no trash cans up there so be prepared to take your trash back. During winter you should take headlamps since it gets dark early and the phone flashlight doesn't cut it. Its easy to trip or roll an ankle in daylight, so in the dark the chances a exponentially higher. I sometimes would run the trail in and out with no more than 16oz of water. When i took the kids we usually had 2 camelbacks of 2L+ of water. Its a lot hotter out there than by the coast and hiking it on a warm/hot day requires plenty of water and a hat.

Iron Mountain Trail offers spectacular views from the summit. There are ample parking spaces along Highway 67, and you can begin your trek at the trailhead marked by the Iron Mountain sign.

Route & Distance
The trail is approximately 2.9 miles from the entrance to the peak. While the path splits in several sections, all routes eventually converge at the summit. Taking different paths on your way up and down is a great way to see more of the landscape.

Trail Conditions & Features
* Terrain: The trail is well-maintained but features several sections with rocky steps.
* Summit: The top offers panoramic views and features a helipad used for emergency rescues.

Safety & Timing Tips
Because Ramona can get incredibly hot, it is important to start your hike early. For context, I started my hike starting at 6:50 AM can conclude by 9:00 AM in 72-degree weather. Your time may vary depending on speed and how much time you want to spend at the summit.  To stay safe:
* Pack plenty of water.
* Avoid midday hiking to bypass peak temperatures.
