* Check conditions via the City of San Diego Parks & Recreation page or AllTrails before you head out.

Definitely on my top 3 favorite hikes in San Diego! Even though it was a little overcast it was truly beautiful! A bit muddy but definitely worth it. Bring water, a nice hat, and your favorite buddy and hike all the way to the top to experience a 360 view of SD!

The beginning of the trail is pretty, with parallel rows of trees that seem to act as a launch site into the hike. The next mile, though, is a little lackluster. Once you get to the east side of the mountain the trail and view improve noticeably! Miles 2 through 4 are my favorite part. The peak provides a small sitting area to enjoy the view and your accomplishment, though a more grandeous sign or plaque at the peak would help with the celebration of conquering the mountain.

Need to get there early on weekends (before 7 ish) to find parking inside and not in the street. Flexible trail I call it you can male it longer that the usual 6 mi. Great views on clear days, a couple of tables with benches ar the top. Please be courteous and respectful of others and habitat, stay on trail, no loud speakers, hikers going up have right of way.

name: La Jolla Trail
formatted_address: 9883 La Jolla Farms Rd, La Jolla, CA 92037, USA

google_reviews.text: Spent my birthday all day at La Jolla Cove just watching the nesting birds, sea lions, pelicans and descending down into the sea cave. What a great day it was! Even though it was sprinkling it was very hard to find parking.
Cool path, very secluded, don’t go by yourself. Bathrooms are horrible. Definitely a great workout.   End at beach!

A short but incredibly beautiful and extremely dangerous trail. It seemed like the edge of the mountain could collapse at any moment. The trail runs right along the very edge. At the same time, there are stunning views of the ocean, mountains, and city. You can descend to the Ho Chi Minh Trail.

BEAUTIFUL!!!! The trail down is amazing and breathtaking. Wish it was longer but it definitely is not short. Not kid friendly not sandal friendly and if your afraid of heights or get dizzy easy I would not attempt. If your not looking to get dirty then this is not the trail. Very slippery in spots, can fall easy, tight spots to fit through. We only went down and did not come back up which I would of loved it but my friend did not want to 😢. To finish to the bottom you must use the rope to propel backwards over a slippery slope that at times has running water, if you know how slippery wet clay or ice is, this is like that no traction. The beach is beautiful and the cliffs are awesome!!! Definitely recommend it if you like hiking but not if your out looking for an easy walk.

What a view!  What a VIEW! Any one of those houses/mansions overlooking that trail is my dream house. A little bit of hills, little bit of valley, a little bit of beach, and a whole lot of ocean. Sunsets here are simply spectacular!

My 11 year old and I tried to hike down to the beach but it was too late to get all the way down. There are some serious drops and places where you have to "kiss the wall" to keep moving down the trail and after about 30 minutes we had to turn back. Go before 3 on if you want to get to the beach spend some time and return with natural light.

There is also a treck to the top of a hill overlooking the ocean. It seems easier although steep. Most people do it in their flip flops. Be prepared to get mud dusty on either trail.

name: Mother Miguel Mountain Trailhead
formatted_address: 2345 Paseo Los Gatos, Chula Vista, CA 91914, USA

google_reviews.text: Love this beloved trailhead so much. ⛰️ Such a great hike & an awesome workout. View up top is worth the sweat. Keep in mind the areas that are otherwise marked & not the actual trail, so to avoid any potential injury if not paying attention.

I go up this mountain at least 3 times or more a week.

Easy to scale and run back

Wear good trail shoes a lot of loose ground

Bring a liter of water if doing the hike peak sun

Views are beautiful. The first half up seems more beginner friendly. I would suggest hiking boots because of the second half up, there are many small loose rocks. The trail is somewhat clear as to what direction to go as there are many signs dictating what is not part of the trail. You know you are at the top when you see the American flag. Lots of little critters like butterflies and birds flying around. Not that busy of a trail as I went on a Sunday afternoon. There is also a park for families nearby.

Great trail really close to the city. Beautiful views at the top. The only downside is that parking isn’t great. You can park at the local dog park (I think it said no trail access but I went early and it was mostly empty) or on the road. No bathrooms around so plan ahead.

I recently hiked this trail for the first time and decided to try jogging/running to the summit. It turned out to be more challenging than I anticipated, and as a first-timer to this location, I found the various side trails created by others a bit confusing. I had to stop several times to ensure I was staying on the correct path and not accidentally taking a shortcut. However, once I reached the top, the views were absolutely incredible and made the effort completely worthwhile. If you're planning to hike this trail, I encourage you to keep going – the view from the top is truly rewarding!

name: Penasquitos Creek Waterfall
formatted_address: Penasquitos Creek Falls, San Diego, CA 92121, USA

google_reviews.text: A long shady and flat hike. Bring water and snacks. The waterfall is very pretty. In dry months you can cross to the other side of the waterfall and come back in a different path. But January was too high to cross without getting wet.

Wonderful! Wonderful!! It’s such a cute and beautiful trail! We went on early May and the grass is still green which makes the whole valley beautiful! We spotted a bunch of rabbits, beetles, fishes in the creek, and a coyote. It’s so close to the residential area but still makes you feel you’re deep in the nature. Calm and healing! Will definitely come back!

The final destination was very very cool, both in view and water temp. See pics. Im not sure of we tool the wrong way in, but it was definitely the LONG way in. I dipped my feet in the cold water, but there were a couple people floating around and swimming. If yiu have done this hike, you should put it in you hiking bucket list. 🪣 I really enjoyed it.
Lovely hike to a great waterfall. The hike is definitely worth the views! 3 miles in and 3 miles back if you start from the park.
Took the north side trail with very little traffic , but quite a few bicycles most of which did not have bells to let you know they were on their way.

name: Cowles Mountain Trailhead
formatted_address: 7001 Golfcrest Dr, San Diego, CA 92119, USA

google_reviews.text: I was thinking this is probably the busiest trail in San Diego behind Torrey Pines.  The trail has a lot of loose rocks and uneven terrain. I recommend hiking boots even though it's so short.  Lots of hikers, lots of runners, lots of people listening to music loudly.  I ran across 2 people playing loud music and playing a bongo drum! It's a good hike and you can get it done in an hour if you hustle.  You just have to be okay with a lot of shenanigans! 😄🤪

Cowles Mountain Trail is one of the most popular hiking spot in San Diego, a fact evident by its frequent crowds and being featured on the news when there is a heat advisory.  The main trailhead is at Golfcrest Drive/Navajo Road, and while busy, I personally prefer it over the alternative paths from Barker Way.

The Barker Way entrance offers two less-trafficked routes. One, heading west (left), is a single trail with steps and a pleasant nature scene. The other is a wide service road heading northeast, which is a constant, less scenic uphill incline that I found less enjoyable. The service road descent can also be slippery due to loose gravel, so caution is advised.

Regardless of which of these trailheads you use, the round trip is about 2.8 to 3 miles.
For those seeking a greater challenge, a continuation to Pyles Peak more than doubles the distance and adds more elevation challenges. The path is less well-known and begins about halfway down the descent from the Cowles Mountain summit toward the communication tower, turning left onto an unmarked trail. This addition adds another 2.8 miles round trip. (For more details, see my review of the Pyles Peak Trail).

Enjoy your hike and stay safe!

Medium challenge hike and great view on top! There is no shade at the top so bring a hat and some sunscreen 😊 Also, wear a good pair of hiking shoes or shoes that have good grip for the slightly steeper, rockier parts 👍

This is a great trail but on the difficult side in several places on the way up. Some big stair drops, some rock stair scrambles and switchbacks. It is challenging but not ridiculously so. I continued on to Pyle's peak which is much less crowded trail. Constant flow of people on the main trail but only saw one person every 15 minutes or so on the other trail. With my bad knees coming down was difficult on some of the bigger steps. A lot of people were just bounding right by. It's all about age and skill level.
I went on a Monday and the parking lot was full. There's plenty of street parking. There's also a nice little cafe across the street and vending machines and bathrooms at the bottom. I can imagine weekends are absolutely crazy here.
My best tip is pick the clearest day you can because you can see for miles and miles and miles and miles oh yeah
Great views at the top. 1.5 miles, gets steeper as you go, but it is totally manageable. Parking can be tricky, but always works out in the end.

name: Sweetwater River Trail
formatted_address: 12101-, 12153 Campo Rd, El Cajon, CA 92019, USA

google_reviews.text:
Loved it! Saw a wild Coyote up close. Lots of good options for the mountain bike, beautiful views all around, cool plants and interpretive signs… Great trails & wild lands free & beautiful like they should be!  My dog enjoyed it as well.

Beware of car break-ins, I saw some broken glass on the ground, and warning signs in the parking lot… Let's try to keep this nice place safe!

Great set of trails here. I do have a question about trails etiquette:
Do horse riders have any obligation to pick up the several pounds of manure their horses leave on trail, or eventually Fix the potholes their horses make in the trail when they are ridden immediately after and possibly during some serious rains? I hope so. It kind of ruins the trail and lessens the experience for the rest of us.

This is a great spot for hiking, running, and walking, but biking is definitely my favorite! There are long, empty stretches of dirt paths that are perfect for riding. It’s mostly flat and open, which makes for a smooth and relaxing bike ride. Just a heads-up: there’s not much shade, so bring water and maybe a hat or sunscreen if you’re out during the hotter parts of the day.

I had such a great time hiking this trail. I feel like there is something here for almost everyone— you can walk the flat path through the valley and over the sweetwater bridge, or be a little more adventurous and walk the loop up the mountain. I ended up walking 7.5 miles just taking in the rugged scenery and quiet air. I got a tan too :p very satisfied.
Really cool spot to connect with nature, bring a hat though because only a small section has shade.

name: Sunset Seat
formatted_address: 130-210 S Camino Del Mar, Del Mar, CA 92014, USA

google_reviews.text: Enjoy an unobstructed high view of the sunset. Parking was $7 on the street for just over 2 hours. Bring a small blanket if you want to sit a while. There is a bench a bit back from the edge. Just be careful at the edge like you would on any other cliff and you're golden!
Great place to watch the waves and see the setting sun 🌅

The view was good for the sunset.  The statue is quite large as well.  The temperature drops quite rapidly as the sun goes down, but there were still quite a few people and even some dogs.  Looking south you can see the cliffs of Torrey Pines Reserve.

The Sunset Seat is a great place along the Camino del Mar at the pacific coast of California. This day in End of September was slightly rainy, foggy and misty, but even then a lot of people enjoyed the beautiful beaches for walking, jogging or surfing. You can reach this place in less than 30min from San Diego downtown.
Fantastic view of the ocean, and not busy at all. This place is great for photos and there are some nice little trails that run through the area.

name: Sunset Cliffs Coastal Trail (Linear Park)
formatted_address: San Diego, CA 92107, USA

google_reviews.text:
Visited Sunset Cliffs in San Diego and it was absolutely stunning. The views of the Pacific Ocean from the cliffs are breathtaking, especially during sunset when the sky lights up with vibrant colors. Walking along the trails, you can hear the waves crashing against the rocks below and feel the refreshing ocean breeze.

The area is peaceful and perfect for taking photos, relaxing, or just enjoying nature. The cliffs stretch for miles, offering different viewpoints and hidden spots to explore. It’s a wonderful place to connect with the beauty of the coastline and watch the sun dip below the horizon.

Overall, Sunset Cliffs is a must-visit in San Diego and one of the most beautiful coastal spots I’ve ever seen.

amazing spot for a beautiful walk.
although the name implies you should probably try to catch the sunset there, it’s beautiful during the daylight as well! not too busy either which makes it special.
Didn't know anything about it till my son told me it was a wonderful surprise he had set up for my husband and I absolutely beautiful.

Very beautiful coastal cliffs! We parked closer to the Sunset Cliffs Staircase and walked up and down to coast about 1.5 miles each way. Very scenic walk with views of the cliffs, ocean, flowers and beautiful coastal homes. Highly recommend. Not really a trail or hike, there is a sidewalk on the other side of the road so anyone can enjoy this. Definitely worth spending a couple hours here for some good free fun while in the area.

Absolutely stunning views of the ocean with some great jagged rock areas to sit and relax while watching the waves crashing or the sun going down. It’s a short walk from ocean beach and is very accessible. Definitely a place to be careful as it can be steep and quite a jarring drop off. Overall it’s a great place to go near sunset. You’ll never regret going here!

name: Kwaay Paay Peak Trailhead
formatted_address: Father Junipero Serra Trail, San Diego, CA 92119, USA

google_reviews.text: Short but a grind uphill. Love it for a quick solid hike. Views are epic.

This was my second time hiking Kwaay Paay. Loved it of course. We arrived around 6:30am and left around 8:30-9am . When we arrived it was perfect, sun was barely peaking out. I recommend arriving as early as possible to beat the heat. Definitely don't want to get caught hiking this trail in the sun. Take plenty of water, wear a cap that protects your face and ears from the sun. I recommend hiking boots. Wearing regular walking shoes will put you at risk of twisting an ankle. There are lots of rocks on this trail and it is uneven. I will say that towards the last stretch, you will be hiking up a steep set of stairs. Push yourself and keep going.

Love this trail!
This is a very short and difficult hike but it has beautiful, amazing views! It's a very challenging trail and I believe van do this! Dont think because is just 1 mile is a piece of cake but don think to give up either 😉My first time I said 1 mile? Cool! But after half mile I was thinking to go back🙈but patiently I did it and damn the view was spectacular! Very steep and some stairs close to the top since that day not idea how many times I already hike this amazing trail❤Just in case Kwaay Paay trail is located by the dam in Mission Trails

If you like hikes with a goal, this is a good option. Unlike Cowles mountain this trail is greener and offer occasional shade. There are some steps to climb, but other than that it's your regular unpaved hiking path.  On the way back you may want to slow down a bit if you want to take easy on your knees. View from top is hazy, but there are some macro photo opportunities. There was no gate where we parked, so the time was not a constraint.
I loved this trail, it was pretty long so we didn’t get to finish it :/ but it was still a wonderful time!!!

name: Los Penasquitos Canyon County Preserve
formatted_address: 12020 Black Mountain Rd, San Diego, CA 92129, USA

google_reviews.text: It is the first time we walk this trail. We got there around 8:30 and we walked the full length of the canyon all the way to Sorrento Valley Boulevard. The walk was nice, easy very few slopes. The weather  started cool then warmed just right for the activity.  The views of the creek, the water, the trees, and shady areas made the whole trip enjoyable.

Very nice place for a relatively easy hike, generally level with some minor ups and downs. Lots of mountain bikers but they were all polite and considerate. Would not recommend on a hot sunny day as there is not much cover. No facilities except a port-a-potty at our parking spot. Lovely waterfall area and lots of spring flowers. Plan on 2-3 hours of hiking.

Almost 7miles round trip, $5 parking cash or pay through website or app. There are portalet bathroom, water faucet, soda vending machine and some picnic tables on the entrance. There are 5 different crossings and maybe you can start from other locations from there for a shorter hike. Lots of trees for shades, there are benches to rest. Mostly flat but some have rocks and sands on the path. Lots of signs to help you know which way to go towards the waterfalls. Went on a Sunday and not too crowded. Good for kids, families, dogs on leash. We had fun!

Perfect short day hike - could be done in ~1.5 hours if you just go to the waterfall and back. Awesome hike on a hot day with plenty of pockets of shade along the path. Good amount of parking and the QR code makes it really easy to pay ($5 for day pass).

The hike is quite moderate, spanning approximately 6.5 miles for the round trip, taking around 3-3.5 hours. The endpoint waterfall is decent, not extraordinary. It's more of a workout than a destination for stunning views. Note that parking costs $3, so bring cash as they don't accept digital payments. Ensure you have change since you place the cash in an envelope, and without change, you might overpay. Dogs are allowed, and our dog enjoyed the hike.

name: Barker Way Trailhead
formatted_address: Barker Way, San Diego, CA 92119, USA

google_reviews.text: I had a wonderful time! Great workout. Make sure you wear the right shoes like trail running or hiking boots. The steep dirt trail is slippery. You need traction under your feet. If you need balance bring trekking poles for stability. Have fun on this trail!
Always come here. there are high steeps really works out both of your anabolic and aerobolic needs. Peak view is amazing. You can see the ocean on a clear day and lake Murray.
Great trail up the mountain. We'll maintained.

My favorite of all the Cowles Mountain trails because you can make it a quicker trip to the top if you want. Still challenging going up the hill if you try to keep a fast pace, but less time spent plodding your way along like the Big Rock Trail. Also less crowded than the main "front" trail. And watch out for snakes!! Second time I've seen a rattler here... they don't bother you though, just give them some space and they'll be on their way.

This trail is a trainers hike.  A couple of hard inclines but great cardio.
Elevation gained 927FT

name: Rose Canyon Open Space Park
formatted_address: Regents Rd, San Diego, CA 92122, USA

google_reviews.text: If you like getting lost with your friends that luv being physically active, this park is the place for them.  Super Dope! Must Visit!

I spent a few hours walking through Rose Canyon Open Space Park.  I hiked on the Rose Canyon Trail which is the primary trail running from one end of the park to the other.  The park may be entered from Genesee Avenue, Regents Road, or Bloch Street.  I’ve always used the Bloch Street entrance which seems to be the “back door” entrance - it doesn’t have a fancy park sign like the one you’ll see on Genesee Avenue.  Bloch Street is in a quiet neighborhood with plenty of street parking.  The Rose Canyon Trail from Bloch Street to Genesee Avenue is 2.5 miles.  Most of the trail is a dirt or dirt/rock road – wide, flat, and easy to navigate.  A bit of easy climbing on a path is required near the Bloch Street end of the park.  At the Bloch Street end of the park, you’ll get great views of Interstate 5 and the train and trolley tracks, and the noise that goes with them.  The park has a creek running through it and at times you’ll walk near it or cross over it.  A major native vegetation restoration project in taking place in the park.  The trail is clean.  The creek, unfortunately, had a lot of trash in it.  The park had very few other visitors when I hiked on a Friday morning.  The park does not have a visitor center, porta-potties, or other facilities.  Pack it in, pack it out.

Beautiful hike with varying elevations, lush veggitation, high and low canyon points, flowing creek; trails are pretty clean and maintained.
Saw walkers, runners, cyclists, and a good amount of people with their dogs, a couple squirrels, a few frogs on the hillside walking parallel to the I-5, but not too much wild life otherwise.
Just have to be aware of spots with loose dirt, gravel, and sand when walking along to avoid slips; at the end of the trail I lost my footing and sprained my ankle, while waiting for my hubby to get the car -I saw 2 other people hobbling injured out of the trail too.

Parking on dirt road for about 15 cars. At the start you descent ona semi-steep path. After about 5 minutes you will reach to an intersection. You can turn right to Genesee or left. We took left, and walked on a wide path good for running, biking, or hiking. It gets rocky with pebbles towards the end. Spring time feels great with hills covered in yellow flowers and rare poppies here and there. Some road noise but not enough to bother us. Great hiking trail overall.

The only place I have been in San Diego that wasnt crawling with people.  I almost don't want to post a review so less people are likely to go there and ruin its scene.  Dogs are welcome but on a leash.  There is mountain bikers to keep aware.  There isnt a lot of parking.  Great if you are looking for a breath of fresh air.

name: Mission Trails Regional Park
formatted_address: 1 Father Junipero Serra Trail, San Diego, CA 92119, USA

google_reviews.text:
Mission Trails is one of San Diego’s hidden gems. It’s the perfect spot to disconnect without driving too far from the city. The trails are well-marked, with options ranging from easy walks to more challenging hikes that give you a great workout. The views from Cowles Mountain or North Fortuna are stunning, especially at sunset.

I love that you can choose different vibes here—hills, canyons, even the San Diego River running through some trails. It feels safe, there are always people hiking, running, or biking, but it never feels overcrowded.

Bring water, sunscreen, and a hat since some areas don’t have shade, but overall, this place is absolutely worth it. A must if you want nature, exercise, and amazing views right in the city.

The Visitor's Center is beautiful and the staff were welcoming as well as very helpful.

Stunning park in San Diego!

Thank you for assisting Visitor's from Colorado with a smile and warmth!

Lovely walk out to the Old Mission Dam. There is currently some significant drainage work being performed along the road from the Visitors Center to the dam.  Still walkable easily.   A bit chilly in the morning with the breeze blowing through the canyon…but warmed up nicely by 11.  Easy “hike” allows for lots of conversation.    Can take some side trails that will provide a significant elevation gain and really nice views.  Sound have a moderate fitness level if you plan on doing those.

Mission Trails is an 8,000-acre regional park in San Diego, California, with over 60 miles of trails and two lakes. As one of the largest urban parks in the United States. The park features rugged hills, river valleys, and historical sites, offering a range of recreational and educational opportunities.

My toddler boys have been on so many different hikes but this remains at the top of their list! We love the visitor center which has an interactive museum attached to it where you can learn about the Kumeyaay people and the rich natural history of the area. We love all of the different trails which are kid friendly with mostly flat terrain and some climbing opportunities and even a creek to cool off in. The trails are also dog friendly, with a water station and a dog parking post in front of the visitors center. But be careful on hot days with both furry friends and kids who can get easily over heated. There is some poison oak on the trails so educate yourself on what that is and make sure your kids are careful too. There is an overflow lot if you can’t find parking here on the weekends or when they are hosting a free event

name: Tecolote Canyon Natural Park and Nature Center
formatted_address: 5180 Tecolote Rd, San Diego, CA 92110, USA

google_reviews.text: The staff here are absolutely wonderful. There are a lot of nice,  informational exhibits that are well put together and interesting. They also have a pleasant native plants garden out back.

It's a beautiful park with a playground baseball diamonds/fields it has a bathroom and plenty of trails and picnic areas and a whole lot of nature's friends from hawks and owls to squirrels and rabbits but don't forget to watch out for snakes especially rattle snakes and it is the season for rattle snakes be aware.
A really cool canyon to have tucked in to such an urban area. A decent flow of water leads to a big diversity of species that can be seen throughout the canyon.

Love this hidden jewel lots of room to run let friendly . Nice and well structured also open hours have games inside rec. Trail is being renovated but it's an awesome trail and it's fun to bike run walk ...

With a primary trail stretching from the Tecolote Nature Center in the south to the North Clairemont Recreation Center in the north – and several side trails linked to neighborhoods – Tecolote Canyon Natural Park is an enjoyable place to go take a hike.  I parked at the Nature Center, where the Tecolote Canyon Trail - South starts.  (App “Hiking Project” divides the TCT into North and South segments.) The City’s map and signs in the park itself don’t name the trail – they just call it “Trail” with arrows telling you which way to go.  There are, however, signs that tell you where the branch trails will take you.  My hike, took me north, then east to Genesee Ave.  The first mile or so of the trail is wide and used by official vehicles.  It rained the night before my hike so that part of the trail had big puddles, mud, and puddles of mud (but no Puddle of Mudd).  The rest of the trail brought me under streets, over streams, down narrow paths, and up steep hills.  I reached the east end of the trail (Genesee Ave.) and spent some time there viewing works of art (graffiti) in the 2 tunnels crossing under the street.  The trails are more interesting from past the golf course (going north) to Genesee because it’s mostly spooky cool trees and streams with plenty of shaded areas.  I had to cross the streams that run through the park several times in each direction.  The hike is about 7 miles round-trip.  I’m saving the TCT - North for another day.  The Nature Center is small but worth a visit.  No fee to enter.  Restrooms available.  NOTE: The correct name for this park is Tecolote Canyon Natural Park, NOT Tecolote Canyon Natural Open Space Park as it appears on Google Maps.

name: San Dieguito River Park
formatted_address: Coast to Crest Trail, Del Mar, CA 92014, USA

google_reviews.text: We had lunch on Sunday at Viewpoint Brewery with our dog and walked along this park.  The entrance is close to the brewery.  We walked around 3 miles.  It is an easy trail with beautiful scenery, quiet, and clean.
Beautiful walk along a very important wetland in San Diego. There were not many people when I went to walk there after work around 5p.  Very serene. I’m pretty sure I found a whale jaw bone.

San Dieguito River Park (Coastal) is a great place to get close-up views of the San Dieguito Lagoon.  There are three trails in the area.  The River Path Del Mar starts/ends at the Grand Avenue Overlook and runs along San Dieguito Drive, crossing Jimmy Durante Boulevard and continuing along the river towards the coast.  At one point there are railroad tracks that must be crossed to reach the coast.  There are no signs encouraging nor discouraging the crossing of the tracks.  Another trail is the short, planked Boardwalk that leads into the lagoon.  The third trail, and the major attraction, is the Coast to Crest Trail.  Google Maps calls the two previously mentioned trails “Coast to Crest” but signs on the ground indicate otherwise.  Enter the trail from Jimmy Durante Boulevard.  The trail is dirt/gravel, flat, and wide.  It is clean and well-maintained.  It’s an enjoyable walk/hike with plenty to observe.  Flowers were in full bloom on the day I was there.  The only negative comment I can make is about the noise pollution from the surrounding roadways – especially when approaching and walking under Interstate 5.  The park doesn’t have any facilities.  The Grand Avenue Lookout has 5 free parking spots (1 of which is handicap parking).  A free public parking lot is at the corner San Dieguito Drive and Jimmy Durante Boulevard and is a short walk to all three trails.

While there is much to see in the park, the endless droning of cars racing along Interstate 5 tends to disrupt the otherwise peaceful setting. The paths are minimal but easy and even, with no hills or even the slightest difficulty. This makes it attractive to a lot of walkers--a significant amount with dogs. Along the path, the wetlands are teeming with birds and it is ready to spot egrets, sand herons, whimbrels, ducks, ospreys, and the occasional blue heron. While you never feel too far from the busy streets, the park is a nice easy break with enough to look at to make it worthwhile.

Trail Series, Part 3
The “Coast to Crest Trail” is located in Del Mar, CA and can be accessed in several places including at San Dieguito Park on San Andres Drive, just South of Via De La Valle.  There is ample parking on the street and there is a large shopping center across the street so you won’t feel like you’re leaving your car in an isolated location.  The dirt trail is entirely flat and welcomes walkers, runners, cyclists, and equestrians. There is a limited portion that is negatively impacted by noise from the 5 Freeway, but that section doesn’t last long. You should be aware that there is a tunnel section that takes you beneath the freeway.  It is short, felt safe, and shouldn’t be an issue for you, but we wanted to make sure that you were aware.  You’ll be only about a mile from the Pacific Ocean, so you’ll likely be greeted by some refreshing and salty breezes.  Although our out and back journey traveled West and was a tad over 3 miles, it represents only a portion of the trail (in case you are interested in a more extended adventure). And that’s…Henry’s Take. @henrystake

name: El Capitan County Preserve
formatted_address: 13775 Blue Sky Rnch Rd, Lakeside, CA 92040, USA

google_reviews.text:
The trailhead for the Elcajon Mountain hike. No joke, when everyone says this is one of the most challenging hikes in San Diego, they are right. Super steep in many areas, ascending and descending in both directions, will threaten to zap all your energy on the way back. 11 miles round trip, 4000 feet of total elevation gain. Started hike at 9:30, returned around 2:30.

Do not attempt in temps above 75 degrees F.

Need 2 gallons of water for the hike, and you won't need to pee after drinking all that because you end up absorbing it all!
Nice place to hike really difficult be prepared lots of water and snacks

All the footprints in the dirt say the trail at El Capitan County Preserve is popular.  Fortunately for me, a trip to the preserve on a weekday morning was the right time to hike without the crowd – I encountered only five other people during my 4-hour hike.  I hiked the El Capitan Trail, the preserve’s 6-mile out and back trail to the halfway point before turning around.  The trail starts one-half mile from the parking lot and is a dirt path that eventually turns into a wide dirt, gravel, and rock trail.  The trail is up and down and up and down again – a change from climbing to the top and then back down after getting there.  The hike was challenging and reaching the 3-mile mark was a good enough workout for me.  (I was born when Ike was president).  Trail signs are good, and a map is not necessary.  There were very few areas where trees and shrubs blocked the sun and if you hike early in the day the hills and mountains will provide more shade.  The trail is clean although I did find two unopened plastic water bottles behind a rock – someone’s emergency stash?  The parking lot/staging area is a gravel lot with enough space for cars, SUVs, trucks with horse trailers, etc.  The staging area is a half mile from the county preserve and trailhead.  The trailhead area has a picnic table and restrooms.  The preserve does not charge a fee.
Nice staging area for the El Cajon mtn hike. Bathrooms and s picnic table are a nice amenity. Just wish the parking lot wasn't so far away, you have to walk about .5 mile to get there.
Definitely not a beginner's hike if you want to complete it. But you can even go to Vista Way and turn around and it's still a nice workout

name: Mount San Miguel Park
formatted_address: 2335 Paseo Veracruz, Chula Vista, CA 91914, USA

google_reviews.text:
Mt. San Miguel Park offers an exceptional experience for outdoor enthusiasts and families alike, making it a cherished destination in the community. Nestled in a picturesque setting, the park boasts stunning panoramic views that captivate visitors from the moment they arrive. The well-maintained hiking trails wind through diverse landscapes, providing opportunities for both leisurely strolls and more challenging hikes. This variety ensures that individuals of all skill levels can immerse themselves in the beauty of nature, whether they are seasoned hikers or families with young children eager to explore.

In addition to its scenic trails, Mt. San Miguel Park features ample space for a wide range of recreational activities. The park is equipped with baseball fields that invite friendly matches and spirited games, making it an ideal spot for family gatherings or community events. The open green spaces are perfect for picnics, allowing families to spread out and enjoy meals together while soaking in the fresh air. During our church family day, we took full advantage of the park's amenities, relishing the clean gazebos that provided shade and comfort. The welcoming atmosphere fostered a sense of community, enhancing our experience and allowing us to connect with one another in a beautiful setting.

Whether you're hiking along the trails, walking your dog, or simply relaxing on a bench while enjoying the sights and sounds of nature, Mt. San Miguel Park is a fantastic place to create lasting memories with loved ones. The combination of natural beauty, recreational facilities, and a friendly environment makes it a go-to destination for anyone looking to escape the hustle and bustle of daily life. As the sun sets over the horizon, casting a warm glow across the landscape, visitors are reminded of the simple joys that outdoor spaces like Mt. San Miguel can bring, encouraging them to return time and again to experience its charm.

Awesome place to meet new baseball/softball enthusiasts. Love the snack bar, they have nachos, hot dogs, regular fries, cheese fries, gatorades, sodas, waters... Etc. Restrooms are pretty clean here actually. They have picnic areas on the back.

Loved it there! Absolutely loved how they reworked the tennis courts to blue and white. Everything there was so clean! I definitely recommend you to go there to play tennis, eat, or anything you would like!!!

Nice view at the end, short hike, free parking in the park outside

Its a mountain so it has some good elevation gain

Like taking this hike just make sure its on one of those off days. Parking was full on the street. Went on a Sunday morning, so did everyone else. Came down the back way much less crowded and adds about 1 1/2 to 2 miles more.

name: Carmel Mountain Preserve
formatted_address: 11600 Clews Ranch Rd, San Diego, CA 92130, USA

google_reviews.text:
Enjoyed a scenic ~3.2-mile round trip shaded walk starting from El Camino Real until the Carmel Mountain Preserve parking area. Lots of trees along a compact dirt hiking trail. For the majority of the walk, there is a parallel bike path, which makes it safer for the hikers / joggers and bikers.

Since this is also an equestrian path, walkers / joggers need to be dodge periodic horse manure piles along the trail.
nice easy hike, make sure you go to the trail head itself, maps tried having me walk through peoples lawns to get here as it was the most direct route lol

Carmel Mountain Preserve, called Carmel Mountain Open Space on some maps, is “Land Set Aside for Habitat Protection.”  Pros: Several access points.  Fairly well-maintained, mostly wide, flat, trails (due primarily, I think, to the power lines that run through the preserve).  Good for hikers, joggers, equestrians, cyclists.  A trail that connects you with Los Peñasquitos Canyon County Preserve.  Different types of habitat areas (the vernal pools had frog eggs in them when I visited).  Cons: No signage with trail names, distances, directions, destinations, etc.  (When you come to a fork in the trail, use Google Maps or the Hiking Project app).  Signage and fences are falling apart, sunbaked, rusted, or neglected in some other way.  No sign in the Carmel Mountain Preserve parking lot on Clews Ranch Road telling you how to access Carmel Mountain Preserve.  Recommendation: Access Carmel Mountain Preserve from Ocean Air Community Park and Recreation Center.  There you will find a huge, paved parking lot, rest rooms, picnic tables, trash receptacles, and a place to plug in your battery-powered car.
Amazing hiking place! So much to do and so many trails. Connects to Los Penasquitos Canyon as well

My dog I and I hike here.  I have a custom loop that yields just under 15 miles.  From the parking area head up the hill (not on the bike path) and you are heading due East and climbing for a couple of miles.  Eventually you swing SSE and finally S to drop down into Penasquitos Cyn and then it is due West to the end of the Cyn (stay on N side of creek) and use Sorrento Valley Blvd to cross under the freeway and then N up Sorrento Valley Rd, eventually picking up the old road that went up to Carmel Valley Rd.  As you continue North, the road will come quite close to the 5 Fwy and right here you can cross under the Fwy and come out the other side to get on the 56 Bike Path all the way back to the CM Preserve.

name: Marian Bear Memorial Park
formatted_address: 5491 Genesee Ave, San Diego, CA 92117, USA

google_reviews.text: This is such a treat. Truly a gem. We're blessed to have this area in San Diego and I'm so grateful to those who have preserved this slice of nature among the busy, congested city. Lots of wildlife- bunnies, lizards big and small, rattlesnakes, squirrels, butterflies, dragonflies, birds, and even fish and crawdads in the river...... this place is my escape.

Shared trail with easy hikes & moderate skilled bike trails.  Love the sand, hard pack, heavy rock & gravel and water sections.  A pleasant morning, afternoon or evenings.
Parking & restrooms available

With a primary trail stretching from one end to the other – and several side trails linked to neighborhoods - Marian Bear Memorial Park is an interesting place to go take a hike.  I parked on Bloch Street, north of State Route 52, where the Rose Canyon Trail starts.  Walking down that trail a few hundred yards brings you to a paved trail connecting to San Clemente Canyon.  Some maps call the trail that runs through the canyon the San Clemente Canyon Trail (North and South), but the City’s map and signs in the park itself don’t name the trail – you’ll have to follow signs that say “Regents” or “Genesee” which is where parking, restrooms, and picnic tables are available.  My hike, going west to east, brought me through these areas, and under streets, over streams, down narrow paths, and up steep hills.  I reached the east end of the trail (Limerick Ave.), got a good look at I-805, and headed west again.  The trails are interesting because they’re wide and narrow, flat and steep, in the open but with plenty of shaded areas.  I had to cross the stream that runs through the park about 8 times in each direction.  Those crossings took place not by bridge, but by stepping on rocks about the size of your shoe.  The water isn’t deep, but you may get wet feet or worse, if you slip.

Cute and simple little hike that is dog and family friendly. The trail begins with a view of a little stream amongst an overhang of trees. There are tons of plants and shade around. Some of the trail is very thin with not much walking space, but that is towards the end of the hike, coming back towards the parking lot. There are a couple cute wooden bridges to cross and there is also a fork in the road where you can venture off to rose canyon. We chose to continue along boundary trailhead where you can walk alongside the coaster tracks and check out all the graffiti (artwork) on the walls. There were 2 stream crossings where our feet got a little wet, but there are rocks set up to help cross easier. You get to walk along the hillside on the way back and that is where the trail really thins out. We did see many bike riders along the trail and some were zooming down pretty fast, so pay attention to your surroundings.
Amazing spot for a quick walk, and it's great for kids. We recently went here with our 11 month old and 3 year old and absolutely loved it, will definitely go here several times :)

name: Murray Reservoir
formatted_address: 5540 Kiowa Dr, La Mesa, CA 91942, USA

google_reviews.text: Lake Murray is such a lovely spot to unwind. Great for walking, jogging, or just sitting by the water and enjoying the view. You can’t make a full loop around the lake because part of it runs into private property, but there’s still plenty of space to explore. You’ll see ducks, geese, and even turtles — fun to feed them if you bring some snacks! Perfect place to clear your head, watch the sunset, or enjoy a peaceful morning walk.
A good long scenic walk could change your life. Simply Awesome! Must Visit!
Gets packed on weekends but overall it's a pretty nice place to go exercise or to just sit down and relax.

This is a nice park with baseball fields, grass to sit and relax on, benches to sit on near the lake, boats you can rent, and a paved walking trail around the lake here. It's a beautiful place. There are wheelchair accessible portable toilets here (porta-potties), but spaced out far around the lake. It feels like a pretty safe area during the daytime as there are people walking, fishing, reading, playing baseball, but still best to always watch your surroundings. Bring a sunhat, sunblock, and sunglasses because there isn't a huge amount of shade on the paved walking path, and not every bench has shade above it. I saw lots of lizards, birds, and butterflies here. Definitely a place I'd visit again!

We visited Lake Murray around sunset and had a lovely time walking along the lake and feeding the ducks. The view during golden hour was beautiful, and the peaceful atmosphere made for a relaxing experience.

That said, the walking paths could use a bit more maintenance as some areas were uneven and not clearly marked, which took away slightly from the overall experience.

Still, it was a great outing, and we’re already planning to come back for a picnic. It’s a charming spot with lots of potential.

name: Big Rock Road Trail
formatted_address: 8301 Big Rock Rd, Santee, CA 92071, USA

google_reviews.text: Our favorite local hike. The service road has some decent incline but the main trail is all age friendly as you will see once you get out there...Enjoy!  PSA...please clean up after yourselves and your pets. Thank you

This is a really great trail. You get a good amount of exercise but it's not overwhelming. I find that there is a good breeze, and a fair amount of shade. Parking doesn't really seem to be a problem either.

Definitely worth the effort. You can make it up in less than an hour and your efforts will be rewarded with great views all around. Two great photo signs show you what you are looking at in both directions. This is a much quieter route up than the one most people take. The service road gets very steep near the end so step carefully on your return trip.

You can think of this as one of the "back ways" up Cowles Mountain. You'll encounter a fraction of people on the trail that you'll pass on the main trail (from Golfcrest).  The first half of the hike is a somewhat narrow trail with dense foliage in most places; halfway up, it connects with the fire road. While the fire road itself isn't much to look at, it's here that you'll probably start stopping to take photos of the expansive vistas over east San Diego.  The last portion of the fire road becomes rather steep and is the most challenging portion of the hike (watch your footing on the way back down; it's easy to start sliding if you go too fast).  The fire road ends in a small saddle between the main peak and the communications tower area; from there you can scramble up a short set of stairs to the top and touch the mountain's plaque.
It's a nice trail a little on the difficult side. More so on a hot day bring lots of water. It is like others have posted a less crowded back way up cowles mountain.

name: Flinn Springs County Park
formatted_address: 14787 Olde Hwy 80, El Cajon, CA 92021, USA

google_reviews.text: This is an excellent place for a birthday party. The picnic tables are spacious, there is a dedicated spot to hang a pinata and spacious “buffet table” set up to place all the food for the guests to serve themselves. Overall, a well designed, functional space. The best part of the park is the little stream where my kids loooove to play!! Remember to bring a change of clothes!!

Attended a wedding under the Wedding Tree over the weekend with a reception afterwards. Plenty of shade available. I could have done with more marked pathways to the parking lot, and a smoother trail with packed dirt. I use a cane for walking and it was loose dirt, tough footing from the wedding to the reception area. But it is very beautiful.
A  nice park , with many spots to e joy the day. There is a gazebo to rent for weddings, there is a steam running through the park giving the outdoors, natural walk vibe

I so love this park ,away from the city and surrounded by nature..very beautiful..brings back so many memories w the family on Easter days for years,its big and plenty of tables or picnic areas,bathrooms are clean. You can see the water stream weeks after rain,during the week its less crowded,so relaxing!!

Hidden gem on the old highway 80 that I learned from my job. Wonderful park for people and dogs to walk through with all the nice shading provided by tons of trees, with lots of parking and seating. A spring runs the length of the park other than where you can hike. The park is a mix of grass and dirt areas, has plenty of playgrounds for little kids, has plenty of bathrooms, and lots of parking. The seating areas are so much that you could still get away from the sounds of other people and still be in shade.  They also have a wedding gazebo for rent and a wedding tree (with a huge canopy to enjoy). Pay $3 to park/help sustain the park and relax in this gem.

name: La Jolla Heights Natural Park
formatted_address: 7333 Encelia Dr, La Jolla, CA 92037, USA

google_reviews.text: Awesome view at night . Veware of the parking theres no wheere to park... My end up ne towed away..

Nice little hike uphill for some nice views of La Jolla and the Pacific Ocean. It got steep at times, so I wouldn't recommend it for little kids. Up on top is a big open area with a bench and trashcan (see pictures).  The entrance was tiny and unassuming, so it was tricky finding it. Otherwise a nice, little hike if you wanna get away from all the noise and all.
Great views of the ocean and La Jolla. Fairly steep so bring proper shoes.

Hidden gem above the noise and traffic on Torrey Pines Road overlooking the La Jolla Cove.  Unpaved path leading to the top of a hill with wonderful view to the north.  On clear days you get to see the mountain ranges north of Los Angeles in the Angeles National Forest.

Best views in La Jolla! Steep at the beginning, then a bit of an uphill for quite a ways, but remember to turn around periodically! Trailhead is right past the bridge on Al Bahr. Or you can skip the climb and drive straight to the top on Brodiaea, but what fun is that?

name: South Fortuna Mountain Summit
formatted_address: San Diego, CA 92119, USA

google_reviews.text:
Challenging but beautiful hike – not for beginners

I did the full loop at Fortuna Mountain, starting from the west side, heading north, and descending through the south via the Stairway to Heaven. The views are absolutely stunning and rewarding, but this trail is not easy.

Expect steep climbs and steep descents, especially on the southern section. The Stairway to Heaven in particular is very steep and rocky, and going down can be dangerous if you’re not prepared.

This is a trail for people with good physical condition and no fear of heights. Make sure to wear proper hiking shoes, I didn’t, and I definitely felt it.

I recommend South Fortuna overall, but whichever entrance you take, be ready for a very deep and intense hike. Do it right and safely,  it’s worth it, but it’s not casual

The South Fortuna Mountain and Fortuna Saddle Loop is a solid, rewarding hike with a great mix of climbs, views, and a little challenge. I did this loop on a hot day, so definitely plan for early morning or cooler weather—the heat can be intense with very little shade throughout the trail.

I started the loop by going left at the trailhead, which means you descend the infamous stairs instead of climbing them. That definitely made it easier on the lungs, but the steep, rocky descent was a bit rough on the knees. Trekking poles would be helpful here if you have sensitive joints.

The trail is well-marked, with steady elevation gains, wide open views, and a mix of single-track and wider fire roads. While it’s not a heavily trafficked trail—I didn’t see too many people—you still feel safe and connected enough in case you need help.

There is parking at the trailhead, and it wasn’t crowded when I arrived. The loop makes for a great workout and offers beautiful panoramic views from the top, making the effort well worth it.

This gorgeous view is of South Fortuna. It’s a moderate hike with multiple trails around it so make sure you follow your map, bring lots of water, there was some scrambling on top and a little walk on the saddle to get on the summit. But getting to top is fulfilling , I saw some young kids doing it as a family so ya, gauge your abilities and see if this is something for your family and friends ☺️

Standing at the summit of a mountain, overlooking endless landscapes, is truly inspiring. One side of the mountain offers a gradual, peaceful ascent, while the other features a steep slope and a narrow trail. When I reached the edge, I could see tiny people and cars below. The trail is amazing, and it's both physically demanding and visually stunning. Be sure to bring plenty of water and wear proper hiking boots.

South Fortuna Summit is a rewarding hike with steep stairs, rocky trails, and stunning views. The climb challenges your legs, but the panoramic scenery at the top makes it all worth it. Great for a solid workout and a peaceful escape into nature.

name: Inspiration Point
formatted_address: Inspiration Point Way, San Diego, CA 92134, USA

google_reviews.text: A magical place in Balboa park. You don’t need much time for it but it is beautiful to sit and chill. Very peaceful, a very magical garden with a nice fountain. You feel like you’re in a maze. Spent 30 mins just wondering around haven’t seen a single soul so I would say it really is a hidden gem!
Beautiful place to walk around. Romantic at night!

Got to catch the sunset here and it was amazingly beautiful. I love the architecture of the buildings and the views of the city skyline. Great place to take pictures and walk around! Highly recommend this spot in Balboa Park!

Nice spot to relax for a while.
There's a good view of the Coronado bridge and plenty of airplanes flying above you.

This is such a nice place to get away from the hustle and bustle of downtown. There's beautiful fountains and a lot of benches to sit on. Not a lot of people here right now. Good place if you're an introvert too and just need a place to relax.

name: Rancho Mission Canyon Park
formatted_address: 6005 Larchwood Ave, San Diego, CA 92120, USA

google_reviews.text: Cute neighborhood park with a woodland theme. It was hotter than a biscuit when we went and the slides were atomic, but everything else was fun for the kids. Should note that there's only a porta potty available.

What a beautiful and tranquil park! There's plenty of open space and a lovely hiking trail to explore. The playground and picnic areas are fantastic, featuring nice canopies for some shade while you play/relax. Plus, it's great that there's no sand or wood chips to deal with! I didn’t spot any restrooms, which was a bit of a letdown, but overall, this park is really well-maintained and has a little something for everyone to enjoy!
*FYI; be prepared to walk up a VERY STEEP hill to get from the parking lot to the playground. Could be difficult for those with mobility issues.*

First times taking the kiddos hear, and they had a blast!! Lots of thing's for them to do and play with. There are multiple slides, monkey bars, swings, and  lots of rock climbing options.  There is no actual restrooms, but I did see a porta potty on the upper field. There are also trails around,  and a small parking section. Once you park, you can walk up to the playground on the concrete path, grass, or the wooden dirt stairs.

Playground: Newer playground with lots to do for older and younger kids. The flooring is rubber and there was not enough shade. There are a few benches to sit on and picnic tables in the middle of the playground.
Bathrooms: port o potty’s a short walk away from the playground.
Parking: parking lot was down a steep hill.
Location: quiet residential street. Lots of grass, a nature trail, and a baseball field nearby.
Safety: one unhoused person sleeping on the playground. No trash, not fenced in playground. Many dogs not leashes walking near the playground.

Cleanest Park area I've found yet in San Diego! Wanted to explore the trails but didn't quite make it today. Loved that it had playground apparatuses for all ages and abilities. Also loved the shady areas available.

name: San Clemente Canyon
formatted_address: San Diego, CA 92117, USA

google_reviews.text:
Good dirt path walk. Some bikers use the trail. Overall a would be great but the highway noise is definitely consistently heard throughout drowning out some of the wildlife noises.

Great trail for running

Entered from Limerick street at the eastern terminus of the trail. I do not recommend entering from this point, as it requires a scramble on some pretty steep terrain for about 200ft. The rest of the trail is a very easy walk- on well maintained, flat trails. On a hot day, this trail is easily 15degrees cooler as it is in a gully, with a lot of tree cover, and a riparian environment.

This place is gorgeous. A bit underrated I think but that's a good thing. Not that many people there. Beautiful trails with a bunch of canopies and shared sections perfect for San Diego warm weather in the summer. The creek that run through add a touch of wilderness and boosts your hiking experience. There are multiple trails that run along the hwy 52, so if you are on a bike, or running, or walking you have options and plenty of space to do your thing.
Plenty of parking at both Regents and Balboa.

Parking off of Genesee provides about 30 spaces and open until 8pm. Bathroom and water fountain by the entrance. Trail is wide, unpaved dirt with few stretches of pebbles. Great for walking, running, and biking. No steep climbing except the short downhill at the start. Pond near the entrance can be crosses easily with the help of rocks on your right. Many trees provide shade. Also benches along the path if you want to take a break. Trail branches out couple times, but we stayed on "Limerick" trail. It must be that season because we saw many caterpillars hanging  from the tree, and crawling on the ground. The only downside is the constant road noise, but you can still hear the birds.

Another great gem for a hike nestled between neighborhoods and freeways. As other reviewers say, decent sized lot off Genesee with bathroom and water fountain. Mostly flat trail until you hit the loop to the far east by the 805 and 52 exchange.

If you head north around the loop by Limerick road that you can see dotted on Google Maps, note there is a rather steep section right at the north - northeastern section of the loop. Go clockwise if you want to climb UP, and go counterclockwise if you want to slide down on your butt, which was me. Good unexpected arm work out though.

name: Proctor Valley Natural Resource Area
formatted_address: Chula Vista, CA 91914, USA

google_reviews.text: Beautiful day for a hike. I started off on the normal trail app trail, but decided to go the way I saw some other head. Glad I did. I hike around the whole lake. It was almost 3 miles. Finished in a hour. Beautiful views and a good hike. Trail was clean and saw some cool graffiti. That I found out was really bad for the area and its habitat. Wasn’t very challenging like my last hike. But Highly recommend this trail. I will have to come back and take the other route next time.

Proctor Valley Road, a five-mile dirt path connecting Chula Vista's Eastlake neighborhood to Jamul, has long been shrouded in urban legends and ghost stories, including tales of the Proctor Valley Monster and phantom headlights. However, during a recent drive along this route, I found the reality to be more unsettling than the myths. The area was littered with trash, discarded items, and broken glass everywhere, creating an atmosphere that felt unsafe. I encountered a few individuals who appeared suspicious, prompting me to turn back. Rather than a scenic drive, the road seemed neglected and potentially hazardous as well as a hiding place or hangout for dangerous individuals. It's advisable to exercise caution if considering a visit to this area.
Great place for hiking/jogging
A good quite place to hike up to the Horseshoe Peak Summit. Pretty decent working out to get there and if u ran into a few ppl on the trails....consider urself lucky. It's anything but crowded here.
Beautiful open area with access to the Reservoirs and plenty of hiking trails to hike and mountain bike, take plenty of water and watch out for rattlesnakes .

name: Tecolote Canyon Hiking Area
formatted_address: 4600-4616 Genesee Ave, San Diego, CA 92117, USA

google_reviews.text:
Update:
01/08/26 We had lots of 🍄 Mushrooms near the trails, mostly by near creek areas. Fewer Birds, no dragonflies, and few butterflies for a while. Lots of green growth, and good creek flow. Balboa Tunnel running higher & foot bridge under Balboa is crooked. Many fallen trees. But still very enjoyable. Some blossoms trying to brighten your way.😃

08/18/25 Very dry, tan, brown & crisp, but still Cattails in creek bed bring Dragonflies, butterflies.
Fewer hawks. Now you can see through the trees along creek.
Be careful about fire, to keep hydrated, and rest in shade.

06/05/25 Field flowers slowing. Creek slowing/low. Coyote in mist.
Some flowers. Many baby bunnies🐇,🐿️Squirrels to🌳. Drying trail & grasses. Seen Big Blue heron recently, still red shoulder, sharp shined hawks, maybe cooper.

04/12/25 Work done!😃 Trail moist still, blossoms🪻🌿🌸🌼💠🏵️, critters too!! 🐿️🦎🦋🐦🐣🦆🕊️🦉🐦‍⬛🦅(hawks),coyote🐺. Come👁️see soon!!

Trail from Balboa Ave(dip) is being used by Trucks. Dusty. Aug/Sept '24. To about halfway up to Genesee Ave (Bus 41).
Sewer project. Lots of shade, birds, butterflies, lizards! Watch for Poison Oak,"leaves of 3 let be" reddening now.
*This trail follows Balboa Ave then heads north. Bathrooms & water at Mt. Etna Park. Picnic tables & play area and small parking lot also.
A nice long scenic walk home can change lives. Super Dope! Must Visit!

Hidden gem! I drove pass the trail entrance a couple times, but never had a chance to explore the area until now. The commute is easy: you can take the 41 bus and it will bring you to the trail entrance on Genesee or you can park in the local parking and take a few minutes walk. The trail is a combination of tree, shrubs, creek, sunshine and fresh air. It will make a good workout or relaxation spot for  you and your family

I grew up in the Colorado Rockies and have lived in Austria for four years and in Japan for ten years. I have a Masters and a PhD in Urban Planning and Design. I may be new to San Diego; however, I have a bit of experience and expertise in trails and trailheads. The Tecolote Trailhead directly off of Genesee Avenue where Genesee Avenue intersects with Chateau Drive is by far the worst trailhead I have ever seen. Genesee Avenue is a four lane arterial with a median. There is no street parking on Genesee Avenue near the trailhead. There isn't even a painted or signaled crosswalk across Genesee from what appears to be a smattering of on street parking on Chateau Drive.

So unless you already find yourself within walkable distance to  the general area where Genesee Avenue and Chateau Drive intersect and also enjoy sprinting across four lanes of distracted drivers on their way to/from I-5, this trailhead is essentially inaccessible.

The mosquitos are definitely out in abundance so wear some protection.  Despite the bites,  I received an amazing glimpse into the daytime activities of a older Black Crowned Night Heron which made the bites forgettable.

name: Mission Trails Clairemont Mesa Trailhead
formatted_address: Clairemont Mesa Blvd, San Diego, CA 92124, USA

google_reviews.text:
Although there are a few steep parts, it's generally a moderate difficulty trail.

I really like the loop layout of this plan; a welcome change from the typical in and out. It's a good workout with great views. Highly recommended.

Moderate hiking trails with some steep inclines but with rewarding views at the mountain summits of the southeast area of San Diego. Sights should be better around Spring rather than Autumn; pictures were taken sometime within November. Highly recommended to use hiking boots OR a hiking stick for stability as some parts of the trail can be a bit tricky to hike up.

An excellent and easily accessible entry to the Mission Trails Regional Park. This is a great location to access moderate to difficult trails in the park. Both hikers and mountain bikers will find this a great location to start your day. For those on two wheels you will also find the park has emergency air pump and basic tools in a few locations who thin the park. Lastly, no matter what time of year make sure you bring plenty of water you will find no drinkable water in this area.

Beautiful hiking location with amazing views! Trail is mostly dirt with some rocky parts and slight inclines. Not all trails open during the week according to posted signs.  Street parking available but limited due to visitors center currently closed for renovation.

I love hiking Mission Trails! This trailhead has less cars which is a plus! I did notice that the area was more populated with flies/bees, but nothing crazy to make you want to leave. Just keep hiking and enjoy the sun!

name: Crest Canyon Open Space Park
formatted_address: 2250 Del Mar Heights Rd, San Diego, CA 92130, USA

google_reviews.text:
Walked all the way to the beach. Road section is ok, wish it was dirt all the way. Road did not seem to have a lot of traffic. Walk was just over 5 miles and 371 feet of elevation gain in the way back.

Saw what I think was a young osprey.

Crest Canyon Open Space Park, incorrectly called Crest Canyon Park on some maps, is a relatively small canyon tucked between neighborhoods just south of the San Dieguito Lagoon Ecological Reserve.  The park’s unnamed loop trail has several trailheads – I say the trail is unnamed because neither the city’s map nor signs in the park name it.  If you’d rather start your hike by going uphill, use the street parking on Racetrack View Drive and enter the trail from there.  The trail heads south to Durango Drive which also has street parking.  The loop is about 2 miles.  The trails are sand/dirt and easy to navigate with the last section going to Durango Drive requiring you to walk up several sets of wooden stairs.  The park is clean but involved in a major habitat restoration project.  Some parts of the park provide a great vantage point for viewing the San Dieguito Lagoon.  A short trail that is separate from the canyon but part of the park – and connects one part of Racetrack View Drive to another – provides a closer look at the lagoon.

This is an absolutely amazing area and highly recommended to check out. This is a beautiful, relaxing canyon that's accessible from multiple points along the side and at the end. It's a really unusual topological area where you see some beautiful nature and a rare treat to enjoy. This area, Torrey Pines Del mar, is filled with magic and there are more spaces of beauty and great energy in the nature there than most. It's a very special area.

A great little canyon hike that has recently undergone major erosion control and restoration planting. If you are interested in that sort of thing it’s a great time to visit. Generally provides an exercise opportunity in a unique area.
Short, sweet hike with Torrey Pines, quiet with a little fork up a steep part if you wish.

name: Torrey Pines State Natural Reserve Extension
formatted_address: Del Mar Scenic Pkwy, Del Mar, CA 92014, USA

google_reviews.text: We love love this hiking area. Suits family and kids. Please respect the grounds, stay in the path, do not disturb or leave any trashes. Some areas Dogs are Not Allowed.  Please read the signs. Pick up your trash.   It’s an amazing view in the top.  Lots of natural animals, birds and the ocean breeze is cool. Best time to visit around May and early June.   Bring your water and sunblock.

Tory pines is beautiful. It's unfortunate that the scenic hiking trail was closed. It's nearly impossible to find parking available along the beach. If you go up the road to park, it's very expensive?

Torrey Pines State Natural Reserve Extension is a hidden gem for hikers and nature lovers.  Although this property is part of Torrey Pines State Natural Reserve, it is a detached, stand-alone property.  It does not have beach access.  It does not have a visitor center.  It does not have restrooms or drinking water.  It does, however, have a bunch of great trails.  The Reserve Extension has four major trails: The Mar Scenic Trail is a north/south trail that has trailheads at the end of Mar Scenic Drive in the north and the end of Del Mar Scenic Parkway in the south.  The Margaret Fleming Nature Trail and Red Ridge Loop Trail are to the east of the Mar Scenic Trail while to the west you’ll find the Daughters of the American Revolution Memorial Trail (“DAR” on park signs).  I’d say that, overall, the trails are easy/moderate to hike.  There are a few rough spots on the trails where erosion has kicked in but nothing that can’t be walked over or around while staying on the trail.  Signage along the trails was pretty good.  The trails are very clean: no trash at all and, because pets (dogs, horses, elephants, etc.) aren’t allowed, doo-doo dodging is not necessary.  Unlike Torrey Pines State Natural Reserve, the Reserve Extension does not charge a fee to enter.  Free street parking is available at the Mar Scenic Trail trailheads.

Lesser visited annex to Torrey Pines. Park in the neighborhood.  A couple of miles of trails with overlooks. The trail was a bit overgrown in spots, some erosion damage too. It was all navigable. Go in late spring for the wildflowers.

A great not so little hidden gem! It is quite a fun adventure going on the few trails here, the bushes are tall and the trail narrow so it is a great escape that makes you feel like you are actually out in nature!

name: Switzer Open Space
formatted_address: 2800 Maple St, San Diego, CA 92104, USA

google_reviews.text: While the parking can be challenging, the biggest issue was actually finding an entrance to this place. We just entered from the roadside following a small path that was created by others. The first and the most interesting portion of this canyon is the tunnel covered in graffiti. It is pitch black inside, and the low ceiling makes it hard to walk but if you keep walking towards the light at the end of the tunnel you end up on the other side of the road. My other gripe is the lack of markings. It is easy to end up in someone's backyard. Also part of the canyon is just a path for water (i.e. big pebbles) which makes it hard to walk. Hearing the birds chirping everywhere is nice, even if we cannot observe them. Overall a nice little outing with a little art on the side.

This delightful little hidden canyon is a great escape from the city, despite being surrounded by it! I think it just needs some more love and care to really be awesome. The trails are not well marked and over grown, which did aid in it's wilderness feel.
Fun place for the kids and teens, good for adults, and good for photos.  The park is clean and well kept up.  No facilities, so take care of business before you go.  😁

We always love to come here and especially enjoy walking the trails after a visit to the dog park. The pic is a few months old. It's a bit dryer there now after the summer. Nevertheless. We have the best time with our pup and enjoy the little off shoots of the canyon.

We had a hard time looking for the entrance. We asked some people in the street and they helped us find the entrance.
The hike is okay.
There are two options: Long and short route. The long route, you have to go through the tunnel.
The short route, leads back to the street.
Beware: There are 3 cayotes in the area. They don't attack humans, but they like small creatures. Like small puppy or toddlers.

name: Juniper Canyon
formatted_address: Juniper St, San Diego, CA 92104, USA

google_reviews.text:
Probably not worth the visit depending on what you’re looking for. This is more of a wash than any sort of hiking trail. Looks like landscaping efforts have been abandoned so there’s overgrown vegetation along the path. The “trailhead” is along a fairly dangerous curved road that people speed through (25MPH limit) and there’s no sidewalk. At least my dog enjoyed it!

FYI: The driving directions are misleading as the entrance is along Juniper St at the dip not within the neighborhood nor Gregory St as Google Maps suggests

This mostly hidden urban trail is a wonderful little pocket of nature in the city. The trailhead from the north is right next to a a residential lot with a small sign. It's small but has several side trails leading out, meaning there's more to explore than the primary trail's length implies.

There was a city maintenance crew trimming bushes and leveling the trail, so it is maintained - although I think they keep it as natural as they can given that much of the trail is made of loose stones that look like they were put there from water flowing there at some points during the year.

The incline is easy but the uneven stones add a challenge. The trail on the north side of Juniper is better maintained and marked than the one to the south - this section dead-ended for me in a giant hole about 5 minutes down the trail.

During most of the hour I spent here, I heard nothing but chirping birds and crickets while the scent of evergreen filled the air. Highly recommend if you want to find a few moments of peace and nature that are easy to access from the city.

So beautiful. I love places to hike in San Diego and I can say of all the spots I've visited so far, this part of Mission Trails is my favorite. So many trees, and a couple waterfalls complete with a gorgeous brook. Excellent spot to take pictures as well, if you're into that.

Waste of time from where I entered, Felton and Nutmeg. I took pics. Maybe other entrances are better, but the trail where I entered had no views (it was below street level) and had lots of large round rocks that hurt my feet. No doggie bags along trail either, so quite a bit of dog waste along trail.
Trail is very flooded. May want to give it a few days to dry out. Otherwise beautiful!

name: Louis A. Stelzer County Park
formatted_address: 11470 Wildcat Canyon Rd, Lakeside, CA 92040, USA

google_reviews.text: I visited Louis A. Stelzer County Park on a weekday in December when the weather was perfect for hiking, and the crowds were somewhere else.  I hiked all four of the park’s trails and didn’t encounter another person.  The wildflowers were gone, and the stream was dry.  Golden eagles, however, were out and on the hunt.  From the summit I watched four pairs cruising above and below me.  One pair spent about 5 minutes circling just above my head – I’m not a bird nerd but I thoroughly enjoyed the show.  The trails are wide and easy to navigate.  Signs and trail markers are in place.  Parts of the Stelzer Ridge Trail require an uphill climb.  The last 100 feet or so of the Summit Trail is a path that requires you scamper over or around vegetation and boulders.  The park has a picnic and play area – one school was participating in a field trip while I was there.  The park also has restrooms and a ranger station.  The construction of a new visitor center/ranger station and a tree-planting project were both happening during my visit, so the parking lot was a bit crowded with heavy equipment.  The parking fee is $3.00.

A hidden gem just outside of town. Beautiful spot for picnics, hikers, and explorers. The rangers were very friendly and knowledgeable. Wheelchair accessible and well maintained walking paths, playgrounds, and hiking trails. Something for any age/ability. So glad we went & we'll definitely be going back soon!

A great place for a quiet walk in nature or a nice picnic. There are a few trails here, most of them are very tame. The park includes plenty of benches, playground equipment and drinking fountains. An excellent choice for people of all ages. If you go after a storm there might be water in the brook.
Plenty of rocks and trees to climb on. I remember getting rashes from poison oak when I was young - we were walking through the stream bed.
Parking is $3 per vehicle for the day. Bring exact change.

Love this find, what a trail! This week we have made a family goal to find different hiking trails in SD. This is the 3rd trail in our finds: Mt. Helix, Tecolote Park, and this location. Compared to the others, this park is more vast and has slightly more concentrated brush area(trees). Upon entry, a park ground keeper warned us to keep an eye down for garden snakes🐍. Walking in from the parking lot, make your stop to the front desk and pay for parking($3). There are bridges, dirt paths, different playground areas, an outdoor reception area, love that this is so lush with trees, boulders sprinkled the grounds along with benches on the trails, make sure to check temp before hiking in harsh conditions, drink plenty of water grab a sturdy stick to check your paths for any snakes sleep in the brush. So many bees! They're harmless. Such a vast area, our family of 5 completed half the trek and plan to return to complete the entire trail. The loop up hill is the best part, if you slow down at the grand blue deck at the half mark on Wooten Loop - you can look west to see the vast V-shape mountain line.
Wonderful park, a few eating areas, 3 different hiking trails, a stage for talent shows or special ceremonies, the park has a map of the grounds for easy navigation. Also small tip: walk in a line oppose to side by side to prevent stepping  on snakes 🐍.  10/10

The park has been renovated since I was last there. It is a hundred times nicer than it used to be. To be fair, last time was after the Cedar Fire, which may have been why it was in such sad shape. Signs were posted not to drink or use the water, not even to wash your hands and the restrooms we're closed. They have remedied that problem, thank goodness. The park has playgrounds for the kids, BBQs, picnic tables in the shade, the hiking trails have rest points and there is a vending machine at the front of the park that offers water and Gatorade and sodas. I don't recommend going on a hot day to hike. A lot of the trail is lacking shade. The trail was well maintained but there are a few steep slopes. Wear appropriate shoes and remember watch for snakes on the trails. Great place for a family picnic.  $3 to park.

name: Coronado Beach
formatted_address: Coronado Beach, 100 Ocean Blvd, Coronado, CA 92118, USA

google_reviews.text: This beach is long, wide and perfect for taking long walks or playing games with friends and family. The open view is amazing because you can easily see everything around you without feeling crowded. There are free showers and restrooms which makes it really convenient after spending hours in the sand and ocean. The only downside is the limited parking. Most people park along the street if they can find a spot so it really depends on your luck. Overall it’s a great place to relax and enjoy the ocean breeze. Families with kids will love how much space there is to run around. Bring snacks and spend the day here. Just be ready to look for parking and maybe arrive a bit earlier on busy days.

Coronado Beach is one of the most beautiful and peaceful spots in all of San Diego. From the soft, sparkling sand to the gentle ocean breeze, everything about this beach feels like a perfect coastal escape. The views are absolutely stunning, especially around sunset when the sky lights up in warm tones over the Pacific.

The beach is clean, spacious, and family-friendly, with plenty of room to relax, take a stroll, or set up for a full beach day. The iconic Hotel del Coronado in the background adds a classic charm, and the entire area has a relaxed, upscale vibe without feeling too crowded or commercial.
