The refuge serves as a sanctuary for the rare Coachella Valley fringe-toed lizard and many other desert dwelling species.

Was hopping to see that lizard as I biked by.

I took Avenue 38 which is closed to traffic from Washington Street and went west along the Refuge.

It's about a 1.6 mile bike ride from Washington to Varner where Avenue 38 ends. To the north is the fence and the vast Refuge.

The sand dune was the coolest part of the ride to be honest.
Very pretty

I took a covered wagon tour with friends. The night was pleasant and food was good, though it would be better to have a large group of friends to take up an entire wagon. We were stuck with a large family with small kids and young parents that were not interested in the history or walking in the Oasis so it made it ho-hum for the ones of us that were interested. If it weren't for the price, I'd try it again, but I won't pay that price again to risk getting stuck with another disappointing group of people.
I say this picture is fantastic Perfectly captured the characterizes of the desert.
Absolutely awesome hike and views!

name: Lake Fulmor Day Use Area
formatted_address: Hall Decker Rd, Banning, CA 92220, USA

google_reviews.text: Stumbled on Lake Fulmor on a scenic drive from Desert Hot Springs back to Temecula. Really nice pathway goes around the lake. Very pleasantly surprised. Does require a day use parking permit that can be picked up at ranger station. We were given one by a couple leaving.

A wonderful place to picnic, take a short 0.63 mile walk around the lake, and judging from the number of fishermen, to fish.  It's too bad the natural water flow through this small lake is inadequate to keep the water quality high enough to swim. Parking is easy and plentiful and only costs $5 for a day use/parking permit which can be purchased at the Idyllwild Ranger Station.

I miss when we used to be able to swim here and jump off the big rock, but it's still a beautiful and a peaceful place to visit. It's great for fishing and walking around in the pines. There are many types of birds and other animal life here too. It's a great little spot to relax and reconnect with nature.
Lake Fulmor is AWESOME! My buddy and I hiked all the way around the lake. We loved it! We are avid photographers and really enjoyed the  beauty of the nature, etc there!

Pretty little spot right off Hwy 243. It's well-maintained. There are picnic tables and grills to enjoy lunch or you can pick a spot somewhere along the edge of the lake to sit and enjoy. There is a round, wooden walkway/pier from which several people were fishing the day I was there. It extends over the water and is a great place from which to get an unobstructed view of the lake. I went on a weekday at noon. It was a little busy. It's a small enough place for that to happen easily. There was a group of young ladies playing music loud enough to be heard from the road. They were just having fun, but it detracted from the otherwise very peaceful setting. If I get back through the area, I'd like to try to visit on a weekday in the early morning or the evening. If you're in the area, it's definitely worth going to see, busy or not. The parking area is across the road from the lake and does require a wilderness pass, obtainable at a number of stores, including local convenience stores. Safe & fun travels.

name: CA STATE LOT
formatted_address: 78425 Varner Rd, Palm Desert, CA 92211, USA

google_reviews.text: Best Park in the Valley! The view is breathtaking and the nature sounds of the local wildlife are stunning to say the least. My wife and I love to picknick in this spot, their are some local eateries. I would not recommend camping overnight however, the wildlife can become agitated and hostile. Best to keep it to a day trip, summer months are to die for. Also there are restrooms all over this park from what we saw. Bring your own water supply, the one at this park gave me a tummy ache.

name: Agua Caliente Tahquitz Visitor Center
formatted_address: 500 W Mesquite Ave, Palm Springs, CA 92264, USA

google_reviews.text: It’s not the easiest hike, but it took me less than 30 minutes and it was quite enjoyable. They make you take two bottles of water for a good reason – you’re hiking in the desert. The payoff – waterfall – is nice and shaded so it’s cooler than the rest of the hike. Definitely worth the time invested.

Easy to get to get to from Palm Springs and easy to park, pay and hike. $15 per adult. Coming from the PNW, I was expecting more out of the waterfall.... but my teens loved climbing on the boulders and the view. There were MANY people on the trail and nice bathrooms.

Absolutely surprising to see a waterfall in midst of desert! The Tahquitz canyon trail is a one hour loop with medium difficulty. The trail is rocky but not too difficult; carry enough water though. The  visitor center has low parking capacity so the staff allows only few cars per parking capacity; so be ready to park on the road leading to the visitor center enter and walk towards. They have clean restrooms and a gift shop plus a water fountain. Do carry sunshades and plenty of sunscreen. If you could sustain cold waters, you can have fun at the 60 feet waterfall. Great overall!

The visitors center has a theatre with an informational video and a small gift shop.
Guided tours of the canyon are free and usually are scheduled four times a day, if you are interested.
You can hike the canyon in your own, too.
It’s an out and back trail that takes about 1 1/2 to 2 hours and a s moderately difficult.
No dogs are allowed.
Parking is included in your $15 fee.

I cannot say enough good things about this hike and especially with the ranger, Angel, who guided us. The tour and history of the land, the plants, the life lived here was spectacular. Thank you for your patience and answering all our curious questions! The hike is rocky and strenuous in some places but that waterfall is worth it! Absolutely stunning landscape. Lovely gift shop with beautiful items and rocks and jewelry and more! If you get a chance to go, do it. And get a guided tour if you have time.

name: City of Palm Desert Homme-Adams Park
formatted_address: 72500 Thrush Rd, Palm Desert, CA 92260, USA

google_reviews.text: Beautiful views of Palm Desert. Nice trails for walking on ridges going up mountainsides. We only stopped to walk the dog, so we didn't make it up to the end or top. It is all hiking up elevation  then you can crossover to other ridges  to head back down. There a steep sides, so make sure of footing on loose gravely and sand. Take water and enjoy.

Great walk up to the Cross. We took our time up and down. It wasn't very busy which was nice. The wild flowers were out and in bloom and a bush was also blooming and smelled very nice. We are in our 60s so the walk was moderately challenging.

The three story, giant coral pink "Dive-in" land art piece by Superflex was installed here for Desert X 2019. Rather eye-catching in Pepto pink, this structure also had a film projected upon in on Saturday evenings, as part of it's original intent.

The day I visited, there was a group prepping for a photoshoot, and they brought goats... 🐐
A dog friendly park that is easily accessible. Not to much to see, but there is a short trail to a lookout point and some other trails aswell.
Great dog walking. Desert X here in 2017

name: Lake Hemet Campground
formatted_address: Box 4, 56570 CA-74, Mountain Center, CA 92561, USA

google_reviews.text: We loved our camping trip to Lake Hemet. The facilities were always clean, staff was warm and friendly, and the campground was beautiful. It was very close to where I live and was such a fun experience that we are already looking forward to our next trip.

This place was amazing! It was recommended to me one week and booked for the next week. I'm happy to have had the experience I did have. It all began with a great check-in process (smooth and kind service), readily available and considerate care during the stay, and it ended with just as smooth of a check-out as the check-in.

The amenities offered were not all listed, so I was pleasantly surprised to see I didn't have to pack as much as I did.

- Bathroom: hand soap, hand towel, toilet paper, shampoo, conditioner, and bodywash dispenser in the shower.

- Kitchen: Dish soap and sponge, paper towels, dish towels, coffee maker, pots and skillet, mixing bowls, measuring cups and spoons, can opener, corkscrew, knives, cooking utensils, toaster, microwave, tableware for 8 (wine glasses, cups, coffee cups, plates, dessert plates, bowls, silverware), broom and dustpan.

- Bedrooms: towels on all beds, space heater, plenty of pillows, heating pads (only in colder months)

I think it's important to know these are available, especially if you're already excited about staying at one of their lodges so you can focus on packing only the necessities.

I enjoyed my first stay so much that I'm already ready to book there again and make it an annual trip.

Has a friendly staff. The woman at the entrance was very helpful and informative. Couldn't stay the two nights in there cabin, do to a family emergency. They gave me credit so I can come again this year and pay the difference. That was very cool of them.  So over all a wonderful experience.

We had a great 4 month stay this summer!
This was our 5th summer at the Lake - as it's the perfect escape from the desert heat!! The staff is what makes it such a pleasure! Every single one of them are just wonderful "stellar" people!!
A special big thank you to Jeff Walls! He was very instrumental in setting us up for a perfect situation to make our future returns even more amazing for years to come! Jeff is attentive and works hard to build a good moral within a very diverse community. He makes himself available for any and all situations.
We appreciate all the staff and their conscious support of keeping the grounds safe and efficient for the many who call it home!
Thank you! Looking forward to next summer already!

We loved the campground - it was well organized and very clean. We were placed beside another RV containing a family of three (one young boy). Unfortunately, the family has 3 dogs. The dogs were kept tethered to a long line by a one foot lead. Each dog was basically pinned in place. They were never off of that one foot  lead unless they were being literally thrown by the scruff of their neck/back into a crate. They did not have access to water and we did not see them fed in the two days we saw them. The dogs stayed outside at night with no place to keep warm - the temperature one night dropped to approximately 30°. I informed the campground person who was stationed at the gate that on day 1, the dogs were left alone while pinned in-place for several hours. Never did we see anyone from the campground visit their campsite. I then texted the campground on their 24 hr number the next day to report the worsening situation. Also, the people had hunting rifles which we saw and reported. I also reported the presence of weapons via text. I got no response from the campground nor did I see a campground representative pay a visit to the campsite next to us. The campground appeared to not respond to my reports and to not uphold their rules: only 2 dogs/site, never leave dogs outside unattended, and no guns in the campground.

name: Stone Creek Campground
formatted_address: 21181 CA-243, Idyllwild-Pine Cove, CA 92549, USA

google_reviews.text:
We stayed two nights and had a great time! We booked online and got site 29. It was very spacious and felt pretty secluded from the other sites. The site is down a little below the parking spot so you're not directly by the road. There were three pit toilets right across from the site but they were very clean and I could only smell it maybe once or twice. The water was a few sites down so pretty close.

The campsite was completely booked when we got there and we could definitely hear people in the other sites and could tell some people at a site far from us were playing loud music around 9:45pm. There is a camp host in the morning but they leave in the afternoon.

There are no showers but we were able to drive to the Idyllwild Campground and get shower tokens and use their showers (a little inconvenient but nice after a long hike).

We just got home recently from a great trip during the 4th of July, except for the blood suckers out there! One of us was eaten alive (30+ bites) even with the OFF spray on and in a bug screened  canopy tent.  The large black and whites were more aggressive, biting early morning and late evening and and the little ankle biters were there too! 🙃   Idyllwild is a great little mountain town to explore. Close to Lake Fulmor, too!
The camp host guy is nice, as are his two cats who greeted us!
There are no showers at this campground, but you can drive to town (10-15 minutes ) and go to the other state park to use. Need a coin to operate them that you get at that ranger station.
Vault toliets you could smell here and there depends on wind. Hand stanizier on outside of them. Saw rangers cleaning a few times.
Our site had a good amount of shade versus some did not have much when we explored.  A few sites have power, which is nice!
Very quite and peaceful. Other campers were respecting hours.
No wood fires due to weather (heat wave) and fire danger, but it can vary since there are pits.
Water spigots through out grounds.
Says bears and rattlers but didn't see them. Some sites had bear cabinets ? But some did not.

Great campground, came up here early may midweek and found the campground literally to myself! Very friendly campground host who even helped me load wood in the trunk. No showers and the bathrooms are pot toilets, however these were the cleanest pit toilet bathrooms I've ever encountered, they weren't even stinky. If your a fisherman there are 2 lakes nearby but one of them is currently closed for renovations and the other turns out to be a private lake so if you insist on fishing you have to drive to Hemet lake. Loved the privacy of this campground however I'm sure weekends are a whole different beast. Some sites looked a little close to others.

All depends on the spot! Camped in spot 35 which was about 50 yards from the restrooms. Convenient, but at night there was a breeze coming down the mountain that sent the slightly unpleasant smell towards my spot. Safe to assume that spots 34,36 & 37 across the road from me were also getting the same experience. Wasn't horrible but campground was full, so moving wasn't an option. Otherwise, host was friendly, area was clean and quiet even though full. Would stay again, but would make sure to pick a spot that was not downhill from a restroom.

Very well maintained campground. It is a primitive one so don’t expect flushable toilet or a shower.
It is well maintained, has a nice hike around the campground. Off you venture around the area you will find plenty of great hikes with amazing views.

name: Fern Basin Campground
formatted_address: Forest Rte 4S02, Banning, CA 92220, USA

google_reviews.text: This a a great little gem to choose if you’re looking for a quick weekend get away or if you’re just passing trough. It’s a small but cozy and very accessible campground. With water and restrooms available, but no showers and no electricity. At $10 you can’t beat the price.

Beautiful campground. We would totally come here again. The water wasn't working, due to issues with the tank. Thankfully we took enough water, as we only stood one night. There was a lot to explore outside of camp and inside.

Stayed here first week in October. Nights were cold but days were gorgeous!! We stayed in the week and the campsites were empty. Bathrooms are vault but surprisingly clean. We stayed in #21 and could smell the faint smell of the bathrooms once in awhile and bees were everywhere which was more annoying then anything.

Campground is nice if small. There is water at most if not all sites. The bathrooms are basically portypottys and have no lights in the building so bring a headlamp. The sites are good sized. I did not see a bear warning until the trail so make sure you have a bear bag or something since there are no bear boxes.

Great little campground with little privacy since the sites are almost on top of each other. For $10 a night its well worth it to get out of the city. Site number 6 is okay but there is a small hill you need to walk down in order to get to the site. I would go with number 7 since its one of the few where the site is literally right next to where you park your car.

name: Idyllwild Campground
formatted_address: 25905 CA-243, Idyllwild-Pine Cove, CA 92549, USA

google_reviews.text: Very affordable clean secluded area camp next to your parked car has picnic tables clean restroom/showers need tokens for hot water 25c 5 minutes NO wood/charcoal burning take gas powered fire pit oct 40° at night take warm clothes quilts. Def a must do for the family.. NO danger relaxing walking distance from downtown bars restaurants stores DO it thank me after.

Nice area to camp at. I took my Tesla Model Y to camp in overnight and was glad to have spot 6 with electricity. I saw two other Model Y cars when I went too, so I guess I’m not as alone as I thought I was in doing this.

A few spots like spot 6 have electricity hookups and are decently spaced. Attached are pictures to show the connections and the size with my vehicle parked in it. The available connections are great for EV campers! You can bring your Level 1/2 chargers and charge your EV here without worry either while you stay the night or if you plan to go hiking nearby or walking through the town. The campground is seated in town, so this is perfect spot for this sort of planning.

I was glad to see that showers were available as were running water for drinking and washing.

I wasn’t really thrilled with the spacing of the sites. Site 5 was more isolated than 6 was with my spot at 6 being directly next to 7 where a truck and trailer camper were seated. It’s like sharing your driveway with someone else, almost. Just too close for my comfort where I planned to be alone.

I also was not expecting to not be able to set a fire. There didn’t seem to be any mention of this on the website or when making my reservation but I was told this when checking in. I’m also not sure why they banned fires at this time of year in winter where it was literally snowing. The chances of a wildfire occurring are very low, if at all.

The price for the spot was $45 which wasn’t terrible either.

There was a nice trail to walk around the campground with some nice scenery, even things left behind by Native Americans, which was cool to see.

All in all, it’s a decent place and I’d recommend, but would recommend checking in to see what’s available before making your reservation to ensure you’re getting what you want and also knowing where your spot will be depending on what you’d like for your getaway.

Stayed here over the 3-day weekend.We reserved two spots and it needed more shaded areas. Spots vary in sizes.The campground is gated all the way around so there was no easy trails access which was a bummer we love hiking. For the showers do make sure to take dollar bills because you need to exchange them for tokens. It is a $1 for 1 token, a shower.The restrooms were clean and the showers had hot water. This campground is good for beginners. It is nice to have the small markets for ice or last-minute shopping. Overall and okay experience.

Really enjoyed my stay at this unique campsite minutes from the cute village center.  I got to camp amongst pine trees with lots of shade everywhere.  The campsite is noticeably cooler than the city, prob due to the trees.
The restrooms are sparking clean super well functioning with all hanging hooks intact and even a paper towel dispenser - the first time I’ve seen this in a state park restroom!
The shower requires their own tokens, which are different from other parks however, one token $1 gives you five minutes, which is well sufficient for a good shower. The Silver tokens from other state parks will not work here!
Now some caveats: it is noisy in the scamp side as there is a road that circles it so you can hear an occasional car at night.  Some sites for example 18, 19, 24,25.. are close to each other so you might get to be next to a large family.
I had the fortune of getting site 20, where you park your car and then you hike up the hill to the campsite that overlooks a creek and a small second bridge.  Unfortunately, I had to haul all my stuff up and down this hill taking about five trips each due to my midsize, lots of equipment and a dog.  However, I had a great experience.
Also expect to encounter bugs, such as flies and gnats and a few mosquitoes so come prepared in the summer.
It’s a seven minute walk to the village center and some coffee shops as well as some really good restaurants. They have all sorts of events going on here in the summer.  I haven’t got to meet the city mayor Max the dog who visited the campsites!  It was a short drive to the brewpub, which I highly recommend visiting when you are in town.
Little bit of snow when I got there but melted pretty fast in the 70° plus weather.

name: Pinyon Flat Campground
formatted_address: Campground Rd, Mountain Center, CA 92561, USA

google_reviews.text:
Pinyon Flat Campground is one of those quiet, underrated spots that’s perfect when you want to escape the city without driving too far. It’s small, with about 18 campsites total, spread out nicely for privacy. Most times I’ve camped here, there were only a handful of other people, which makes it feel peaceful and remote. That said, it can fill up unexpectedly during holidays or random weekends, so it’s smart to arrive early if you’re coming during a busy time.

The sites themselves are well-maintained, and each has a picnic table and fire ring. There are water spigots and trash cans located throughout the campground, which is a huge plus for cooking or cleaning up. The restrooms are basic and clean enough for a primitive campground, but just note that the toilets don’t have running water — they’re dry vault-style. It’s not a big deal if you come prepared, but it’s good to know ahead of time.

The views of the surrounding desert and mountains are stunning, especially at sunset, and it’s quiet enough at night to actually hear the wind moving through the pines. You can even see the stars really clearly thanks to the low light pollution.

Overall, Pinyon Flat is a great choice if you enjoy simple, peaceful camping with a real backcountry vibe. Definitely one of the more underrated campgrounds in the Palm Desert/San Jacinto Mountains area.

Campground is nice with vaulted toilets and water.  Close to main road and housing. (traffic noise and barking dogs - but have dealt with worse) Some good hiking and Mountain Biking (MTB not allowed in wilderness areas).  Overall pleasant. I’d come again for the activities but, not for a relaxing stay.
Not busy, 100% chance to get a site 7 days a week, portable water and vaulted john for 8$ a day, no hookups and road is noisy all nite long. Coyotes yelping all nite also.

If your trying to get away and enjoy a peaceful and quiet experience this is the place for you.if your looking to enjoy gossip and music and having a good loud time I'd look else where. Dog friendly. Bathrooms were pretty decent, everything else was  Clean and extremely peaceful for a childless weekend.
My experience was great. I only stayed one night but I will be coming back. Thank you very much

name: Boulder Basin Campground
formatted_address: Black Moutain Trail, Banning, CA 92220, USA

google_reviews.text: Remote campground with a 5 mile (30 minute drive) dirt road up 2,000 ft of elevation on the backside of idyllwild. There road continues on for dispersed camping as well. These campsites were unique and spaced out. There’s no running water but bathrooms with vaulted toilets were clean and well kept. Would recommend a four wheel drive car or at least 6 inches of clearance but saw a Nissan versa and Toyota Camry up there. I wouldn’t do that personally though. Great for bouldering (rock climbing without a rope)

This is a great campground roughly 5 miles down a fire road. Road conditions weren't terrible at the time of writing this.

This campground can be pretty idyllic. There are boulders all around and great views to be had with just a short walk. Even better views are available up the trail to the fire lookout tower. It's an easy out and back ~1.3 miles.

This is a popular spot for bouldering and climbing and there is a lot of day use behind campsites 20 & 21 so if you aren't keen on joining them, grab a different site.

Wonderful National Park with well layed out camping sites. The road is very difficult in car or low clearance vehicles (Do not attempt unless very experienced in a car). There are no real marked hiking trails other than the road up to the lookout tower, well worth a visit. The Rangers were all super nice and friendly. Pit toilets only and no water was available during our trip. Bring a telescope 🔭 if you have. It's a great dark sky area.

While a little treacherous to get to at times (definitely need a higher-clearance vehicle) the campground is situated on a prefect location surrounded by boulders and tall trees. We stayed in spot 30, which has an amazing view and drop behind it. Lots of great opportunities for bouldering!

Pretty nice campground. Lots of spots here. We probably saw 4 other spots occupied on a mid-June Monday. No water anywhere (no surprise). At least 2 outhouses (male/female) with standard pit toilets. A bit stinky but normal for a forest. Light to moderate bugs in mid June in a poor winter earlier in the year.

name: Silent Valley Camp ground
formatted_address: 46305 Poppet Flats Rd, Banning, CA 92220, USA

google_reviews.text: Nice little gem up in a secluded valley. The drive up and down at 7% grade is a little white knuckle, but doable. Price was more than reasonable, however, without satellite or starlink there is no cell service. At least not for T-Mobile. We drove down the hill for dinner and downloaded a bunch of Netflix shows and movies. There are lots of critters and birds; and the view of the night sky is absolutely spectacular. The entrance has concrete rock walls and there about 40 feet from the rails of the bridge entering the park. We literally had to drive within a few inches of the rail to make the right-hand turn over the bridge without our tail swing hitting the wall. The exit is more than ample for any size rig.

This spot is amazing for the family. We come and visit and always have a great time. There is a lake to fish, multiple playgrounds, mini golf course, disc golf course, pool, a Saloon, a general store, a laundry mat and I'm sure I'm missing something. We recently visited for the Memorial Day weekend. You need a membership to stay here. The community is amazing.

Love this mountain retreat setting and campground! Friendly and knowledgeable associates. A bar, restaurant and seasonal outdoor church chapel on site. A grocery store and laundromat too. Seasonal activities unlike any other campground we've ever been to thats geared towards families making memories for years to come. You'll love it too!

An excellent camp site and a great place to camp. There are lots of amenities to camp comfortably and have a memorable time with family and friends. The layout of the campground is very unique and has a very beautiful view from every part of it. It is definitely a great place to getaway from the busy city to enjoy the piece and quiet surroundings.

We have been member's since 2021 and have two memberships! We love our time spent at SVC. Great fun for the whole family! Restaurant, bar, pools, pond/fishing, separate adult & family clubhouses. All the employees/rangers have been so great to us.
Tell me, where else can you camp for less than $10 a day and have all those great amenities?
So a BIG Thank You to all those who make SVC so great!!

name: Desert Rose Collective Camping
formatted_address: 52292 Canyon Rd, Morongo Valley, CA 92256, USA

google_reviews.text: As a solo traveler, I look at value and safety; both of which this place have! I truly enjoyed my stay here and would recommend to a friend. The campground is eclectic, made up of a plethora of recycled and reused materials. The common areas are abundant and include fire pits, a bar, several tables/chairs and even comfy laying-out chairs! I had the pleasure of staying in the tent cabin and it was wonderful. Nice airflow during the day, and a heating pad for the cooler evenings. The compost toilet was new to me, but I was pleasantly surprised with how un-stinky it was! The hot shower was also a nice touch. Hosts on site, along with Rosemarie, were very pleasant. Thanks for the wonderful stay!

This place is full of art and great vibes.  The cabin has a heated bed and is very comfy. It has a feel of home that is amazing to come home to after a long day of taking in the sights.  There is a platform it is on that is perfect for yoga.  Many areas to sit and sip hot tea with friends or a good book.  Great for get togethers or gets some alone time.

6 of us from San Diego Land Rover club enjoyed the trails in Joshua Tree NP and then stayed with Rosemarie at her excellent campground.  The fire pit and bar area was super nice for relaxing and sharing stories of the day. Rosemarie was a great host.  Thanks!

Seriously this space is a magical gem hidden in the desert. Staying here you have showers, cell service, a fully equipped kitchen, lots of places to lounge in the shade and at night fire pits with free fire wood. The landowner and host Rose is extremely kind-hearted. One of the sweetest women I've come to meet during my time on the road, providing travelers with an inexpensive sanctuary to rest in-between travels. This place is a must-stay. An added bonus is the incredible wildlife you see around the property. Prime spot for birders! 💗💗💗

We arrived at night after Joshua Tree was fully booked. We were not sure if there would be availabilities. Rose Marie happened to be waiting for other guests and found a space for us. I found the property to be charming. I wish I could have stayed longer but we were just passing through. I would come back though.

name: Lake Cahuilla Campground
formatted_address: Cahuilla Park Rd, La Quinta, CA 92253, USA

google_reviews.text:
Stopped here on the way through, haven't been here in 2 years. They've been doing some upgrades. Place still isn't great. Though the host who was manning the gate was nice.

Roads are asphalt, sites are dirt. So if it's raining you're in the mud. The road is very narrow for 2-way traffic so don't pull out until it's clear. All hook-up sites have new electrical monuments and wash out rings. They also have new metal picnic tables. Which probably seemed like a good idea but get blazing hot in the sun. Sites are close together, no privacy. Trash in my site when I got there, and trash all around the lake shore area. Even after they 'clean' there are still beer bottles everywhere. Getting drunk appears to be the main activity here next to fishing.

Main CG bathroom looks like it has had an update, but was still dirty. Showers have doors, but not curtains, and water does eventually get hot, but there are no shelves or benches to put stuff on. Website says the showers take coins but they don't.

The day use has a new lighted sports court with basketball and pickleball. Pool is closed for the season. All areas have new trash and recycle cans. There is a large covered playground in the day use, looks newer.

No fences, anyone can come in any time. No police presence or armed enforcement rangers, park is unsafe. No enforcement of rules. People play their blaring mariachi and thumping gang music. During the day there are dozens, sometimes hundreds of people around the lake fishing. There are even men in work uniforms fishing during the day instead of working. It's that kind of place.

Horse camp has hookups but all roads and sites are dirt. Bathroom in the horse CG hasn't had any love in a while. Doors are rotted through and paint peeling.

Just outside the gate is a trailhead to Santa Rosa & San Jacinto Mountains National Monument. We just did a couple of miles out and back. I recommend good hiking shoes and poles. We camped at Lake Cahuilla Veterans Regional Park in a Fifth Wheel.

It looks like it's been recently remodeled; everything looks new: the metal tables, the grills, the basketball courts, the pickleball field. You can only fish, not swim.
My two stars are because they didn't think things through and placed the grills so close to the next site. They're so close you can't use them without risking setting your neighbor's RV on fire. We couldn't use it, not even to make a campfire; you can't put chairs around it. We were at site 14, including sites 16 and 18 of some friends and maybe all the others which I didn't see.
Change that!

Camped in RV, electric and water hookups, and a free dump station, restrooms and showers were fine. Spacious sites  with nice views and some shade from eucalyptus trees. It's the desert so dirt and can kick up if it gets windy. Disperse camping in front of the lake. No swimming or boating, has fishing but mostly carp and catfish, with a few pavilions scattered around the lake. Hike around the lake was 2.5 miles, will do the mountain pass next time. Watch your rear view mirror if driving on the dirt to avoid kicking up dirt for your neighbors. Happy camping!
The lake is very nice. You can’t swim in it, but you can fish. Also, it is very peaceful in the bathrooms are very clean, the showers are also amazing, next time I go camping I’ll come here again.

Good points - the lake and surrounding mountains are beautiful.  The colors change every hour.  The sites are bigger than many other campgrounds we have stayed at, most have electricity and water.  If you have a California fishing license, you can fish in the lake which is stocked with catfish and trout.  We caught a foot long trout that was delicious.  You can fish from 0600 AM to sunset, which is enforced by a ranger.  There are limits on how many fish you can take per day.  You can hike and ride bikes on a trail around the lake.  The sites have trees, but you will mostly be in the sun during the day and the desert sun can be quite hot.  There are showers that cost 25 cents for about a minute, water is comfortably hot and preset, you cannot change the temperature.  There are flush toilets along with supplied toilet paper, 2 sinks and an electrical outlet in the rest rooms.  There is a dump station that is included in the cost of camping.  In addition to the nightly/weekly fees, pets are an additional $2 per night and an $8 processing fee is added to the total cost.
Bad points - the speed limit is 15 MPH but NOT enforced, many go WAY over that speed.  The main road through the campground runs parallel to the camping area.  If you have pets or children - BEWARE as the vehicles approach rapidly.  These speeding vehicles make it quite noisy most of the day, nights are very quiet as the public must leave at sunset.  There is an artillery range very near by, expect the sound of gunfire.  There are no paper towels in the restroom, and no doggie waste bags in the pet walking area, please bring your own.

name: Campground
formatted_address: 53755 Pine Crest Ave, Idyllwild-Pine Cove, CA 92549, USA

google_reviews.text: Camp ground was nice and peaceful... restrooms were clean. Lots of Flies and Nats, be sure to bring the Deep Woods Off spray... it really helped

We love coming up to Idyllwild! We have come in July when the grounds are packed, and in November when it's 50% full. Wood burning Fires and charcoal grills have been banned this year both times we came, so we brought propane cooktops and a propane fire pit to hang out by at night. The sites are a bit small and often on hills or inclines...fine for tent camping, but few spots are big enough for our truck camper. Very few if any are big enough for a trailer or 5th wheel. Every site does have a metal picnic table you can move around, and a wood firepit with fold over grill top (for when you are allowed to burn wood fires). What we love is how this campground is so close to the cute little Idylwild town (like 1.5 miles), there's pine trees that make you feel like you're in NorCal yet you're only a couple hours from San Diego or LA, there's squirrels, wood peckers, and wildlife abundance, and there hiking trailheads inside the campground. The bathrooms are clean, and the showers are hot. The rangers are really helpful and friendly as well. There's also a volleyball court at the day use area. The dump station and water fill up station are also well maintained, easy access and functional.
This campground was magical!!!
We love it here very quiet and clean...I have been coming here for a long time 🥰🥰🤩🤩

Labor Day weekend was loud.  Multiple sites had music that you could hear from both the other side of the the campground and from the hiking trails in the mountains.   Multiple sites were ignoring the fire restrictions too.

name: Palm Springs / Joshua Tree KOA Holiday
formatted_address: 70405 Dillon Rd, Desert Hot Springs, CA 92241, USA

google_reviews.text:
We had a great stay at Palm Springs / Joshua Tree KOA. The campground was clean, safe, and very comfortable overall. We stayed in site 125, which had a covered picnic table and a gas fire pit, and it was absolutely perfect. The fire pit alone made the stay feel extra special and was such a nice way to relax in the evenings.

I usually prefer off-grid camping, but this was an ideal spot to slow down and enjoy some comfort without feeling crowded or chaotic. The showers and restrooms were clean, check-in and check-out were easy, and everything felt well maintained.

I would definitely stay here again and would request a similar site. If you’re looking for a comfortable base near Joshua Tree with thoughtful amenities, this KOA is a solid choice.

Just camped here last night, very quickly arranged trip. This KOA offered so much for our 1 night stay in a pop up camper. The staff was very kind, gave us suggestions for where to eat in Desert Hot Springs. Site was spacious and had a sturdy wooden table and bench. We had our 3 dogs and they offered 2 different dog parks, one of which was HUGE with plenty of room for our pups to explore and they LOVED it. Doggie bags located throughout campground. Mineral pool was very relaxing (though a little cooler than I had expected). Three hot tubs, and those WERE nice and toasty. Beautiful views of the mountains. Everything was CLEAN, the restroom was spotless, the campsite had no litter. Surrounding the mineral pool were rooms set for Reading, Card Playing, Exercise, Club House, Billiards. Place felt secure, code needed for bathroom and gate entry. Mini golf which I missed out on, but actually looked nice. Store on premises with pretty much everything you can imagine. Our site was a tent site since we have a canvas pop up, but power and water were still included. I thought the rate was excellent given all the amenities. Also, your fee allows you to stay almost 24 hours. Check in is at 12 pm, check out is at 11 am. Hugely appreciate this long day stay as more hotels and resorts only allow you to check in at 3 pm, then early check out. Only drawback that applies to most of Desert Hot Springs was intense winds during the night. But this is characteristic of the low desert region. Great stay, highly recommend.

I *wanted* to love this koa so much! The pools and hot tub were huge hits, with putt putt second. Our family of 5 plus my senior father stayed in a cabin... The "loft" is only like 4 feet tall, be warned!! My father had to sleep up there with two kids - a queen bed & 2 bunk bed mattress were on the floor. Downstairs you had to go THROUGH the bathroom to get to the other room where a king bed was. Wasn't an issue until I got locked in the bedroom unable to get out! It was awful. Both my father and eldest son got really hurt by that ceiling when waking up. Disabled spouse younger child & I took the main level room if curious.

Basketball posts were up but the backboard and nets were completely removed, unplayable.

Coffee pot was broken.

Fridge was broken. Only freezer work. Maintenance was called, they switched the breaker and left. That was that. Didn't even tell us they left!

The jumping pad? Open 1-5. No. They opened it from 2-3 and took it down the second we went to use it. My kids were pissed. That was our only full day there and didn't get to utilize the accomodations. The older overweight lady in the office was so incredibly rude about it.

Gate service at night time was amazing, she was phenomenal.

I liked how low-key and quiet the area was. The lady hosting the movie night was the sweetest little thing.

Will probably revisit, but was really disappointed in this experience, especially considering the cost.

Great RV camping resort/campground. Very clean in all aspects including pool and activities areas. Staff very helpful and pleasant. The area is quiet and peaceful with all types of camping types (RV hookups, tent, Eco Tents, and deluxe rental cabins). With all the activities in Palm Springs just 15 minutes away you can't go wrong.

The men’s restroom by the pool has 2 showers. The hot water faucet handle is broken on both of them. There is an outdoor shower by the pool so I thought I would use that. The faucet handle came off in my hand. I stuck it back on and managed to turn on the water but it was cold water only. The men’s restroom at the other end of the park has 2 working showers but no toilet paper. The wifi is useless unless you’re in the common area near the pool. On the plus side, the staff is very friendly and the pool and jacuzzi are really nice.

name: Marion Mountain Campground
formatted_address: 52745 Forest Rte 4S02, Idyllwild-Pine Cove, CA 92549, USA

google_reviews.text: Pretty beautiful spot with spacious campsites. We were at site 20 which I highly recommend because it is far away from other campers but you do sacrifice the sunset view which you can get from the campsites on the other side. Also the campground has access to multiple trails as well. All in all a good place to camp especially if you go on a weekday.

Started here for our hike up to San Jacinto Peak. Absolutely gorgeous trail. One of my favorites of the SoCal Six Pack of Peaks Challenge. Lots of greener you wouldn't expect to see in summer in Southern California. This route was about 14 miles out and back with over 4500 of elevation gain. The view from the top is amazing! A 360 view of the desert, mountains, and ocean, even Catalina on a clear day.

Clean campgrounds, nice hiking spots, and pretty cheap sites. When you book your site make sure you check that campfires are allowed. I went in August of 2020 and they had to close firepits down due to fire season. Just double check before booking. The campsite is great for tent camping and hiking.
What a GORGEOUS view this campground offers. It is pretty cold this time of year so bring your heating system if you're planning to stay overnight. ❄️

Nice little getaway spot!
Clean campground with restrooms, awesome views and nearby hiking trails. Close to Idyllwild town and many other attractions within the area.

name: Thousand Trails Idyllwild
formatted_address: 24400 Canyon Trail, Idyllwild-Pine Cove, CA 92549, USA

google_reviews.text: Cool privately owned campground. We tent camped with the Boy Scouts. I was very impressed by the clean and heated bathroom facilities along with the rec room. Only knock is that the tent campsites are not particularly flat so it feels like you’re sleeping on an incline.

This is actually one of my faves. If the roads hadn’t been so washed out and sketchy it would get 5 stars. The recent storms really messed them up. The bathrooms were tired but the water and heater were hot. The area is beautiful and some sites are better than others. The vibe was quiet and serene. The laundromats had a nice folding table. I would go back.

This park is beautiful. Having said that if you have anything bigger than 25 feet you are in for a rude awakening. The road coming in is very tight and the park is just as tight. There needs to be a large amount of road and site work done to make the park more accessible. We have a 29 foot fifth wheel and did damage the truck backing in due to incline of the site. I have to say if I still owned my tent trailer that I had 25 years ago this would be my favorite park.

My family and I went over the weekend and stayed at this campground. The staff is super friendly and the rooms were clean. We will definitely be booking another day here soon. Thank you so much to all the staff for making us food at home we truly appreciate it. 🙏🏼

Beautiful spot up in the San Jacinto Mountains with a nice little fishing spot on the campground. Cool place and we live locally and still try and come every so often. We stayed in the cabins because we’re doing some renovations on our RV this year and they’re pretty nice. Kinda small but has everything that you could need while out camping.

name: Stone Creek Yellow Post Sites
formatted_address: 52973 Fernland Dr, Idyllwild-Pine Cove, CA 92549, USA

google_reviews.text:
Stayed at yellow post 2, great campsite. Made the mistake of going on a green sticker day, so the trail was packed with side by sides blaring music. Campsite was nice and quiet after dark, though.

Update- a lot more trash around this time. Will bring extra trash bags to clean up a bit. Please don't litter, people!
Site #2. 2WD had no issues getting here. Service was spotty. Site was clean
Site 1 Nice camping spot, only issue is people not taking care of it.

Cell service wasn't there , there were 2 sites to camp at , perfect spot to stop and rest when going up or down the trail. If hunting the forest area off the trail perfect place to stop and process your game.
Great place if you want to pan for gold but I'm not where I found it but it's close to the yellow posts if you are going to these yellow post 4 wheel drive

name: Thomas Mountain Campground
formatted_address: Mountain Center, CA 92561, USA

google_reviews.text: This place has turned into one of my favorite campsites. A little rough going in but doable. The pyramid peak rd route is by far the preferred route. Once you arrive it’s all worth it. Peace, quiet and beauty all around. It’s the best medicine for body, heart and soul.
Great campsites. I would recommend 4x4, but a 2WD SUV or Truck with decent clearance can get by if you take it slow and it’s dry. Very secluded. Love the peacefulness and the views.
Great camping spots with amazing views . Peaceful and beautiful.  The road is washed out in some spots from all the rain .

Be prepared for a LONG bumpy scary rutted rocks boulders dirt sand cliffs steep hillsides, nasty little bugs, misquitos,  beautiful skies day and night, big valley views, indian reservation territory with locked gates, the destruction of previous forest fires, amazing steep mtn sides with giant tall trees, sticker bushes , sunrises,sunsets, and dust! Great jeep road, no low chastity vehicles, no bald tires, need good shocks, sunglasses, binoculars, good head lamps, and  you will be in isolation....from one side of the road entrance by Lake Hemet, to the other side of the ridge near Paradise Valley, it will take 5 hours . Do not drive  FAST. Horses, hikers, bicyclists, and general lost wanderers use the road to the Thomas Mtn campgrounds as well. Not for the impatient or l weak hearted souls.
It’s the woods. Dry and high. Definitely want something a little more rugged to get up to the camping site. Good dirt biking for small bikes and short rides.

name: Round Valley Campground
formatted_address: Palm Springs, CA 92262, USA

google_reviews.text: Very peaceful place.  Campsites are very far apart.  It is a tough, up and down, two mile hike to get here from the tram.  Water is available from a spring that is not always guaranteed to be flowing.  You are up high at 9000 feet and storms can be sudden during the monsoon season.  Pit toilets are nice but spread far apart.  Make sure to speak to the park ranger and get a permit before venturing into the campsite. Bears are not an issue on this side of the mountain.

This camp ground gives you a nice little view of the meadow that is surrounded by towering trees, giving the great feel of truly being in the deep woods. Water source and toilets are also not too far from the camp ground. If you stand in the middle of the meadow, you can see the top of the peak. The spring time is beautiful and better for newer hikers as the winter and snow can add an aspect of difficulty with making sure you stay on the trail and having the right gear to stay warm.

We camped on Oct 18 permit required both for camp and stove, nice hike in and experience, there were other hikers and campers around that was nice, note theres only one water faucet around at the entry. A bit hard to find the campsites, and it got freezing cold at night.

Camped here over the weekend as a part of a training hike for a Philmont trek. It is a hard hike with just about 4,000 ft of elevation gain. The campsites are not really conducive to large group camping as they terrain is very rough,  but we did find a couple close together that worked for our group of 17 backpackers.

Overall great!
However, had a difficult time understanding where to set camp.  According to instructions it should be 25ft within the wooden post (See picture attached)
However it was not clear any indications if where I set camp was correct.

For hikers looking to camp here, if you do please post a review and a picture of where you camped to facilitate the location of the campsites.  I did C2C and it was hard to focus to find a spot.

name: Little Round Valley Campground
formatted_address: California, USA

google_reviews.text: Gorgeous campground.  Plenty of ice cold flowing water for filtering as of our visit in June. OMG the flies in the out house! 😳. Best in the early morning.
Started at Deer Springs Trailhead. Camped overnight here. Campsites adequate. Running water along the way and here in the meadow (10-2024). Outhouses beat digging a hole.

Fantastic Sites, stayed at Thunder View...spectacular sunset.  Primitive camp, pack it in, pack it out...no water (9-2016)...toilet is not worth entering.
Spring water is available on Marion entry trails, but will take some work to pool and filter.  Idyllwild side is dry.
Primitive campground, very clean sites. Gorgeous place to stay overnight. Water creek is still running 0.5mile down from Little round valley (Marion trail) .
Many campgrounds which are clean and flat. No water nearby though. The hike up to the peak is definitely challenging but worth it.

name: Butler-Abrams Trail
formatted_address: Butler-Abrams Trail, Rancho Mirage, CA 92270, USA

google_reviews.text:
Loved it ! Decent distance, water at the park for the puppy. Respectable dog owners & friendly walkers.
A little spot to visit some goats and a beautiful scenic view with just enough shade along the way.

No people potties ( only negative )

Lovely, wide, paved trail that’s a little over a mile long (one direction).  Perfect for a walk with a dog (in my case an old dog in a stroller).  Beautiful views of the golf course with the mountains behind it.  There’s a park at one end with a few parking spots. The other end of the trail is a gravel lot with more parking options if the park is full.

The park where the trail begins is really nice. First part of the walk is not very shaded, but there is shade later once you cross the wash.

Nice to have a water fountain for dogs at the beginning of the trail but wish there were more water fountains along the way

Love this hidden gem in greater Palm Springs.  A nice easy walking trail, gorgeous vistas of the mountains, peek at the golfers on the range, and friendly folk taking strolls or walking their dogs.  Plus Wolfson Park on one end of the trail is a treasure.  The only elevation change is a small 30 foot dip into and out of the Wash, but while you’re down there is oddly where you get the best views.
The views are amazing. And it's so clean and quiet. Just a lovely trail. I felt sad when it came to an end.

name: Maze Loop Trailhead
formatted_address: 64227-64237 Park Blvd, Joshua Tree, CA 92252, USA

google_reviews.text: Came here a few weeks ago with my SO and we enjoyed this quick hike! It really does almost feel like a maze where you can kind of walk up to the side and see that there’s so much more land and it all kind of looks like it mazes around. The trail is well marked so you won’t get lost just follow the trail. This a good place to check out some of the rock formations and the textures of the rocks. Some of the plant life is interesting there and looks beautiful. Not much of a destination hike but a good one if you want to do a nice moderate length hike.

I came here on a photography retreat and was blown away by the beauty and peacefulness of this place. I will definitely be bringing my kids back to experience it as well. Sunrise is just gorgeous and sunset just as beautiful. Definitely go and take in all aspects of this area!

Great recommendation from the park ranger at the visitor center. We did a 6 mile loop. We stopped multiple times to appreciate the stunning views.  We could have added more miles but wanted to see more of the park.  Trail easy to moderate but that seems so subjective.
Great trail for a run or hike, not too many people

Great little hike. Lots of shrubbery and cacti and rolling hills. It's quite exposed so be sure to wear sunblock and bring lots of water if you're a leisurely hiker. Very flat trail, but is around 5 mi/8 km so come prepared for 2-3 hrs of hiking, depending on physical fitness level.

name: Will Abell Memorial Trail at Arrowhead Ridge
formatted_address: Lake Arrowhead, CA 92352, USA

google_reviews.text: Beautifully built trail! Lots of filtered shady patches, so very pleasant even in the heat of the day. Just the right amount of a workout to feel like you did something good before lunch! Clear signage, with cute lizards, chipmunks, and jays. Good shoes recommended - couple steep spots on the descent. Can't wait to do this one again!
Easy hike plenty of shade and parking.  Stay on the trail or you end up taking a wrong turn and end up going back around again like I did.

This is a moderately easy trail through the forest in the San Bernardino Mountains off Grass Valley Road west of Lake Arrowhead. The parking area is good and the trail is well maintained. The switchback part of the trail is somewhat steep with loose gravel, so I highly recommend good hiking shoes to prevent slipping and falling. At the top of the trail are some good views of Lake Arrowhead through the trees. All in all, a very pleasant two mile hike.

Easy light walk, enjoyable and shaded.  We did it with our 5 month old- who slept throughout.  Links to other trails but so easy to follow.  Didn’t bother with water because you’re never far from the car.  Lots of car park space on weekdays.

This was a very pleasant trail. Long enough to work up a sweat but short enough to complete in about an hour. I recommend using the AllTrail App to have an idea of how the elevation gains during the hike to understand how difficult it might be.

I saw plenty of leashed dogs on the trail along with families with small children - gives you an idea of the difficulty level and what you can expect crowd wise.

We arrived around 10 am on a Sunday, and by the time we were wrapping up, we saw the number of people in the parking lot triple in size, so that tells you it gets a bit crowded.

Definitely recommend this hike - take time to enjoy the Viewpoint.

Remember to pick up after yourself, and if not an inconvenience, take the time to pick up after others.

name: San Timoteo Nature Sanctuary
formatted_address: Carriage Trail, Redlands, CA 92373, USA

google_reviews.text: Nice easy walk in the nature sanctuary. The walking path is well maintained, with some shaded areas with benches for resting.
my favorite trail now super easy beautiful walk if your lucky there's what feels like a hidden river on your left hand side DOG friendly which is awesome.

Nice nature preserve, the restoration of native plant communities has just begun so expect to see lots of invasive species the further you go in to the middle of the park.

The trails are pretty flat with little to no elevation change. Most of the trails you'll be under direct sunlight, with little shade.

No dedicated restroom facilities. Parking lot had  broken car glass in a few spots. Use common sense when parking, do not leave items in plain view inside your vehicle.

Very lovely for a trail walk or run! Many dogs were on leash and you can ride your horse here as well. There’s streams of water and nice flat ground. At the bugging of the trail theres first aid kit and doggy bags. Highly recommend you come visit.

I've been on this trail three times already and did both the Carriage Trail and the Cocomaricopa Trail. The northern entrance has a small parking and you're greeted with a cute pond, gazebo with a picnic table. The south entrance, you also have small parking, it have a bench with a shade and bulletin board with updates on the trail. This area gives off a good vibe, relaxing, really scenic walks and an easy hike.

name: Heaps Peak Arboretum Day Use Area
formatted_address: 29358 Rim of the World Hwy #18, Skyforest, CA 92385, USA

google_reviews.text: Beautiful nature walk. Short enough to do it with young kids and dogs on leash. Well signposted. There was still snow on the trail when we visited and it was melting so bring waterproof hiking shoes for the puddles. Plenty of parking for $5 fee you pay online. There’s a water fountain at the start of the trail but it wasn’t working when we arrived.

A really relaxing, short loop trail just off the Rim of the World Highway, slightly east of Lake Arrowhead. Parking is $5 but I find that they rarely check for this. It is especially beautiful during spring and summer, but we stopped in during late February. The trail itself takes about 30 minutes to get through and has 25 different viewpoints that are laid out on the map that you get at the front. The main highlights you will see are different types of trees, nesting boxes, small bridges, and just really tranquil, beautiful scenery, but my favorite was the giant sequoia trees. I would say that the sequoias are the highlight of the trail and were planted in the 1930s. It’s a really peaceful walk where animals are allowed and we also saw many people jogging so it really is a multi use area. I would recommend to anyone who enjoys a scenic stroll through the forest.

The hubs and I took our pups here on Labor Day.. We arrived around 8am and did the whole hike and it took about an hour with a couple of stops to just enjoy the scenery. This place is BEAUTIFUL! The trees are massive and majestic! The different smells in the air from the trees was delightful!  There's a donation box which I highly encourage people to donate, it doesn't have to be a lot but we all know even a little bit helps out. We will definitely be going back, for sure in the spring when all the flowers are blooming. Definitely a great experience! Nature is just so stunning!
Nice and easy well groomed trail. Signs are posted with a pamplet for a self guided tour.  Must pay $5 to park. There are places to sit a long the trail to relax and enjoy the view and fresh air.

Smell the (Jeffrey Pine) trees! They smell of vanilla, the cedar incense smell has nothing on the Jeffrey Pine.

What a fantastic place! The loop trail is about a mile with guided pamphlets you can take to learn more about the plants/trees around you.
Located just off the highway with plenty of parking and it is very wheelchair accessible. There are public restrooms but I suggest trying to go before you reach this destination, they aren't maintained very well.

We only found 2 pieces of trash along the looped hike, it was so refreshing to be away from people's garbage. The amount of plants/trees crammed into one place... it is amazing. Some things are currently mislabeled but if you know your botany, you'll spot the right plants. I suggest using Google Lens to identify plants more accurately, plus it makes it fun!

name: Deep Creek Hot Springs Trailhead
formatted_address: 5900 Bowen Ranch Rd, Apple Valley, CA 92308, USA

google_reviews.text: 6 mile drive down a dirt road and keep left where the road splits to the original Bown Ranch to pay $5 per person to park and access the trail. The trail is 2 miles to the hot spring with a mostly downward decent. There are elevated and narrow ridges along the way with beautiful views. There are at least 4 hot springs, but you do have to cross cold water to get to them. The hike back is 2 miles with a steep incline, but not bad for an avid hiker. There are signs that the trail is nude so people are hiking and swimming without clothes. Please do not leave your trash!

The trail is very challenging, so if you aren't physically capable I would strongly advice not to attempt it. You are hiking very high up for about an hour to 2 one way depending on how fast you go. While the trail itself is extremely narrow, only the width of one person while being on the very edge of the mountain the entire time without any guard rails or safety. Be extremely careful as there are many loose rocks when going downwards, and you definitely don't want any mishaps...We were told a rescue mission is going to cost each person 3 grand. The signs aren't clear at all, just some painted arrows here and there. I can see many people would get confused where to go.

There are two campgrounds, right next to each other. The actual original Bowen ranch campground is pretty much wild camping area, gravel road leading to parking spots picnic tables and rugged rock fire pits. There are approximately 6 spaces.  Camp sites  are good for self contained, self sufficient vehicles like camper vans. The fee is $10 per person per day. The person in charge is Mike Costa, met him in person and we had a very good welcoming by him(Dont believe everything you read online about people) wait until you can verify for yourself. There is  negative review on Mr. Costa many years ago that I find unsubstantiated. My interactions with him were very pleasant. While I did not visit the camp nexdoor, “Deep Creek”, I met someone who did and he stated there are a few more amenities, like a shower and bathrooms for $15 per person per night. It definitely get more visitors and perhaps more adequate for tent camping.
The hike to the hot springs is approximately 2.5 miles, easy down hiking, much harder hiking up, approximately 1k feet of elevation. This hike is not for everyone,carefully check weather conditions ,plan accordingly, bring food, electrolytes and water, especially for the hike back. Overall is a great experience.

Challenging (but pleasant) hike for beginners, easy hike for advanced. The views are beautiful and the trail is decently defined. Getting started can be tricky depending on which parking lot you use. I recommend downloading an offline map before going. You HAVE to cross a very cold river right before the springs (

Great hot springs hike for an  outdoor adventure. Moderate to difficult hike. I would say difficult because you have to swim an ice cold river. Also, I suggest you turn off on the first left before the sign. Admission is half the price but it is rugged. The second entrance that is labeled does offer clamping though.

name: San Gorgonio Wilderness
formatted_address: Angelus Oaks, San Bernardino Peak Divide Trail, Angelus Oaks, CA 92305, USA

google_reviews.text: Challenging hike for a Midwestern flatlander like myself. Every turn offered amazing views. Shaded areas had bug activity, but fortunately, no mosquitoes. Several water sources and campsites were along the way. The night sky view was stunning. For day-hiking, I suggest starting early, dressing in layers, and wearing a sun hoodie for exposed areas. Bring 3 liters of water, trekking poles, and water filtration. The only advice I have is to avoid hiking down 4 hours before sunset. There’s not enough daylight to make it back down safely. 5-6 hours before sunset is ideal.
San Gorgonio it’s tough going up to the summit. I did it under heavy snow storm on 12/30/23 A challenging  mount with amazing views. It took me around 9 hours to complete out and back.

This was an amazing place to hike.  It’s a Pine forest for much of the trail (I took San Bernardino Peak Trail), so you are shaded from the sun most of the time, even at the top.  The trail is 8 miles one-way, and where it’s not shaded it’s a manzanita grove.  There are at least 2 campgrounds along the way to the peak, so take advantage of that if you can’t do 16 miles in one day.  As of now, November 2019, there is no longer a requirement to apply for and get a day hiking permit.  I applied for one anyway and got a response back that it’s not necessary.  I did not encounter many bugs on my hike.
The trails were in good condition.  Incredibly, I made to the summit!  11,503 feet! 💪🏕️🥾🎒⛰️
So far I have hiked up to San Bernardino Peak and San Gorgonio Peak, they both are tough peaks to hike up, but the views are stunning.

name: Bogart Park Ridge Trail
formatted_address: Cherry Valley, CA 92223, USA

google_reviews.text: I just moved to the area and decided to do a hike. This is a simple and easy hike and I'm so happy it was because this is my first hike in a very long time.

Good park ,
Hiking...bbq or just to relax...plenty of parking too
Love this park. It has easy trails for beginners like me….and more challenging ones too. There’s always plenty of parking, never been there on a holiday so I don’t know how pack it gets.
Super fun little hike. From the parking lot to the top of the hill and back is probably about 3 miles but 2 of my 3 kids made it up.
A little rugged after the recent " "Hurricane Hilary" storm for horse ride

name: Arrastre Falls Trail Head
formatted_address: Apple Valley, CA 92308, USA

google_reviews.text: Beautiful and easy little hike, bring the kids for this little adventure on the Desert side of the San Bernardino Mountains, there was a little water flowing, most was frozen as it was in January on my visit, after they start getting some rain in late February it should be a nice waterfall.

The falls are dry at the moment but still worth the trek out there. Lots of trash so I decided to clean up the area while I was out. Take your stuff with you people. It's not that hard. Don't ruin what little we have left out here. Also don't try this in your altima. You won't make it.
It was a lovely hidden gem in the desert.  Often, go there to clean up the area.
Gate is closed and locked on milpas right before power line road.   Could not find an alternate way in as all roads around were labeled private property entrances by the local residents.

Leave only footprints and don’t forget your camera and a trash bag that’s empty when you visit and fill it up when you leave while enjoying the natural beauty on our public lands. It’s a gem in the desert help protect this treasure for those who follow. Kid friendly

name: Oakmont Park

google_reviews.text: What a great little park. Well there isn't a ton of parking in the park, there is plenty street parking. Lovely views everything was super clean and obviously well maintained. I'll definitely be back when I want to go for a quick walk and I'm in the area.
Beautiful spot to hike and bike. The trails aren't muddy after rain.

Recommend using All Trails App.

If you go pick up after yourself.

The park is a great gateway to a system of trails that you can use for running hiking or bike riding. At the entrance of the trail they even provide you with a bike bell for you to use while you use the trails. The park offers some parking for your vehicle it has picnic tables and trees. The trails are mostly exposed so bring plenty of water and sunblock if you are going for a long walk. You can bring your furry friends on the trail as well.

Great Trail connected nearby to this sleepy park. Very little trash, light traffic on rainy day, a full meadow, beautiful wash with excellent stones nearby. Snake shed pressed against a wall, as it warms up nature comes to life off these great trails.

Not a lot of shade, best times to go are 60-80⁰
10/10 Would woof again.

name: Arrastre Falls Kiosk
formatted_address: Apple Valley, CA 92308, USA

google_reviews.text:
We managed to find our way here just following the off-road trail on a lazy Sunday drive. The view from this place is amazing.

The falls are extremely close to the trail entrance, no more than a hundred feet or so. And the "hike" wasn't very difficult, more like a steep hill.

We didn't explore much farther up the path, as we were planning on exploring more of the off-road trail.
Never knew this place was here. We drove our razors up there. The waterfall was dry but still very nice.
The kiosk had maps of the area and a scavenger hunt paper. The falls were sad more of a trickle.

A very short hike to the tiny waterfall area of Arrastre Falls. It is a cool little ravine area in the hills with some trees. I went in early Winter so it was dry even after a light rainfall. Based on other photos shared even when there is water it is only a small trickle down the canyon and nothing to amazing. The off-road trail to get here though is fun and worth it I think.
Had a great time hiking the area

name: Cold Creek Falls
formatted_address: Angelus Oaks, CA 92305, USA

google_reviews.text: This is a small water fall on a narrow winding dirt road with a enough room for basically 1 vehicle at a time with some areas to pull to side and pass. No real parking. No hiking involved to see this.

Beautiful after a heavy rain. Not far from Hwy 38. If coming from Middle Control Rd or Mill Creek at 38 in a vehicle. Road gets more challenging as you head towards 7 Oaks Resort. Especially after some heavy weather.

Road was closed at the top of the hill so we hiked the half a mile down to see the falls and then hiked back up. Decent incline climb on the way up. Road would have been possible with a Jeep or side by side easily.
Tranquil roadside waterfalls.

name: Yucaipa Regional Park
formatted_address: 33900 Oak Glen Rd, Yucaipa, CA 92399, USA

google_reviews.text: This is clean park that has a very neighborly vibe.  The sites weren't too close together.  We were in site 20 and it was just perfect.

Had never driven through this park before after wanting to for many years. $10 entry fee gets you lots to see and do for the day.  RV Camping: overnight area was ok but not too convenient to any of the lake areas.  Tent Camping: $100 gets you a large camping site for 20+ tents. Too bad there aren't any smaller individual campng areas right next to a few of the fishing lake spots. Overall a great park to spend the entire day relaxing or family outings.  Water slides looked fun. Will definitely come back when I have the whole day to stay. Just did a "drive thru" to check it out and on one of my stops around the lake had a bald eagle fly right over my head.

Yucaipa Regional Park truly lives up to its "huge" reputation, offering a vast expanse for a variety of outdoor activities. The park provides ample opportunities for recreation, from numerous ponds and walking trails to a swimming pool with water activities. You can even enjoy paddle boating for an additional $12 per hour. For those who enjoy angling, fishing is also an option, though it comes with an extra fee.

Entry to the park is $15 per car, with the same fee applying if you wish to use the swimming pool. While the park boasts many public restrooms, their cleanliness leaves much to be desired, which is a significant drawback. On a brighter note, there are well-equipped picnic areas with BBQ facilities, making it an excellent spot for a family outing.

The park offers beautiful scenic views, enhanced by several large pine trees that add to its natural charm. You'll find plenty of free parking, and the overall atmosphere is quite and peaceful, making it a relaxing escape. It's also a pet-friendly environment, so your furry friends can join in the fun. For families with children, the park offers kid-friendly activities, including soccer playing areas and dedicated playgrounds.

Yucaipa Regional Park is a good choice for those seeking a spacious outdoor park with a wide range of activities. Its size, scenic beauty, and family-friendly amenities are definite highlights.

However, the cleanliness of the restrooms is an area that significantly detracts from the overall experience and could be improved.

Beautiful place ! Absolutely beautiful, you can ride your bike, walk around.  Is a safety place , awesome atmosphere, nice parks for our kids , lakes , trees 🌲!! Just visit this place , your family, kids and pets are going to love it 🥰

And perhaps maybe stocking the pond with actual fish might be smart sometime soon since there were others fishing there that spent money on their activity and came to this location for recreational fun camping and fishing, and they seemed quite disappointed as was I.

Located downtown is this great little park in the heart of the city.  It’s very small but it is a nice place to take a break after window shopping downtown State Street or to have a bite under the sun. This park is used for many great activities.  During Thursdays Market Night you can always find great entertainment from a local band, or occasionally see a local school perform on the weekend. During the holidays, it is the center of excitement hosting the tree lighting ceremony as well as the New Year’s Eve Orange Drop.  I highly recommend checking out this little park when looking for a nice place to relax or have a fun activity.

The bathrooms are never locked, the place is kept in decent shape and fairly clean, and it's in general been a great small respite close to home!

Original review: I'm a really big fan of this little park. The shade over the playground is top notch stuff that I wish all playgrounds had.

I also love that the playground is down on a hill away from the street. Just feels so nice and there are plenty of families that come too. Haven't had any issues whatsoever, people have been very considerate and friendly. So many good things to say but please go visit and enjoy the park with your family.

It's disgusting that dog owners are ruining places designed for children and families. And then people wonder why kids don't play outside anymore. It's probably because dog owners and homeless people have made it completely unsafe for them.

Brookside Park is a great family park to walk around on its cement path that rounds the perimeter of the entire park. There are numerous benches and trash cans for your convenience, as well as restrooms 🚻. There are also a few barbecue pits in the front area of the park on the west and east sides. There is a big playground that has canopy coverage and a huge grass area perfect for picnics and dogs. One last thing to note is that their is a soccer field with lights and a seating area.

Brookside Park is such a nice, open spot with tons of green space to just hang out and enjoy the day. There’s a great playground for the kids and plenty of picnic areas too. Perfect place to relax, play, or meet up with friends for a little outdoor time.
