We camped at site E3 at Holcomb Valley Ranch and it was such a memorable experience. The ranch itself is incredibly peaceful, with beautiful views & surrounded by towering pines, open meadows and wide-open skies that make for amazing stargazing at night.
We had a bit of an adventure when we first arrived late in the night—our Tesla Model 3 got stuck on the way in. To our surprise and gratitude, few of the folks from the ranch along with camp host Ed came over to help pull us out, even though one of them had his daughter’s wedding to attend within the hour. That kind of generosity and helpfulness is rare and we are so grateful and blessed to encounter this.
The campsite at E3 is spacious and quiet, a great spot if you want a mix of privacy and views. That said, it’s important to note that it’s not suitable for low-clearance vehicles—you’ll definitely have a smoother time getting in with something higher off the ground.
The facilities were mostly fine, but the flush toilets were unfortunately clogged during our stay and could use some maintenance or upgrades. It wasn’t a dealbreaker, but it stood out compared to the otherwise well-kept surroundings.

All in all, Holcomb Valley Ranch is a gem—stunning scenery, a serene atmosphere, and people who truly go above and beyond. We’d happily return again (next time with a higher-clearance car!).

I am not one to write bad reviews - I know everyone has a bad day or certain things happen. However, what was supposed to be a peaceful getaway in the forest because a terrible experience.

So, I received a call on the way up the hill from HVR staff. I was told there are NO fires permitted due to safety reasons; after all there was just a big forest fire (the Gold Fire) that had burned nearby. I was told that charcoal or propane fires are acceptable. I was also told that there was a huge wedding this weekend (first I had heard about this, else I would not have booked a site here, but more on that, later...). He said I will indeed see people with fires at various sites (which we did), and those are OK (for them, not me or anyone not in the wedding party), because they had booked this a long time ago under some contract. I mentioned I was really surprised to hear this about the fire situation at the campground, and it was disappointing - I was also told during this call that I had to have been told earlier in one of the communications about the fire situation. I was not told anything.

Who wants to go camping without a campfire?? Answer = no one.

1. If a very serious environmental/safety condition exists due to dry/hot conditions (as demonstrated by the very recent fire very nearby), how does a contract void that? It's UNSAFE! Well, for us, but not for them 100 ft over there at that camp site...what? It is at a bare minimum inconsistent and unfair - and it makes absolutely no sense. I would have been much less unhappy, but at least would have understood, would the rules have been applied evenly for all guests.

2. Why is a charcoal or propane fire OK when wood is not? Another inconsistency. If it's safe enough to use charcoal to cook, then how does a piece of wood in my fire change that (see #3 with camp host threats below)??

3. The camp host came rolling up on us in his truck and gave us a hard time, threatening us with big fines repeatedly. We had made a charcoal fire so we could cook our dinner. I had added *ONE* piece of hickory - to add flavor. We were trying to stay within their (ridiculous and unevenly applied) rules. It turns out one of the kids apparently threw a pinecone into the fire, and we did not realize it until this guy showed up and started reading us the business. He threatened us over and over with a $500 fine. Finally, said 'he'd let it go - this time only'. Very relaxing stay? Nope.

The wedding was obnoxiously loud. Lots of shouting and loud partying. If I had known this it would not be a problem - it would have been expected. They had a loud band and/or DJ that went from the afternoon until 10pm. So much for peace and quiet and 'getting away from it all' on our one night there. I guess if someone pays enough money, they can do anything they want, violating the sites' own rules on fire, safety and noise.

I would not have said a word about this since it's (sadly) common at any camp site. But after all I just mentioned above, it should be said that the site was dirty with lots of small pieces of trash - wrappers, bottle caps, plastic water bottle caps, shiny glittery stuff, pieces of metal - all throughout the site and forest nearby. I left with a half of bag full of trash because I just could not stand to see it. I was a bit surprised at this; for a place that 'so deeply cares about the forest' (according to their email), it seems they'd keep the place neater and more 'natural' since that's what they're apparently selling.

I, nor my friend I stayed with, nor anyone else we know will be staying here again.

My husband and I just spent the weekend here, and it was beautiful. The ranch is very well taken care of, there’s clean port-a-potties at all the camp sites. We rented a one acre plot and felt like we had the ranch to ourselves! It’s close to the lake and downtown. Would definitely come here again

name: Big Pine Flat Family Campground
formatted_address: Coxey Rd, Apple Valley, CA 92308, USA

google_reviews.text: This is a secluded campground located northeast of Big Bear lake. It takes roughly 30 minutes on a winding dirt road. Lots of off-road vehicles and ATVs here.

Nice campground. We came in late at night so we stole the camp host spot. He came to collect fees in the morning and was totally understanding and super helpful explaining where the other campgrounds and yellow post sites were. Unfortunately they were out of water when we went but everything was clean and well maintained. Will definitely be back!

Guy running the camp ground is rude as heck. Literally was interrupted right in the middle of eating lunch by the guy just to be solicited that it was a camp ground.  He could've been nice about it. So we had to pack up our half eaten lunch and picnic table into our trucks and drive across the street to the visiting area.

Nice spot to camp with OHVs. Getting a trailer/RV here is a lot harder than the maps would have you believe. It’s 45-60 minutes of dirt road travel once you leave Fawnskin.

Any high clearance car/suv would be much easier but there’s plenty of areas where passing isn’t possible so meeting traffic could mean someone backing up to a clearing.

Check website for annual season opening dates.

Great for OHV Camping. 4 stars due to no natural water sources like creeks, lakes etc nearby. Campsites are small, but it has running water and outhouses. 33' is the max trailer length. Weather is always better than surrounding areas no matter the season. No cell service, nearest is 2 miles away or on a ridge. Fire rings and picnic tables present. Shooting range a half mile away, but closes 1 June to 1 November I think.

name: Yucaipa Regional Park RV Campground
formatted_address: 34772 Oak Glen Rd Trail, Yucaipa, CA 92399, USA

google_reviews.text: Fantastic park filled with amenities: water slides, disc golf, hiking trails, 3 fishing ponds and great weather year around. Local population of bald eagles regularly visit, along with all other types of wildlife. Campground is clean and well monitored.

Wow pretty cool spot, pool area we got there at 7:30am to be 3rd in line. Opens at 10-4pm. Definitely do the water slide pass .

Camping with our RV was great. There is no fire pit unfortunately just a BBQ that we used as one. Fishing was cool and my son caught his first fish. We definitely will be going back and most likely gonna rent a RV there.

Very pleasant and fun sight.  Stayed in a group site that held 3 rigs very well. Park was very clean with a good amount of facilities.  Besides your camping fees, you are going to be charged fishing fees and water park fees of $12 a person per day if you use them. That was disappointing and added over $100 to or stay. The fish cleaning facilities need repair.

The fish that they stocked were about a pound a piece. I was told they do a supplemental stock of larger fish every once in a while.

All in all, for camping and being located where it is, this was a nice spot. I wouldn't go just for the fishing but will definitely be back for the overall experience.

This RV park is a peaceful and well-maintained spot with beautiful scenery. There's quite a few spacious sites that make it easy to relax.

The park offers plenty of outdoor activities like trailing and fishing. It can get a little crowded on weekends so take note of that!
Large pull through RV campsites. Proper hookups. Nice hiking trails, good fishing spots though we didn't catch anything

name: SkyPark Camp + RV Resort
formatted_address: 29297 CA-18 BOX 369, Skyforest, CA 92385, USA

google_reviews.text: Thai is one of the best RV parks I’ve been to. Very well maintained, clean and well landscaped. The views are stunning. Their toilets and showers are spotless. The whole place is a gem, cannot wait to be back. No pool or other amenities on site but there is so much to do in the area as it’s very close to Arrowhead.

Great campground—centrally located, close to the village, and just a short hop from some solid hiking trails. Bathrooms were clean (a rare camping miracle), and the showers were warm and had great pressure—always a win after a day outdoors.

The sites have concrete pads, though some are more level than others—just part of the camping charm. The valley view was beautiful, and at night the stars really put on a show.

Every host we met was super friendly and full of great recommendations for food and libations around Lake Arrowhead. We’ll definitely be back for another SoCal mountain escape!

Stayed at one of their tent sites during late October, starting on a Saturday and departing on the following Tuesday.

Special notes about the tent sites. Very few are available in comparison to the rv sites and the further tent sites are also downhill making bathroom trips in the night harder. The tent plots themselves are NOT sloped, but just having to walk up and down those inclines can be tedious. Just something to keep in mind.

On-site trailer rentals are available but are over priced in my opinion since none of those have their own bathrooms.

Bathroom facilities are fairly clean and well maintained (considering, once again it was an off season; only two of the five or six showers at the time had hot water issues but that was it) plus the showers themselves are free AND shampoo, conditioner and body wash were all provided. No charge on those either!

Laundry facilities are provided, no ironing boards. Just two washers and two driers.  THESE DO require payment and laundry detergent and dryer sheets are not (normally) provided.

The fire pits are automatically lit up at around 6 pm daily and are a great spot to socialize and make smores.

One last warning about the tent sites at night; Jerusalem Crickets are a thing.  They're not poisonous but their bites can sting and they're huge.  About 2 or 3 inches, hard to miss even in the dark so just be sure your tents are zipped up enough that they can't fit through.

We recently stayed in the Airstream at SkyPark. The camper was clean, comfortable, and well-furnished, and the staff was super friendly and helpful.

However, I was disappointed that the website didn’t show clear photos of the outdoor space. I booked Camper 6, which turned out to have the smallest outdoor lounging/dining area — not ideal when visiting with kids who brought outdoor toys. There just wasn’t enough room.

The outdoor chairs broke when we tried to move them, and they caused splinters for my children. The propane fire pit was a nice feature but kept going out and had to be relit constantly.

Overall, the indoor experience was great, but the outdoor space and amenities could definitely use some improvement — especially with better info on the booking site.

UNBELIEVEABLE!! At the very top of the San Bernardino Mountains. And I mean, at the very top! Elevation of 5,910 ft. Nestled in the mountainside with incredible views of San Bernardino. Beautiful landscaping. Amazing firepits for all to utilize. It's simply breathtaking! All along the winding road up are places to pull over and take in the scenery. Once you get closer to the top, there are little shops and markets and restaurants to experience. I recommend this drive alone, but if you need a place to RV. I cannot recommend this place enough!

name: Shady Cove Group Campground
formatted_address: 20555 Keller Peak Rd, Big Bear, CA 92314, USA

google_reviews.text: We had a great time camping with our large group! The campsite was clean and easily accessible. The toilets were adequate for camping and there are numerous trail heads within walking distance. Keller Peak offered amazing views and was easily handled by our 5 year old daughter. Definitely recommend this site 😀

Shady cove camp ground in Big Bear is one of the places to group camp. If you are from SoCal it is just a couple hours away. Getting out there really makes you feel removed from the normal city life.
This camp ground has vaulted toilets, and a spigot with non-potable running water. There are plenty of picnic tables, and charcoal BBQs.
There are good hiking trails around and this site is about 30 minutes from Big Bear lake.
Awesome place for camping!!!

We camped at Shady Cove with our Cub Scouts over an exciting weekend.  There are three sites (A, B, and C), each with nearby parking areas and bathroom facilities.  The rock formations around camp site A are amazing!

Site C is a great one. You have your own set of vaulted toilets and a spigot (not potable). It's dusty, however, and is subject to strict fire restrictions (we weren't allowed to make a campfire!). There's a rock formation about 10 minutes away via foot.
Great set of three group campsites nestled next to the ski slippers of Snow Valley. Great place to bring a group for a weekend of hiking, mountain biking and rock climbing.

name: Oak Glen Retreat - Halo Resorts
formatted_address: 38955 Oak Glen Rd, Yucaipa, CA 92399, USA

google_reviews.text: Ryan did an excellent job helping with arrangements, answering questions about the resort.  It's located in a great area of Oak Glenn with nice sunsets.

It was a nice place to get out of the sun and enjoy a ice cold adult beverage. There was a bad playing   The food looked like bar food which isn’t a bad thing I personally didn’t have anything to eat.

Horribly maintained looks abandoned, can't even recommend for 1 night stay like we did. Potholes galore broken cement slabs looks like a ghost town amidst the junky trailers that stay there. The drive in to park in the spot was quite precarious and very uneven disaster waiting to happen. Disappointed this place is even in the RPI network what a joke.

The Oak Glen Retreat Rod Run Car Show was a terrific car show and had a a great selection of hot rods,rat rods,customs,antique,vintage,motorcycle,trucks,muscle cars and more!
Oak Glen Retreat in the beautiful Oak Glen mountains above Yucaipa and Mentone California is a great place to have a car show and to visit! Mountains,blue skies,great people,great cars and hospitality from the folks at Oak Glen Retreat made it a great family,friends,visitors and car lovers delite!
.
.
.
.
.
#oakglenretreat #oakglenretreathaloresort #cardshows #musclecars #lowriders #motorcycles #yucaipa #mentone #seetheusa #godisgood #hotrods #photografxworldwide #oakglen #family #outdoorlife #blueskies #ilovecars #familyfun #camping #rv
Nice location and nice views.  The first thing I noticed is the bear statue by the office is missing his arm.  Very much in need of some serious updating, as it looks neglected.

name: Horse Springs Campground
formatted_address: Coxey Rd, Apple Valley, CA 92308, USA

google_reviews.text: Great campsite, I was the only one there. It was under maintenance/ construction so no fees. Cell service was in and out of one bar. (Verizon) There was two pit toilets faculties both with his & hers crappers. The campground in general  was clean, but a little over grow. The road is a good if you have a high clearance vehicle.

It looks like it's currently under restoration. We were disappointed that people would trash the place.  Beer bottles, cans, and trash were in the fire pits.  You can see broken bottles and spent ammo cartridges on the outside perimeter of the campsite.  Used tissue would litter the campground .  I can only speculate this is a spot locals use frequently since it's a long way from Big Bear Lake and closer to the Lucerne Valley side. Phone reception is spotty and non existent most of the time.  There are a lot of red ant colonies around the sites and they are HUGE!!!

The potential of this place is great.  One of the few areas where we saw wildlife -2 deers to be exact.  The views are amazing of the surrounding valleys.  Just be warned this place can get WINDY.  We were fortunate that it was windy during the day but completely calm at night.

We cleaned up the fire pits that contained glass, cans, and a Starbucks cup.  We also picked up unsightly  litter on the ground and hopefully will give others a much better experience if they do come here.  This place has potential.  Let's all leave places like these better than how we found it.

This is a lightly used campground out of the way. $10/night per site, plus $5 per car (truck!) 2 max. Self service pay, but I couldn't find drop box. I don't think the forest service really cares about this site because out of way. I visited on 6/16/19. Maybe more active in earlier spring?
2 restroom structures, I didn't go in. Not sure if there is water in campground. Basically just did a drive by on way to White Mt.

A great place to camp if you want to be as isolated as possible. Definitely going to need a vehicle that can handle some light to moderate off-road conditions to get there. Very simple and primitive layout with two sets of vault toilest, fire pits and park benches.
Primitive campsites with wooden post boundaries and 2 composting toilets. Pay drop-box is self-serve, $10 /night or $5/day use. Some tables and a fire/bbq pit to use, if legally permitted.

name: Grape Ave Trail
formatted_address: Grape Ave Trail, Yucaipa, CA 92399, USA

google_reviews.text: This is a great trail. This extends for many miles, and not many other hikers are around? This is my got to for peace and quiet.

This 1.8 mile trail permits access the Crafton Hills Ridge Trail. Multiple switchbacks allow for a steady consistent climb ending over 3,200ft. This trail has heavy MTB traffic as evidenced by many bermed corners and is well maintained. Much of the trail borders a water reservoir where crane and hawk are commonly spotted. The trail is well marked, look for marker posts at all intersections and you'll find the trail easy to follow.
Nice trail. Great view of the surrounding area and IE. Two big loops to choose your own adventures.
Nice trail. Do not miss it if you live close by.
Other than the rattle snakes this is a pretty nice place to hike, not to long of a trail. Has some shade on a part of the trail.

name: Thunderbird Trail
formatted_address: Chapman Heights Rd, Yucaipa, CA 92399, USA

google_reviews.text: Trails galore for running, cycling, hiking, etc! Watch for animals like snakes!

This 1.3 mile trail is great for walking, hiking, running, mountain biking and frequent used for Yucaipa High School sports teams. Dogs should be leashed. Watch for snakes. The trail can be linked with Three Hawks for a 2.3 mile loop or access The Ridge fire road, which follows the Crafton Hills ridge in its entirety.
Great hiking trail 🥾🥾

name: Three Hawks Trail
formatted_address: Chapman Heights Rd, Yucaipa, CA 92399, USA

google_reviews.text: I thought this was a great trail. I was originally coming off the crafton hills pathway. Beautiful and well maintained. A great starter trail for a young family or a weekend 😁🤟🏽 warrior like me

Great starting point you can definitely choose your own path and make it as long as you like. Lots of friendly people but not crowded. We went early morning and as the day got later we definitely encountered more people, a few mountain bikers but all in all a great hike. I suggest downloading alltrails app and mapping out the trails so you can see what your getting into because they vary from 3 miles to 9 miles loops or just in and out.
Ride up Three hawks and down Thunderbird if you are mtb rider

This 1 mile trail is popular with walkers, runners, hikers and mountain bikes you may even see the Yucaipa Cross Country team using it for training. Dogs should be on leash.
The trail is accessible from Crafton Hills, Crafton Rd. and the Ridge access road. It can be linked with the Thunderbird trail for a 2+ mile loop.
Great little hike, awesome views and right now it is full of blooming flowers.

name: Pinnacles Trail 3W16
formatted_address: CA-173, Hesperia, CA 92345, USA

google_reviews.text: This trail rocks! I failed to reach the peak I got Lost! It's no joke people take care of each other out here. Google maps said the trail was closed and maybe for good reason the trials were somehow erased hard to find luckily fellow hikers have left small clues behind for others not to lose their way. Still I lost my way on the way down I followed what looked like a river once that flowed with water but was empty now which took me straight downward into the half way point of the trail i was suppose to be on the paths met between the 2 small mountains. The rest was easy as I remember the way back from there. Still I reccomend go with 2 or more people.
What a great place to go for a hike. If you have an adventure pass, you can park. Great trails. Make sure you bring the water.
Great difficult hike. Went early and had the trail to myself. Can see Mt. Baldy and Silverwood Lake from the top.

This is sort of a dangerous trail.  Not because of a technical difficulty or grade, but because it's uncovered and the trail is vanishing in a desert.  There are maybe 4 trail markers along the trail, each one less useful.  Two of the six of us that walked this are very experienced hikers.  We guessed the correct turns until about half way up the 'peak' at the end.  Then the trail either went straight up very steep drainage steps or under brush on something akin to a game trail. We tried both and the steep 'path' MIGHT of been it, but we didn't want to take our less experiences hikers any further. ... We cooked in the noon sun. ... This would be a moderate to easy trail (depending on fitness) if the trail was clearly marked.  The view (in the summer) was sort of a rolling desert landscape.
Beautiful hike! We took the dogs and they loved it.

name: Hidden Valley Nature Trailhead
formatted_address: 74485 National Park Dr, Twentynine Palms, CA 92277, USA

google_reviews.text:
Absolutely stunning trail! Breathtaking!!! 🪨☀️

Very very easy walk. It’s a loop at 1.8km long.

No shade on this walk. Bring a hat. Lots of water. We came in March so weather was perfect.

You can climb the boulders if you like or stick to the flat trail that is well maintained. No need for special hiking shoes. All ages can walk it!
No dogs allowed sadly. :(

Hidden Valley Nature Trail is one of the best short hikes in Joshua Tree National Park.
When I visited, it was Thanksgiving Day, and it was packed—the parking lot filled up fast. It’s one of the most famous trails in the park, so the crowds weren’t surprising, but it’s something to plan for.
That said, the trail itself is still absolutely worth it. The loop is easy, well-marked, and perfect for beginners who want a scenic walk without a tough climb. If you’re visiting Joshua Tree, this is a must-stop trail—just try to go early if you can.

Well worth the little effort for the 1 mile loop trail. There is some up and down motion when you're along the rocks, but the views were amazing! The day I went in even saw some climbers along the larger of the rocks.

We found the chasm of doom! Great “secret” hike/scramble into a cavern area. Watched a couple YouTube videos and that got us there. Did the hidden valley trail after and it was pretty; slightly different and isolated compared to the rest of the park. Plenty of bathrooms and picnic table

Awesome trail, the signage says about an hour but it’s achievable in under that. At a leisurely pace I would say that’s about right. Some parts of the trail towards the end are a bit trickier, just take your time if you struggle with stability. Popular route with families, I would recommend this if you’re short of time in the park! Picnic facilities and toilets available nearby.

name: Skull Rock Trail
formatted_address: Twentynine Palms, CA 92277, USA

google_reviews.text: Awesome little stop!  You can see Skull Rock from the road basically.  I didn’t put a pic of it.  We hiked around all the boulders it was very nice just be careful because it can be very dangerous and slippery.
Very cool. Park. Right off parking lot. Trail across street is nice too.

Great loop. About halfway through the trail there’s an option to take a short detour to see skull rock. About 2.5 miles. Very scenic, weather was perfect. Not super steep, but was uphill and had few stairs/step areas along the route. Rocky path.
I had the opportunity to visit Joshua Tree with my family and loved it. It's an experience that you won't want to miss in your lifetime.
Great and easy hike with beautiful views. The park is also a nice place for stargazing.

name: Painted Canyon Trailhead
formatted_address: Painted Canyon Rd, Mecca, CA 92254, USA

google_reviews.text: Lovely hike, the road in is rough but easily passable in a 2wd Sedan if you go slowly and carefully. Not very sandy just lots of holes and Ruts. The hike is great, we did the ladder canyon to painted canyon loop, I recommend going up ladder and down painted canyon. I took a map which helped as there are trails going off in all directions. I'll add a photo of my map for reference. My 9 & 10yr olds did it no problem. Enjoy!

This was awesome! We went up the Ladder Trail and down the Painted Canyon. Our group included kids as young as 8, some people with a fear of heights, and some big-and-tall guys. There were some anxious moments and a tight squeeze or two, but we all made it and had fun. The slot canyon was so cool! The hike after the slot canyon was more of a climb than we anticipated, but not particularly steep. Going up the ladders was definitely easier than going down-- there are a couple on the Painted Canyon trail as well-- but it was all part of a great adventure.

The road into the trailhead wasn't bad with only one small muddy spot that we used 4wd on the way in. It had dried a lot even by the time we were leaving and there was a Tesla and a Camry in the parking lot.

We showed up early (on a Saturday), so there weren't many others along the trail with us. When we left, the parking lot was overflowing. Because there are several spots where you need to wait for one person at a time to go up or down a ladder, I would think the crowds would add some stress and un-fun. It's worth finding a time without as many people.

Definitely going back to try the ropes canyon someday.

This was the best hike of our trip to Palm Desert. The GPS took us directly to the parking lot with no trouble. We had a 4x4 but didn't ever need it. There are parts of the road that are one way with a bit of off-roading to make it 2 way but most people just waited. No bathrooms so plan accordingly. The hike path is well marked and the paths are well groomed. I recommend clockwise as do most of the other reviews. You start off with a short walk and then the first ladders appear on your left. Its a fun scramble and you definitely need both hands so I would bring backpack or crossbody to keep your stuff in. You walk for about a mile through the slot canyons which is super fun and cooler temps. You start to climb and reach the ridge for stunning views over the landscape including Bear Lake Mt (with snow!) and Salton Lake and it is much warmer though with a nice breeze. After about 2 miles you start your descent and many people took a rather steep and rocky hill down vs the recommended route here. The rest of the hike is a slight downhill through a larger canyon mostly shaded with dozens of colors and styles of rocks and formations. This hike was special and definitely recommend it.

Very fun trail and amazing view. Very interesting rock formations. Best for winter hiking. Bring enough water and better to bring gloves along for better grip. We recorded 5.2mi somehow to complete the ladder canyon loop, took us 3h in total, with a lot of breaks. Walking on the wash for almost 1hour was a little boring for us, that’s the only con for us. If no small kids, I would recommend connect the ladder canyon with the rope canyon for even more fun.

6y and 8y old give it a thumbs-up.

This trail offers a mostly moderate to easy hike, but be prepared for some challenging sections involving ladders and ropes. While not overly difficult, these climbs can be steep and occur in confined spaces. The initial ladder section and the following 1-2 miles are the most strenuous, but also the most unique I have ever seen. Here, the trail transitions from narrow mountain paths to a rocky section, which can be particularly tough for beginners.  However, this climb rewards hikers with breathtaking 360-degree views of Salton Sea and the beautiful mountains!  The remainder of the hike is generally easier, though there is at least one steep descent. Avoid the hike if the weather is hot, as the only shade comes from the shadow provided by the mountains. The rocks are painted, but you have to look for them :)

name: Cap Rock
formatted_address: Cap Rock Nature Trail, Twentynine Palms, CA 92277, USA

google_reviews.text:
Cap Rock has an easy level loop trail with lovely close views of Joshua Trees and the large Cap Rock. As of September last year there is a sunset ranger walk along the loop trail which ends just as the sun crests over the mountains. I asked the ranger if Cap Rock was a good place for stargazing and he did confirm it was.

That night I was graced with a rocket launch just past sunset which was incredible for the few of us waiting for full darkness. It was breathtaking and made me a little envious of those who witnessed the US space launches of the past (and hopefully the future).

The ranger was right and the stars there were amazing! Ideal night would need low or no moon in the sky and no clouds to let the stars shine the most. There are some tutorials online for long exposure shots for photos on the phone. I did bring a small tripod so the shots were steady. The video is actually star trails showing the Earth rotating as the night goes on (about 3 hours total). Stunning to see both satellites and planes streak across the  starry night sky basically unobstructed from city lights ✨️

Cap Rock truly lives up to its name, with a massive boulder standing proudly like a boss’s cap, creating a striking and memorable scene. Nearby, you’ll find beautiful Joshua trees that make this spot perfect for photography, especially during golden hour.

At night, the sky often fills with a stunning view of the Milky Way, making it a wonderful place for stargazing. Since it’s conveniently located along the road to Keys View, it’s well worth stopping by to take a short walk, explore the area, or capture a few photos if you’re heading that way.

A must stop in Joshua Tree!!!.  There is an easy trail around the features and if your into rock climbing, here is a great place to stop.  Also a historical place where Gram Parsons frequently visited, even upon his death!!!!!

Cap Rock was absolutely incredible and a little scary in the best way. The massive rocks look like they could fall at any moment, which makes the place feel powerful and thrilling. Standing so close to them was intense and unforgettable. It’s a short stop, but truly amazing and worth seeing in person.

We arrived in time for the walk and talk with a park ranger (Emmet). It was an easy walk and got to hear about the park the Joshua tree the person who wanted to protect the park and some history as well as an explanation of the rock formations.

name: Indian Cove Nature Trail Trailhead
formatted_address: Twentynine Palms, CA 92277, USA

google_reviews.text: Really cool rocks to climb on and even through!

The scenery is very beautiful. It may not be enough for those who like the trail, but it's a great trail for beginners and kids. We were able to see rabbits, rats, lizards, caterpillars and yellow to pink flowers. If you are lucky, you can see the turtle, but due to the threat of extinction, you only need to take pictures and look at them without touching them.
Great little easy, short trail with informational signs.
It ok, first time camper and so far it's good

An easy 0.5 mile loop interpretative trail, more off the beaten path so less crowded than the rest of Joshua Tree National Park. Drive through the campground to the southwest end. The parking lot is smaller.

name: Split Rock Loop Trail
formatted_address: Twentynine Palms, CA 92277, USA

google_reviews.text:
Great hike! 2+ miles. Little up .. little down... nothing to hard. Have good grippy soled hiking shoes or boots.
Went 2-6-26. So many wildflowers!

Its a nice easy 2.5 mile loop not far off the main road with a couple of picnic tables by the trailhead.  This is a geat trail to walk or run with good views throughout. If you have kids, there are some pretty cool rocks they can climb in or under for some good pics as well.

I started a bit too late, around 4 p.m. At some point, the trail stopped feeling like a loop, and as the sun began fading and the wind picked up a cold breeze, I decided it was best to turn back. Thankfully, I had filled up my water beforehand and paused to grab my lightweight down jacket. I also use hiking poles, which I find really helpful in general—they’d definitely come in handy here for added stability.

My gut told me to turn around, and I’m glad I listened—once the sun went down, it got cold fast. I saw a few ladies heading in as I was almost out and suggested they might want to rethink it. Fortunately, they turned around about 20 minutes later, so I didn’t have to worry.

There was no GPS signal out there; otherwise, I might have kept going. I’m not a fan of hiking at dusk without a headlamp—I only brought an LED hand flashlight—I think about the animals that come out at night too. Luckily, I carry bear mace, which I’d recommend bringing too since the outdoors are unpredictable.

As for the scenery—huge rocks, beautiful desert views—I’d highly recommend this hike. I’d love to go back, just earlier in the day and when there are more people around so I don’t have to second-guess myself. I was rushing and not thinking clearly this time.
Best trail I’ve taken in Joshua tree and it’s my 5th time here! 1 h loop, and we did small shortcut at the end:)

Unique hiking route around boulders that have cracks that look like they’re filled with cement.

We did a small portion of the 2.5 miles but you can start at this trailhead and make it out to skull rock and back if you’re up for a challenge.

name: Arch Rock Nature Trail
formatted_address: Twentynine Palms, CA 92277, USA

google_reviews.text: Arch rock and Heart Rock are on the same trail within Joshua Tree National Park.  Some parking available close by. Bring water and good grippy shoes to climb rocks. Less than 1.5miles to walk trail to see main interest points,  however, best to enjoy walking around the wash, trails and climbing rocks.  Soak up the sun, get out and enjoy nature!
Absolutely WORTH checking out. Make sure to wear shoes appropriate for a small hike if you want a pic under the arch. Joshua Tree National park is my favorite
A short and easy trail, suitable for all age groups. The rock formations come in many shapes and look impressive. It is also a fun place for kids to climb and explore.
Stunning Arch Rock. It was an easy hike and the trail is well marked. Lots of beautiful rocks that I enjoyed scrambling through them.

Arch Rock Trail feels like someone modded the desert with a sci-fi texture pack. Boulders stacked like alien playgrounds, curves and shadows that look hand-sculpted by bored space gods. It’s short, surreal and full of views that don’t look real.

You wander through and think “yep, Earth glitched.”
Amazing trail. Unreal vibe. Recommended for anyone who enjoys feeling like they accidentally stepped onto another planet.

name: Fortynine Palms Oasis
formatted_address: Fortynine Palms Canyon Rd, Twentynine Palms, CA 92277, USA

google_reviews.text:
An excellent hike inside Joshua Tree National Park. There is no pay station when entering this area, the only thing on this road is this parking lot trailhead. I was the very first person on New Years Day 2026 to reach the Oasis early this morning! I'll hold this title until next year when somebody else can hold it. The hike started off cloudy and forecast was "rain", thankfully it held off just long enough to complete the hike.

I'd say its "moderate/easy" in difficulty. For an average person, more moderate. For a seasoned hiker, easy. The path is natural, so a bit of gravel, a bit of dirt, and stone steps. The oasis itself is nestled into the mountains and very neat to see once you make it there. I stayed there for a little bit before heading back to the parking lot. There are no actual Joshua Trees along this hike, but many different cactus varieties. I completed the hike in 1.5 hours.

Visited on a rainy day with lots of fog and chilly weather, however it was still beautiful! Worth the hour hike. The trail is labed hard difficulty 2-3 hours but any experienced hikers can make it there and back around 1 hour with the cooler temps. The elevation gain is fairly easy with the stone steps. Get there early as the parking lot fills quick even in the off season. No pets

Tough one. Steep uphill to the mountain then steep downhill to the palm trees. Easily takes 2.5 hours. Needed to stop multiple times along the way. Plenty of water required. No entrance fee and clise to highway 62.

Beautiful Evening Hike at 29 Palms Oasis Trail!
We started our hike around 3:30 PM, and the timing was perfect. The day was hot, so I wore shorts, thinking it would stay warm, but as we got to the trail, the temperature started dropping. Luckily, with all the hiking and movement, I worked up a little sweat, so it stayed comfortable—neither too hot nor too cold.

The higher we climbed, the more breathtaking the views became, revealing more of the surrounding desert landscape. Unfortunately, we didn’t make it all the way to the palms since we were with a large group and didn’t want to be up there at sundown. However, I definitely want to go back and complete the full hike.

As for difficulty, I’d say it’s moderate—some rock steps, but very manageable. Compared to Red Rocks, this one was a bit easier. If you’re looking for a scenic and rewarding hike, I highly recommend it!

One of the best HARD hiking trails in JT national park/forest. This hidden gem is worth the hike that takes you winding up, down, side-to-side into the rocky terrain and desert wilderness. This trail offers no shade and yet so much warmth on cool winter morning in December. Recommend starting the trail walk around 9am to get the max. out of mood, atmosphere, temperate weather and scarceness of other hikers around. One of the best hikes I have been on.

name: Keys View
formatted_address: California, USA

google_reviews.text:
Keys View in beautiful Joshua Tree National Park is a must see when visiting   this National Park! Sitting at over 4000 feet above sea level and one of the Parks highest locations,Keys View offers stunning views of the valley below! Views of Salton Sea,Palm Springs,local mountains and much more!
There are breathtaking photo opportunities here so bring a camera,mobile phone and water to spend some time at this scenic location!
They do have bathroom facilities and parking for cars,rv's,campers and buses-but be prepared because it does get busy especially on the weekends!
Highly recommended for a visit to Keys View,Joshua Tree National Park,California!
.
.
.
.
#keysview #joshuatreenationalpark #nationalparks #godisgood #joshuatree

If you’re in Joshua Tree, you cannot miss this viewpoint. The sunset is absolutely phenomenal, the colors over the valley are unforgettable.

A few tips for the best experience:
• Arrive Early: The parking lot fills up fast, so get there well before the sun starts to dip to snag a spot.
• Bring Gear: I highly recommend bringing a beach mat and some snacks so you can sit back, relax, and thoroughly enjoy the view without rushing.

One of the most peaceful and stunning spots in the entire park. A must-see!

Definitely worth the short drive up & back! Be advised that parking is minimal, and although this was not a problem for us in February (still 80°F), the busier tourist months would be challenging. Also, for those who are older, disabled, etc., the path up to Keys View is very steep, but smooth concrete.

beautiful. and there are a few plaquito Tell you what you are looking at. parking was good. we were there during Spring Break (Feb 17-21). good amount of peanut still able to get great photos without randos. we got our Joshua Tree tickets in advance and stopped at the visitor center where a guide helped us plan what we wanted to see.

Unfortunately they closed the road to Keys View and redirected the traffic with no explanations. I’ve seen the view before and I can say it’s beautiful. My only advice is, if you don’t find desert environment fascinating and enjoyable Joshua national park is not for you because it looks just like other deserts around the freeway with some rock formations around the roads. And waiting in line in your car for 45 minutes and paying $30 entrance fee adds to the insult. But if you love the quiet and serenity of deserts, you’ll enjoy hiking every trail of the park.

name: Joshua Tree National Park
formatted_address: California, USA

google_reviews.text: A stunning place for a scenic drive and some light hiking. The rock formations are fascinating, and the Cholla Cactus Garden looks like something from a Dr. Seuss book (just don’t touch them!). It’s very well-maintained, but it gets crowded on weekends, especially Valentine’s Day (Heart Rock) so arrive early to beat the heat and the gate lines.

Spectacular views and scenery. Sunset is simply amazing and unique from the park. There is a cactus nursery that is worth the stop as well. Skull rock and some climbing spots are also very popular. Beautiful park overall and well worth the price and visit.

This is a really magical place. I hope I can go back one day.

We entered the park while on a road trip from Tucson —> Palm Springs. We entered at the Cottonwood visitor centre, and were greeted by a beyond lovely ranger, who engaged my little kids immediately. We checked out the cottonwood Oasis, then drove to Skull rock where the kids loved exploring on the boulders. We then drove to the keys view (gorgeous) and did another short walk (about an hr w kids) at Hidden Valley. Being there on a warm day, while the sun was starting to set, made it extra special

One of the most breathtaking places I've ever visited.
Joshua Tree National Park holds a very special energy — quiet, ancient, and deeply grounding. The landscape looks almost surreal, like stepping into another dimension where time slows down and your breath grows deeper.

We visited during sunset, and the sky unfolded in layers of gold, lavender, and soft pink. The silhouettes of the Joshua trees against that glowing sky were absolutely unforgettable.

The park is vast and peaceful. Whether you’re hiking, meditating, taking photos, or simply sitting on a warm rock watching the world go silent, this place invites you to reconnect with yourself.

Perfect for:
• star-gazing
• sunrise & sunset photography
• quiet reflection
• nature lovers
• anyone wanting a moment away from the busy world

Roads are well-maintained, signs are clear, and the rangers are helpful. Bring plenty of water and stay until the stars come out — it's one of the most beautiful night skies you'll ever see.

A must-visit national park. Highly recommended.
We will definitely return.

CHOLA CACTUS GARDENS WERE CLOSED February 5th, 2026 - check before you go. If you enter in from the west wide of the park from Joshua Tree, you will pass a small parking area with a bathroom before reaching the place to pay. You'll pay at a booth $30 for a seven day pass. They have other options as well. You'll be met with a landscape of Joshua Trees almost immediately upon approach or the park. There are impressive and almost alien rock formations throughout the entire park. It will take at least almost two hours to drive from one end of the park to the other. If you do drive through the entire thing, it will take you to 29 Palms on the east end. One you hit the turnoff for the Chola Gardens, there will be NO outhouse/restrooms so go before. Hundreds of viewpoints along the road to stop at. They also have multiple campgrounds throughout the entire park. It is only a two land road in and out so you may find yourself behind a slower moving RV etc. plenty of great trails to hike or varying levels and so many great options for places to have picnics as well.

name: Oasis Of Mara Nature Trail Trailhead
formatted_address: 74485 National Park Dr, Twentynine Palms, CA 92277, USA

google_reviews.text:
Visiting the Oasis of Mara was a truly meaningful experience. Standing there, it’s easy to feel the history and understand how important this place has been for generations. The oasis has long been a vital water source, sustaining wildlife and the Native people who lived in the area, and it continues to be an important symbol of the region’s heritage and connection to the land.

What makes it especially impactful is how thoughtfully preserved it is for the community and visitors. The easy, paved loop trail makes it accessible for almost everyone, which helps more people experience the history and beauty of the area. During our visit, the loop was closed for repairs, but even so, it was still worth stopping to see and learn about this important place.

It’s peaceful, beautiful, and full of meaning. I highly recommend taking the time to visit and reflect on the history it holds—it’s one of those places that quietly leaves a lasting impression.
It was closed for the most part as of Feb 21 2026 but I still got good photos and the people in office was really nice.

Nice spot to stop and see an Oasis. It's an easy 5-minute walk and there's a public bathroom available.

Definitely worth stopping in for a look. Some good information placards too.

This trail area is small but beautiful. It has a palm grove and myriad cactuses of different shapes and sizes in the trail. Very kid friendly and even wheelchair accessible. One can spend half an hour to an hour here.
Joshua Tree is fantastic and a place of unlimited exploration of natural desert beauty = thousands of acres! Trail types vary. Beautiful boulder & rock formations. Pets are not allowed in the park.

name: Arch Rock
formatted_address: Arch Rock Nature Trl, California, CA, USA

google_reviews.text:
Short trail from the designated parking area leads to both arch rock and heart rock among some large boulders. We went two separate times while staying in Palm Springs.

Having been before, a lot of people we crossed didn't know the exact location so we pointed it out to them. When walking along the trail, you'll slowly walk upwards towards a large ridge were people usually spread out. The trail skirts LEFT around the ridge before you reach the top. From the trail it appears like a small opening. The best view is to scramble up on either side from where the trail ends and the arch opens as pictured.

Heart Rock has separate fork trails that lead to it and roughly doubles your total time/distance. Look for the small trail marker signs and keep reference to the large top ridge near Arch Rock.

During our visits in winter season, temperature was lovely for trekking but conditions in Joshua Tree can vary wildly from within the park and from the cities/towns around. While we went we had times of both clear skies and cloudy weather, and a single occasion with a short period of heavy rain so we huddled under a small ledge. However, it is worth the effort even on a cloudy day for some desert flowers (after rainfall) and dramatic nature photos.

Super easy hike for all ages and abilities. Right up to view of the arch. To actually climb up to stand under it it’s not too difficult, but take your time and wear good shoes that grip.

Take time to hike around the backside there are some very impressive structures!

Beautiful rock formation, felt being in another planet.  Must visit if you are visiting Joshua Tree. Expect to purchase a pass (roughly $30 per car) to enter the park unless you have it already.  10 to 15 mins walk, easy for kids as well,  you'll definitely spend good time taking pictures. It's COLD. Come prepared with good walking or hiking shoes, layers to protect from cold. Note that there are no public bathrooms.
A fairly easy hike and if you keep going past less than at 1/4 mile then you'll see Heart Rock too. Beautiful place!
I always enjoy Joshua Tree National Park. This was a fairly easy hike. There’s a little climbing if you go through the arch. Beautiful views.

name: Barker Dam
formatted_address: Joshua Tree Parkway, Twentynine Palms, CA 92277, USA

google_reviews.text:
Beautiful easy trail! Stunning views. Lots of desert plants and Joshua trees to see. Mainly flat easy path. Some incline but easy.
Sadly no dogs allowed.
Was about 2km loop.
Fun petroglyphs to see too.

Barker Dam is a truly special and meaningful place, especially when experienced as a family. The trail to the dam is easy and mostly flat, which made it perfect for walking with our kids—they could play and toss a football along the way, and we made such warm memories together.
What makes this area even more powerful is the sense of history you feel as you walk. Barker Dam was built by early settlers as a place to store water—something absolutely essential in the desert—so they could survive and raise livestock. And even before that, this was a livable and important place for Native people, including those from the Serrano groups. You can almost tell from the surroundings and the landscape itself that this must have been a good place to live in the past, especially because water in the desert changes everything.
Add in the beautiful rock formations and peaceful desert scenery, and it becomes an ideal trail for families to walk together, talk, take photos, and enjoy the moment. If you’re visiting Joshua Tree National Park, I highly recommend Barker Dam—it feels scenic, peaceful, and genuinely important.

There's so much variety if you choose to make it a loop trail to/from the Dam. Both rocky and flat Joshua Tree filled sections and even some water at the Dam after an unusual September rainstorm. I especially liked finding the cave paintings on the way back to the parking lot.

Do note that as I went Google Maps recommended going on the unpaved Big Horn Pass. It was a really bumpy ride and I should have thought ahead with my sedan. Though I had no issues, there is the paved Barker Dam road that enters the other side of the parking lot and should be the first suggested route.

Amazing location.  Consists of a 1 mile loop trail that takes you to the dam area.  Plenty of rocks to climb on, and towards the end of the trail are painted rock art.  A must do.  I would allocate a couple hours here and get here first thing in the morning to grab parking.
This is a mostly level moderate family type hike. Probably best known for the water retention dam but there are a few " Disney petroglyphs" if you search.  Random photos non Barker added.

name: Quail Springs Trail
formatted_address: Twentynine Palms, CA 92277, USA

google_reviews.text:
Fun hike. Gets very hot very quickly though. Saw quite a few birds, desert chipmunks, and jack rabbits on this one.

Quail Springs parking lot is popular for night photography as well. That was probably the most fun part!

The short trail is close to the west entrance. The Friday after Christmas was a terrible day to visit this national park. My family was lucky to find a parking spot here. These rocks were not hard to climb. The view on the top was fabulous, where a lonely road pierced the desert. We only climbed rocks here because other attractions’ parking lots were fully packed. We also saw a heart stone surprisingly near the entrance. We had a lovely and unforgettable family time here.
Take water and hiking boots don’t leave the trail
amazing rock features for climbing, scrambling and bouldering. easy to get off trail to explore but easy to find your way back. very pick your own adventure with easy to hard options.

Nice, quick, sandy trail.

We stopped for star gazing and the trail was very easy. The rock formation was fun climbing. I would rate this 3-4 stars.

The star gazing was super enjoyable, there is a little bit of light pollution coming from over the mountains though. Bring a red headlamp, a comfy chair and a drink to enjoy the stars, or sit on a rock. We did see a handful of shootings stars every hour. I would rate the star gazing 5 stars.

name: George S Patton Park
formatted_address: 83700 Avenue 43, Indio, CA 92203, USA

google_reviews.text: Lovely park to stop and stretch your legs on a road trip with kids. There's plenty of tables, benches, a huge grassy area, and two play structures. It is clean and tucked away in a residential area.

Great park behind Winco, has a wonderful area for children to play on swings and playground clean bathrooms doesn't get overly crowded seating throughout the park there are about 2 shaded areas for parties each  has 2 tables and a grill get there early to claim it. This park does not have a baseball area or tennis area it DOES have a good size area to play  catch or soccer but it's an open area, so you will need to be mindful of sharing the field with other park guests dogs are allowed as long as you have them leashed. Great parking lot as well.
Great place for elementary kids and middle school kids up to 12 years old. Two separate structures with plenty to do.
Nice neighborhood park. Not very big but large enough to walk our small dog Nugget. Very well maintained.
Nice park, small courts, but not to bsy so no waiting.   We just popped in to play pickleball.

name: Bagdouma Park
formatted_address: Douma St, Coachella, CA 92236, USA

google_reviews.text: This is a great park that has lots of options available. Baseball fields, basketball courts, tennis courts, swimming pool, playground. Public restrooms are maintained too. It's a safe park to spend the day at.

Wonderful.
During a baseball game, the community really came together. Lots of seating and fun pathways. I saw some fun jungle gyms for the little ones as well.

Has been renovating to watch it get improved. Plenty of locations to enjoy the day with some sports or hobbies. A track that wraps around the whole park made out of compressed dirt path cemented in. Has a playground for the kids and even pickle ball and tennis courts 🤷😇 small issues but nothing worth mentioning.
The park is always clean. It is a good place to take your kids out instead of them being inside the house. It is nice to get together with family.

Lots of space for car camping and bringing trailers. The road goes into Joshua Tree.

---

5/5 Stars - A Must-See If You Love... Dirt?

If you're looking for a place with absolutely breathtaking amounts of dirt, congratulations—you’ve found paradise. Cottonwood Springs is like nature’s minimalist art installation: dirt, more dirt, some suspiciously brown mountains, and the occasional lizard giving you side-eye like you’re the one out of place.

No shade (literally, there’s no shade), just vibes—and by vibes I mean sunburn and a constant reminder that water is life. But hey, the night sky? UNREAL. Like, “is this a screensaver or am I having a spiritual awakening under the Milky Way?” kind of unreal.

Bring a camera, leave your expectations, and prepare to be wowed by the cosmic display and humbled by the sheer amount of... you guessed it—dirt.

#DesertVibes #LizardWatch2025 #JoshuaTreeAesthetic #DirtTok #SkyPorn

Would I go again? Yes. Do I understand why the lizards run this place? Also yes.

Follow me for more brutally honest travel reviews you didn’t know you needed!
@YourFavoriteWanderer

Final Rating: 5 lizards out of 5.

It was really nice to be able to see a rare cluster of California Fan Palms, there seems to be about 3 distinctive clusters off of the trails here. The hike around the area is fairly easy and took us about 2-3 hours including rest time and taking in all the scenery.

Did the Mastodon Peak hike starting from Cottonwood Spring Parking Lot on a Saturday afternoon in February. There was no room in the parking lot, and cars were parked along the road for a good ~500 feet leading up to it. The trail actually runs right along the road, so it didn't add any extra mileage to park a bit farther from the trailhead. Did the loop counter clockwise - came to around 500ft gain, all in the beginning, over 2.4 miles. You start with some good photo opportunities of the trees, and reach the mine at around the 1 mile mark. Good trail, easy to follow. I entered the Park from the south, and didn't have to wait or show my Annual Pass for this.

name: JEEP Trailhead into Joshua Tree Park
formatted_address: Riverside County, CA 92241, USA

google_reviews.text: Not sure why this doesn't have more ratings or maybe it's just a hidden gem but 1000% recommend. The trailhead will lead you all the way up to Joshua tree national Park and it's truly one of the most beautiful rides ever. Tons of areas throughout the route to rock crawl. Don't be like me and forget the air compressor, you will definitely want to air down your tires as it's fairly sandy in most areas so I had to sadly skip a few areas I really wanted to try. If you can make it up the mountain and to Joshua tree as the sun is setting, you will be floored by the colors.

Fun intro trail for your stock 4wd. The trail itself is not difficult with only one obstacle that may require higher ground clearance. I did see a stock 2wd Silverado come down it coming out of Joshua Tree. There’s lots of trash, shell casings, and even people dumping old refrigerators/washing machines. But… it free camping, free entrance to Joshua Tree, and another adventure. Please don’t leave anymore trash out there.

My family and me did this trail in a 2022 Subaru Outback (stock except for having installed All Terrain Wild Peak tires) and a 2023 Subaru Forester Wilderness (Stock). The majority of the trail is pretty easy going with sandy washes and rock gardens strung throughout. Be mindful of the sharp rocks. There were three obstacles that really tested the clearance of our cars.

If you’re going towards Joshua Tree through the canyon, the first is about 2-3 miles in where you’ll pass the first brown metal gate and continue for another 1000 feet and you’ll reach a narrow pass between two large rocks with a dip in the middle. This part proved too much of a challenge due to the clearance, however there is a fork in the road to the right about 200 feet back that will take you around the obstacle and into a few rock gardens.

The second obstacle is about another 3 miles in where you’ll have to go through another set of rocks, a dip, and an incline. There’s plenty of loose rocks to help you get you through. We kept placing rocks until we were able to get past the dip.

The third obstacle is not far after the second and is the trickiest due to how narrow the rocks are. There was very little room for us to squeeze by but as long as you have a spotter you should be fine. After this there is an incline where you will need to climb up three sets of rocks. After this it’s smooth sandy roads that turn into Geology Road.

You should definitely have a good set of tires to do this trail and airing down will greatly help you.

Also, we saw a Jeep Rubicon and GMC Sierra with larger tires drive through with no difficulties at the obstacles.

You will need high clearance and 4WD for a couple spots. Very bumpy in the lower sections, gets better as you climb up higher
It is definitely worth the drive, especially when you come out on top. This can be an especially beautiful drive in spring, after some rains promoting vegetation
Fun alternative entrance to Joshua Tree NP. Difficult for regular AWD SUV to pass, additional clearance is a must.

name: Jumbo Rocks Campground
formatted_address: Jumbo Rocks Campground, Park Blvd, Twentynine Palms, CA 92277, USA

google_reviews.text:
It was like staying in Bedrock, I expected Fred Flintstone to pop his head over the rocks at any moment. It's a truly unique campground. It can get very windy and the mice are fast if your not paying attention and leave something out... even for a few minutes.

Even our 17yo dog did some exploring, something the old guy hasn't done in awhile.

This has to be one of the most phenomenal places on the planet. The beauty is beyond comprehension. The rocks have so much to explore, I used to say the desert was dead but this has proven me strongly wrong. It's full of life and it's full of amazing life. Lizard, snakes, birds, coyotes, mountain goats. These are all things you can see not saying they had I saw all of them. But this place is amazing if you want to camp here you should. The only thing is is that it's an extremely popular camping spot so there's going to be a lot of people besides that it's absolutely worth it

Tell you what! When the winds pick up here it becomes magical. Almost like the energies are transfered through the rocks. The campsite was nice and open. They had jumbo rocks to climb and hike around. Lots of dumpsters to keep the land as natural as possible. The fires reflecting off the rocks was cool to see and the skies we're bright with Stars. It does get cold in January (35°F) so be prepared to bring a heated vest or something really warm. And the first night we heard howling of some sort. Wasn't too sure if it was coyotes, foxes, or even wolves. But that's the wild for ya! Nature at its finest.

Great campground, very clean! Amazing views. Campsites were a little small. You may end up with a shared space with the campsite next to you. The volunteer rangers were not very kind at times but other then those 2 things trip was amazing!

Jumbo Rock Campground is one of the most iconic campgrounds in Joshua Tree National Park, surrounded by massive granite boulders that create a truly unique desert camping experience. The sites feel surprisingly private thanks to the natural rock formations, and the night sky is exceptional for stargazing.

Facilities are very basic—no water, electricity, or showers—so you’ll need to come prepared. One of its biggest advantages is the location, with Skull Rock, one of the park’s most famous landmarks, just a short drive away.

Price: about $30 per night per campsite.
Ideal for campers who value atmosphere, scenery, and proximity to key attractions over comfort.

There is not a lot of parking here, but if the park is busy, it is OK to park just off the road.

It is a small pullout.
If you are lucky you can see ocotillo blooms without leaving your car.
It's a neat cactus patch as you enter/exit Joshua Tree. Looks like the cacti are planted but it allows for car tourists to easily see the native plants.
A bunch have died, but still fun to explore for a few minutes.  Note:  there is no trail.

Not a whole lot of these plants to be found here, but way more than I saw anywhere else in the park. They are really unique looking and beautiful, so if you have the time, I'd recommend making a quick stop here and checking them out

name: Heart Rock
formatted_address: XXQP+7J, Pinto Wye, CA, USA

google_reviews.text: This is a super easy and cute 30-minute hike in and 30- minutes out. The entire trail is marked with signs guiding you toward the heart-shaped rock. At one point, you might think the hike ends or that you're going in a different direction. Just keep going straight from the last sign you see, and you'll find the heart rock. Since it's popular, follow the crowd. Some people mistakenly believe the arch trail rock is behind the heart, but you'll need to go back and check the signs to find the correct trail.

Easy hike to this rock from the parking lot. I still recommend having water and some sort of head/face protection from the Sun during hot/warm days. A reminder to people with pets that the sand does get hot. Make sure you also have water for your pets. Although it says NO PETS people still take them and if you are then have water for them and monitor the temperature of the sand.

It's a nice hike.  We spent more than one hour in the trail with the arch rock and heart rock.  It will take a little bit effort to get to the position just below the arch rock for a nice picture.  It's easy to get to the heart rock without effort.
AMAZING ROCK, TRAIL SO BEAUTIFUL AND THERE IS CAMPING PLACE, VERY CLOSE TO THE PARKING SPOT
Easy to park near trailhead, low impact walk on sand/gravel, easy to find, beautiful scenery around you.

name: Maze Loop Trail
formatted_address: Joshua Tree, CA 92252, USA

google_reviews.text: If they pulled in a couple of platoons of nearby Marines they may well  have found them alive!
Not much in the park map though
6 miles

A quick stop a few meters before the Maze Loop Trail. Parking options outside of the designated parking areas are quite limited. It's a bit easier with motorcycles. Therefore, the most beautiful photo opportunities can be found here. I've now cycled through the national park three times, and I always discover something new. A beautiful wonder created by nature.

name: Wall Street Mill
formatted_address: Twentynine Palms, CA 92277, USA

google_reviews.text:
Cute hike. Totally hard to navigate. Very little sineage. However cleaner bathrooms than the rest of the park.
The view on the way are very nice.
If you want easy casual hike it’s great. If you want wow it’s not the place.
And again like with any hike in Joshua. No dogs. Very disappointing :(

A Desert Time Capsule. 🏜️ If you want a hike that’s heavy on history but light on the knees, Wall Street Mill is a must-do in Joshua Tree. An easy, flat, out-and-back trail (under 2 miles total) that feels like walking through an open-air museum. You get a legitimate glimpse into the Gold Rush era. Keep your eyes peeled for the rusted-out vintage cars abandoned in the wash—they make for incredible desert-noir photos against the Joshua trees.

Similar to other JT NP trails, there isn't much shade, so bring water even for a short trek. If you have extra energy, it’s easy to pair this with the nearby Barker Dam loop. A perfect trail for families, photographers, or anyone who prefers a scenic stroll over a strenuous climb. BONUS: bathrooms at the trailhead!
Nice walk. Not the most exiting one, but gave us an idea how they was digging for gold and in 1943 and the greed between to neighbour's in a vast land.

You can start this trail from the Barker Dam parking lot or drive up a dirt road and save a good 10 min of walking.

This trail wasn’t crowded like the rest of the park, which was nice. Took us 50 min round trip and we took some time to admire the ruins of the old mill and the Wonderland Ranch. Would love to find a photo of what it looked like in its glory days, but haven’t been able to yet. Good hike!

Probably my favorite trail today, especially because it breaks off and allows easy access to the Wonderland Ranch ruins and the Worth Bagley stone. Lotsa cool scenery and plenty of scattered gems to stumble upon if you’re flexible about your walk.

name: Contact Mine Trail
formatted_address: Twentynine Palms, CA 92277, USA

google_reviews.text: This is a 7 mile total, in and out trip. Do 3.6 miles to the mine and same distance back to start. Majority of it is gradual uphill with gravel footing. Bring water, at least a gallon per person. The hike and views are outstanding. Lots of brush, lizards, and birds to see. Low people count too

Super cool trail lots of unique cactus while walking. The mine is gated off, but there's plenty of interesting things around it. Plenty of old cans from when it was still active. And there's a lot of nature to see. Definitely would not explore in the summer.
Fairy easy and short, maybe around 4miles up and back, awesome scenery

Contact Mine Trail is a pretty enduring hike, this took us about 3-4 hours with resting and taking in the scenery. The trail isn't very well maintained, however it's got a large amount of Barrel Cactus up to the mine. The mine itself is sealed off and just two metal shafts going downwards with some left over machinery from the 1916s.

Get away from the crowds and be king of the mountain. Great trail, easy 3 mile hike up and excellent views. To get get the best views, once you get to the mine, head up a little further to the top of the mountain (off the trail). You won't be disappointed.

name: Keys Ranch
formatted_address: Twentynine Palms, CA 92277, USA

google_reviews.text: The tour was great. Loved all the information from the park ranger. Definitely don't forget your sunscreen like I did 😫🥵 and plan to not have cell service!

My wife I took the Keys Ranch tour in Joshua Tree National Park. It was an exceptional experience, and we must have chosen the perfect time to visit the weather perfect! The tour was excellent, with an outstanding and knowledgeable ranger, as well as dedicated volunteers. Bill Keys was an enigmatic and figure, which added to the atmosphere of the place. I highly recommend it a must-see attraction in Joshua Tree Park. Make sure to call and make it's worth it.
Wonderful experience,guided by Ranger Dave who gave a wonderfully for informative tour.

My husband and 2 grandchildren did this and loved it. The ranger was very knowledgeable, and it was a nice hike to get to all the stuff. I would definitely do this if you have the time. You must make a reservation a good bit ahead of time . We also drove down and hiked to the Keys Mine, which was great also.

What an amazing experience after all these years of just driving by the sign.  I started going to Joshua tree in the mid-70s with my high school buddies.  I always drove by the keys ranch signs saying by appointment only.... Well finally made an appointment and had a wonderful tour guide take us through the history of this awesome property.
Great storytelling! That made us imagine how life was at the turn of the century over 100 years ago.

name: Indian Cove Boy Scout Trail
formatted_address: Indian Cove Rd, Twentynine Palms, CA 92277, USA

google_reviews.text:
This a great hike , 16mile
A few inclines tho
This a hard one

Nice long trail accessible from outside of the park from Twentynine Palms. The trail is a bit slow going at first because of the sand path but it is pretty flat and there's some nice views of plants, rocks, wildlife (birds, lizards, rabbits and mice), and a dried river bed. Hiking for about 45 min will get you part way up the rocky hills where you can take some great pictures of the valley. Also includes a restroom near the entrance.

One of the longest trails in Joshua Tree National Park, this beautiful and varied trail can be hiked for the day or overnight if you have a backcountry permit. It travels through sandy washes, shallow canyons, rocky passes, and sprawling valleys with lots of opportunities to spot wildlife.

The Boyscout Trail is an excellent hike through the western mountains of JTNP! I took the trail on January 1st, 2020. There were several inches of snow on the north facing parts of the trail.

The trail has two endpoints: Keys West and Indian Cove. You have several options.
1) Start at either end and arrange a pick-up vehicle at the other end. This option takes about 2-4 hours depending on your hiking speed. The distance is about 7.6 miles with 1300 feet of elevation gain if you start from Indian Cove. Also note, Indian Cove to Keys West is the more demanding climbing.
2) You can do an out-and-back starting at either endpoint. This option will take 4-8 hours depending on your speed and breaks. It is about 15-16 miles. Total elevation gain will be between 2400-2600 feet.
3) You can hike to the high point of the trail and take the Big Pine trail. This gives you access to the Maze Loop and several other trails.
Backcountry camping is allowed South of the trail.

I have been going to this trail for years now and  it never ceases to amaze me. The first mile is all flat, heading towards a canyon. For the 2nd mile, you travel through a cool canyon with a sandy floor. After that, you permanently trek up switchbacks up the mountain ranges.
There are multiple areas to camp along the mountains with great photos to take. It took me 2.5 hours to backpack 4.5 miles. When you camp overnight, make sure to stay on the RIGHT side of the trail to avoid violating the bighorn sheep wilderness area. During the day, the numerous rock piles make great climbs with breathtaking photos.

Bring at least a gallon of water for an overnight trek

10/10 would do again

name: North View Trail
formatted_address: Joshua Tree, CA 92252, USA

google_reviews.text: This hike is located 1.8 miles from West Entrance. Parking is limited so many cars park on the side of the road. This hike has all different natural environment you can find in Joshua Tree. I absolutely loved it! BEAUTIFUL views, lots of up and down moments so you will gain some elevation. I decided to the loop with Maze Loop Trail, the shorter way, so the total hike was 4.9 miles. Great  and pleasant workout. Perfect for hike, walk and run.

A beautiful hike with far fewer people than on some of the other trails in the park. The trail takes you through each of the natural environments that are found in Joshua Tree so it's a great way to get a sense what exists in the park if you have limited time. Would definitely recommend packing more water than you think you'll need, we consumed 2L each.
Awesome. Less crowded. Spectacular views.

name: Skull Rock
formatted_address: Skull Rock, California, USA

google_reviews.text: This is a really cool rock formation that, yes, looks a lot like a skull. May one quibble is that people have discovered Joshua Tree National Park, indefinitely for the worse. Parking is near impossible, people are everywhere, and it’s hard to climb up to get the best view of the formation.  I miss the days when it was easy to get around our national parks.

We visited Joshua Tree National Park early on Friday morning, the day after Thanksgiving, with our family. Because it was the holiday weekend, the park was noticeably crowded, but the experience was still absolutely worth it.

Our first stop was Skull Rock, a famous rock formation that truly looks like a human skull. There were many people taking photos around it, and it’s easy to see why—the rock really does resemble a skull, complete with what looks like eye sockets, a nose, and a mouth. It was surprisingly convincing in person.

Our two sons especially enjoyed climbing the rocks across from Skull Rock. The rock surfaces here are very rough and textured, which makes them great for climbing. Standing on those rocks and taking photos felt almost cinematic, like being on a movie set rather than a place you casually visit. It definitely had that “otherworldly” feeling that Joshua Tree is known for.

If you have the chance, I highly recommend hiking around this area and taking your time to explore and take photos. It’s a great spot for families, photography lovers, and anyone who enjoys unique landscapes that feel far removed from everyday life.

Skull Rock is not just a rock — it’s an experience that stays with you long after you leave. At first glance, it looks playful, almost humorous, like nature is showing off its creativity. But the longer you stand there, the more powerful it becomes. The hollowed “eyes,” the rough texture shaped by wind and time, and the sheer scale of it make you realize how alive this landscape feels.

Standing in front of Skull Rock, you can’t help but imagine the centuries of erosion that carved it into this unforgettable form. It feels ancient, patient, and strangely expressive, as if it’s silently watching over the desert. Kids see a giant skull, adults see the unstoppable force of nature — and both reactions are equally valid and magical.

The area around Skull Rock is just as captivating, with endless boulders, open desert, and incredible light that changes every minute. Sunrise and sunset turn the rock into a completely different character. It’s easily accessible, yet deeply profound. Skull Rock perfectly captures the spirit of Joshua Tree: wild, imaginative, timeless, and unforgettable. If you visit the park and miss this spot, you’ve missed something truly special.

Such a fun spot inside Joshua Tree NP, so much fun scrambling around unique and fun looking rock formations! Always have a blast when I go there. Even if I'm just passing by driving on the 10 between LA and Phoenix/Tucson, I always make it a point to stop even if just for a couple of hours!!!

Skull Rock is a rock that someone desperately wanted to be a skull. Spoiler: it isn’t. Whoever named it either never saw a skull, or only ever studied alien anatomy while mildly intoxicated. There are two dents and a vibe. That’s about it.

Still, it’s fun to stop, poke around, and take the obligatory “look mum I found a face in a boulder” photo. Nature tried, humans exaggerated, tourists obey. Classic.

name: Porcupine Wash
formatted_address: California, USA

google_reviews.text: Great Trail! Overall Joshua Tree National Forest Is Always A Great park To Visit! Plenty Of Trails, Diffrent Campground Areas witch you need to pay for. Entrance fee to the Forest is $30 ticket last for 7 days. (Campground fee is seprate). If you enjoy nature & outdoors. This is a must!🙂🙃

My wife and I wanted to star gaze for a while and she checked some observations online about what is the "best" place in JTree to do this. She has found between the awe inspiring Cholla Gardens and Cottonwood "somewhere about half way" was the "darkest" part of the park. I found this to be fairly accurate. Beautiful night. Could see the Milky Way pretty clearly. Found it interesting as well as you could not only see stars but also satellites and caught 4 shooting "stars" over the 2 hours we spent here. Can't really offer a comparison of other places in the park but I will say this spot was pretty cool. Brought 2 chairs. Walked about 100 yards up the trail some water and really enjoyed the night sky.
Nice hiking area loved it.

The Joshua Tree national Park is beautiful after this much water. There were at least 50 different flowers and blooming plants in this area, along with a massive number of butterflies and bunnies everywhere. March thru April the park is beautiful.
Trail head parking lot cool spot to star gaze

name: Boy Scout Trail
formatted_address: 64568 Park Blvd, Twentynine Palms, CA 92277, USA

google_reviews.text: Great hike, we camped at site one it was a lot of fun with some challenge with the elevation change. There were many beautiful rock formations to look at. I would recommend this hike.
