Reached the trailhead at around 10am on a Sunday in Feburary. Had to wait 10-15 minutes at the park entrance. Parking lot was full when I got there but luckily, I circled around couple of times and within that time, someone got out, so I got a spot. I was planning to do the 4.5 mile hike called "Boy Scout and Willow Hole to Outer Magnolia Climbing Area" on allTrails, but realized at the turnaround point that I was only a mile away from Willow Hole, so kept going. It wasn't worth it. As you get closer to Willow Hole, the trail becomes a dry river bed, so it gets tougher to move fast, and you're always dragging your feet, trying not to sink much. Willow Hole itself is just an empty area surrounded by giant rocks, so if you've seen enough of those elsewhere in Joshua Tree, I would recommend against this hike. Total out-and-back was around 7 miles with 300 feet elevation gain, and took me 2h10m.
Great trail. Great secluded camp sites.

This is a really nice 7.5 mile trail for all skill levels. We take a scout troop here every year and camp overnight a little more than halfway in. It loses one star because it's not a loop (although you can make it a loop by connecting to other trails).

From the main road in the park, the first 3 miles are a gentle elevation gain with the last mile being through a sandy wash and some rock areas to get to a nice stopping point in a natural bowl. The next day involves switchbacks on a relatively steep downhill with a lot of scree and some short jumps until you get to the bottom and more sandy wash. The last 2 miles or so are relatively flat through the washes and regular trail which spits you out at the Indian Cove road and trailhead. Unless you have a ride back to the parking lot, you will have to do the trail again in reverse to get back to your car.

This trail can easily be done round-trip (15 miles) in one day with nothing more than a day pack and enough water provided you are in good shape and get started early enough but I would consider this to be reserved for advanced/expert hikers.

From the highest points you get good views of the towns and the lower Mojave.

Rock formations are fun.

Most of the Joshua Trees are on the South side of the trail.

Trail is well marked.

Night time is quiet and, when skies are clear, the stars are spectacular.

From the South: A great 3ish mile intro to backpacking trail. Parking lot is bigger on this side, but this side is also busier.

From the North: A more difficult 6ish mile, Fellowship of the Ring type backpacking adventure. Much less parking at this side... maybe room for about 5 cars at the registration board.

name: Cholla Cactus Garden
formatted_address: Cholla Cactus Garden, California, USA

google_reviews.text: Gorgeous walkway of the spiky cacti, but as of January the parking lot was under construction. There are some space along the road to park on the shoulder but be wary of how far to safely park along some sandy sections. All along the road are other cholla cacti to view and some of the outlooks are extremely quiet and have amazing views as you traverse between the Colorado and Mojave Deserts.

Fantastic! Don't miss this one. The parking lot area was closed but we were able to find a spot on the side of the road and get out and walk around a bit. We really enjoyed it what a beautiful and unusual group of cactus!
Stunning dessert sunset in Cholla cactus garden area!! Inside Joshua Tree national park.

Bring a comb!! Many people in our party stepped on or got cactus stuck in them. Someone with a comb helped us out. Great area for photos. We didn’t stop on our first trip to Joshua tree because it was a little out of the way—definitely work the drive. We  went out at sunset and it was glorious!!
absolutely stunning place. My highlight from Joshua tree NP. Visited during sunrise and it was magical!

name: Intersection Rock
formatted_address: Intersection Rock, California 92277, USA

google_reviews.text: Popular with rock climbers and there is a nearby campground so it is pretty crowded.

The Home base for Joshua Tree rock climbing! Big parking lot, bathrooms(vault) and trash cans. Usually the meetup spot to a greater Joshua Tree Adventure. Climbers will find most of the Sport/Trad/Bouldering Classics within walking distance from this parking lot. Be safe, respect the outdoors and have fun.
Good rock
A wonderful nature's expression
Rocks Rock

name: Wonderland of Rocks
formatted_address: Wonderland of Rocks, California 92277, USA

google_reviews.text: This is a great area for bouldering, climbing but also hiking in Joshua Tree. From the Barker Dam parking lot, there is a trail that goes East for a bit and will lead you to the remains of a pink house. Opposite the pink house is the entrance to the Wonderland of Rocks, making your way North into the boulders. We just did a 2 miles out and back hike, very fun, tons of prickly pear cacti!

Went here last Friday night on a full moon.
This is my third trip to joshuatree and it
Was great seeing a part of this park I've
Never seen before. So beautiful and
Awesome rock formations
Essential destination if you enjoy rock climbing/scrambling.
This place rocks.

name: Indian Cove Campground
formatted_address: Indian Cove Campground, 68917 Indian Cove Cir, Twentynine Palms, CA 92277, USA

google_reviews.text:
Indian Cove Campground is hands down one of the most unique and peaceful places to stay in Joshua Tree National Park. Surrounded by massive rock formations, it feels secluded, quiet, and incredibly raw — like you’re camping inside a natural stone cathedral.

The campsites are well spaced, giving you privacy and that true desert solitude. No RV hookups, no noise, no distractions — just rocks, sky, wind, and silence. This is real camping, not a parking lot with tents.

Sunsets here are unreal, and at night the stars are absolutely breathtaking. If you’re into hiking, climbing, photography, or just disconnecting from everything, this place is perfect. You can step out of your tent and immediately feel like you’re deep in the wilderness.

Facilities are basic but clean and well maintained, which is exactly what you want in a place like this. Indian Cove isn’t for everyone — and that’s why it’s special.

Highly recommended for anyone who wants an authentic Joshua Tree experience

I recently had an excellent experience staying at an Indian campground near Joshua Tree, and it ended up being one of the highlights of my trip. The area was peaceful, beautifully maintained, and surrounded by the kind of desert scenery that makes you slow down and appreciate the moment. The staff and hosts were incredibly warm and welcoming. They took the time to explain the layout of the grounds, share local history, and point out the best spots to explore both inside the campground and out in Joshua Tree National Park.

The campsite itself was clean, quiet, and thoughtfully arranged. Each site had enough space for privacy while still feeling like part of a friendly community. The amenities—restrooms, fire pits, and shaded areas—were well kept and made the stay very comfortable. Even at night, the campground felt safe and serene. The absence of city lights created a stunning view of the stars that I won’t forget anytime soon.

This campground offered exactly what I was hoping for: a calm, welcoming place to unwind while still being close to all the natural beauty of Joshua Tree. I would absolutely return and recommend it to anyone looking for a peaceful desert getaway.

Stayed two nights in the group site G02, and wow what a great spot! Really cool rock formations with the firepit being tucked right up against them. The site is very wide, very flat, well kept, and has a lot of larger plants in between to form some privacy between groups. Vault toilets well stocked with TP, multiple tables, a large fire pit that has a grate over it, and a large bbq stand that also has grates over it. Was enough space for 40ish of us to all move around and park easily. Also early February was not even that cold overnight. Maybe high 40s, mid 50s. 35$ for regular, 55$ for group.

Gorgeous campground but it feels designed for tent camping, not RVs. We were able to get into site 61 with a 25ft Airstream but it was tight and  uneven. Absolutely worth it but if you're an anxious parker and have a longer rig, study the map and site before you arrive. Class Bs and shorter travel trailers would have the best luck and most options.

One of the best campgrounds in the desert that I have enjoyed. No water or dump stations. Has vault bathrooms that are kept well stocked and clean. All campsites come with a picnic table, fire ring and grill. Beautiful place to stargaze or go hiking and rock climbing. Peaceful and pleasant 🫡

name: Hidden Valley Campground
formatted_address: 74485 National Park Dr, Twentynine Palms, CA 92277, USA

google_reviews.text: Amazing campground with beautiful scenery and proximity to boulders.  Some of the most popular bouldering blocs are located within a short walk from this campground such as Outback and the Caveman area.  Fire pits have adjustable grills and garbage bins and restrooms are located close by.  Just remember to bring lots of water as there is no running water at the site.   Great camping experience!

First time visiting Joshua Tree. The park was amazing. Staff was pleasant and made the experience all that much better. Depending on the season, be prepared for the changes in temperatures. That bright sunny sky can be deceiving in January.

What an outstanding deal.   $25 to stay right in the heart of Joshua Tree National Park.   Hidden Valley Campground is located right in amongst a field of huge weathered boulders which make a great hiking opportunity right from your campsite.   The rangers I spoke to during my stay were very helpful and friendly and I could not have been more pleased.

Hidden Valley Campground is by far my favorite in Joshua tree. It is great for just venturing out without a car and just climbing on the rocks. It is so fun. If you want to watch serious rock climbing, walk or drive down to cap rock a bit farther on park boulevard. This area is the best for amateur/recreational climbing.

I'm also glad campfires are allowed here, because in most other campsites in Southern California, they aren't allowed due to fire danger.

Hidden Valley Campground is a really cool place! The big rocks are easy to climb and give you amazing views of the desert. For serious rock climbing - there’s a huge rock where climbers are always hanging out—it’s fun to watch!

There’s also lots of parking, so finding a spot is never a problem. If you want to climb, explore, or just enjoy the view, this place is for you!

name: Festival Campgrounds
formatted_address: 83272 51st Ave, Coachella, CA 92236, USA

google_reviews.text: This place is amazing for Coachella. Just make sure you budget for their shuttle. Other than that they give you free water/Gatorades & they have tons of good food you can buy for a really good price.

Amazing Oasis for Coachella weekend! Well worth it. There was a pop up sauna and cold plunge that was a huge game changer. The family that owns it are wonderful. 10/10 we will be back. Thank you so much!

The BEST place to stay for Coachella! I've been coming here for the past 4 years. Claudia and Johnny remember us every time we come and they're so amazing and excited to see everyone! They have showers, bathrooms, food trucks all the time. Its great for when we come back at 3 am and they have tacos ready to make! The pool and DJ area are great too, we make sure to get our hair done at the beauty bar every time too. We love coming here, super cute area and all of your camping neighbors are great and its so fun to meet everyone! 10/10 would recommend staying here.

We LOVE it here. We came here for Stagecoach 2022 and now 2024. Claudia, Johnny and all of the Rancho family go above and beyond to make you feel welcome. The bathrooms/showers are always clean. There’s a food truck on site from the moment you wake up, well into the night and the food ALWAYS hits.  The pool is very large with plenty of shade and great seating for the crowd. We will be back!

This place is amazing to stay if going to a festival nearby! Just recently stayed here for Coachella 2023. First time going to this festival and staying here made the trip 100x better. Definitely take advantage of the shuttle this place provides.
The bathroom and restroom are clean. A huge plus is that there’s never a long line or waiting time to use either of them.
The pool is an amazing. Everything this place offer, is just wonderful.
While booking, I choose the option of the tent they provided. It is very spacious and clean when checking in. The checking in process is very smooth.
Claudia is an amazing host and no words to describe how incredible this person is! She goes above and beyond!
If you have a specific request, she will try her best to meet it.

name: Belle Campground
formatted_address: Belle Campground, Pinto Basin Rd, Twentynine Palms, CA 92277, USA

google_reviews.text:
Lucked out on getting this first come first serve site. We were able to catch it right as someone was leaving. And it was only $25.

You get the idyllic, quintessential Joshua Tree landscape. Came here to view the meteor showers.

Campground comes with grill and fire pit. Also, you could either pitch a tent or bring in an RV.

Also, area is very ideal if you are into rock climbing; the campground had an area for that. Day parking available in that area.

Site is more primitive, so be prepared. No running water whatsoever, you’ll need to bring your own to the site. Also, only has a pit toilet; bring your own sanitizer, wet wipes, etc.

Beautiful Campground!
Stunning sunsets and sunrises paint the sky with vibrant hues, making each day feel magical.
The unique rock formations add a distinctive charm, and the clear night skies create an unforgettable stargazing experience. Highly recommended!
Fantastic Place to spend a couple Night....Vault Toilets...very basic

Great place to camp!  Tons of large rocks that provide a neat view and privacy between sites.  Small, and super popular right now (Feb 2021).  If you aren't circling the site as people are waking up, you likely won't get a spot.  However, we were just car camping so we found a site with one other person and were able to join their site thanks to their goodwill.  Clean pit toilets, otherwise no facilities here.

Belle is a first-come campground one of two like that. I got there early 1 pm and by 3 pm it was full. The only hassle is you have to drive to an entrance gate and pay, card only no cash. I was enjoying the beauty and quiet but woke up Sunday morning to a blowing wind, ugh.

name: Cottonwood Campground
formatted_address: Cottonwood Oasis Rd, Twentynine Palms, CA 92277, USA

google_reviews.text: This is a campground site location. There are nice covered canopies and picnic tables. The really nice thing too are clean bathrooms with urinals and flush toilets. Lastly, there is potable water via spicket available in each location.
What a great camping spot. Easy in and out. The odd numbers park along the road way while the even numbers are either tent or back in spots. The night sky is great for star gazing.

A few years ago (September of 2022), my friends planned my bachelor party to JTree. We stayed at site B31. It was a great experience. We had a total of 3 tents that were 2-4 person sized, a large pop-up for shade, and we still had plenty of space for 5 large guys. Restrooms were clean and well maintained, and there was access to water throughout the campgrounds. This trip was the catalyst for my friends and I to plan a similar camping trip annually.
The following year, we came back to this campground and stayed at site A07. This site was great as well, but it did have some gulleys and sloped spots. We were able to get the same 3 tents but we were on the boundary of the site. The gulleys were maybe a foot wide by a foot deep from water runoff. Nothing crazy, but watch your step at night.
JTree is considered a dark sky area. Perfect for seeing the night sky, milky way, and if you've never seen a truly dark sky, go to JTree during a new moon! It will put life into perspective.
Great campground! Would absolutely stay again!

This is not a place for glamping. If you're looking for a challenging hike with an elevation gain from 9K to just over 11K and bitchin view at the end, then I highly recommend it!
Not for beginners, unless you plan on overnight camping.
I did a turn around that was approximately 5 hours and roughly 11 miles. Definitely felt it the next day.
This review has nothing to do with Cottonwood Campground, it's for Cottonwood Trail, located in Inyo County. The road that leads to the trailhead starts in the city of Lone Pine and takes you to Cirque Lake.
If you get up early, you might catch a local restaurant on the way back down the highway.
Lone Pine closes early.
Love the Campground, enough room for 2 small families, restroom was close by. The unique landscape is amazing, will come back next time for sure, Didn't get a chance to hike the trails.

name: White Tank Campground
formatted_address: White Tank Campground, 2 White Tank Campground Rd, Twentynine Palms, CA 92277, USA

google_reviews.text: By far a favorite campground. It’s remote, no phone service and no amenities but it is so so cool. We were able to get a camping spot early enough but it is first come and it was full by afternoon. The walks near are great and the sunrises and sunsets are gorgeous.
If it had flush toilets it would easily be 5 stars. Just come early since it is first come first serve.
One of the best campgrounds and best hike! So fun there!

We had a short two day stay, but boy was this the perfect place to be. I recommend spot #13 as it has the best place to flatly pitch your tent and get a great sunrise from your tent as you wake up, and get the fantastic sunset as the sun sets. Will be coming back ASAP.
Great place with great views. Nice campground with 15 spots. Downside is lots of traffic people coming up to hike and parking n the camp spots. Lots of people just driving through raising dust.

name: Chiriaco Summit Campground
formatted_address: 62500 Chiriaco Rd, Indio, CA 92201, USA

google_reviews.text: We were checking out Joshua Tree National Park, and General Patton Museum. This was a great fit for location. Spots were decently level. Nice there is a camp host present. You have to fill out a paper when you check in. You can stay for a week free. Awesome that it’s free!

Great spots, well tended, fireplace at each site, both campers and tenters welcome.  Quiet hidden gem just a short drive outside of Joshua Tree NP and the sunset views are great! Spent 3 days and would stay again  anytime we passed thru. Restaurant, gas station, coffee shop and General Patton museum surround it for the necessities but it is a dry camp so no trash cans, water or dump station but great place for a few nights to relax and disconnect.

Good rest stop. Small Cafe, but cozy. The Dry Campsite has about 14 spots for cars and RVs up to 40 feet, with one pull-through for an RV over 40 feet. It also has about 4 tent only spots. No facilities but there are rest rooms at the Cafe an gas station. There is a cell tower within 1/4 mile so reception is full bars. Food and gas are kind of pricey but there is nothing in any direction for about 40 miles.
Nice campground that's free to use.   No amenities and a little road noise.   Got a restaurant and convenience store in walking distance.

We were a bit uncomfortable finding a dispersed camping site near Joshua Tree NP for our 42 ft motor home, so we came here.  We are glad we did.  It is privately owned, but free, well distanced from other campers, and close to convenience store, gas, food, etc.  Cell reception is good.

name: Coachella Lakes RV Resort
formatted_address: 44790 Dillon Rd, Coachella, CA 92236, USA

google_reviews.text:
We loved our (now) two stays at Coachella Lakes RV Resort. The RV sites are large and clean. The sights of the lakes and mountains are spectacular. The ability to unplug and relax in a great setting has been great during eaxh stay.

Lindsay of the office stay has been awesome and assisted us with a group event set up.

The staff in the office are friendly and welcoming....with easy check in and out.

All around an amazing venue and place to unwind and GLAMP in a great way!!!

This is a fantastic place, the staff were completely incredible.  We asked about some ants at our location and they promptly came over and took care of the problem.  The pool areas are very nice.   Bring a putter to play the course or rent a putter and ball for $3.   Very fun and not gimmicky.  Honestly I have nothing negative to say about this place and definitely want to come back.  The only minor thing is the hwy is a bit loud but it’s like this throughout the entire Palm Springs area.  Great place! Thank you

Very spacious facility in a great location for visiting Joshua Tree NP. The grounds are well kept, RV sites are larger than most. The pools and restrooms were clean and well maintained. The beer and wine bar with food and live music is a nice touch. There is an 18 hole putting course which is very big and lots of fun. The morning and evening views of the surrounding mountains are unobstructed and the lakes on the property make it feel even larger.

Nice place!  Wifi took a number of calls before working. It's evident that the ownership is not paying attention to maintaining the property and injecting capital to fix and maintain some elements. You can hear us10 fairly loudly at times. Overall a nice place but could be made nicer with better attention. Just trailer wash is great and highly recomend him and his wife.

We stayed in a 50 amp RV site with full hookups. The grounds are pretty and well kept. Lots of amenities. Laundry is app based and cashless, makes things easy. They have a clubhouse with beer and wine. Pools and hot tubs! Lots of space for everyone to have a good time. I love that it is gated. Will stay longer next time!
Liz I. The office was very helpful and friendly. Thank you to all of the staff for keeping this place amazing!

name: Sheep Pass Campground
formatted_address: 74485 National Park Dr, Twentynine Palms, CA 92277, USA

google_reviews.text: I had an experience here. Ha having grown up visiting the monument in the early days  this campground did exist and upon my entrance and confounding campsites My hippie uncle and his friends brought me here and taught me ecology although I didn’t know it at the time. So although sheep’s pass was there although I had not became an adventure of years gone by. Each area of the monument has its unique features although they appear to look alike. So when I had paid for a week entrance, stopped by my he 29 palms Park visitor center. Remarkably the newest displayed the Park in a holistic way bringing the people of the past in our vision. Knowing every camp site circa 1980 in the park I was on the hunt for afternoon shade over the taboe. Eventually I found the space I was looking for. The quiet solitude of footsteps gave way to deafening silence! Ah! Joshua Tree!

Great campground for family or a large group. We went with our Boy Scout Troop for the weekend and managed to fit about 15 tents plus have plenty of room for several cooking areas. Fire pit is huge. Pit latrines are close enough and far enough from the site to be comfortable and not have the unpleasant odors waft into the camping area. Will note, there is no water or firewood available in the immediate area so you should bring a good supply with you. Cell service is not available in most of the park, but wandering around a bit can find pockets of service. Plenty of trails to go hiking and bouldering on in the vast desert grounds. Be sure to be prepared for the desert weather. It is cold at night and can be very hot in the direct sun. Pop up shelters are recommended for an escape during peak temperature hours.

One of the most beautiful experiences of my life. The place is extremely quiet but you feel safe 100% . Bathrooms are located at a walking distance and you find toilet paper, it's clean and you see the park officers cleaning and disinfecting for Covid 19. The view is gorgeous and the tents are easily installed, you find fire pit and a grill.

Great tent camping with very low light pollution at night so the stars are amazing! Facilities are close by and the site was very clean. Nice bouldering close by both walking distance as well as a few quick drives. Can't wait to go back this season.

Spacious sites. Clean, odorless bathrooms. I stayed at g4 which couldve been more isolated but it was great for what it was. Next time i would choose a different site a little further away from the rest. You’ll need to set up shade somehow, theres no shade at this site.

name: Cottonwood Group Campground
formatted_address: 74485 National Park Dr, Twentynine Palms, CA 92277, USA

google_reviews.text:
Large, spacious, and somewhat private group campsites. Each site is large enough for around a dozen tents scattered among the shrubs. There's a large picnic area with an overhang, a water pump, and a firepit.

A berm and nearby hills protect you from the wind.

Be vigilant of mice.

We stayed in group site number one which had four picnic tables under an awning, a pump for getting water right nearby, and a big fire ring with a grille that can be folded over on top of your coals. There was plenty of space for tents.

Good location to check Milky Way
Bathroom and tap water in the campsite are very convenient

While this campground is far from all the climbing in the park, it offers a more quiet and relaxed park experience. There are actually some trad climbing and bouldering nearby this campground but you just have to explore a little to get to them.
This is not the correct address for the Cottonwood group campground. The campground is located south of the park near Cottonwood Visitor Center.

name: Mastodon Peak Loop Trail
formatted_address: Lost Palms Oasis Trail, Twentynine Palms, CA 92277, USA

google_reviews.text: Excellent trail loop of around 3 miles.  It takes you to the top of Mastodon Peak and passes an abandoned mine.  Great views of the inland sea and all the rock formations.  It took up around 2.5 hours to complete the loop.

It's honestly an easy, scenic, beautiful loop with lengthy views, plenty of flora, and wildlife. The palm sanctuary in the beginning is a majestic treat, offering most of the shade, which lacks on this loop trail. Combined with a hot sun, it can sneak up on you. I would recommend going counterclockwise, hit the peak, see the mine, head back. I had complete pics on my first trek three years ago but lost them. My son and I recently stopped on our way back from L.A. I'll be back to recapture.

Pretty easy hike but getting up the peak top is very challenging as it is not a maintained trail.  It can be risky if you are not into rock climbing.  The hike in and out is full of interesting rock formations.

Hiked this with my wife and 3mo old in a chest carrier. It was doable in January but wouldn’t have wanted to do it during warmer weather. Path is crudely marked at times but there are multiple spots where you can see across the landscape and get your bearings. We didn’t do the scramble to the top of the peak, just the loop. Would do it again. Missing a fifth star just because there could be more signage about the landscape, plants, and animals in this area (and how they differ from the rest of the park).
A short loop trail that involves a bit of rock scrambling near the summit. But otherwise is a fairly easy hike.

name: Lost Horse Trail
formatted_address: Twentynine Palms, CA 92277, USA

google_reviews.text: This trail is a little hidden between the camp grounds. If the campgrounds are open then drive to the campground and you should see the trial If the campgrounds are closed you will park at the bathrooms and walk to the campgrounds and you will find the trial. The trail is called Ryan’s Ranch Trail but, it leads to the Lost Horse Trail. I would recommend bringing snacks and enjoying the sunset on the rocks. This trial also leads to the Ryan’s Mountain. This hike is easy. Make sure to know where the road is and always look around for people.
Beautiful section of Joshua tree! I love the rock formations here and there are just some standout trees here. Also a great place to park your camera for some great sunset and night shots!
Great and amazing trail. Ensure you have water with you as there is no water along the trail. The views are amazing!!!
Great views after the main mine. Take plenty of water.
Great place to explore!

name: Mecca Hills Wilderness
formatted_address: Chuckwalla, CA, USA

google_reviews.text: Drove through this wilderness Saturday 12/6/25 at 11:26am. It is absolutely stunning here. It is definitely worth a trip here to explore this area in more depth. There were lots of people scattered around day hiking and shooting 🔫 here.

Did the trail named Red Canyon and Bat Cave Loop from AllTrails the Sunday of Memorial Day weekend. Went with a friend, and I would highly recommend not going solo. Started at 1pm and spent around 4 hours there. We definitely went off the trail from AllTrails, and spent a bunch of time in the slot canyons, going up and down ladders. Fun experience, but we were always worried whether we would have to go back all the way or able to reconnect to the trail we had on our Garmin watches. Ended up with close to 9 miles on foot and 1400 feet of elevation gain. It's a fun experience for sure. Very very hot and dry - it was 100 degrees throughout. Each of us ran through 2.5 liters of water easily. The trailhead is not marked in any way, so we initially drove past it, then used our Garmin navigation to come back and find where to start.

This is a great place in Mecca, California to find the craziest experiences that California has to offer. Talking about secluded slot canyons and pure wilderness as far as the eye can see. It’s very beautiful any season. You won’t be disappointed because it’s so wild that you can even veer of the unmarked trail and discover new worlds and new canyons. It’s not a chocolate factory but it’s as close as you’ll get to one with all the brown topography and the chocolate Hills.

There is great hiking in the Box Canyon area of the Mecca Hills Wilderness. Probably the most popular is Painted Canyon. To get there take 66th Ave. east from Mecca, which turns into Box Canyon Road at the east edga of town. The signs warn about Box Canyon Road being closed, which is true, but it is closed just after the Painted Canyon Rd turnoff. The Painted Canyon Rd is a gravel road and is marked for 4 wheel drive only. Previously 2 wheel drive cars were able to traverse the road all the way to the Painted Canyon Trailhead but since the recent flooding this is no longer possible. They can get most of the way but the last 1/2 to 1 mile has soft, deep sand, so you will see them parked along the side of the road.

Even though the Box Canyon Road is officially closed, it is possible to follow a rough, sandy 4x4 trail to get to popular hiking areas in Box Canyon. Don't be fooled at the road closed sign, where there is still pavement, it soon ends and is 4x4 only!
Really amazing place. The views along Painted Canyon Rd. are amazing!

name: Red Canyon Jeep Trail Head @ 10 fwy
formatted_address: Pinto Rd, Indio, CA 92201, USA

google_reviews.text: I don’t know what’s considered the full length of the trail but I went up into the canyons and back. Trail itself is not too difficult it can probably be done with a 2wd with some ground clearance. Expect amazing views!

First time off roading. After reading reviews and watching half a dozen YouTube videos of Red Rock, my wife and I decided to give it a try. We took our new Ram Rebel with a two-inch lift. The trail was relatively easy. Very bumpy the first 4-5 miles, but then it got better. There are a couple places I was glad I had the lift on my truck. Very fun trail. We took it to Bradshaw Trail, turned right (south) and went down to the abandoned train bridge where we stopped to have lunch. Fun day. On the way back, we took Bradshaw Trail back north to the 10fwy.  Not a difficult trail for this newbie and his truck. Will definitely do it again.

This trail is easy and a lot of fun.  It takes you up along a canyon with great views.  You can to drive into the canyon with endless opportunities to explore.  We took a crostrek and it had no problems with the terrain.  We camped overnight in the canyon.  Lots of spots to camp.  We went on a weekend in summer, it was a bit hot but we had the place to ourselves.  Not a single other vehicle since we left the freeway.
Surprisingly we even saw a deer in the canyon.  There is more to this desert than first meets the eye.

Awesome trail that I was able to complete in a few hours. I took it easy and still somehow managed to lose a shock bushing and had to limp back to the interstate with a field repair. Be careful and go with a buddy and you’ll be fine, some areas of the trail are rough and somewhat technical.

Incredible Sunsets in isolation. We camped at the trailhead for 3 days and only saw one hiker
.

Be Aware -- the Teddy Bear looking cactus explode when you bump into them. If you take a short hike through terrain and bumb into one of those bad-boys your bound to get stuck. The needles have barbs, making the plucking process quiet painful.

name: Black Star Canyon Falls Trailhead
formatted_address: 13333 Black Star Canyon Rd, Silverado, CA 92676, USA

google_reviews.text: Black Star Canyon Falls is amazing — such a beautiful spot! Best OC Waterfall hike. The trail is a mix of flat walking about 2.5-3 miles, then a creek scramble, and the waterfall is totally worth it. There is even a cave pool we explored a year ago there. We checked it out three days after a big storm and shared it on our Hikebytes YouTube channel — with the upcoming storms, it’s only going to get even better!

One of my personal favorite trails here in Southern California! About 4 hours total. No restrooms. The actual trail itself is very easy and beautiful since it's a flat. Once you hit about the two and a half mile mark you'll be welcomed with a small but obvious pillar sign. Left to continue the trial, and right (where I assume most people go) to head towards the waterfall! The best way to describe the creek is a gradual increase in difficulty. It will be relatively "flat" in the beginning but you'll quickly find the need to use your hands and do mild to moderate climbing (Don't be afraid to use your hands!). Nothing like rock climbing, just going over big boulders and traversing slopes. Best part about the creek? choose your own path! there is no dedicated trail of course but there will be multiple trails left by foot traffic. There will also be ropes left by others to aid in your climbing/navigations once you get close to the waterfall.

From personal experience the best times to see the waterfall is usually winter/spring or a few days after a storm. I would avoid going to the waterfall during the summer b/c of the intense heat and no water flow but you do you.

Tips/my 2 cents:
>If your going to the waterfall, the first bridge you see goes over part of the creek. You see/hear moving water? Great! As of 1/8/26 there is A LOT of water and the waterfall is flowing beautifully!
Nothing or still water? 9/10 times it will be dry once you get up there.

>Bring at least 1-2L of water per person & snacks!

>Bring hiking shoes not boots! reason being shoes can dry out but boots hold in ALL the water. I brought my Tim's one time, not fun. (Accept the fact your feet will get wet or drenched and your life will be easier in the creek, navigation wise)

>Clothes -- just bring your outdoor clothes or clothes your okay getting dirty. Mostly applies to pants.
For tops bring something that covers your arms, there's a lot of foliage and branches + sun protection. You'll be completely fine with a t-shirt though.

>First-aid -- I can bet money you'll never use it if you take your time. But it never hurts and you can help others if they get cuts or scrapes!

>Creek water??? --- Advice I think everyone can benefit from;
For some reason I hear some people saying they want to drink the creek water once they get to the waterfall. I don't know why? Safe water IS DIFFERENT than clean water! If its not obvious, clean/clear water can and WILL carry life threatening diseases and bacteria in the creek/river. While dirty/muddy water can be safe to drink if treated properly via boiling for example. PLEASE PLEASE treat the creek water properly if you do wish to drink from there.

Thanks for reading! I'm just a dude who loves the outdoors, if you need a guide please do your own research!
Awesome hike. If you dont mind getting wet and a lil muddy. Some parts are tricky you will have to go up and over some things. Becareful pickbup after yourself and enjoy

It was a long hike. The round trip was about 4 hrs. The first half of walking was pretty easy but the second half was difficult, requiring you to climb rocks and hike. So prepare enough water. FYI, there is no restroom.

Unexpectedly fun. Initial 1.5 mile is easy and the rest 1.5 mile is hard for many as it involves a lot of climbing and rocks are slippery.
Please plan to take water, bug spray and sun screen and definitely some food for fuel.

We saw a lot of people with their dogs.

Do it with a group to have the best experience.

name: Crystal Cove State Park
formatted_address: California, USA

google_reviews.text:
When it's time to step out of life and into nature this is where the soul can rest, relax and recharge. This was my first time visiting this park and ot truly delivered joy and lots of peace. The Beautiful trails, restaurant, walk on the beach with just enough people around without feeling over whelmed. Parking is hourly, bathroom are clean and it's safe for all alike
If you need to recharge, check it out for the team Smiley experience 😀

Pretty cool place to visit during a super low tide. We came here just to check out the tide pools and they were really neat. We parked at the far right side parking lot and then walked from there downstairs to the other end and back. It was an easy walk and the views were really nice. The little walking path was also an easy walk and they have restrooms at each parking lot. For a little weekend day trip it was perfect. Next time we will pack a lunch and find a place to relax and eat. I would definitely recommend coming here on a low tide day. We will also come check it out in the summer because it was a really nice beach.

Great location for a winter hike for families and kids of all ages. Plenty of parking and bathrooms are easy to get to. Be careful going down to the beach as the trail can be slippery due to constant flow of water.
This park has lot to offer for anyone who wants to visit. Has historic beach connected to it and nice walking trails and amazing view from cove and above cove. Nice sunset view!!

Crystal Cove State Park is absolutely stunning! From the moment you arrive, the views of the ocean and coastline take your breath away. The hiking trails are well-maintained and offer a mix of easy strolls and more challenging climbs, all with incredible scenic overlooks. Walking along the beach, especially at low tide, reveals tide pools full of fascinating sea life.

The beach itself is pristine, with soft sand, clear blue water, and plenty of rocky areas for kids and adults to explore. There are several access points and parking lots, making it convenient to find a spot. The visitor center is helpful and welcoming, and the bathrooms are clean, definitely a plus!

We enjoyed stopping at the nearby restaurants along the beach for a meal while soaking in the ocean views. Whether you’re hiking, exploring tide pools, or just relaxing by the water, Crystal Cove is the perfect getaway. Highly recommend bringing a camera. The sunsets here are unforgettable!

name: Peters Canyon North Trailhead
formatted_address: 8548 Canyon View Ave, Orange, CA 92869, USA

google_reviews.text:
Highly recommended trail . Try the North loop for nice lake view and nice sunset view behind the mountains .

This is medium range hike and reach mid point on the hill top with nice sun and lake views . Pets are allowed. Kids are ok but I would recommend 6 plus kids only . I have seen Bob cats myself at this place so I would keep my pets with me in leash all the time . Avoid this Place during rains and evening after  sunset as it closes at sun set and not recommended to stay late   due to bugs after sunset.  Parking is there and have to pay for it . Parking fee is flat 3 dollar 💵. Min and max 3 dollar . Plan to spend 2 hours for one north trail around the lake  . If you want to cover the whole regional park then ya it would be 4 hours plus .  Parking is free on the other side in south end .

Medium Sized Hiking Area (Dog Friendly)
$3 Parking
Hiked in November

Biggest downside to this park is you have to pay $3 to park here.  Parking is spacious at least when I went on a Tuesday.

Terrain varies from relatively flat to pretty steep.  However it does offer great overlooks.

Plenty of places to rest or have a picnic

Dog friendly hiking.  There are several poo bag stations however not as many as some other parks. I suggest bringing your own.

The people I met on the trail all seem to be friendly.

Bicyclists and  Horses are allowed on a good portion of the trails offered.

If any of the information above was useful to you or any of the pictures that are part of my review gave you a good perspective of this  place please give my review a thumbs up so that I know it's worth the time making these for fellow travelers.
Great little hike to unwind and get in tune with nature. There's a variety of hikes intensity depending on your appetite. Just make sure to leave before sunset since they close the parking gates

At the entrance to Peters Canyon Regional Park, off of Canyon View Avenue. It’s at the beginning and end of the loop trail and connecting trails into the south side of the park.
As mentioned in the Peters Canyon Regional Park review, there’s little if any shade along the way. With higher temperature extremes, I’d suggest an early start and to dress accordingly!
Since the dirt trails may incline sharply, I’d suggest comfortable traction shoe soles for hiking and knobby tires for mountain bike riding.

$3.00 parking fee sold by machine per car. Trails combined easy, moderate, and difficult levels. You can choose the trail based on your own physical strength. Not very shady so it can be very hot for both humans and pets. Make sure to bring enough water with you. Views along the trails are generally nice.

name: Upper Newport Bay Nature Preserve
formatted_address: 2301 University Dr, Newport Beach, CA 92660, USA

google_reviews.text: What great free outdoor trails during lovely weather and some blooming flowers 🌼. There was an event earlier in the day so many spots were filled but opened up by afternoon. Trails were great and the views of the water below with the flowers was incredible. Could even make a full loop if intended with the shared bike path, but we decided only to do the west bluffs and the interactive museum. Plus you can get some nice photos of the overhead planes leaving John Wayne ✈️

Lovely views of Upper Newport Bay with easy walking trails. The nature center offers excellent interpretive exhibits to engage both adults and children, a video about the area, a small gift shop, staff to answer questions, and an activity room (plus, toilet facilities).  Free parking.

This place is really nice if you don't mind the sound of cars and airplanes. Free parking lot and street parking available. I love that it has both dirt trails and paved bike lanes which makes it convenient if you're on foot, bike, wheelchair, skateboard, etc. Restrooms available as well (very clean 🙂)

Had a bit of time to kill before lunch so we came here for a walk. Really nice trails with some pretty views. Saw quite a few birds and the interpretive centre was informative. Was really foggy when we went so couldn't see very far but still quite enjoyable. Parking was free so that was nice too.

Upper Newport Bay Nature Preserve was a peaceful and beautiful place I visited with someone. It is a nature preserve but it felt like a giant outdoor classroom full of discovery. I saw a lot of teenagers walking the trails taking pictures of the scenery and enjoying the quiet atmosphere. The atmosphere was serene and inspiring with birds chirping and views of the water. Teens would love the chance to explore and take nature photos. There is no formal service but the trails were clean and easy to follow. The value is excellent because it is completely free to enjoy the outdoors. The quality of the preserve was high with safe paths and beautiful views. The selection of trails and lookout points gives teens plenty to explore. I highly recommend it to teenagers who love nature photography and quiet exploration.

name: Quail Hill Trailhead
formatted_address: 34 Shady Canyon Dr, Irvine, CA 92603, USA

google_reviews.text: This is a nice well kept trail you can enjoy with your dog. If you are doing the short loop, it’s 1.8 miles and you can start on the left by working up high and then going downhill or you can go right and work from downhill going up. Personally I always take the left. I have encountered snakes on the trail on a few occasions and have even seen a rattlesnake once before, but they are well hidden and its is a rare to come across one. Best time to visit for flowers is from March to May and you will come across black mustard (the tall yellow flowering plants), cardoon, cheeseweed, mouse barley and musky storks bill to name a few. There is a seasonal pond on the low end of the trail but that will only have water after the rains and dries up fairly quickly. There is a telephone guide where you can call a number and enter a marker number for audio commentary on marked locations. Cell signal is strong so you should have no issues if you wish to use this service. There is a small picnic area at the start of the trail and well maintained restrooms. No shade on the trail so use sunscreen and take a hat during the hot summer months.

Quail Hill Trailhead ended up being such a refreshing and unexpectedly enjoyable place to visit, and I did not realize how much there was to appreciate until I actually stepped onto the trail. Even though it is a hiking area, the atmosphere feels peaceful and energizing at the same time, and I saw a lot of teenagers there enjoying the open space. Teens would love the atmosphere because it feels safe, open, and full of freedom. The wide views, the fresh air, and the long stretches of trail make it feel like a place where teens can explore, take pictures, and enjoy being outdoors without feeling restricted or watched. It has this natural beauty that makes you want to walk a little farther just to see what is around the next corner.

The service aspect at a trailhead is different from a restaurant, but the experience still feels organized and teen friendly. The signs are clear, the paths are clean, and the entire area feels well maintained. Teens appreciate when a place is easy to navigate without confusion, and Quail Hill makes that possible. The posted information about wildlife and safety feels helpful rather than intimidating, and it gives teens the confidence to explore the trail while staying aware of their surroundings.

The value at Quail Hill is something teenagers would appreciate immediately because it is completely free. Teens love places where they can hang out without spending money, and Quail Hill gives them a full experience without costing anything at all. They can walk, jog, bike, take pictures, talk with friends, or simply enjoy the scenery. It is the kind of place where teens can spend an hour or an entire afternoon and feel like they got something meaningful out of it without paying a single dollar.

The quality of the environment is another thing that stands out. The trail is clean, the views are beautiful, and the whole area feels peaceful in a way that teenagers would find refreshing. Teens who love taking pictures would have a great time here because the scenery looks almost unreal at certain points, with bright skies, rolling hills, and natural lighting that makes every photo look good. The environment feels like a perfect backdrop for creative pictures, and teens love places that make their photos look amazing without any effort.

The selection at Quail Hill comes from the different ways you can enjoy the space. Teens can walk, jog, bike, bring pets, take pictures, or simply sit and enjoy the view. It is not a place where you are limited to one activity. Instead, it gives teens the freedom to choose the pace and style that fits their mood. That kind of flexibility is something teens really appreciate because it lets them shape the experience however they want.

This was my first time visiting Quail Hill Trailhead before writing this review, and it ended up being one of the most enjoyable outdoor places I have been to in a long time. I would highly recommend that teenagers go there because the atmosphere is peaceful and energizing, the organization makes it easy to enjoy, the value is unbeatable, the quality of the scenery is beautiful, and the selection of activities gives teens a chance to feel creative and free. It is a perfect spot for teens who want something fun, refreshing, and memorable.

Wouldn’t recommend this trail during this time of year because of ticks. We had to remove 3 from our dogs within the first 7 minutes of walking. Besides ticks, there’s also foxtail — that got stuck between my dog’s paws and made it hard for her to walk. Other than that, the trail is beautiful and full of blooms.

Nice trail with plenty of parking. It is dog friendly and a pretty open space. Not much shade on the trail itself, but a little bit of shade around the parking area. There is men and women’s restrooms and it is ADA accessible. It is dog friendly if they are unleash, but does warn of coyotes in the area so be very cautious, especially if you have a small dog.

Beautiful and easy walk on the Quail Hill loop. Saw two herons and dozens of desert cottontails. The trail audio guide (accessible by phone menu) is pleasant and informative. I’d love to come back when the vernal pools are there, for example. Took our dog and she had a great time too. She would’ve loved to run off trail, but there be dragons (or at least rattlesnakes and ecosystems that are sensitive).

name: Bluff Top Trail
formatted_address: 34342 Street of the Amber Lantern, Dana Point, CA 92629, USA

google_reviews.text: I was looking for spots in Dana Point with great views! This did not disappoint! Started in a residential area and made it down to Doheny Beach and walked along the harbor. Gorgeous cliffs, houses and historical places.
A quick, short trail with a pleasant view. Great for getting some fresh air, walking your dog, or just general sight-seeing. There's a few cafes at the starting and end points of the trail.
This is a very short hike, you can go down from either end. It offers many scenic points and photography opportunities. We spend about 20min there. If you just walk, it's only about 8min.

During my recent visit to the Dana Point Bluff Top Trail, I was thoroughly impressed by this hidden gem nestled above the harbor. Despite its modest length of approximately 0.2 miles, the trail offers breathtaking panoramic views of the Pacific Ocean and Dana Point Harbor. The well-maintained path is adorned with historical markers, including the intriguing remnants of the 1920s Dana Point Inn arches, adding a unique charm to the experience. The trail is easily accessible, with free street parking available on Amber Lantern Street, though it's advisable to check for street sweeping schedules.

Dana Point Bluff Top Trail is an absolute gem for anyone looking to enjoy breathtaking coastal views and a peaceful walk. The trail is short and manageable, making it perfect for all ages and fitness levels. What makes this spot truly special is its panoramic views of the Pacific Ocean, especially during sunset – it’s like walking through a postcard!
The trail is well-maintained, with a few benches along the way to sit and soak in the scenery. Whether you’re looking for a quick solo escape, a romantic stroll, or a place to bring visitors, this spot has it all. Parking is convenient, and the surrounding area is beautiful as well.
If you’re in Dana Point, don’t miss this trail – it’s a perfect blend of relaxation, natural beauty, and coastal charm. Highly recommend!

name: Pacific Ridge Trailhead
formatted_address: Ridge Park Rd, Newport Beach, CA 92657, USA

google_reviews.text: I come here often since there are so many trails going out to the beach, through Crystal Cove, through Laguna Coast Wilderness, and can even connect to trails that cross the 133 freeway. Parking fills up pretty quick on weekends, but you can always drive back a bit and park along the road, just have to walk then to the trailhead. The trails you start on - Ridge Road or Bommer - are both very wide and well-maintained, and so are quite a few other trails. Not much shade, so make sure to bring a hat and apply sunscreen. If you go straight across, parallel to the 73, you'll get to the Willow Staging Area which has restrooms and a tap for drinking water. There are maps at each and every junction, so always easy to figure out which direction to go to get to your target. Dogs not allowed, and they check at a few places like the Moro Campground area.

In all trails they list this as the crystal cove double loop. It ends up being 12 miles if you do both loops. If you follow the trail on all trails be aware that some parts are pretty steep. It was a hard trail run and I had to walk some of the steeper uphill sections. We went on a Saturday morning. We got to the parking lot at 7 and it was full and cars were parked on the side of the road. When we finished a few hours later. There were even more cars. So this is a busy trail. There is one section that is a single track section that is pretty overgrown. Go early because this trail will get very warm. .

Challenging...Just 100% undoubtedly difficult. Steep up hills and steep downhills
Bikes jogs walkers runners . It's a true area that opens so many possibilities.  With so many trails, all of which are moderate to hard, for any sport.  Great views of the ocean,  of the pastures,  little critters everywhere and just flowing rolling hillsides. From the top,  boomers west entrance or ridgeline park trail or no name trail, runs on the west side. Yet,  there are over 10 trains that ultimately lead to the beach. Average mileage,  6 to 12 miles of exercise, and be ready yo sweat.  Parking at el moro beach or Ridgeline park or boomer nature park entrance. Take water snacks and be patient.  Excellent for scenic shots and for serious people into exercise or hikes.

Moderate to Hard trail. Some trails lead to the ocean/Crystal Cove. There are many people mountain biking so it's best not to wear headphones. Pack sunscreen, hat, sunglasses, and water! If you're allergic to 🐝 bring an epi pen just in case. Enjoy!

I tried a large loop clockwise.
The trail overlooking the ocean is very pleasant and the walking is light.
The second half is all uphill. There is no shade and it is best to go early in the morning or on a cloudy day. It is better to have plenty of water.
The load intensity gradually increases, so you will feel a sense of accomplishment as well as a fat-burning effect.
Time required
About 4 hours if you walk slowly while enjoying the scenery.

Posted photo date 2/18/2024

name: Crystal Cove Trail
formatted_address: Crystal Cove Trail, Newport Beach, CA 92657, USA

google_reviews.text:
Great pitstop along the PCH!
You can stay up on the boardwalk and see overlooks of the ocean or get access to the beach and have lots of access to the ocean below.
Peaceful morning and not a lot of people on a Thursday.
Very foggy but empty! Love it cuz it’s like your private beach. But parking is crazy expensive tho

This is such a spectacular place. I highly recommend it to go in the morning take your shoes off and you know ground yourself go for the hike literally hiked for 45 minutes marked up and down the stairs and it seemed like we were there for like 10.

Parked in lot to to head down to the beach. Easy parkyat 1145am on a Sunday. 20$ to park. It's a California state park. Easy walk of about 10 minutes to beach. Have your stuff set up to carry. A wagon will work most of the way but there are stairs at the end. No restrooms on the beach. Only at the trail head parking lot area. Some seasonal lifeguard stations but not full coverage of the beach. Lots 1 &2 have more tide pools to check out. No food available. Enjoy.

Beautiful trail with unbeatable scenery. Flowers in bloom, moderate inclines, ocean views, restrooms easy to locate, access to the beach and worth the $15 entry fee. Dogs not allowed on the beach but our Lily enjoyed her walk on the trail to the viewpoint. We will return.

name: Aliso and Wood Canyons Wilderness Park
formatted_address: 28373 Alicia Pkwy, Aliso Viejo, CA 92656, USA

google_reviews.text:
Not the typical exposed trails in soCal, Aliso and Wood Canyon trails are so comfortable to hike or bike due to many shaded path from Riparian environment. Huge oak trees. The main artery is the Aliso Creek trail which is paved as it branches out to Canyon trail which is more of dirt trail that has so many offside attractions like the dripping Cave and single tracks like coyote run. Most trails are for multi use and due to its popularity, I recommend going as early as sunrise to avoid the biker crowds. I will give a 5 if not for speeding e bikers. Says NO E-BIKES on the trails but 70% were using e-bikes. Those folks kept on overtaking even in canyon trail “eat my dust” style. This is a nature trail for everyone to enjoy not a freeway to race on. Pls respect the authenticity of experiencing real mountain biking

Today was specially awesome as the parking lot was free, otherwise the 3 usd won’t break the bank. Nice OC Park staff, very courteous and welcoming

I visited Aliso and Wood Canyons Wilderness Park with someone and was amazed by how peaceful and scenic the whole area felt. There were many teenagers hiking and taking pictures of the rolling hills and trees. The atmosphere felt calm and refreshing with the sound of birds and the wind moving through the leaves. Teenagers would love how freeing it feels because it gives them a break from noise and stress. The service at the entrance was helpful with clear maps and friendly staff. The value was excellent because it is free to explore. The quality of the trails and views was beautiful and everything felt natural and untouched. The selection of paths and lookout points gave teenagers many opportunities for adventure and photography. I have only been there once before writing this review but it felt like a place I would want to return to. I highly recommend that teenagers go there because it gives them a chance to connect with nature and enjoy a peaceful escape.

Spanning 4,500 acres with trails from easy to difficult, this park has something for hikers and bikers. The Visitor's center and parking lot are right off Alicia Pkwy and they have a good sized parking lot and restrooms. Limited parking is available on the street for free or $3, cash or credit card in their parking lot. From there you have to walk about 1 1/4 miles to access any trails.  A better option for hikers is to park at Canyon Vista park and enter the park from there so you don't have to walk so far to get into the park itself.  The first trail you will come to from that side is Cholla trail which is a good hike up to the West Ridge Trail. From there you can access many of the other trails in the park. Go straight to Car Wreck trail which is challenging and fun and has a real car wrecked on it! The most challenging trail I've found was 5 Oaks Trail which starts off easy and scenic but gets steep. Great view from the top though! If you want something shady and not steep try Wood Creek Trail.

I had the pleasure of exploring this beautiful trail for the first time. We chose the easier path, and the scenery in the canyon was absolutely picturesque. At the entrance of the park, there is ample parking and restrooms available, as well as portable bathrooms located midway through the hike. I highly recommend this hike for anyone looking to enjoy stunning views in a serene setting!

I can here with my girlfriend and 2 teenage boys. There was plenty of parking, it's pay but only $3 for the entire day. I went to the visitor center to ask s9me questions about the trails and general about this area. They were very helpful and even had a really cool 3D map that lights up with core locations.

Hiked for 5 miles up to dripping cave and a few stops on the way. We'll designated trails, many were easy to moderate. We'll kept paths and trails. I will definitely be coming back here to check out more of the trails. If you like to hike and be outdoors, this is an excellent place. I did also see many mountain bikers and a couple skaters. There is a main path that is paved and many other trails that are dirt. Great place to hike!

name: Dana Point Preserve
formatted_address: Nature Preserve and Conservation Parks, 34558 Scenic Dr, Dana Point, CA 92629, USA

google_reviews.text: I saw some of the most unique animals I've ever seen in a tide pool. I love it here. If you come please be careful where you step, and don't pick up the animals. Follow the leave no trace rules, don't take any shells or rocks, only trash!

We had such a great family experience tide pooling near the Ocean Institute. At low tide, the rocky shoreline behind the institute turns into an incredible little adventure zone — full of tide pools, sea life, and places for kids to explore safely (with supervision, of course).

This spot is especially fun with kids because it feels educational and exciting at the same time. We saw crabs, anemones, and all kinds of shells, and it was amazing watching the kids slow down and really observe what was happening in the pools. It’s a great extension of a visit to the Ocean Institute or even a destination on its own if the tide is right.

Tips for families:
•	Go only at low tide — check tide charts ahead of time
•	Wear tennis shoes or water shoes (the rocks are slippery)
•	Be prepared to get a little wet
•	Take your time and remind kids to look, not touch

It feels like a hidden gem tucked right into the harbor and is such a memorable, hands-on nature experience. We’ll absolutely be back the next time the tides line up.

My grandson and I stayed at Beachfront Inn. The room was really nice. We has a great view of the ocean.  It is in a great location! We had the very best receptionist. A shout out to DJ. Thanks for making our trip a smooth one. Your the Best!

This place is a hidden gem! Beautiful, natural protected coastal area. The trails are well marked and groomed. The visitors center at the top of the trails is small, but very nice. The staff were all volunteers who were very friendly and knowledgeable. We didn’t have much time to stay, but we learned a lot and really enjoyed it. We will definitely be back!

Beautiful beach side trial offering stunning views. We only walked a small section of the trail and wish there was more time to explore, but what we saw was already breathtaking. The entrance is through a somewhat hidden gate, but once your make your way through the tunnel it is like you are being transported into another world. The instant you get to the other side it becomes serenity, traffic and city noises are replaced with calming ocean waves and the slight breeze brings absolute peace.

name: Whiting Ranch Wilderness Park
formatted_address: 27901 Glenn Ranch Rd, Trabuco Canyon, CA 92679, USA

google_reviews.text: This is a great area for hiking. Can combine trails to do anywhere from couple of miles to 15 or so. My first time here was in mid-December - went with a meetup group. Parked in front of CVS then crossed Portola to get to the trailhead - the parking lot there is small and requires 3$ payment. We did Mustard trail to the gate, then back to Vista Lookout, then the loop around Billy Goat (lots of stairs) almost touching Santiago Canyon Rd, then back to the intersection to do a mile out-and-back to Red Rock Chateau, then back to the parking lot. It came to around 11 miles with a little over 2000 feet elevation gain. Very well maintained trails. If you're going after rains, make sure to check the OC parks website to see if the park is open. At Red Rock Chateau, there are clear signs to not cross the ropes, but people were still climbing the rocks to pose for social media photos, which was infuriating - please respect the wilderness.

Whiting Ranch is a great spot for biking, with lots of scenic trails. Just be careful — many paths have loose rocks, and it’s easy to slip if you’re not paying attention. I’d also recommend not going too late, because once it gets dark, visibility drops and it can get dangerous. Overall, it’s a beautiful place to ride, just stay safe!
It was really fun we saw a couple of deer along the trail
Loved hiking the trails at Whiting Ranch! The views were amazing, and it was so nice to get outside and enjoy nature. Definitely a great spot to unwind.

Beautiful hiking trail tucked next to residential and shopping centers. The trail goes quite a bit back. You can make the hike as short or long as you’d like, different options for trails. We went back to the red rock trail which wasn’t too difficult. Lots of birds if you are into birdwatching. Woodpeckers, blue jays, hawks and hummingbirds are some that we saw. The first mile in is mostly shaded under trees so that is a plus.

name: Huntington Beach Santa Ana River Trail
formatted_address: Huntington Bch Bike Trl, Huntington Beach, CA 92646, USA

google_reviews.text: Ran the trail and so wish I had the bike to do the entire trail.  It connects to Huntington Beach Trail.   Safe, clean, secluded, quiet.  If running you have pavement or crushed sand/gravel.  Well worth it and is away from the hustle bustle of beach trail !  Didn’t want to pay 15.00 at start of trail so found neighborhood parking.

This is a really, really nice trail. Especially for quiet bike riding without any street traffic, vehicles, people, everything.   The issues, for me, were absolutely zero lights and clearly marked trail exit signs.
Great bike trail to beach. Not too many hills. Peaceful ride. Beach area is nice too with great views for sunset.

A hidden gem of Southern California, this trail has been beautifully landscaped since I was a kid in Huntington Beach in the 70s, but still has wildlife and birds. At high tide, the river is filled with wildlife and even fish, all the way to Adams street. Park here, ride in and go all the way to the beach along the river trail. Cross the bridge and hike the Fairview park which has a grassy area with barbecue picnic tables and restrooms. Dog friendly!! Stay away at night, coyotes and homeless people… but very safe in daylight. Beautiful sunsets here!

Best  bike 🚴 path in oc that connects inner cities to Huntington Beach state beach , starting paint can be yorba  Linda regional park or Anaheim transportation center free parking on both location 30 to 40 mile return trip to and from beach ..

name: Willow Staging Area
formatted_address: 18751 Laguna Canyon Rd, Laguna Beach, CA 92651, USA

google_reviews.text: Gentle slope up to ridge. Beautiful views. You can park here for 3 bucks, hike over the ridge down Laguna bowl and shop or eat lunch before hiking back or taking tram back if you bought too much.

Temperatures in 80s with pleasant breezes
Definitely recommend wearing closed shoes as ther are numerous red ants on the trails!
The terrain varies and offers wonderful views
I took the LAKE BARBARA TRAIL which is one of the few NATURAL lakes in The OC and ventured up to the top RIDGE TRAIL that takes you all the way around the perimeter and back down to where you begin at the WILDERNESS LODGE, if that's where you park to begin your trek.
Plenty of water fowl at the lake to observe and birds in the trees and bushes.
Some squirrels and that's it for fawna this visit.
Trails were not crowded as this was midmorning and midday on a warm day.  For those that love having the place to yourself, it's perfect!
Quite the mix of vegetation from very green and new to the extremely dry and brown.
The maple trees looked regent against the hills.
There were some oaks along the lake and acorns on the ground.
Well formed and traveled trails and some are steep on the way down as the trail traverses but with some rocks to act as stoppers it helps those who need to be more cautious.
Loved the red dirt as the trail gains altitude.
The staff at the lodge are very helpful and friendly!
Bradley was helpful with change for the $3 parking station if you don't have your membership card.
Laura is very knowledgeable and engaging to chat with especially!  She renewed my card as well.
$50 for Seniors like me for OC PARKS
Hope you enjoy!

This trail is awesome! You gain a lot of elevation in the beginning so be prepared for some serious cardio. There's a lot of elevation change with the rolling hills that span the entirety of the hike. There is a bunch to see and overall it's an amazing hike especially during May when it hasn't been too hot and the costal breeze is so refreshing. I traveled the entire 7.6 miles and man is it one hell of a work out especially with 26lbs on your back.

The views of the hike are pretty impressive and at times you forget you are smack dab in the middle of the OC. There's a good amount of scenery to look at and the costal overview is awesome!

Note!

Just make sure you get your car out of the paid lot by 5pm otherwise you will get closed in and may get cited! It happened to me as I came short on time but luckily the parks were gracious enough to give me a warning.

Nature area with a ton of hiking/biking trails. There are various trails for different fitness levels, and the maps indicate the level of difficulty. We took the Willow trail to the Bommer trail to Laurel Ridge trail for a large loop that was approximately 5.7 miles. The trails do have steep climbs at certain parts, but are moderate for the most part. The trails are mostly exposed with very little shade. The paths are wide enough for groups to exercise together, and I did note quite a few families with small children. You can see the ocean off in the distance and the breeze can be quite pleasant. The ground is very compact, although note that the Laurel Ridge trail coming down can be a bit technical.

Parking lots are available but they fill up quickly; it’s likely that you’ll need to wait for someone to leave or to try a different lot if you arrive past 9am on a weekend. Otherwise, you can park across the street on the highway, but he sure to read the posted signs. Credit cards are accepted at the pay station ($3 for parking). Porta-potties are available with foot pump sinks.  Overall, this place provides lots of opportunities to explore nature and get some fresh air.
Great hiking area. Get there earlier on the weekends before the lot fills up.

name: San Clemente Beach Trail
formatted_address: San Clemente Pedestrian Beach Trail, San Clemente, CA 92672, USA

google_reviews.text: Love this beautiful ⛱️ beach. Nice, peaceful, quiet and not too crowded coming from beaches in San Diego.  Clean  area. There a pier to walk out to and a restaurant at the beginning of the pier... beach is also nice for barefoot walking

A one-of-a-kind gorgeous walking trail that runs parallel in both directions to the beautiful darker turquoise ocean waters. Truly lovely landscaping, very thoughtful addition of amenities (restrooms, pet stations, restaurants) and many magnificent residential structures aligning the often sheer cliff sides of the trail. Parts of the trail feel a bit more isolated even though you are just feet off the beach shores with lots of people relaxing and frolicking all along the coast. Please be prepared to greet several folks walking, jogging, cycling, and walking their pets along this trail which is 2.3 miles long in each direction. Highly recommended although please don't forget to bring your hats, sunglasses and sunscreen with you as there is almost no shade anywhere along the trail
Nice and quiet on the Thursday that we came on. A very clean beach compared to what I’m used to in OC and LA!

Fantastic!!! A California dream🏝☀️Waves & surfers 🏄‍♂️🌊.Gorgeous sunset! Perfect place for fun,walking,swimming in the Pacific Ocean.At the end of the trail enjoy the good seafood cooked in the restaurants around.I went there many times and i had fun and relax always. Highly recommended 🤙hang loose⭐

Great place for a run.  No dogs, few kids and few bicycles due to the fact that most of it is hard packed sand...  In San Clemente, west-to-east  you have the ocean, the sandy beach, the Amtrak rail line (not many trains go by but they do have safety gates that go down) and THEN the Beach Trail that I ran on (there is a portion if you head south from the pier where the trail is before the rail bed, but you cross over after about 1/2 mile)

name: Canyon View Staging Area
formatted_address: 22124 Utility Access, Aliso Viejo, CA 92656, USA

google_reviews.text: Been here multiple times to hike Carwreck and to get to Top of the World via Rock It or Lynx. Trails are all well marked and easy to follow. West Ridge and Wood Creek are very wide trails, so you'll encounter mountain bikers. Top of the World is around 3-4 miles from this trailhead depending on whether you take West Ridge or Rock It.

This is the easiest access to Wood Canyon. The main trail is wide and gradual. The side trails along the creek are awesome and shaded. Absolutely beautiful this is one of my favorite canyons in OC. The other side trails are awesome and steep but beware of mountain bikers, a few mtb-ers are nice and share the trails with hikers but most are aggressive, rude and bull over you, keep eyes open cuz they sneak up and don’t ring bells. Otherwise they are great trails with views and good incline.

Great starting place to take a medium length hike. Has many trails. Some lead to the dripping cave and can also go to the top of the world. Free parking in the area. Only one portable bathroom available, no running water, but was pretty well kept.
Beautiful hiking & park, toilet facilities lacking (one under-serviced outhouse).

If you need to recharge, disconnect, and remember just how close you live to nature, this is the hike you're looking for. Because it is not at all strenuous, you will slow down and hear yourself breathe. You will also be immersed in a visual and olfactory experience of the local native flora and fauna. There may be deer, a bob cat, quail  or woodpeckers. California sycamore are abundant. And although they are not redwoods, they will still surprise you with their majesty. Skip the spa day. Go into the woods. You'll thank me later.

name: Laguna Coast Wilderness Park
formatted_address: 18751 Laguna Canyon Rd, Laguna Beach, CA 92651, USA

google_reviews.text: Had a wonderful time here. Hiked the Laguna Ridge trail for about an hour. Loved the fact that there was a lot of uphill, curves and different terrain. Got some great shots. The view of the ocean was amazing. Learned about the Pacific Pocket Mouse, didn't see any trash, and people were generally nice and said hello. Word of advise, if going solo and have noise cancelling headphones on; be alert of the cyclist because they come in hot. I took mine off and didn't need music. The breeze was singing a real sweet melody.

A lovely train through the canyons. It’s certainly a long incline at first so be prepared to be properly hydrated. Once you make your way up, you get to see nice 360 views of canyons, mountains, Pacific Ocean and Orange County in the distance. Spring is best the time to hike as you get to see a lot of greenery.

There are many beautiful wild flowers here. The scenery is very beautiful. The location is close to the beach. After hiking, go to the beach for a walk, feel the sea breeze, and have a brunch. It is perfect.

$3 parking for the day. The trails are either very easy or very difficult/steep. The “moderate” trail is very long and steep and slippery. Bring excellent hiking shoes, water, hat, sunglasses and sunblock.

I saw cacti, hummingbirds, various flowers and lizards.

Nix Nature center has a 3D map, displays and bathrooms.

Little intense, about 4.7 mile, first half of the trail is high elevation. First 1 Mike too around 25 mins.

We liked the view on the top of the trail

name: Hilltop Conservation Park
formatted_address: 34392 Street of the Green Lantern, Dana Point, CA 92629, USA

google_reviews.text: One of the best views in South Orange county.   There is a 360 degree view. and can see as far north on the coast line from Palos Verdes, Long Beach , to the south at San Mateo point in a sunny clear day, wich we get a lot of those.
Went to the top of the hilltop park. Parking was roadside. Mesmerizing views of the pacific. It was quite chilly around 3PM but a must visit place.
Love this place come here twice a month so beautiful love the little trail.

A very short walk with spectacular views of Dana Point Harbor and beaches south.
It's a view of forever.
Beautiful views on the Pacific Ocean.

name: Coastal Peak Park
formatted_address: 20403 E Coastal Peak, Newport Coast, CA 92657, USA

google_reviews.text: The trails around the park are what make this place so awesome. Its quite a haul to hike down to Crystal Cove from here, but definitely a great hike from Coastal Peak (8 miles each way). The park itself is also very nice, especially if you play baseball, basketball, or want to take the kids to the very nice playground.

We have been coming to this Park Since 2009. Back then barely anyone knew about it or would go. Now it's really popular and gets packed.We mostly park on the street then go hiking on the trails. Nice for mountain biking and hiking mainly. It's really nice they also have baseball fields, basketball and picnic areas. Very scenic and nice views of the mountains!

Great playground with covered gazebos and nice clean restroom. Easy parking on the streets. Easy access as well.
We got a nice workout there 💪

Update:  so many trails up and down!! Pick your distance!! 3.5-mile loop with great views and great hills!!!

Wonderful for hiking! Has a playground for kids and restrooms. Also has benches and tables. Would recommend 100%! Trails can be slightly hard for those who aren't physically active as much though,  but not too bad. You should try and decide for yourself.

Any park with well kept, moderately challenging trails that rewards the intrepid hiker with tantalizing views of the Pacific Ocean deserves 5 stars.  It reminded me about all the things I love about hiking outdoors.

name: Peters Canyon South Trailhead
formatted_address: Tustin, CA 92782, USA

google_reviews.text: Its so beautiful at Peters Canyon. There are several ways to walk the trails.
