There are dry streams along the way(we didn't think there would be any water in the grotto) but there was! The deepest is about 2 feet and the shallow area about 4 inches.

It was nice and everyone was wearing masks. We stayed for a while and then realized that we were in for that 90% uphill climb that was so easy coming down from.

name: Sage Ranch Park
formatted_address: 1 Black Canyon Rd, Simi Valley, CA 93063, USA

google_reviews.text: Marvelous open space with trails and views in every direction.  I didn't get to do much on the trail system as I jad a tired dog that was not interested in any more hiking but they looked very well maintained.  Lovely setting for contemplation or use of the picnic tables for a lunch.  Simi Valley on one side, San Fernando Valley on the other.  Wonderful.  And and added bonus Box Canyon road on to Woolsey Canyon road is a cool drive that reminds me of the Topanga Canyon which is right around the corner.  I'll be back and go there first with the dogs so I can explore more.  Highly recommended.

Beautiful hiking area ❤️  Pretty family friendly too, my 2 year old daughter and my 3 year old niece had no problems with the smaller loop! In the past I have brought my older nieces and nephew here. They had so much fun exploring the rocks and taking in the views!

We came and did the full loop it's about a 2000 foot elevation make sure you have the proper footing you will be going uphill downhill over rocks and on loose gravel. We also brought the dogs great time very peaceful great place to clear your head have a great hike.

We had an amazing Cub Scout event!! 2 nights with over 70 people. Safely conducted with a great 3 mile loop trail hike. Only one bathroom nearby was weird, but that was our bad for not calling ahead to have cleaned out. Overall a great location to camp and all within a short drive if living in the San Fernando Valley!! :-))

Hike or bike here! It's worth it. It's already elevated so you will see the valley and skyline. Sunset is very pretty here. There are some benches here you can bring your snacks. Hike is beginner to intermediate level. Do wear hiking shoes. It gets pretty sunny here and no shade, sunscreen is a must. Parking has a fee, self service $3. Free parking is available right at the entrance.

name: Eagle Rock
formatted_address: E Topanga Fire Rd, Malibu, CA 90265, USA

google_reviews.text:
A good destination to hike to, but it appears other guests might not know about the seven principles of Leave No Trace. Graffiti, trash, and people playing music cut my stay on top of Eagle Rock short.

Be careful of where you step!

We had a wonderful summer solstice hike, starting from Trippet Ranch (street parking, because the lot is closed for construction), along Musch/Backbone trail, to Eagle Rock, and then back along the fire road. Most of the Musch trail has been recently and beautifully cleaned by the Sierra Club, but some of the higher up portions are pretty badly overgrown.
A little steep till you get to trail. Google map ok not great once park starts. No trail markings but road clear

This beautiful rock is called Eagle rock due to shape. Its a easy walk up over the rock, which allows a nice panoramic view of the mountains and Pacific ocean. As it faces the West, it looks glowing during golden hours. It's located on the Backbone trail in Santa Monica Wilderness.

Easily hiking trail, and also it uses for mountain biking. some are moderately steep. Nature and small wild games are flourished across Topanga State Park.   You will see deer, rabbits, wildflowers, and rock formations.  Go there on a clear sunny day that you can see the Paciic Ocean.  Parking fee for day uses cost 10 dollars.

name: Temescal Peak
formatted_address: Pacific Palisades, CA 90272, USA

google_reviews.text: Temescal Peak is accessible to hikers and bikers via a number of trails, including the Backbone Trail. It rises above the surrounding hills so you get great views! At the base of the peak itself, there are a few different trails you can use to make the short trek to the top.

Nice conditioning hike from Will Rogers State Park. Once you leave the park a lot less crowded. About 80% percent open sun so bring plenty of water, sunblock and pace yourself. Trail is a steady climb to peak and back. Great views of Santa Monica, Century City, and LA skyline beyond. Parking is $12 at the park and plentiful so look for street spot if available.

Quick hour-ish hike from the Split Rock trailhead. You can take the fire road almost all the way to the peak, or jump onto the little, more strenuous hiking paths that enter and exit the fire road throughout the hike. The views are pretty sweet. I happen to go on a day that the city was covered in smog, while the ocean was covered in fog.

Went from Hub Junction to Eagle Rock then back... Passed Temescal Peak on my way towards views of Santa Monica Bay and ended up at Skull Rock. TP is only around 70ft. above Temescal trail and not really worth the aggravation of trying to find the trail up to a "summit" with the same views you basically get from below. Hard Pass... Want Santa Monica Bay views? Trek about 2 miles down and there's an unmarked trail that goes up to a Vista with views of the Santa Monica bay and Downtown LA. Total distance from the Top of Reseda to this point and back.. 13.22 miles
One of my fave hikes. Park in Marvin Braude. Most of the trail looks like a wide fire escape route, so easy to walk on. Will take 2-3hrs enjoyable walk.

name: Conejo Valley Botanic Garden
formatted_address: 400 W Gainsborough Rd, Thousand Oaks, CA 91360, USA

google_reviews.text: Beautiful gardens with plenty of trails. Love how everything is organized by region. Tons of well placed benches to rest and a take in gorgeous views. Very well maintained and clean.

One of my new favorite places! Free to enter and lots to see and do. It seems like there are several hiking trails. This place also has specialty gardens as well as a bird habitat and butterfly garden. The children’s garden is only open Sunday mornings, but the rest of the grounds is always there for you to explore. Keep in mind, the hills are relatively steep, so the hikes could be moderately challenging for some. Another cool things is that a lot of the plants are labeled, plus there are plaques with literacy and activity suggestions for kids.

I don’t know how we missed this place in all the years we have been finding something to do on weekends. Really cool garden with lots of hiking trails and super neat flowers, plants and trees to see! It’s free too! The only con is parking is limited in the parking lot so you want to get there early. There is also a large restroom with flushing toilets/sinks outside of the garden. On Sundays they have the children’s garden open along with a small plant sale from 11am-3pm. You can take the trail from the children’s garden and it loops into the main garden. We enjoyed this much more than hiking in Malibu Creek.

I’m embarrassed to say I almost let the aerial turn me away. “There’s too much city around it - that won’t be the hike we are looking for.” Happily proved wrong! Not only was the nature trail perfect for us (about one mile) but we were so impressed with the views and with the great variety of plantings to admire. This isn’t a fancy botanic gardens (also free to enter) but you can see the love that went into the preparation of each area. Help these guys out and go visit. (Pro tip: restrooms are at the community center)
Beautiful trails, gardens, creeks and views. Definitely will return.

name: Corriganville Park
formatted_address: 7001 Smith Rd, Simi Valley, CA 93063, USA

google_reviews.text: Has a nice single track trail across from the train tracks that leads to Rocky Trail across the bridge above the freeway about 1.5mi away. Sign says the gates open at 6. Got here at 6:25 and no one around. Saw many cars parked near Rocky Trail roads near the freeway entrance and exit area. Portapotties here but no water fountains.

This was a beautiful park to hike in. There was a labyrinth you can walk that is very relaxing and you can meditate or just have a quiet moment. The hiking trails were nice and the scenery was  spectacular. There were also placards throughout the trails that described were movies were filmed. So, a lot of fun if you are a film buff to see all these filming locations. If you are in the area definitely visit here. I had a great time.

this park is a long dirt trail with many paths. 70% of the time i didn’t know where i was going, i wish there was some directional signs. however this park does have some fun unique features, signs describing the old movie sets here as well as nature facts, and there’s an old horse stage? and a labyrinth i liked. wasn’t a personal favorite of mine but is still interesting.
This park is where they shot some old movies and TV shows. You can see exactly where the Lone ranger reared his horse in the theme song. It was so cool

This is a fun family experience. We went to see the poppies that have blossomed this spring following a rainy California winter. We traveled to the Antelope Valley last year to see the poppies, but this is much closer. Nature is beautiful here, and there's movie history to see here, too. There are several easy walking trails that are stroller-friendly. The poppies are at the end of the main trail that leads out of the parking lot, on the side of a hill adjacent to the east-bound 118 Ronald Reagan Freeway. We took snacks and rested at a picnic table. There are many beautiful and shady oak trees around.

name: El Matador State Beach
formatted_address: 32350 Pacific Coast Hwy, Malibu, CA 90265, USA

google_reviews.text: Great spot to stop by for a picnic! We didn’t anticipate a short hike but it definitely was a short and sharp decline down some sand and then some stairs. Totally worth it for the iconic views. We explored down the beach a little bit and saw more rock outcroppings in the water. It wasn’t super busy. Parking was $3 in a gravel lot and they did have porta potties.

One of the best beaches ever! Love going inside the little caves. Park on the main road for free. Watch for signs you’ll have to walk several minutes extra. $3 an hour parking or $10 all day for the lot.

Beautiful beach with interesting rock formations including caves and tunnels. Parking is limited and you have to pay (like most state beaches). There is a little hike down to the beach that is a bit steep in places but short and very worth it.

The place was so beautiful and serene, with amazing and mesmerizing views. It’s a perfect stop if you’re driving along the Pacific Coast Highway. There’s a small paid parking area, and you can walk down the stairs to reach the beach.

Malibu here we come this was an absolute lit spot the waves were nice and the beach wasn’t too crowded. Also learned that they filmed some of the notebook here which is mind blowing. Only downside there is not a single restaurant within 30 mins of Malibu sooo that sucks. Not even  McDonalds so eat before or bring your own food.

name: Nicholas Canyon Beach
formatted_address: 33850 CA-1, Malibu, CA 90265, USA

google_reviews.text: Pay kiosks not working, so i went ahead with not paying. Asked a few others and they seem to have the same experience. Very private feel to this beach as you can't really hear or see traffic on PCH. Has an elevated pic nic area with 4 tables, great for a group. Cove is small yet long and extends northward to another beach.

Went midday on a chilly weekday, just to have a picnic by the beach. It was empty, and quiet, and beautiful. The kids ended up playing in the water and had a great time. We saw pelicans, sand pipers, and a couple sea lions popped their heads out of the water. The only drawback is a couple of the ones who went into the water ended up getting some sort of tar stick to the bottom of their foot.

Beautiful beach!  Surf or Boogie board beach. The break near shore churns rocks, making swimming entry painful and hazardous.
Clean plenty of parking (paid check website for cost)  5 minute  walk down to the beach from the 1st parking lot. There are some spots closer near the lifeguard  stand. Stairs down to the beach. Not crowded  usually.

Love this place. 1st time here. Quit clean good place to meditate. Went back again spent 6 hrs there. Love this place. My meditations I use hear you'll need something to lay down on. Vagus Nerve Exercises to rewire your brain from anxiety. This is great for beginners Allen Watts-Guided Meditation ( Awakening the mind). Both on YouTube. Went back again but no more huts water washed them out. That's okay I'm still coming here.

Beautiful place for picnic and swimming, parking is only $10. But restrooms are mobile plastic toilets 🙈 the only negative thing about this beach , also traffic will be super heavy if you are driving after 9am even on weekends

name: El Pescador State Beach
formatted_address: 32860 Pacific Coast Hwy, Malibu, CA 90265, USA

google_reviews.text:
El Pescador is one of Malibu’s quieter, more peaceful beaches — part of the Robert H. Meyer Memorial State Beaches — and it’s a great spot if you want a less-crowded alternative to El Matador.

The parking area is easy to navigate, with a pay station and a short walk down to the beach. There are a few picnic tables with beautiful ocean views, and the trail down is wide, natural, and manageable for most visitors.

Even on busy days, this beach feels calm and open, with plenty of space to explore tide pools, enjoy the bluffs, or just relax by the water.

A few helpful notes:

Parking is paid (machine is right at the lot entrance)

Restrooms are available near the parking area

The walk to the beach is sloped dirt path — sturdy shoes recommended

Great spot for photography, picnics, and scenic coastline views

Overall, a beautiful stretch of Malibu coastline and a perfect place for a quiet beach day. I’ll definitely be back.

Super cute beach.  Very lowkey, and definitely has a private beach feel.  Enough sand to lay out a blanket, and chair, and relax.  Lots of seaweed gets tossed up & not reclaimed by the Ocean...but nothing intolerable. Tide pools on either end (very cool🤓) and rocky areas on either side, so be careful swimming (maybe use water shoes to be safe if swimming near the rocky ends).  Per usual in California, the water is FREEZING.  However it is very clean & was pleasant to dip my feet in while strolling along the edge of the ocean.  If you do swim, the waves are STRONG & powerful & it looks like rip currents are running thru. Also there is no lifeguard stand, so be safe & careful 🌊

Meanwhile, a lot of people have described the hill & stairs that bring you from the bluff to the beach, but I dropped in pictures so you can really see. Its not far at all.  However, you definitely want to put on hiking sandals/shoes or sneakers to walk down & back up.  Its not terrible tho...just, please avoid any slide type shoes/sandals.  Also if you have any knee or mobility issues, grab a hiking/walking stick to support you, and you'll be good!  The stairs & rail are sturdy, they just look intimidating.  Go slow, you'll be fine😊

As everyone has stated, parking is very interesting.  There's a tiny lot at the top of the bluff ($10/day) - otherwise you have to find parking along the PCH, then walk back to the park entrance.  (Watch for the "don't park" Signs!)

This was a great visit & definitely has been added to my list of fave Malibu beaches.  I can see myself coming here even after summer is over, just to sit, and write & meditate.  Zuma & Point Dume were my go-to's prior.  Super happy to have another to add to that list. 🙌🏾🥳

🚨Ps if you're preggers, if you drove to Malibu & drank your bodyweight in caffeine otw, or if you just in general have a finicky bladder, pick another beach - unless you're cool w/sprinting up 50-ish stairs + a quarter mile 75° incline + another 200 meters to get to the porta-potty🚻😬🥹 ...there are not any down on the beach.

Visited this beach and it was good! We got lucky with parking which is $10 I believe we paid. It’s a small lot so you may have to find street parking if no spot is available. The trek to the beach is a pretty steep hill down and then a flight of stairs but the beach itself is really nice, and secluded. We went in the afternoon so there wasn’t too many people. (Note: porta potty’s are in the parking lot)

This beach is not really crowded. There are many people but you have enough space for privacy. However I did go in the afternoon time during a weekday. I came here to read a book on my birthday last year. You do need to pay for parking I think it was around $3-7. Very peaceful. Getting here there is curves you need to turn on so be careful if you get car sick easily.

Great place to relax. Not overpopulated,not dirty,water feels sooo good and calming. Only drawback are the rocks in the water so be careful. There are no lifeguards here so swim at your own risk. Bring your own food because there is no place to get food here.

name: Lechuza Beach
formatted_address: Sea Level Dr, Malibu, CA 90265, USA

google_reviews.text: Secluded beach perfect for low-tide swim and surf. Very soft sand, soft ocean floor save for some rocky areas and kelp. Lovely sunset, gets a little chilly after 6pm so bring layers. Access through residential Malibu streets. Gorgeous and not crowded✨

Scenic and secluded beach that’s not bombarded with too many people. Beautiful neighborhood and easy parking. However, there was a security guard patrolling the beach and attempting to delegate private/public areas. As well as, residents may become annoyed. However, I’d like to remind them that beach property is maintained and belongs to the Mountains Recreation & Conservation Authority.
This beach is excellent! Feels very private and secluded from the road and highway. There is a staircase to go down to the beach from the road, and it’s really beautiful and relaxing.

Love this beach!  Only downfall is there’s no public bathrooms or lifeguards on duty.  Also, if you have any physical impairments there’s only a staircase to access the beach at this locale. And if you do decide to trek down, be careful as the staircase can be a little slippery at times.

Absolutely exquisite beach with sand rocks tide pools and incredible calmness and beauty!
Parking is located on residential with respect easy to travel down the path and two staircases to the sand.

name: County Line Beach, Malibu
formatted_address: County Line Beach, Malibu, California 90265, USA

google_reviews.text: Nice clean beach, not overcrowded so it was very relaxing and tranquil, good waves so there was entertaining surfing to watch and enjoy, enjoyed my time there.

Awesome stretch of oceanfront beach! A rare spot at the ocean surf where parking is free, surfing is good and the views are unobstructed.
There’s lots of sand beach to find space to layout on within view of our parked vehicles.
Early surfers get the choicest spots, while latecomers may need to start back across from the Neptune’s Net restaurant to park and then walk boards over to a desired location. Summertime may be very crowded, tho with lifeguards in attendance. Fall thru autumn visits here have been ideal, even at mid-day!
While I didn’t notice a lifeguard stationed here, I did see a roving patrol to help oversee the safety during the offseason.
The restrooms are substituted with port-o-potty’s, with additional options available across the highway back at Neptune’s Net while going to get prepared food.
I’d urge caution when parking alongside either side of Pacific Coast Highway, leaving enough space to step out of the driver’s side from fast moving traffic. Also being mindful both ways if when crossing the highway.

West facing rocky point break. year round hard rights. Firmly placed within my Southern California top spots. And when procuring rations becomes necessary, Neptune's Net is cooking lunch daily right across PCH.

Nice beach with beautiful California sunsets.  Park on the street and walk down.  This beach can be popular with local surfers.   Neptune's Net is across the street for food and drink.  It isn't that crowded.  Spectacular view from the bluffs on top.

Nice beach. Arrived at the beach at 0800 and parking was filling up. Appears to be a lot of PCH side parking, but with nice weather and beautiful beaches there can never be enough parking. The break is a mix of sand and stone, which seems to help with the shape of the waves. There is a sand beach with space for towels and beach umbrellas if that's your thing. Surf was 3-5' and water temperature was ~62, which was fine spring suit weather on a sunny day. Surf was crowded and with only a couple of take off spots, two or three good surfers were catching all the waves. That said, there were enough leftovers for everyone.

name: North Beach
formatted_address: North Beach, California 90265, USA

google_reviews.text:
One of the few dog friendly beaches around. It requires pets to be on leash but not everyone adheres to this. It's not too crowded usually that it matters much and all dogs I have seen there are friendly. A lifeguard will occasionally come check on the beach and give warnings.

There is a pay station parking lot with a switchback pathway down the bluff. There is also parking in zones along the highway with a second stair access point further east towards the main beach.

Seems to have some nice smaller waves for surfing. I typically see at least a handful of people in the water.
What a great place to visit. Got here by accident, but I enjoyed it. Living in Ca for all my life , but had never visited this location. It’s truly a hidden gem. My doggy was happy too

Parking lot has 20-25 spots, $3/hr or $12 all day. There is a narrow path that saddlebacks down to the beach, and the beach itself is pretty thin with a lot of rocks separating the dry "chill" areas from the waterfront. Unfortunately there were a lot of offleash dogs even though leashes are required, and I saw some of the bigger ones go after leashed tiny dogs.

Dogs need to be free. It's a shame people are so petty and complain if a dogs off leash.

This is where our city officials always drop the ball.

There needs to be more #offleash pet friendly beaches.
Great pet friendly beach.  Nice place to visit.

name: La Piedra Beach
formatted_address: Parking lot, La Piedra Beach Rd, Malibu, CA 90265, USA

google_reviews.text:
La Piedra Beach is one of Malibu’s most beautiful and underrated stretches of coastline. Tucked between El Matador and El Pescador, it has the same dramatic rock formations and crystal-clear water — but with far fewer crowds. It’s the perfect spot for photos, quiet walks, and enjoying the natural side of Malibu.

As a local real estate agent who lives and works in the area, I always appreciate finding those places that feel like “true Malibu.” La Piedra is definitely one of them. Parking is easier here than at the neighboring beaches, and the views are spectacular at any time of day. Highly recommend if you’re looking for one of Malibu’s most peaceful beaches.

Nice beach spot, parking is cheap compare to others beach along the coast. a little bit of walk to go down the beach, and the terrain is a bit steep on some section. So be careful with kids or if you have a lot to carry. But, the trail is well maintain and clean.
It was a good time. enough space on the beach, not too crowded.

I loved it!!!!!!
It was a slightly steep slope before reaching the beach. Then there was a natural shack.
I sometimes really think what if no need to worry about living, eating to survive, and distance. I wanna keep walking to every corner of the world.

An often overlooked beach just north of trancas Canyon shopping center. So all along the SoCal Coast they took out one possibly two mega Mansions to allow the public to have the view. Nice flat parking lot decent porta potty and a short little hike down to the beach provides gorgeous views

Low tide is best for walking or hanging out at this beach. Great overlooking views. You can park on PCH if the lot is full or don't want to pay. Look for one of the three picnic benches at the top for a nice view while you eat. Path way is not maintained and steeper than average so pack light.
Pro-tip: during low tide (

name: Puerco Beach
formatted_address: Puerco Beach, Malibu, CA 90265, USA

google_reviews.text: DISGUSTING ABSOLUTELY SAND FLEA INFESTED DONT COME HERE UNLESS yhu ARE OK WITH MORE FLEA THAN SAND IT WAS A NIGHTMARE DO NOT TAKE UR GF HERE
A beautiful and peaceful beach with breathtaking ocean views! Puerco Beach is ideal for walking, relaxing and taking photos. A perfect place to relax, have a picnic or watch the sunset by the sea.

name: El Matador State Beach
formatted_address: El Matador State Beach, Malibu, CA 90265, USA

google_reviews.text: Great place to spend the day or just a brief visit. Parking fills up quickly plus you need to pay. Not sure if there is an overflow lot. Lots of steps to reach the beach so maybe not for everyone. Highly recommended.

name: Original Muscle Beach Santa Monica
formatted_address: Ocean Front Walk, Santa Monica, CA 90401, USA

google_reviews.text: Saw the buffest 71 year old! He was ripped as hell and a great inspiration to work out, get fit now because every bit helps us later.

Cool spot!  Lots of equipment including rings, rope climb, bars, swings and some kids sized too.  Lots of people including families around, it feels safe.  We spent a bit of time just monkeying around.

This area is quite nice for working out and has some nice ocean views and breeze to look at while working out. The equipment like the ropes and swings are quite nice to use, and the location right next to the Santa Monica Pier is very convient.

The perfect place to connect with other active movement creators. They have plenty of tools to discover and explore. Also, the community here is very friendly and open to skill sharing. I love the view of the beach, accessibility of the bathrooms and parking. Also, the boardwalk is not too far of a walk from this location as well. Definitely a must-see if you're a local in Los Angeles or for tourism alike. People from all around the world come to connect through body movement of all kinds. There are tumblers, acro yoga,  hand-to-hand, aerailist, slackliners and more. I love coming to the space to meet up with other like-minded people and connect with the community. This place has so much to offer.
Nice place for fitness enthusiasts who like training in the fresh air and 🌞

name: Dorothy Green Park
formatted_address: 2600 Barnard Wy, Santa Monica, CA 90405, USA

google_reviews.text: Amazing experience. Santa Monica has to be one of the bike-friendliest places I've visited in years. It's actually more efficient to ride around town because you can rent/park the e-bikes/e-scooters just about anywhere.

This park is great for kids of all ages. There are small slides to accommodate toddlers and bigger slides for your more coordinated kids. There is one set of swings for small babies and another set of traditional swings for everyone else. A small rock climbing wall is fun to teach your kids how to climb. The center playground is covered by shade which is nice on hot days.

Peak times are usually from 8am to about 12pm and then there is hardly anyone between 12-2pm after two there is an little after school rush of kids but not as bad as the morning.

Another great thing about this park is that it's so close to the beach. So you can walk to it before or after going to the playground.
A great summer day with friends at the beach. Make sure to get there early because all of LA is using the same parking lot. Also limited parking in the neighborhood.

There was so much parking on a cloudy Sunday. Free parking for handicap but $15 for a day. Close to the pier...just a 7 minutr walk. You can rent bikes on either side of the park. There's a cute cafe with sand and beach chairs set up to enjoy your snacks. They closed up early with the rain. The bathroom werent that bad but no place to dry your hands and no soap. So pack some wipes or sanitizer. The toilet  was nice and soft though. A playground for little ones is also close by. Theres a few benches for eating and watching life at the beach.
Dorothy green park is a wonderful green oasis. Not only for the grassy areas but there are bike rentals and a Perry's  right there. We even got to see them shoot a episode of millie here.

name: Serrania Park
formatted_address: 20726 Wells Dr, Woodland Hills, CA 91364, USA

google_reviews.text: what a beautiful park! especially in the middle of january when everything is nice and green! all the dogs bring such good energy. one part i loved was the cool bent tree that you can sit on (on the sidewalk trail). also gave golden gate park vibes about 2 mins into that same trail!

We’ve been coming here for about a year since moving into the neighborhood, and we love visiting with our dog. The park is very spacious, so there’s plenty of room for dogs to run around. There are a lot gophers and my little toy poodle loves trying to catch them, but they are way too fast for her lol. There’s also a nice path that loops around the park, which we usually stick to since some people either don’t want to pick up after their dogs or don’t notice when they go. I removed one star mainly for that reason.
Great place to bring your dog before sundown. Clean and nicely maintained. Only complaint is all the gopher holes 🕳️ They make the ground sink while you’re walking in the grass and dirt.

Traveling with my baby girl, I visit various playgrounds around Los Angeles, and this one is definitely one of the nicer ones. It has plenty of sensory play features and well-maintained slides, all under large shade structures. While it seems designed for younger kids, toddlers we saw children of different ages enjoying it. Easy to access with nearby parking. Although it's situated on a hill, the playground area itself is flat and stroller-friendly. There's a gate for added safety and restrooms located right next to it. It's surrounded by greenery—not noisy highways or streets—which makes it peaceful. It's also next to a popular dog park with a friendly vibe and lots of dogs and owners. Nearby, there’s a picnic area where you can relax on the grass. It’s also a great starting point for a hike or jog.

Not a park for your kids. I live 5 mins away from here and will not be going here because of the type of trashy individuals who come here. Like all the reviews mention, barely no one keeps their dogs on a leash. I was here once and someone just stood by while their dog pissed on the fence around the playground and the dog's urine got into the playground. There are normal and friendly people here but also some nasty people like I've read in other reviews lol. So believe the bad reviews and look elsewhere if you don't want to deal with pretentious dog owners and dog urine everywhere. Also the playground fence is falling apart and hazardous to small children.

name: Rancho Madera Community Park
formatted_address: 556 Lake Park Dr S, Simi Valley, CA 93065, USA

google_reviews.text: I went to Rancho Madera Park to scout out a location for a family picnic. It is a beautiful park, lots of open space, two playground areas with structures, two restroom facilities, and a large set of pavilions that have picnic tables and grills. There are a number of basketball courts as well as tennis courts. There is a bit of shade here and there, and the picnic tables are covered, but for the most part this is wide open space, so come prepared for a lot of sun. This is a great family park to have a party or event, or to exercise at one of the courts. It is not primarily a walking park, it has some sidewalks but no real trails.

Passing through and loved spending time at this beautiful & peaceful park. I think it's great they give the sports players extra time in the evening to play on cooler, shadier courts. One thing is funny, this is yet another park where the birds are little beggars. Probably because I noticed some people come there just to feed the birds. They weren't too pesky. I shooed them and they kind of got the hint. Other than that, definitely will come back to relax and enjoy the day and watch the sunset!✌❤😍

Beautiful park! It has awesome basketball courts and a fun playground for the kids. There are also tennis courts, volleyball courts and a ton of open space and sidewalks/trails for walking. Additionally there is a lot of grassy open space. There is plenty of parking and everything seems to be very well kept.
One of the best Resorts. One of my favorite places to visit. I love the atmosphere, beautiful people, beautiful place.

This is a very clean safe everyday Park that also hosts special events for Simi Valley today November 3rd was snow days and let me tell you it didn't disappoint not only did my kids loved it but so did my wife and I thank you Simi Valley and thank you Parks and Rec

name: Lake Balboa/Anthony C. Beilenson Park
formatted_address: 6300 Balboa Blvd, Van Nuys, CA 91406, USA

google_reviews.text: Nice lake with a very peaceful atmosphere. There are public bathrooms available, which makes the visit comfortable. There are so many birds around, great for watching nature and relaxing. The walking trail around the lake is beautiful, and a full circle takes about 30 minutes at a relaxed pace. A great spot for a calm walk and some fresh air.

Always a fun experience. I often take photos at this park, and always have fun here! It's quite relaxing. Sometimes I bring my dog to the park here, and we both have an amazing time. Its calming, parking is never a problem either.

Lake Balboa is our favorite place in the entire Valley. My wife and I love walking around the lake. It’s about a mile loop and always feels peaceful and calming, no matter what kind of day you’re having.

There’s so much wildlife to see. We’ve spotted pelicans, ducks, geese, birds, fish, little gophers, and even coyotes and skunks from time to time. It really feels like a slice of nature right in the middle of the city.

It’s a great spot for a walk or a run, or to just bring a blanket and have a picnic. On the weekends, we love the street vendors around the park. It adds to the charm and makes it feel even more like a community space. There’s also a great playground if you have kids and plenty of space to just relax under the trees.

Spring is beautiful with everything in bloom. Summer is perfect for finding shade and escaping the heat. Fall brings cooler weather and changing colors, and even in winter it stays sunny and warm enough to enjoy a peaceful afternoon outside. The mountain views around the Valley are a bonus.

Honestly, I almost didn’t want to write this review because it feels like such a well-kept secret, but it’s too good not to share. Lake Balboa is a true gem and we hope it stays that way for years to come.

Lake Balboa/Anthony C. Beilenson Park is like an oasis in the Valley area. It’s conveniently located near the intersection of Highways 101 and 405, making it easily accessible.

However, the parking lot has many unpaved areas, so be cautious if you have a low car, as the undercarriage might scrape the ground.

The park is very large and features a golf course, walking trails, and a beautiful lake.

It can get crowded on weekends, so it’s not the best place for those seeking peace and quiet. While the lake offers a stunning view, be aware that there are some small bugs around, so keep an eye out.

The walking trails are diverse and well-maintained. Overall, it’s a great place for outdoor activities, but the crowd and bugs can be a concern for some.
My buddies and I have been fishing here for over 10 years. I would not eat the catch but it's a great place to bend your rod.

name: Triunfo Park
formatted_address: 980 Aranmoor Ave, Thousand Oaks, CA 91362, USA

google_reviews.text: In the short time I spent at this lovely park. I got a few shoots of basketball up. Saw many respectful dog owners walking with their dogs. A few girls relaxing enjoying the sunlight and meditating. People coming down from the trails. We'll rounded park.

This is an interesting park. For one, it is against a hill and it is shaped weird. It is average size but because of the shape, it feels small. The play area for example, is small and limited. One thing, there isnt really a pay area doe different size children. When we went, there were tols of older kids so our 2yo couldn't really used the play area. The one thing that i found positive is the trails. There is this short, but wide trail that goes behind the park at the base of the hill that is perfect for small children of all ages. Perfect for beginners, and good enough to give them a taste of what it is like to go hiking...sort of, it very shaded and cool unlike most trails in Southern California. Another thing we noticed was tons of dogs that happened to be off leash but that seems normal for small community parks.

In my opinion it's the nicest park in Thousand Oaks! It's big and beautiful with clean grassland and many trees. Dogs and friendly people hangout every evening, and there are tennis and baseball courts too. The other side is for kids and families activities. It's really cool. It's our favorite dog friendly park.
Beautiful park and well maintained.  Great to walk around and fun for children to play. Lovely birds frequent this park. Just please have your children respect the wildlife

name: Spring Meadow Park
formatted_address: 3283 Spring Meadow Ave, Thousand Oaks, CA 91360, USA

google_reviews.text:
This park was updated in 2017 with several fitness stations and a playground along the 1/3 mile paved path that circles the park. The fitness stations include elevated bars for arm walking, and low bars for hopping over, as well as abdominal training fixtures. The exercises are based on resistance from one's own body weight. There are also croquet and basketball courts. The playground offers something for kids of various ages grouped from 2-5 and 5-12 years. You can also download mobile apps to use in tandem with playing on the playground. There are signs posted with instructions.

There are several picnic tables and built-in grills spread around the perimeter of the park. Use of the courts and picnic areas is on a first-come first-served basis. There is a creek that runs down the center of the park that contains water after it rains, and there are tall trees along the creek that offer shade. There is a large grassy area in the center of the park where football, soccer or frisbee can be played. If you're in the area, I recommend you visit it.

There’s a nice playground, basketball court, exercise stations, corn hole, a couple picnic tables, a grill station, walking path, two huge grass areas, and a creek. Parking is on the street and there aren’t bathrooms.

Nice park, with good size! The playground is fun and the fields are HUGE!

There is even a creek area that's fun for the kids to look at but I wouldn't recommend playing in it. There is also a lot of grass space, a running track, and some work out areas. There is only street parking in the surrounding neighborhood but I always find parking. Great little park.

This park is nestled in the Library area of Thousand Oaks, surrounded by majestic oak trees and lots of grass. Ducks fill the park's pond, and turtles fight for their own spot among them. Large grass areas allow for games and picnics, or events. BBQ areas are available. Volleyball and basketball amenities are included as well. Public restrooms are on site, and handicap parking is available. There is a nice track that runs around the park's circumference, and many visitors like to walk or jog it's length. Private area for parties is also available and a semi-covered picnic area is available for large gatherings. Two playgrounds make this park a hit with children, and the many areas of shade available make it a hit for moms and dads too! Many of the city's events are held here, as it is a great venue for large gatherings. Wonderful park to hang out at!

name: Rancho Simi Community Park

google_reviews.text: Beautiful park, I spent 3 hours here. The river is small, but the boys enjoyed it. There are plenty of open grassy fields that are great for sports, games, or just relaxing. Picnic areas with tables and shade make it easy to spend the whole afternoon here with family and friends. Restrooms are available and usually well maintained, which is very convenient. The park is also dog-friendly, so it’s a nice place to bring pets for a walk. With walking paths, beautiful trees, and a peaceful atmosphere, this park is one of the best spots in the community to unwind and enjoy nature.

Rating: ⭐⭐⭐⭐☆

If you’re a cyclist, this is a fantastic destination—it’s a nice view for a bike ride, with paths that allow you to really soak in the scenery while getting some miles in.
The One Downside
My only complaint is the irresponsible dog owners. It’s frustrating to see people not picking up after their pets. Watching where you step (or where your tires go) shouldn't be part of the workout.
Pro Tip: Great for a sunset ride, just keep an eye on the grass!

A park within a park, this park is typical of an upscale residential park. This particular park is very well maintained and beautifully landscaped!
Here locals can walk in, jog around, play some ball or take the kids over to the ultra-mod playground.
Sheltered, shaded and open space picnic tables and green areas are available for general public use.
There’s a small restroom available and trash containers for upkeep by the users.
Soccer, football and other field sports may be played in the open green space. The basketball court is also a good place for local individuals to play. The ball sports played in this park would less likely be team oriented, lacking the stands, bleachers, larger restrooms and designated parking to accommodate the crowds.
Playing horseshoes or having a workout here seems simple, and void of complications.
Since it is a neighborhood park, street parking along Lang Ranch Pkwy seems adequate, tho a parking lot is available thru the Chumash Indian Museum gates, which are closed at sunset. The additional spaces perhaps also mindful of incoming  Oakbrook Regional Park visitors, above and beyond the neighborhood park.
The Conejo Recreation and Parks District of Thousand Oaks, is the overall manager of the parks here.

name: Arroyo Park
formatted_address: 2105 Socrates Ave, Simi Valley, CA 93065, USA

google_reviews.text: This park is always pretty quiet, never crowded with large groups of people. And when I do see others there beside myself it is almost always one or two people either walking/playing with their dog, or a family over at the Children's Play Area watching as the kids run around playing.  I feel like the park is always in great condition and well taken care of, for example the trash cans are never full or overflowing,  but must be emptied very frequently. Also, the park itself is very clean with no trash littered all over, no cigarette butts on the ground everywhere, and no graffiti sprayed anywhere. I feel like my best time spent there is when I go there to get some quiet time alone and I like to write in my journal. There is a perfect spot for me to just chill there and write at one of the two separate picnic tables available for visitors to sit at. I prefer the table closer to the street, further from the playground. Both tables have awesome trees providing shade along with all the other trees around the park. It's comfortable to be there and quiet and the back end of the park is separated by a chain link fence with an elementary school baseball field on the other side. So being that close in proximity to a school I feel like it adds to the security aspect where I feel like the park doesn't attract trouble as much as maybe others might because of the school, as well as the fact both the school and park are located in the Greek Track which has a close knit community that connect and communicate together regarding neighborhood issues through a sort of neighborhood watch type website called Nextdoor.  It's a charming little gem hidden from the main road but I would recommend visiting if you find yourself in the neighborhood.

I recently visited Point Dume Natural Preserve in Malibu and it was simply spectacular!

From the well-marked parking area atop Cliffside Drive, I embarked on the short but scenic 0.8–1 mile loop up to the promontory. The ascent is gentle with about 100 ft of elevation gain, and the boardwalk trail leads to a viewing platform with sweeping 360‑degree vistas across Zuma Beach, Santa Monica Bay, and even Catalina Island on a clear day.

The coastline views are breathtaking—and on my visit I spotted dolphins surfacing and sea lions perched on rocks below. In spring, migrating whales pass by close enough to see from the lookout, and wildflowers like coreopsis and poppies add seasonal color to the trail.

There’s also a steep staircase descending to Dume Cove and tide pools—where I observed starfish, urchins, and colorful marine life at low tide. Be cautious on the narrow, rusty stairs, but the payoff is worth it for the secluded beach spot

Parking is limited—there are just about 8–10 spaces on Cliffside Drive, and they fill up fast. If those are full, I parked in the larger paid lot at Westward Beach Road and walked up (about 0.5 mile)—worth the effort to access this peaceful preserve.

Pros:

Gorgeous ocean and bluff views
Easy, well-maintained trail and boardwalk
Excellent whale and wildlife spotting opportunities
No entry fee

Cons:

Very limited parking at the bluff
Beach stairs are steep and not ideal for small children or mobility-impaired visitors.

Tips:

Visit in spring for wildflowers and whale watching
Bring water, sunscreen, and good walking shoes.
Arrive early for better chances at bluff parking—otherwise use the Westward Beach lot and hike in
Stay on the designated paths—off‑trail hiking is prohibited
Overall, Point Dume Natural Preserve offers a tranquil coastal escape with stunning nature and panoramic views. Perfect for a short hike, wildlife sightings, or just a peaceful moment above the Pacific. Highly recommended!

google_reviews.text: A nice hike that makes you forget you are in a big city. Plenty of parking.

Arroyo Conejo Trailhead is one of my favorite local hikes — it's the perfect blend of natural beauty, peaceful surroundings, and just the right amount of challenge. Whether you're in the mood for a casual walk or a more adventurous trek, this trail has something for everyone.

The path is well-maintained and easy to follow, with scenic views of rolling hills, native plants, and the soothing sound of the creek along parts of the route. It’s a great spot for families, dog-walkers, and trail runners alike.

One of the highlights is the waterfall (when it’s flowing) — a rewarding sight and a perfect photo op! There are also shaded areas that make it comfortable even on warmer days.

Parking is usually easy, and the trailhead is clearly marked. If you're looking for a local escape into nature, Arroyo Conejo never disappoints. Highly recommend for anyone in the area!

Reconsider unless you don’t mind dealing with poor management and a front desk that makes false claims. This was our second stay here, and it was a completely different (and worse) experience.
We stayed 12/10–12/20 in Site #11, directly next to the construction zone. When we booked, there was no disclosure that construction would be occurring. Despite much of the park appearing empty, we were placed right next to the work area.
We arrived near the end of the workday and didn’t realize how disruptive it would be until the next morning. We were woken up before 6:30am by construction activity, including a concrete truck, even though the park’s own information indicated work wouldn’t start until 7:00am. It was loud and constant throughout the day.
The most unacceptable part was the contractor behavior. Workers took long breaks directly outside our RV, drinking and gambling at a table next to our window while heavy equipment was operating nearby. We have photos. When we asked management to address it, they told us there was “nothing they could do,” including moving the table farther from our window.
When we raised the overall issue, the front desk claimed they tried to offer us another site at check in. That did not happen. We spoke to the manager the next day and he refused to acknowledge we were never warned about construction, offered an 8% discount, and only offered to move us to an inferior site compared to what we paid for. They also refused to provide owner contact information and told us to “write a review.”
For a park at this price point, basic transparency and guest experience should be non negotiable. If construction is happening, disclose it before booking, don’t place guests next to it when other sites are available, and enforce quiet hours and contractor conduct. We booked for the views and a calm stay. Instead, it felt like camping inside a construction site.

name: Andora Trailhead
formatted_address: 9861 Andora Ave, Chatsworth, CA 91311, USA

google_reviews.text:
If you're looking for a classic California-style walking trail with a mix of challenge, scenery, and nature, the Dusty Oak Ridge Trail won’t disappoint. This trail offers a real back-to-nature experience, especially during the summer months when temperatures can soar up to 90°F (32°C), so make sure to bring plenty of water and sun protection.

Trail Surface & Conditions:
The track is mostly dry, dusty, and clay-based, with some loose gravel patches in places. After long dry spells, the trail can get quite powdery underfoot, making it slippery on steeper sections. Good trail shoes with grip are highly recommended.

Scenery & Views:
The trail winds through a beautiful landscape dominated by mature oak trees that provide much-needed shade along certain sections. As you ascend, the terrain opens up to reveal stunning panoramic views of the surrounding city. These city views are definitely a highlight, especially if you make it to the top around sunset.

Flora & Fauna:
Keep an eye out for local wildlife—it's common to spot rabbits, lizards, and a variety of birds. Wildflowers and native shrubs line the paths in spring, but during the peak summer months, the landscape turns a sunburnt gold.

Trail Difficulty:
There’s something for everyone here. The trail network offers varied levels of difficulty—from flatter, family-friendly walks to steeper sections that will definitely get your heart pumping. Beginners can enjoy shorter loops, while seasoned hikers can tackle longer, uphill routes for a good workout.

Final Thoughts:
Overall, Dusty Oak Ridge Trail is a great option for locals and visitors looking to enjoy nature without leaving city limits. Just be prepared for heat, dust, and a good leg workout. Best times to visit are early morning or late afternoon to avoid the peak summer sun.
Beautiful hike at the Andora Trailhead and the Santa Susanna Historic State Park.

What an excellent hike! Lots of vertical thigh burning  and a few not too difficult rock scrambles to get up to the main waterfall but well worth it!
We went on a Tuesday and met very few people on the trail. It was a 2 mile out and back, we did a few loops in the flatlands to see more scenery. My phone battery conked out halfway up so I don’t have pictures of the top waterfall. It was stunning, surrounded by blooming lilacs!

name: Sapwi Trails Community Park
formatted_address: 2225 N Westlake Blvd, Thousand Oaks, CA 91362, USA

google_reviews.text:
This place is much larger than it looks from the street. Sapwi Trails is a beautiful hidden gem with what is clearly a newly installed parking lot and picnic area beside the newly opened disc golf course. Informative signs describe the park as a conservation effort and provide information on the native plant life growing around the park. The hiking trails are very easy and are great for kids and walking dogs.
I like how the park merges the fairly new sport of disc golf with the conservation of our community's unique native ecosystem. The tees are tucked along the hiking trails and provide a number of stunning vistas of the whole park and make it easy to explore this open space. The new picnic area is a beautiful investment in the future of our community and the maintenance of our threatened wildlife. In ten years the picnic area will be well shaded by the various native oaks that were recently planted. In twenty years it will exist under a lush canopy of majestic oaks. Sapwi Trails is clearly a labor of love and now that I've found it I want to tell everyone I know about it.

This is a great new park with a kids' playground and picnic tables on the Erbes/west end, a dirt bike park, disc golf course and hiking trails in the center, and on the east/Westlake Blvd side, a parking lot with picnic tables, paved walking paths and beautiful views of the park and hills to the north, south and west. I recommend you pay it a visit. It may have been affected to some degree by the Woolsey fire of 2018 in Thousand Oaks.
Known for its 19-hole disc golf course and bmx jump track, this is a huge park with good walking trails. No grass fields here, but there are restrooms.

Lots of trails, trash cans throughout, restrooms, water fountains. There is no shade at all in the upper trails where they had demos on opening day (09/22/2018).  I can't say anything about the other trails at the moment :)

Great park. We love the disc golf course, challenging and fun. Nature is beautiful.
Signs about rattle snakes are no joke. We’ve seen a few so far

name: Los Robles Trail
formatted_address: 1373 Potrero Rd, Thousand Oaks, CA 91361, USA

google_reviews.text: I’ve lived here almost 30 years and never knew about this path.  Just be mindful of people’s privacy, the homes that back up to it, & especially the mountain bike riders.  Just stand far to the side and let them pass as it’s much easier on foot than on a bike. It’s a great workout.  My son took me here and I’m so glad he shared it with me.

This is the southern trailhead for the Los Robles West trail.
Off-street parking here is available sporadically - gates often open late, and close early.  On-street parking is extremely limited.
There is a drinking fountain here, but no restroom facilities.  A box at the kiosk holds free bike bells provided by Conejo Open Space Foundation.
A route across Potrero Road leads to the Wendy Trailhead.
The route accessible from the parking lot leads east behind residential areas and either exits at Felton or continues to Angel Vista.
This is a great trail for everyone and every skill levels. Please remember to respect the trail rules, hikers and other riders.
Theirs nothing like being outdoors when its a beautiful day...
A nice little single track trail

name: Mandeville Canyon Park
formatted_address: 2660 Westridge Rd, Los Angeles, CA 90049, USA

google_reviews.text: Great hike and easy for those not looking for something too challenging. Has awesome views of the ocean and panoramic views of downtown as well once you get up high enough. Best thing is that it’s dog friendly!

One of my favorite hikes here. It’s an in and out and there is one flatter option and one with hills up and down. Takes you around 2-3 hours. Great for talking to friends and enjoying some views over the city.

This is a great hike because it isn't a loop. You can set your goal by mileage or time. If you know you have an hour,  you can go up 30 minutes and back 30.

Also amazing about this hike is that there is an easy trail and a hard one. And you can hope between both since they run parallel.

Final bit is that they allow dogs! So bring your pup and enjoy the SM mountains. One of my favorite hikes in LA

Just a note, it gets HOT. No shade on this hike. So bring water and Sun Screen! Also, it's a dusty trail, so maybe don't bring your new training shoes. Lastly, they also allow mountain bikers so just be aware that sometimes they come bowling down the trail.

Mandeville Canyon Trail in Brentwood surprised me with quiet fire‑road paths lined by big homes and native plants. I hiked in late afternoon and saw lovely canyon views, birds, and a peaceful sense of space. Far fewer hikers here than nearby popular trails. The branching trails give a sense of exploration. Great if you want greenery without the bustle
Great place tu run, hike or walk

name: Upper Las Virgenes Cyn Open Space Preserve LV Trailhead
formatted_address: Las Virgenes Road, Calabasas, CA 91302, USA

google_reviews.text: We enjoyed the early morning hike here. The trail is very wide and there are lots of trails to explore. We made it all the way to Bell Canyon and back so we got a pretty good workout. Parking was easy since it was a week day. Definitely a fun one!
Beautiful well maintained hiking trails.

We thoroughly enjoyed hiking the upper Las Virgines canyon preserve.  We started from the gate at the end of Las Virgines road (near Malibu Canyon Apartments). Take a right once you enter , keep going and take the left after crossing the fenced dish tower.  We took the next left for canyon view. Which was amazing. Little steep towards the end  but should be fine for a person with average  fitness. Please don't get deterred by the dog poop every where on the trail, hope some one take action on that though. Irresponsible dog owners should not be allowed in the trail.

The Superbloom was begun here, the rolling hillsides are covered in yellow, purple and some orange and the trail is nice and clean.  You can park on the street right off the trailhead and walk as far as you like.  Not a ton of shade, so bring water, wear sunscreen and a hat...or your gonna have a bad time.👍
Lotsa trails and particularly nice in the spring. Great for birding too.

name: Oak Canyon Trail
formatted_address: 5398 Bromely Dr, Oak Park, CA 91377, USA

google_reviews.text: So happy it's open. So happy it is green! So happy the ducks are back! Eery to see the black silhouette of the trees against the greenery; but thrilling to see new blooms coming from several of them.
It's a great place to bike and walk and for the kids you should look our there is a a cliff side.
It's fairly short and fairly easy for the able bodies person. It was nice to bring the kids here and do some geocaching.
Fun
Nice dh trails

name: Ascot Hills Park
formatted_address: 4371 Multnomah St, Los Angeles, CA 90032, USA

google_reviews.text: This is an urban oasis in that it is in the heart of LA, but is a open space park with steep hills, a few trails and affords some amazing views of downtown LA and nearby Highland Park and community. No amenities to speak of. (Didn’t see bathrooms). Others seem to be coming to get the steep climb exercise. Today it is super green with some native shrubs blooming
We went early in the morning, it's beautiful. You can walk a straight path or go up the hills, so many to choose from.

A Lively Trail Experience with Blooming Flowers and Wildlife

Right now, all the trails in this area are bursting with vibrant blooms, making it a perfect time to visit. While some parts of the trail can be quite steep, there are also easier, less inclined paths available for a more relaxed hike.

If you look closely along the way, you’ll spot plenty of milk snails — charming little creatures besides birds, bees and other little species. Interestingly, they are not native to the U.S., but originally come from parts of the Mediterranean. Introduced to the U.S. in the early 20th century, they have since multiplied rapidly and are now considered an invasive species that can pose a threat to native plants and agriculture.

The trails are spacious and dog-friendly, but visitors are expected to be respectful by cleaning up after their pets. On hot days, however, it’s best to leave your dog at home, as the heated surfaces may harm their paws. You might want to look into this further for your pet’s safety.

Parking: One of the biggest perks of this location is the abundance of free parking, a major plus!

I noticed signage indicating the possible presence of coyotes in the area. To increase your chances of spotting one, consider arriving early in the morning when the trails are quieter. I did not see them this time.
Great train 🏃‍♀️ on trail for beginners friendly. Or for a great sunset views from downtown la
Beautiful trail. It loops around there is some residential part. Please be mindful.

name: SCE Bike Trail
formatted_address: 8777-8789 Lola Ave, Anaheim, CA 92804, USA

google_reviews.text: Needs lights right by the middle of it and on Ball road end , it is very dark at night
Nice small trail from Broadway to Cerritos with a park at each end. Has safe road crossings. Wish it was longer
I enjoy this bike trail because I have nothing else like it around me. It's pretty short and small of a trail but decent. The only negative part is the Major Road Crosswalk on a very busy street.
Good safe bypass between Broadway and Cerritos. You take your bike on dale or magnolia you going to get hit.
It’s a very nice place but you need to be careful with the coyote’s

name: Van Dam Peak
formatted_address: Open Space Trail, Poway, CA 92064, USA

google_reviews.text: Kinda the local bike park. Great climbing and even better descending. There is a trail for everyone here. I love AM/PM hill and minion trail. Ballyhoo is also super fun. Plenty of cool features to checkout. I do see hikers occasionally but definitely more of a bike dominated area.

Van Dam Peak is a nice spot, but be prepared—it’s a bit rough. The trail is rocky, overgrown with bushes, and full of bugs, which can make it tricky to walk through at times. At one point, it didn’t even feel like a real trail anymore since there was no clear signage to guide the way.

That said, it was still a good experience for me. However, it feels like the trail could use more maintenance to make it more enjoyable for hikers. If Poway city put more effort into upkeep, this could be a great spot for everyone.

The biggest highlight here seems to be mountain biking access—it feels much more suited for bikers than for walking. If you’re hiking, just be ready for a bit of a challenge!

Fun, short trail that is steep in some sections that may require some 'bear walking'. There are switchbacks that are less steep. Trail from Iola Way is about 1.35 miles to the peak, with about 600 feet of elevation gain, and offers many views at the top. 60-90 minute hike round trip.

The other side is a mountain bike park for advanced riders. It has some steep downhill sections, blind jumps, and loose gravel.
Steep and very eroded beginning from Evening Creek Park. However it's a great leg and butt burner with views of Carmel Mountain and clear enough to see the ocean.

This trail takes you away from all the city chaos within 20 minutes. When I went I was not sure what to expect, at that time I was wearing my running shoes and I slipped multiple times while coming back so make sure to wear your hiking boots. I went on a weekday and  met only 2 guys throughout the journey and I read in reviews that people saw rattle snake so I was bit scared as I went alone, thankfully I dint saw any rattle snake, but there are more people during weekends. It's an absolutely beautiful hike with lots of scenic views

name: El Cajon Mt Trailhead
formatted_address: Lakeside, CA 92040, USA

google_reviews.text: Yes, the hardest hike in San Diego but you are prepared for it!!! No water, no shade but beautiful views,  not many people and relatively close to home 🤩 Some parts are very technical with different terrain and you don't get bored with a flat stretch 😉 You are constantly going up or down whether you are going up or down the mountain 😉😀

This was a tough hike, but well worth it. Lots of steep ups and downs and one of the only hike I've done in San Diego that I would consider strenuous. The view however is exceptional. I plan on doing it again as there were offshoot trails I'd like to explore. The whole hike took me about 4.5 hours. Depending on your fitness level I wouldn't start this hike later than 8am. The trail is well marked and once you get to the halfway mark a sign will say if its past a certain time dont continue. You can also see El Monte Park and the Flume trail that starts there when you are at the halfway point lookout. Bring lots of water, I know that's staying is cliche, but even though I know it, I ran out a mile from the end. Be safe, have fun, enjoy the views.

When you hike this trail come with these things: lots and lots of water, trekking poles, energy food, and sheer determination! I got to the summit in 3.5 hours and it was the same distance back. So arrive early and start early. Now go for it!
10-11 mile hike, 3500+ of climbing,  make sure you take lots of water because it can get really hot.  It's extremely difficult for beginners,  its a good challenge for those who have experience.
It's getting 5 stars because, nature. That aside, the hike was brutal. It's brutal enough that they close the trail in August because of the heat

name: Giant Dipper Roller Coaster
formatted_address: 3146 Mission Blvd, San Diego, CA 92109, USA

google_reviews.text:
The Giant Dipper Roller Coaster at Belmont Park is an absolute must-ride for anyone who loves classic wooden coasters! Built in 1925, this historic ride has been a San Diego icon for nearly a century, giving generations of thrill-seekers an unforgettable experience.

The ride itself is fast, bumpy, and full of nostalgic charm, with its signature wooden track making every turn feel exciting. The climb to the top builds up just the right amount of anticipation before dropping into a series of fun twists and dips. And the best part? The incredible ocean views—there’s nothing like the feeling of racing along the track with the Pacific right beside you.

It may not be the biggest or fastest coaster out there, but it has a personality and history that can’t be replicated. If you’re visiting Belmont Park, the Giant Dipper is a must-ride experience that perfectly captures the fun and nostalgia of this classic beachfront amusement park!

$8 for a ride.
10 minute wait.
Crazy good rollercoaster that was built in 1925.

Well worth it.

★★★★☆ (4/5)

Came here with a couple of longtime friends for a Sunday walk, covered  4-5 miles along the beach, plenty of fresh air, beautiful day, and slightly questionable legs by the end. 😄

The Giant Dipper is a classic: creaky in a charming way, scenic, and just thrilling enough without pretending to be something it’s not. It’s not the smoothest ride on earth, but honestly, that’s part of the personality. You feel every dip, every rattle, and every laugh from the people riding with you.

Great vibes, ocean views, and a solid dose of nostalgia. Not life-changing, definitely smile-inducing, especially after earning it with a walk. 🤪

Very nice coaster!
Awesome even to present day roller coaster standards. Nice dives, high speed (55 mph) and a long ride (2 minutes). The ratteling wood really adds to the sensation. The bounces when cornering are thrilling as well. We went in the morning when the row was still short.
Haven't ridden the rollercoaster, but it's a good park to walk around, the boardwalk is always fun for people watching, & if you walk a few blocks, you can eat without the tourist trap price gouging.

name: Camino Ruiz Park
formatted_address: 11489 Camino Ruiz, San Diego, CA 92126, USA

google_reviews.text: I generally go to this park for my daily dose of walk. It feels nice to take multiple rounds while enjoying the view of the Los Penasquitos Canyon reserve. There are plenty of sitting options, boards with the history of the area, details of flora and fauna are pretty informative. I regularly spot some small wildlife there. There's a hiking trail also which can be accessed from just outside the park.
Great view but the playground is not well maintained and it's actually hard to enjoy the view because of the tall fences

Very nice park for walking 🚶🚶

Half basket ball 🏀 court is there.

Kids play area , Rest room facilities available.

Nice view around the 🏞️  park.

There is water fall in 2.1 mi from Park parking area.

It’s our neighborhood park.
We regularly go for a walk and our kid love to play on play structure there.
There are baseball ground to play.
Sunset view is beautiful.
There are gazebo to have small gathering and fun activities.
Park is old but nice.
You may have access to Peñasquitos waterfall trail from the park.

This is by far the best park I’ve gone to. I am not a park connoisseur but everything about it is very good. The trails for walking, the playground with variety of activities and the vibe there is just so peaceful. I will be going again, I recommend this park to everyone!

name: Santa Ana River Trail- Katella bridge
formatted_address: Santa Ana, River Trail, Anaheim, CA 92806, USA

google_reviews.text: As an aficionado of trails, I love it when it rains... the walks jogs and runs become so exhilarating.  The santa ana river trail has miles and miles of great places to visit enjoy and even ponder about. This part is by the Anaheim stadium 🏟 area, and parked by the rear coves park área.  Mostly gravel and dirt trails and the occasional paved trail. Even though the trail isn't colorful,  it does present long strips of endless running trails.

This is the only SART bridge that is made of cement. It is a part of Katella that is traversed by cars but is uniquely for bicycles and is separated from the automobile portion by a permanent cement barrier. As one of the things I like about the SART is that it provides cycle riders a safe route that allows riders not to encounter cars, stop lights, stop signs- this bridge is perhaps the most powerful icon of the SART.

name: Aliso Peak Trail
formatted_address: 22710, 22740 Talavera Dr, Laguna Niguel, CA 92677, USA

google_reviews.text: Very steep trail. Short but steep. Views are fantastic from both the peak and from the face of the peak on valido trail. It is a neighborhood trail so it can be crowded and of course great views of Laguna Beach so very popular as an over look. It’s a great trail if you want a short hike or to do as a 2nd hike of the day type thing. Definitely not a “plan your day around doing this trail” type of hike.
The view is amazing, but the trail is a mess. The path is dirt and rocks at a high angle. Be careful there.

It's exciting to see rain clouds approaching from a distance, but part of the waterway overlaps with the course.
This time I got a little scared and retreated.
I will be back again.
Nice trail, it’s really not difficult like the sign says. Very short actually
Great view. Easy hiking.

name: Santa Ana River Trail- mile marker twelve
formatted_address: Santa Ana Riv Trail Bikeway, Orange, CA 92868, USA

google_reviews.text: This SART mile marker had Orangewood to its north and Chapman to its south
For Anaheim, it's a nice place to run or bike. Unfortunately, they've moved out all the homeless, so it wasn't the Safari I was looking forward to...

name: Blanco Park
formatted_address: 5801 Sawtelle Blvd, Culver City, CA 90230, USA

google_reviews.text: Park is next to a school. Nice neighborhood, parking is somewhat tight, but so far have been able to find street parking close by. There is a Vons Pavilions next to the park. Be careful of the round hard seeds from the trees on the lawn.  They can cause you to twist your ankle.
I very nice park to take your kids to, and great for family gatherings.

This is a wonderful park for kids and adults.  Very friendly people.  The area is large enough for a few groups to have parties and has a playground for everyone to enjoy (even adult kids!).  On Fridays there is a very small Farmer's Market where you can purchase very good produce and tamales!
I was there yesterday and offered samples of my Awesome Cookies.  I plan to visit again!

This is a great park to bring children. It is closed Mon. - Fri. In the AM until about 4 or 5 PM because the school reserves it during that time for their students. After 5 PM it's free to open and access daily. It is open all weekend except late at night. I brought my niece here and she loved playing on the jungle gym and swings.

The park is part of a school, it is nice. Downside no parking lot and very very little street parking.
Only a few picnic tables. The field I'd usually busy with soccer teams...again no parking.
No restrooms

name: Rattlesnake Canyon Park
formatted_address: Santa Barbara, CA 93105, USA

google_reviews.text: GPS location does not take you to this Trail. Location pin needs to be updated in maps. Ended up driving further up to Tunnel Trail which was a pretty stellar hike.

My favorite starting point to hike. 2.3miles(one way) from trailhead to Gibraltar road and one EXCELLENT view! Starts following a stream in the canyon and rises out into the mtns. Terrain is single track, varied surface with fairly constant gradient. Motsly shade. Can use connecting trails to get to Tunnel trail and East Camino Cielo road (6miles one way). Large Cats are in this area (have seen a few over the years - no issues), take proper precautions.
Had troubles finding the entrance but ended up exploring in the area. There is an OHV up off of Camino Cielo East. Also save pine mountain fun adventure
Haven't been here for a few years this time the creek has a lot of water forming small waterfalls and a nice to visit again
Views are amazing; very little shade; the hike can get really sketchy very quickly if you go on an “unofficial” trail.

name: Rattlesnake Canyon Trailhead
formatted_address: 1899, 1783 Las Canoas Rd, Santa Barbara, CA 93105, USA

google_reviews.text: This is one of the coolest trailhead with running water along the path. I love this place…

Not the most popular hiking trail by foot traffic but definitely one of my favorites. It’s mostly canopied so a good option for hotter days. Not terribly technical, and the view over the river once you get to the boulders is a highlight.

Great shaded trail. Gets a bit warmer at top of course. Would take it easy on some of the rock sections. Intermediate climb for most people was a nice 5 mile out and back for us took 2:15 RT. Great parking just across from trailhead.
Nice shaded trail with a creek running through it. Fairly steep grade. Good places to cool down in the little pools in the creek.
Pretty challenging hike with steady climbing almost all the way up. Steep sections and river crossings can get slippery if its rainy. Make sure to carry lot water and snacks!

name: Lizards Mouth Rock
formatted_address: Santa Barbara, CA 93105, USA

google_reviews.text: Fun natural rock features to jump around on or squeeze through., though I do wish the graffiti was not present.

Small easy hike to the rock garden and lizards mouth where you can frequently find folks rock climbing and relaxing to take in the spectacular views. Or, investigating the formations and small cave like intrusions on some of the rocks.

It was a short and easy hike, mostly about exploring interesting rock formations and expansive views. I liked that the rocks were grippy, like sandpaper so they were easy to walk on and climb. It's a great spot to enjoy lunch or some snacks and take in the view.
Best view-to-effort hike in Santa Barbara County! An easy 1/8th mile walk from the nearest roadway

If you enjoy exploring around rocks, in small caves, rock scrambling or rock climbing, this park is always worth a visit.  You can spend all day hiking and bouldering until your heart is content, and for serious climbers, there are multiple locations for belaying, rappelling, and some free climbing.

Views of all of Goleta and Santa Barbara are below, as you enjoy the varied and challenging terrain.  Easy trails for hikes and kids, and more challenging routes for scrambling allow all skill levels to enjoy this beautiful rocky place.  Lizards Mouth Rock is the main attraction here, but there is plenty more to see.

name: Seven Falls
formatted_address: Tunnel Rd, Santa Barbara, CA 93105, USA

google_reviews.text: Seven Falls is one of the easiest yet most beautiful hikes I’ve experienced. The distance from the parking area to the main waterfall is about 1.04 miles, making it very manageable. The trail is mostly easy with only a slight incline, so it’s suitable for beginners and even families. Along the way, there are several stunning spots featuring different waterfalls and natural water pools, each with its own unique charm. The sound of flowing water, the lush surroundings, and the peaceful atmosphere make the experience truly unforgettable. The main waterfall is absolutely breathtaking and well worth the short hike. If you’re looking for an easy, scenic outdoor adventure in Santa Barbara, this trail is a fantastic choice.

The hike is fairly easy for a any hiker. There wasn’t much water so there wasn’t any water falls but still worth going. To get to the falls, if you’re going during a fairly dry season, I suggest walking through the creek. It’ll require some climbing over boulders but nothing difficult. It’s such an amazing hike from below as opposed to the top trail. It’s the way less taken. So you can enjoy a nice quiet walk in nature.

Once you get to the “pool” you can climb on one of those rocks to get a different vantage point and see the other “pools”. I was there by myself and it was amazing.

To get back, I suggest going up the path (where the trail would take you down). There’s a rope that you can use to get on the top trail. Then hike the trail down. It goes down from there, so that should make it easier.

After rain during winter it's absolutely gorgeous!!!! Rushing waterfalls, way more than 7!!!! If you make it all the way up there's a larger one. Kind of busy. There is a trail that goes around the river or enough rocks to hop straight up the river (way more fun imo). Definitely dangerous and there was some rock climbing involved but tons of fun!!!! Highly recommend and will be going back until it all dries up again. We went during the summer after rain and there was no water at all, keep that in mind.

Fairly easy to moderate hike if you begin immediately to the right as soon as you see the creeks at entrance. You can either go up the creek or follow a tiny trail alongside it. Not much water but the sight is beautiful. You can then go up the steep climb using a rope for other interesting trails to the top. Your shoes need to be hiking for better support. If you take the other main trail closest to the creek there’s the steep climb down to the falls, I believe could be dangerous and higher risk for slipping. Know your limitations as your safety should always be top priority. Be careful with poison oak as well

Too Awesome! Seven Falls
Seven Grandchildren!
Always Say A Prayer 🙏 CLEAR COAT

Enjoy (OUR) WATER 🌊💦🌊
SPLASH DOWN SUCCESS
SPACE X 🚀🚀🚀🚀🚀🚀🚀🌌
BOOM 💥 PERFECT GIFT 🎁 FUTURE CODED. JAMES 1 17 KJV.

name: Cold Spring Trailhead
formatted_address: 811 E Mountain Dr, Montecito, CA 93108, USA

google_reviews.text: My new favorite running trail. One trail leads to Tangerine falls, the other trail leads to Montecito peak (easier access to pools which the dogs love).

Limited free parking near trailheads. Moderate if going counter clockwise and limited shade, the remaining half is easy and has some shade. No restroom. Dog friendly. Well maintained trail. Great view. Check Alltrails.
There was construction to get to the starting point so had to go around and jump some rocks. It’s an easy hike with lots of terrain. It’s a lot of going uphill but the views are beautiful! ❤️
